#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define a Stack ADT. List any four operations supported by a stack.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Stack** is a linear data structure that follows the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle, where insertions and deletions are performed at one end called the **Top**.

**Four principal operations:**

1. **Push(x):** Inserts element $x$ at the top of the stack.
2. **Pop():** Removes and returns the topmost element.
3. **Peek() / Top():** Returns the top element without deletion.
4. **isEmpty():** Returns `true` if the stack has no elements, else `false`.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for operations)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between stack overflow and stack underflow conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Stack Overflow** | **Stack Underflow** |
|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Condition | $Top = MAX\_SIZE - 1$ | $Top = -1$ |
| Triggered by | Push operation on a full stack | Pop operation on an empty stack |
| Meaning | No more space to insert | No element to remove |
| Recovery | Cannot insert until pop is performed | Cannot delete; stack must be filled first |

*(3 marks — tabular differentiation with conditions)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the array-based implementation of a stack with its Push and Pop algorithms. Discuss how overflow and underflow conditions are handled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the following infix expression to postfix using a stack, showing the **complete stack trace** at every step:
$$\left( A + B \right) \times \left( C - D \right) / E + F$$ **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

In **array-based implementation**, a stack is represented using a one-dimensional array of size $MAX\_SIZE$ and an integer variable $Top$ initialized to $-1$.

**Push(x) Algorithm:**

```
Step 1: IF Top == MAX_SIZE - 1 THEN
            PRINT "Stack Overflow"
            EXIT
Step 2: Top = Top + 1
Step 3: Stack[Top] = x
Step 4: EXIT
```

**Pop() Algorithm:**

```
Step 1: IF Top == -1 THEN
            PRINT "Stack Underflow"
            EXIT
Step 2: x = Stack[Top]
Step 3: Top = Top - 1
Step 4: RETURN x
```

**Overflow Handling:** Detected when `Top == MAX_SIZE - 1` before insertion. The push is rejected and an error message is displayed.

**Underflow Handling:** Detected when `Top == -1` before deletion. The pop is rejected.

**Time Complexity:** Both Push and Pop execute in $O(1)$ constant time since only a constant number of operations (comparison, increment/decrement, assignment) are performed irrespective of $MAX\_SIZE$.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagrams, 3 for algorithms, 2 for time complexity and error handling)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

We apply the **Shunting-Yard Algorithm** with precedence: $() > \times, / > +, -$.

**Stack Trace Table:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Token} && \text{Stack} && \text{Postfix Output} \\
&\hline
&( && ( && - \\
&A && ( && A \\
&+ && ( + && A \\
&B && ( + && A \, B \\
&) && \text{(empty)} && A \, B \, + \\
&\times && \times && A \, B \, + \\
&( && \times ( && A \, B \, + \\
&C && \times ( && A \, B \, + \, C \\
&- && \times ( - && A \, B \, + \, C \\
&D && \times ( - && A \, B \, + \, C \, D \\
&) && \times && A \, B \, + \, C \, D \, - \\
&/ && / && A \, B \, + \, C \, D \, - \\
&E && / && A \, B \, + \, C \, D \, - \, E \\
&+ && + && A \, B \, + \, C \, D \, - \, E \, / \\
&F && + && A \, B \, + \, C \, D \, - \, E \, / \, F \\
&\text{(end)} && \text{(empty)} && A \, B \, + \, C \, D \, - \, E \, / \, F \, +
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Final Postfix Expression:
$$\boxed{A \, B \, + \, C \, D \, - \, \times \, E \, / \, F \, +}$$

*(7 marks: 6 for the trace table, 1 for final answer)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the **linked-list-based implementation** of a stack. Compare it with array-based implementation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Evaluate the following postfix expression using a stack. Show the **complete step-by-step evaluation trace**:
$$5 \, 1 \, 2 \, + \, 4 \, \times \, + \, 3 \, -$$ **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

In **linked-list-based implementation**, each node contains two fields: `data` (the element) and `next` (pointer to the node below). The stack's `Top` is a pointer to the head of the linked list, initialized to `NULL`.

**Push(x) Algorithm (Linked List):**

```
Step 1: CREATE newNode
Step 2: newNode.data = x
Step 3: newNode.next = Top
Step 4: Top = newNode
```

**Pop() Algorithm (Linked List):**

```
Step 1: IF Top == NULL THEN
            PRINT "Stack Underflow"
            EXIT
Step 2: temp = Top
Step 3: x = Top.data
Step 4: Top = Top.next
Step 5: FREE temp
Step 6: RETURN x
```

**Comparison Table:**

| **Feature** | **Array-Based Stack** | **Linked-List-Based Stack** |
|-------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Memory | Fixed size $O(n)$ | Dynamic, $O(k)$ where $k$ = current elements |
| Overflow | Possible (`isFull` returns true) | Not possible (until heap exhausted) |
| Underflow | Possible | Possible |
| Memory Waste | Possible (unutilized slots) | No waste |
| Access Time | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Implementation | Simpler | Slightly more complex (pointer management) |
| Resize | Not possible without recreation | Natural growth |

*(7 marks: 3 for algorithms, 3 for comparison table, 1 for diagram mention)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Postfix Evaluation Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Read: 5} && \rightarrow \text{Stack: } [5] \\
&\text{Read: 1} && \rightarrow \text{Stack: } [5, 1] \\
&\text{Read: 2} && \rightarrow \text{Stack: } [5, 1, 2] \\
&\text{Read: +} && \rightarrow \text{Pop 2, 1; compute 1+2=3; Push 3; Stack: } [5, 3] \\
&\text{Read: 4} && \rightarrow \text{Stack: } [5, 3, 4] \\
&\text{Read: *} && \rightarrow \text{Pop 4, 3; compute 3*4=12; Push 12; Stack: } [5, 12] \\
&\text{Read: +} && \rightarrow \text{Pop 12, 5; compute 5+12=17; Push 17; Stack: } [17] \\
&\text{Read: 3} && \rightarrow \text{Stack: } [17, 3] \\
&\text{Read: -} && \rightarrow \text{Pop 3, 17; compute 17-3=14; Push 14; Stack: } [14]
\end{aligned}
$$

#### ✅ Final Result:
$$\boxed{5 \, 1 \, 2 \, + \, 4 \, \times \, + \, 3 \, - \, = \, 14}$$

*(7 marks: 6 for the trace, 1 for the final answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Operand Order Error:** When popping two operands for an operator, students often compute as `op1 op op2` instead of the correct `op2 op op1`. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Missing Trace Table:** Writing just the final answer without the step-by-step stack trace results in losing 4–5 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Confusing Prefix with Postfix:** When converting infix to prefix, the algorithm direction is **right-to-left**, not left-to-right. Mixing these up is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Forgetting Parentheses Handling:** In the Shunting-Yard algorithm, always pop until a matching `(` is found when encountering `)`. Skipping this loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Not Mentioning Time Complexity:** KTU 2024 scheme requires justification of $O(1)$ for stack operations. A bare algorithm without complexity analysis loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Stack** is a **LIFO** (Last-In-First-Out) linear ADT.
- ✅ The two primary operations are **Push** (insert) and **Pop** (delete), both at the **Top**.
- ✅ Auxiliary operations: **Peek/Top**, **isEmpty**, **isFull**, **Size**.
- ✅ In **array-based** stacks, initialize `Top = -1`; overflow when `Top = MAX_SIZE - 1`; underflow when `Top = -1`.
- ✅ In **linked-list-based** stacks, initialize `Top = NULL`; underflow when `Top = NULL`; **no overflow** under normal heap availability.
- ✅ All standard stack operations (Push, Pop, Peek, isEmpty, isFull) have **$O(1)$ time complexity**.
- ✅ Space complexity: **$O(n)$** for array-based, **$O(k)$** for linked-list-based (where $k$ = current number of elements).
- ✅ **Infix → Postfix** conversion uses the **Shunting-Yard Algorithm** (Dijkstra, 1961).
- ✅ **Operator Precedence (descending):** Exponentiation ($\wedge$) > Multiplication/Division/Modulus ($\times, /, \%$) > Addition/Subtraction ($+, -$).
- ✅ **Associativity:** Exponentiation is **Right-associative**; all others are **Left-associative**.
- ✅ **Infix → Prefix** conversion: scan the expression from **right to left**, then reverse the result.
- ✅ **Postfix Evaluation:** Always pop in the order `op2 op op1` (second-popped operand is the **left** operand).
- ✅ **Key Applications:** Recursion (call stack), expression evaluation, backtracking algorithms, browser history, undo/redo mechanisms, DFS traversal, parenthesis matching, syntax parsing in compilers.
- ✅ **Balanced Parentheses Algorithm:** Push opening brackets; on closing brackets, pop and match; valid if stack is empty at end and no mismatch occurred.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:** Always draw the **stack trace table** in tabular form with three columns — *Token*, *Stack*, *Output* — for conversion problems. Examiners award full marks only when the **complete trace** is shown, not just the final result.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Queues - Circular Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Queues - Circular Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Circular Queue** is a linear data structure that adheres to the **FIFO (First In, First Out)** principle, implemented using a single one-dimensional array in which the **last position is logically connected back to the first position**, forming a ring. This logical wrap-around is achieved through **modulo arithmetic** applied to the `front` and `rear` pointer updates, thereby enabling efficient reuse of the vacated memory cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The circular queue is introduced in Module 1 of *PEECT522 – Data Structures* to overcome the *wastage-of-space* defect of the **Simple Linear Queue** (also called the *Straight Queue*). It is one of the most frequently asked topics in KTU ESE and is often clubbed with a **trace-based numerical problem**.

### 1.2 Concept Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **circular railway track with stations numbered `0, 1, 2, …, (N-1)`**. A train can enter only at the `rear` station and exit only from the `front` station. After the train leaves from station `N-1`, the next station to be used is station `0` (the track loops back), instead of wasting all the empty tracks beyond `N-1`.

A more relatable real-world analogy is the **revolving door of a hotel**: people enter from one side (rear), exit from the other (front), and the door keeps rotating — no slot is permanently locked.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms to Remember:**
> - **`SIZE`** → Total capacity of the array.
> - **`front`** → Index of the element at the front of the queue (used for deletion).
> - **`rear`** → Index of the last inserted element (used for insertion).
> - **`queue[MAX]`** → The underlying array storage.
> - **`count`** → An optional counter tracking the number of elements (resolves the *full vs. empty* ambiguity).

### 1.3 Salient Features

- **Reusability of memory:** Cells freed by `dequeue` are immediately available for `enqueue`.
- **No shifting required:** Unlike the simple queue, elements are **not relocated** during deletion; only pointers move.
- **Time Complexity:** Both `enqueue` and `dequeue` are **$O(1)$** — a constant-time operation.
- **Space Complexity:** **$O(N)$** for an array of size $N$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" — Motivation Behind the Circular Queue

A **Simple Linear Queue** suffers from a phenomenon called the **"False Overflow"**. Consider an array of size `5`:

1. We insert 5 elements — the queue is `Full`.
2. We delete 2 elements — 2 slots at the front are now empty.
3. We try to insert 2 more new elements — the system says `Overflow!`, even though 2 slots are physically free.

This is because `rear` has already reached the end of the array (`MAX - 1`) and cannot move further, irrespective of the actual occupancy. **The circular queue eliminates this issue by re-routing the rear pointer back to index 0 once it crosses the boundary.**

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**A. Initialization**
- `front = -1` (or `0`, depending on convention; KTU most commonly uses `0` after the first insertion, so initialized to `-1`).
- `rear = -1`
- `count = 0` (or we leave one slot empty as a sentinel).

**B. `isEmpty()` — Empty Check**

$$ \text{isEmpty} = (\text{count} == 0) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad (\text{front} == -1) $$

**C. `isFull()` — Full Check (Two Standard Approaches)**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU textbooks follow **two different conventions** to distinguish `Full` from `Empty`. **Memorize both** because the exam may ask either form.

**Approach 1 — Using a `count` variable:**

$$ \text{isFull} = (\text{count} == \text{SIZE}) $$

**Approach 2 — Leaving one slot vacant (sentinel slot):**

$$ \text{isFull} = \bigl((\text{rear} + 1) \;\bmod\; \text{SIZE} == \text{front}\bigr) $$

**D. `enqueue(x)` — Insertion Logic**

1. Check `isFull()`. If true, return **Overflow** error.
2. If the queue is empty (`front == -1`), set `front = 0`.
3. Update the rear using modulo:

$$ \text{rear} = (\text{rear} + 1) \;\bmod\; \text{SIZE} $$

4. Place the element: `queue[rear] = x`.
5. Increment `count` by 1.

**E. `dequeue()` — Deletion Logic**

1. Check `isEmpty()`. If true, return **Underflow** error.
2. Retrieve the value: `x = queue[front]`.
3. If `front == rear` (only one element was present), reset both:

$$ \text{front} = \text{rear} = -1 $$

4. Otherwise, advance the front using modulo:

$$ \text{front} = (\text{front} + 1) \;\bmod\; \text{SIZE} $$

5. Decrement `count` by 1.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Formula / Condition | Returns | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Empty check (with count) | $\text{count} == 0$ | Boolean | $O(1)$ |
| Full check (with count) | $\text{count} == \text{SIZE}$ | Boolean | $O(1)$ |
| Full check (sentinel) | $(\text{rear} + 1) \bmod \text{SIZE} == \text{front}$ | Boolean | $O(1)$ |
| Rear advance | $\text{rear} = (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod \text{SIZE}$ | New index | $O(1)$ |
| Front advance | $\text{front} = (\text{front} + 1) \bmod \text{SIZE}$ | New index | $O(1)$ |
| Total number of elements | $\bigl(\text{SIZE} - \text{front} + \text{rear} + 1\bigr) \bmod \text{SIZE}$ | Integer | $O(1)$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPU Scheduling:** The *Round Robin* scheduling algorithm uses a circular queue to manage process turn-taking in operating systems.
- **Memory Management:** Circular buffers in **device drivers, audio/video streaming, and network packet handling** (e.g., producer-consumer problems).
- **Traffic Systems:** Traffic light controllers cycle through signal states in a circular fashion.
- **Disk Drives:** Circular buffers store data in fixed-size ring buffers in I/O subsystems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Trace

### 3.1 Detailed Trace: A Worked-Out Example

**Problem Statement:**
Consider a circular queue of capacity `SIZE = 5`. Initially `front = -1`, `rear = -1`. Perform the following operations in order and show the queue state, `front`, `rear`, and `count` after each:

1. `enqueue(10)`
2. `enqueue(20)`
3. `enqueue(30)`
4. `dequeue()`
5. `enqueue(40)`
6. `enqueue(50)`
7. `enqueue(60)`
8. `dequeue()`
9. `enqueue(70)`

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 0 — Initial State**

$$ \text{front} = -1, \quad \text{rear} = -1, \quad \text{count} = 0, \quad \text{queue} = [\,,\,,\,,\,] $$

**Step 1 — `enqueue(10)`**
Queue is empty → set `front = 0`. Then `rear = (-1 + 1) mod 5 = 0`. Place 10 at index 0. `count = 1`.

$$ \text{front} = 0, \quad \text{rear} = 0, \quad \text{count} = 1, \quad \text{queue} = [10,\,,\,,\,,\,] $$

**Step 2 — `enqueue(20)`**

$$ \text{rear} = (0 + 1) \bmod 5 = 1. \quad \text{queue[1]} = 20, \quad \text{count} = 2 $$

$$ \text{front} = 0, \quad \text{rear} = 1, \quad \text{count} = 2, \quad \text{queue} = [10,\,20,\,,\,,\,] $$

**Step 3 — `enqueue(30)`**

$$ \text{rear} = (1 + 1) \bmod 5 = 2. \quad \text{queue[2]} = 30, \quad \text{count} = 3 $$

$$ \text{front} = 0, \quad \text{rear} = 2, \quad \text{count} = 3, \quad \text{queue} = [10,\,20,\,30,\,,\,] $$

**Step 4 — `dequeue()`**
Element at `front` (i.e., 10) is removed. `front != rear`, so advance:

$$ \text{front} = (0 + 1) \bmod 5 = 1, \quad \text{count} = 2 $$

$$ \text{front} = 1, \quad \text{rear} = 2, \quad \text{count} = 2, \quad \text{queue} = [\,,\,30,\,20\text{(logical order)}\,,\,] $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical View:** Although index `0` is now physically free, the queue still contains `[20, 30]` in that order. We do **not** shift elements; the **modular logic** handles reuse.

**Step 5 — `enqueue(40)`**

$$ \text{rear} = (2 + 1) \bmod 5 = 3. \quad \text{queue[3]} = 40, \quad \text{count} = 3 $$

$$ \text{queue} = [\,,\,30,\,40,\,20\text{(logical)}\,] $$

**Step 6 — `enqueue(50)`**

$$ \text{rear} = (3 + 1) \bmod 5 = 4. \quad \text{queue[4]} = 50, \quad \text{count} = 4 $$

$$ \text{queue} = [\,,\,30,\,40,\,50] \quad \text{(logical: 20, 30, 40, 50)} $$

**Step 7 — `enqueue(60)`**
Check `isFull`: `count = 4 < 5` → not full.

$$ \text{rear} = (4 + 1) \bmod 5 = 0. \quad \text{queue[0]} = 60, \quad \text{count} = 5 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the moment of wrap-around!** `rear` was at index `4` (the last slot), and modulo arithmetic wrapped it back to index `0` — exactly where 10 was previously deleted. **This is precisely the advantage of the circular queue** — that freed slot is now reused.

$$ \text{front} = 1, \quad \text{rear} = 0, \quad \text{count} = 5, \quad \text{queue} = [60,\,30,\,40,\,50,\,20\text{(logical)}] $$

**Step 8 — `dequeue()`**
Element at `front = 1` is `30` — removed.

$$ \text{front} = (1 + 1) \bmod 5 = 2, \quad \text{count} = 4 $$

$$ \text{front} = 2, \quad \text{rear} = 0, \quad \text{count} = 4 $$

**Step 9 — `enqueue(70)`**

$$ \text{rear} = (0 + 1) \bmod 5 = 1. \quad \text{queue[1]} = 70, \quad \text{count} = 5 $$

### 3.2 Final State Summary Table

| Operation | front | rear | count | Logical Queue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Start | -1 | -1 | 0 | `[]` |
| enqueue(10) | 0 | 0 | 1 | `[10]` |
| enqueue(20) | 0 | 1 | 2 | `[10, 20]` |
| enqueue(30) | 0 | 2 | 3 | `[10, 20, 30]` |
| dequeue() | 1 | 2 | 2 | `[20, 30]` |
| enqueue(40) | 1 | 3 | 3 | `[20, 30, 40]` |
| enqueue(50) | 1 | 4 | 4 | `[20, 30, 40, 50]` |
| enqueue(60) | 1 | 0 | 5 | `[20, 30, 40, 50, 60]` |
| dequeue() | 2 | 0 | 4 | `[30, 40, 50, 60]` |
| enqueue(70) | 2 | 1 | 5 | `[30, 40, 50, 60, 70]` |

### 3.3 Mathematical Derivation of Element Count

To compute the number of elements present at any time without using a `count` variable:

$$ \text{Number of elements} = \bigl(\text{SIZE} - \text{front} + \text{rear} + 1\bigr) \;\bmod\; \text{SIZE} $$

**Reasoning:** The circular distance from `front` to `rear` is `(rear - front + SIZE) mod SIZE`. Adding 1 gives the count of all positions **inclusive of `rear`**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Circular Queue Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize: front = -1, rear = -1, count = 0] --> B{Choose Operation}
    B -->|Enqueue x| C{isFull check}
    C -->|Yes| D[Return OVERFLOW Error]
    C -->|No| E[front == -1 ? Set front = 0]
    E --> F[rear = rear + 1 mod SIZE]
    F --> G[queue rear = x]
    G --> H[count = count + 1]
    H --> Z[Operation Complete]
    B -->|Dequeue| I{isEmpty check}
    I -->|Yes| J[Return UNDERFLOW Error]
    I -->|No| K[x = queue front]
    K --> L{front == rear ?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Reset front = -1, rear = -1]
    L -->|No| N[front = front + 1 mod SIZE]
    M --> O[count = count - 1]
    N --> O
    O --> Z
    B -->|Display| P{isEmpty check}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Print Empty Queue]
    P -->|No| R[i = front]
    R --> S{i == rear ?}
    S -->|No| T[Print queue i]
    T --> U[i = i + 1 mod SIZE]
    U --> S
    S -->|Yes| Z
    Q --> Z
```

### 4.2 Logical Ring View of the Circular Queue

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    idx0[Index 0] --- idx1[Index 1] --- idx2[Index 2] --- idx3[Index 3] --- idx4[Index 4] --- idx0
    idx0 -. holds 60 .-> rearNode(Rear Pointer)
    idx2 -. points to 30 .-> frontNode(Front Pointer)
    idx1 -. holds 70 .- idx0
```

### 4.3 Algorithm Pseudocode (C-style) — Standard KTU Reference

```c
#define SIZE 5

int queue[SIZE];
int front = -1, rear = -1, count = 0;

void enqueue(int x) {
    if (count == SIZE) {
        printf("Queue Overflow");
        return;
    }
    if (front == -1)
        front = 0;
    rear = (rear + 1) % SIZE;
    queue[rear] = x;
    count++;
}

int dequeue() {
    if (count == 0) {
        printf("Queue Underflow");
        return -1;
    }
    int x = queue[front];
    if (front == rear) {
        front = -1;
        rear = -1;
    } else {
        front = (front + 1) % SIZE;
    }
    count--;
    return x;
}
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Define a circular queue. How is it different from a simple linear queue?**

**Model Answer:**

A **circular queue** is a variant of the queue data structure in which the last position is connected back to the first position, forming a logical ring. The `rear` and `front` pointers are updated using **modulo arithmetic** so that vacated slots can be reused.

The key difference from a **simple linear queue** is summarized below:

| Property | Simple Linear Queue | Circular Queue |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Reuse | Not possible — causes false overflow | Vacated slots reused via modulo wrap-around |
| Rear Update | `rear = rear + 1` | `rear = (rear + 1) % SIZE` |
| Overflow Type | May report overflow even when space exists | Only true overflow when array is genuinely full |
| Element Shifting | Sometimes required (in some implementations) | Not required |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for comparison)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**State and explain the conditions to check whether a circular queue is full or empty.**

**Model Answer:**

Two standard conventions are used:

**1. Using a `count` variable:**

$$ \text{isEmpty} : \text{count} == 0 \qquad \text{isFull} : \text{count} == \text{SIZE} $$

**2. Using the sentinel-slot method (no `count`):**

$$ \text{isEmpty} : \text{front} == -1 \qquad \text{isFull} : (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod \text{SIZE} == \text{front} $$

In the sentinel method, we deliberately leave **one slot empty** to distinguish the *full* state from the *empty* state, because otherwise both states would result in `front == rear`. The `count` variable approach is cleaner and is preferred in most academic solutions at KTU.

*(3 marks — 1 for empty condition, 1 for full condition, 1 for explanation of the ambiguity)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Internal Choice A]`**

**(a)** Write the algorithm for `enqueue` and `dequeue` operations on a circular queue implemented using an array. Clearly state the conditions for overflow and underflow. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The standard KTU textbook (Tenenbaum / Lipschutz / Forouzan-style) algorithm is as follows. Using the `count` variable approach for clarity:

**Algorithm: `CQINSERT(QUEUE, FRONT, REAR, COUNT, MAX, ITEM)`**

1. **If** `COUNT == MAX` then
   - Print `"Queue Overflow"` and **Return**.
2. **If** `COUNT == 0` then
   - Set `FRONT = 0`.
3. Set `REAR = (REAR + 1) mod MAX`.
4. Set `QUEUE[REAR] = ITEM`.
5. Set `COUNT = COUNT + 1`.
6. **Return**.

**Algorithm: `CQDELETE(QUEUE, FRONT, REAR, COUNT, ITEM)`**

1. **If** `COUNT == 0` then
   - Print `"Queue Underflow"` and **Return**.
2. Set `ITEM = QUEUE[FRONT]`.
3. **If** `COUNT == 1` then
   - Set `FRONT = -1`, `REAR = -1`.
   **Else**
   - Set `FRONT = (FRONT + 1) mod MAX`.
4. Set `COUNT = COUNT - 1`.
5. **Return**.

**Overflow Condition:** `COUNT == MAX`

**Underflow Condition:** `COUNT == 0`

> **Marking Distribution:** Conditions – 2 marks, Enqueue – 2.5 marks, Dequeue – 2.5 marks.

---

**(b)** Consider a circular queue of size `6` with the following initial values: `front = 2`, `rear = 2`. Perform the operations listed below and show the values of `front`, `rear`, and the queue contents after each step:

`enqueue(15), enqueue(25), enqueue(35), dequeue(), dequeue(), enqueue(45), enqueue(55), enqueue(65), enqueue(75)`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Initial State:**

$$ \text{front} = 2, \quad \text{rear} = 2, \quad \text{count} = 1, \quad \text{queue} = [\,,\,15,\,,\,,\,] $$

(We assume index 2 already contains some prior element, OR we can interpret `front = rear = 2` as a queue with one element. KTU examiners usually treat it as a single-element queue.)

| # | Operation | front | rear | count | queue (logical) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | enqueue(15) | 2 | 3 | 2 | `[15, 15]` — **WAIT:** re-read initial |

Let us re-interpret cleanly. The standard KTU convention: assume `front = rear = 2` means **the queue is currently empty** (just initialized) **OR** it has exactly one element. Given that the trace says `enqueue(15)` is the first operation, we treat the initial state as **empty with `front = 2`, `rear = 2`, `count = 0`** (a slight KTU irregularity to test the student).

**Step 1 — `enqueue(15)`:**
`count = 0` → set `front = 2` (already so). `rear = (2 + 1) mod 6 = 3`. `queue[3] = 15`, `count = 1`.

$$ \text{front} = 2, \quad \text{rear} = 3, \quad \text{count} = 1, \quad \text{queue[3]} = 15 $$

**Step 2 — `enqueue(25)`:**
`rear = 4`, `queue[4] = 25`, `count = 2`.

**Step 3 — `enqueue(35)`:**
`rear = 5`, `queue[5] = 35`, `count = 3`.

**Step 4 — `dequeue()`:**
Remove `queue[front]` (logical first element). `front = (2+1) mod 6 = 3`, `count = 2`.

**Step 5 — `dequeue()`:**
`front = (3+1) mod 6 = 4`, `count = 1`.

**Step 6 — `enqueue(45)`:**
`rear = (5+1) mod 6 = 0` — **wrap-around!** `queue[0] = 45`, `count = 2`.

**Step 7 — `enqueue(55)`:**
`rear = 1`, `queue[1] = 55`, `count = 3`.

**Step 8 — `enqueue(65)`:**
`rear = 2`, `queue[2] = 65`, `count = 4`.

**Step 9 — `enqueue(75)`:**
`rear = 3`, `queue[3] = 75`, `count = 5`.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Operation | front | rear | count | queue[0] | queue[1] | queue[2] | queue[3] | queue[4] | queue[5] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Start | 2 | 2 | 0 | – | – | – | – | – | – |
| enqueue(15) | 2 | 3 | 1 | – | – | – | 15 | – | – |
| enqueue(25) | 2 | 4 | 2 | – | – | – | 15 | 25 | – |
| enqueue(35) | 2 | 5 | 3 | – | – | – | 15 | 25 | 35 |
| dequeue() | 3 | 5 | 2 | – | – | – | – | 25 | 35 |
| dequeue() | 4 | 5 | 1 | – | – | – | – | – | 35 |
| enqueue(45) | 4 | 0 | 2 | 45 | – | – | – | – | 35 |
| enqueue(55) | 4 | 1 | 3 | 45 | 55 | – | – | – | 35 |
| enqueue(65) | 4 | 2 | 4 | 45 | 55 | 65 | – | – | 35 |
| enqueue(75) | 4 | 3 | 5 | 45 | 55 | 65 | 75 | – | 35 |

> **Note to students:** The key point being tested is the **wrap-around behaviour at Step 6** — `rear` going from `5 → 0` using modulo.

---

#### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Internal Choice B]`**

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of a simple linear queue. With a suitable diagram, explain how a circular queue overcomes these limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of a Simple Linear Queue:**

1. **False Overflow:** Even when free slots exist at the front, the queue reports overflow because `rear` has reached the end of the array.

2. **Inefficient Memory Utilization:** Vacated cells are never reused; memory is permanently wasted after deletions.

3. **No Wrap-Around:** The `rear` pointer cannot loop back to `0` once it hits `MAX - 1`.

**How the Circular Queue Solves These:**

- `rear` and `front` are updated using **modulo arithmetic**: `rear = (rear + 1) mod SIZE` and `front = (front + 1) mod SIZE`.
- After a deletion, the freed index is immediately available for the next `enqueue`.
- This achieves **100% memory utilization** (up to `SIZE` elements always storable without false overflow).

**Diagram Comparison (Textual):**

| Linear Queue (after 3 inserts and 2 deletes) | Circular Queue (same operations) |
|---|---|
| `[_ _ X X X]` — slots 0, 1 wasted, slot 5 unavailable | `[X X X _ _]` — all 5 slots reusable in a ring |

*(7 marks — 2 for limitations, 2 for the solution, 2 for diagram, 1 for example.)*

---

**(b)** Implement a circular queue using an array in C. Clearly show the structure definition and the `enqueue`, `dequeue`, and `display` functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#define SIZE 5

int queue[SIZE];
int front = -1, rear = -1, count = 0;

void enqueue(int x) {
    if (count == SIZE) {
        printf("\nQueue Overflow!");
        return;
    }
    if (front == -1) front = 0;
    rear = (rear + 1) % SIZE;
    queue[rear] = x;
    count++;
    printf("%d enqueued.", x);
}

int dequeue() {
    if (count == 0) {
        printf("\nQueue Underflow!");
        return -1;
    }
    int x = queue[front];
    if (front == rear) {
        front = rear = -1;
    } else {
        front = (front + 1) % SIZE;
    }
    count--;
    printf("%d dequeued.", x);
    return x;
}

void display() {
    if (count == 0) {
        printf("\nQueue is empty.");
        return;
    }
    printf("\nQueue: ");
    int i = front;
    for (int k = 0; k < count; k++) {
        printf("%d ", queue[i]);
        i = (i + 1) % SIZE;
    }
}

int main() {
    enqueue(10); enqueue(20); enqueue(30);
    display();
    dequeue();
    display();
    enqueue(40); enqueue(50); enqueue(60);
    display();
    return 0;
}
```

> **Marking:** Structure – 1, Enqueue – 2, Dequeue – 2, Display – 1, Correctness – 1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use modulo `% SIZE`** when updating `front` or `rear` — this is the **#1 reason students lose marks**. Always write `(rear + 1) % SIZE`, never just `rear + 1`.
> 2. **Confusing the empty and full conditions** — both can give `front == rear`. The KTU examiner deducts **1–2 marks** if you don't explain how you disambiguate (either via `count` or a sentinel slot).
> 3. **Resetting `front` and `rear` to `-1` when the last element is deleted** — failing to do so causes future `enqueue` operations to malfunction. This is a **classic 1-mark deduction** in trace problems.
> 4. **Shifting elements physically** during `dequeue` — the circular queue **never shifts elements**; it only advances pointers. Writing code that does `for(i=0; i<rear; i++) queue[i]=queue[i+1]` will cost you marks.
> 5. **In trace problems, forgetting to update the `count` variable** alongside `front` and `rear`.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Circular Queue** is a FIFO structure implemented in a **ring-shaped array** using **modulo arithmetic** to wrap pointers around.
- ✅ The **fundamental update rule** is `pointer = (pointer + 1) mod SIZE`.
- ✅ The **Full** and **Empty** states both produce `front == rear` — they must be **disambiguated** using either a `count` variable or a **sentinel slot**.
- ✅ Both `enqueue` and `dequeue` operate in **$O(1)$ time**; total space is **$O(N)$**.
- ✅ The circular queue **eliminates the "false overflow"** problem of the simple linear queue, enabling **full memory utilization**.
- ✅ The KTU syllabus also tags this topic as a common **trace question** — practice enqueue/dequeue sequences that produce a **wrap-around** at `rear = SIZE - 1 → 0`.
- ✅ Real-world applications include **CPU round-robin scheduling, circular buffers in device drivers, and producer-consumer problems**.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always state both the **overflow** and **underflow** conditions explicitly in algorithms — KTU examiners reward this with bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priority Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Priority Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Priority Queue (PQ)** is an Abstract Data Type (ADT) that behaves like a regular queue, but with one critical difference: every element carries an associated **priority** value. Removal (deletion) of elements is always performed on the element with the **highest priority** (or lowest, depending on convention), regardless of its insertion order. In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, it is formally defined as a container that supports the operations: *Insert, Find-Min / Find-Max, Delete-Min / Delete-Max,* and *Build-Heap*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core ADT Properties (High-Weight for KTU Board)**
> - Each element is a pair: **(key, value)**, where the *key* is the priority.
> - **Min-PQ** serves the *smallest* key first; **Max-PQ** serves the *largest* key first.
> - It is **not** a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) structure — order of service is governed by key.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **Hospital Emergency Room (Casualty Ward)**. Patients arrive at random times in random order. However, they are not treated in arrival sequence. A patient in cardiac arrest (high priority) will be attended to **immediately**, even if someone with a mild fever (low priority) has been waiting for hours. The triage nurse is, in effect, running a **Priority Queue** — she continuously extracts the element with the **highest clinical priority**, not the earliest arrival.

Other real-world parallels:
- **OS Job Scheduler** — the CPU scheduler picks the process with the highest priority (or shortest job) from the ready queue.
- **Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm** — the next vertex to visit is always the unvisited vertex with the *minimum* tentative distance.
- **Air Traffic Control landing queue** — emergency / fuel-critical aircraft land first.

### 1.3 KTU 2024 High-Yield Implementation Choices

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects students to know **at least three** implementation strategies. The default expectation in Module 1 is the **Binary Heap**, but the comparator table below is a frequently asked question.

| Implementation | Insert | Delete-Min/Max | Find-Min/Max | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unsorted Array / List** | $O(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Simple, slow extraction |
| **Sorted Array** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Fast extraction, slow insert |
| **Binary Search Tree (BST)** | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(\log n)$ avg | Worst case $O(n)$ if unbalanced |
| **Balanced BST (AVL/Red-Black)** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Overhead for rebalancing |
| **Binary Heap** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | **KTU default — optimal** |
| **Binomial / Fibonacci Heap** | $O(1)$ amort. | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | Used in advanced algorithms |

The **Binary Heap** is the *de facto* standard because it offers an excellent practical trade-off: all main operations are bounded by **logarithmic height**, and the structure enjoys cache-friendly contiguous storage in arrays.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Definition of a Binary Heap

A **Binary Heap** is a **Complete Binary Tree** (CBT) that satisfies one of two ordering invariants:

1. **Max-Heap Property:** For every node $i$ other than the root, the key of the parent $\geq$ key of $i$.
$$\text{Key}(\text{parent}(i)) \;\geq\; \text{Key}(i)$$

2. **Min-Heap Property:** For every node $i$ other than the root, the key of the parent $\leq$ key of $i$.
$$\text{Key}(\text{parent}(i)) \;\leq\; \text{Key}(i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> A *Complete Binary Tree* is a binary tree in which every level, except possibly the last, is completely filled, and all nodes in the last level are as far **left** as possible. This property enables an array-based, pointer-free representation — the cornerstone of heap efficiency.

### 2.2 Array-Based Indexing Scheme

For a 1-based array `A[1..n]` storing a heap of `n` elements, the pointer arithmetic is:

| Relationship | Formula (1-based indexing) | Formula (0-based indexing) |
|---|---|---|
| Parent of node $i$ | $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$ |
| Left child of node $i$ | $2i$ | $2i+1$ |
| Right child of node $i$ | $2i+1$ | $2i+2$ |
| Height of heap with $n$ nodes | $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | $\lfloor \log_2 (n+1) \rfloor$ |
| Leaves start at index | $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$ | $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ |

### 2.3 The Five Core Heap Operations

1. **Insert(key)** — Place the new key at the next free slot (end of array). **Sift-Up / Bubble-Up** restores the heap property.
2. **Find-Min / Find-Max** — Trivial: it is always the root `A[1]`.
3. **Extract-Min / Extract-Max** — Save root, move last element to root, decrease heap size by 1, then **Sift-Down / Heapify**.
4. **Heapify(A, i)** — Assumes the subtrees rooted at `2i` and `2i+1` are valid heaps; pushes `A[i]` down to its correct position. Also called **Sift-Down** or **Max/Min-Heapify**.
5. **Build-Heap(A)** — Calls Heapify from $i = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ down to $i = 1$ (bottom-up). This is more efficient than inserting $n$ times.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — at least one of these complexities is tested in every KTU ESE paper for this module.

| Operation | Unsorted List | Sorted List | **Binary Heap (KTU default)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Insert | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Find-Min / Find-Max | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Extract-Min / Extract-Max | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Decrease-Key / Increase-Key | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Delete arbitrary | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Build-Heap | $O(n \log n)$ if $n$ inserts | $O(n \log n)$ | $\mathbf{O(n)}$ |
| Heapsort (Build + $n$ extractions) | — | — | $\mathbf{O(n \log n)}$ in-place |
| Space | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ (in-place) |

### 2.5 Why Binary Heap Beats Linked List / BST in Practice

- **Cache locality:** Array storage means consecutive memory access during sifting — far better for modern CPU prefetchers than pointer-chasing in a BST.
- **Constant factor:** Although BST is asymptotically $O(\log n)$, the heap's simpler arithmetic ($2i$, $2i+1$) means a much smaller constant multiplier.
- **In-place Heapsort:** Gives a competitive, in-place, $O(n \log n)$ sort with no auxiliary memory.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Huffman Coding (greedy compression)** requires a min-priority queue to repeatedly pick the two lowest-frequency subtrees.
- **Prim's and Dijkstra's MST / shortest path** use PQs as the central engine.
- **Event-driven simulation** (e.g., network simulators like NS-3) maintain a future-event list as a priority queue keyed by simulation time.
- **Real-time OS task schedulers** (Linux CFS, FreeBSD ULE) use red-black trees as PQs; older systems used multi-level feedback queues.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Build-Heap Complexity Derivation (The Classic KTU Question)

We must prove that the bottom-up **Build-Heap** procedure runs in $O(n)$, not $O(n \log n)$.

#### Step 1 — Cost of Heapify on a node of height $h$

Let the total height of the tree be $L = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$. A node at height $h$ (counting leaves as height 0) has at most $h$ sift-down steps, each $O(1)$. Therefore, the cost of Heapify on such a node is $\Theta(h)$.

#### Step 2 — Number of nodes at height $h$

A complete binary tree of $n$ nodes has, at height $h$, a node count bounded by:

$$
N_h \;\leq\; \left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \right\rceil
$$

The first $L$ levels are completely filled, so for $h < L$ the bound is tight, while the last level contributes at most a partial count.

#### Step 3 — Total cost summation

The total cost of Build-Heap is the sum of Heapify costs over all nodes that are *not* leaves:

$$
T(n) \;\leq\; \sum_{h=0}^{L} N_h \cdot \Theta(h) \;\leq\; \sum_{h=0}^{L} \left\lceil \frac{n}{2^{h+1}} \right\rceil \cdot h
$$

We drop the ceiling (a standard relaxation that loses only a constant factor) and rewrite:

$$
T(n) \;\leq\; n \sum_{h=0}^{L} \frac{h}{2^{h+1}} \;=\; \frac{n}{2} \sum_{h=0}^{L} \frac{h}{2^{h}}
$$

#### Step 4 — Bound the infinite geometric-arithmetic series

We use the well-known bound for $x = 1/2$:

$$
\sum_{h=0}^{\infty} h x^{h} \;=\; \frac{x}{(1-x)^2} \quad \text{for} \quad \vert x \vert < 1
$$

Substituting $x = 1/2$:

$$
\sum_{h=0}^{\infty} h \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{h} \;=\; \frac{1/2}{(1/2)^2} \;=\; 2
$$

Because all terms are positive, the partial sum to $L$ is strictly less than the infinite sum:

$$
\sum_{h=0}^{L} \frac{h}{2^{h}} \;\leq\; \sum_{h=0}^{\infty} \frac{h}{2^{h}} \;=\; 2
$$

#### Step 5 — Conclude

Substituting back:

$$
T(n) \;\leq\; \frac{n}{2} \cdot 2 \;=\; n
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T(n) \;=\; O(n)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The intuition: most nodes are near the bottom (leaves), where Heapify is *cheap* ($O(1)$). The few expensive sift-downs near the root are vastly outnumbered by the cheap ones, so the *amortized* cost is linear. This is the exact opposite intuition of inserting $n$ elements one-by-one, where each insert costs $O(\log n)$ and gives $O(n \log n)$.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Heapsort Complexity Derivation

Heapsort = **Build-Heap** + **$n - 1$ Extract-Max** operations.

$$
T_{\text{heapsort}}(n) \;=\; T_{\text{build}}(n) \;+\; \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} T_{\text{extract}}(n - k)
$$

Step 1 — Build phase: $T_{\text{build}}(n) = O(n)$ from §3.1.

Step 2 — Each Extract-Max performs one swap, one heap-size decrement, and one Heapify on a heap of size $m \leq n$. Each such Heapify is $O(\log m)$, bounded by $O(\log n)$.

Step 3 — There are $n - 1$ such extractions:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \log(n - k + 1) \;=\; \log(n!) - \log(1) \;\leq\; \log(n^n) \;=\; n \log n
$$

Using Stirling's approximation $\log(n!) = \Theta(n \log n)$:

$$
T_{\text{extract-total}} \;=\; \Theta(n \log n)
$$

Step 4 — Total:

$$
T_{\text{heapsort}}(n) \;=\; \Theta(n) + \Theta(n \log n) \;=\; \Theta(n \log n)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{\text{heapsort}}(n) \;=\; \Theta(n \log n)\,}
$$

**Space complexity:** $O(1)$ auxiliary — the sort is performed in-place by storing the extracted maximum at the end of the array.

### 3.3 Derivation: Height of a Heap with $n$ Nodes

A complete binary tree of $n$ nodes has $L$ levels, where $L$ is the largest integer such that the first $L$ levels are completely filled.

The number of nodes in a perfect binary tree of height $L - 1$ (with $L$ levels) is:

$$
N_{\text{perfect}}(L) \;=\; 1 + 2 + 4 + \ldots + 2^{L-1} \;=\; 2^{L} - 1
$$

For a CBT of $n$ nodes, we have:

$$
2^{L-1} \;\leq\; n \;\leq\; 2^{L} - 1
$$

Taking $\log_2$ of the right inequality:

$$
\log_2(n + 1) \;\leq\; L \;\Longrightarrow\; L \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_2 n \right\rfloor
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h(n) \;=\; \left\lfloor \log_2 n \right\rfloor\,}
$$

### 3.4 Summary: Critical KTU Formulas

$$
\text{Parent}(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i}{2} \right\rfloor, \quad \text{Left}(i) = 2i, \quad \text{Right}(i) = 2i + 1
$$

$$
\text{Build-Heap} = O(n), \quad \text{Insert / Extract} = O(\log n), \quad \text{Heapify} = O(\log n)
$$

$$
T_{\text{heapsort}} = \Theta(n \log n), \quad S_{\text{heapsort}} = \Theta(1)
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Binary Max-Heap

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the *complete* functional architecture of a max-heap implementation: the array storage, the index-arithmetic engine, the two restoration routines (Sift-Up and Sift-Down), the public ADT operations, and the external call sites that consume the structure.

```mermaid
graph TD
    client[Client Application]:::client
    pubapi[Public ADT Interface]:::api
    opinsert[Insert new key]:::op
    opextract[Extract Max]:::op
    opfind[Find Max Peek]:::op
    opbuild[Build Heap]:::op
    opdelete[Delete arbitrary key]:::op
    siftup[Sift Up Routine]:::core
    siftdown[Sift Down Heapify Routine]:::core
    idxarith[Index Arithmetic Engine]:::core
    store[Array Storage A 1 to n]:::storage
    heapsize[Current Heap Size Variable]:::storage
    swapunit[Swap Utility]:::util

    client --> pubapi
    pubapi --> opinsert
    pubapi --> opextract
    pubapi --> opfind
    pubapi --> opbuild
    pubapi --> opdelete

    opinsert --> siftup
    opextract --> siftdown
    opbuild --> siftdown
    opdelete --> siftdown

    siftup --> idxarith
    siftdown --> idxarith
    idxarith --> store
    siftup --> swapunit
    siftdown --> swapunit
    swapunit --> store

    siftup --> heapsize
    siftdown --> heapsize
    opfind --> store

    classDef client fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef api fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef util fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram for KTU viva:** Arrows from `opinsert → siftup` and `opextract → siftdown` show *which* restoration routine is invoked by *which* ADT call. Note that the **same Heapify (Sift-Down)** routine serves **both** the `opextract` and `opbuild` paths — this is the single most important reuse point in the data structure and a common viva question.

### 4.2 Example Max-Heap as a Tree with Array Mapping

Consider the array $A = [\_, 90, 70, 80, 40, 50, 60, 30]$ (underscore is a placeholder for the unused 0-th slot in 1-based indexing). The corresponding complete binary tree is:

```mermaid
graph TD
    n1[1 90 Root]:::root
    n2[2 70]:::internal
    n3[3 80]:::internal
    n4[4 40]:::leaf
    n5[5 50]:::leaf
    n6[6 60]:::leaf
    n7[7 30]:::leaf

    n1 --- n2
    n1 --- n3
    n2 --- n4
    n2 --- n5
    n3 --- n6
    n3 --- n7

    classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef internal fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

**Verification of the heap property (max-heap):**
- $A[2]=70 \leq A[1]=90$ ✔
- $A[3]=80 \leq A[1]=90$ ✔
- $A[4]=40 \leq A[2]=70$ ✔
- $A[5]=50 \leq A[2]=70$ ✔
- $A[6]=60 \leq A[3]=80$ ✔
- $A[7]=30 \leq A[3]=80$ ✔

### 4.3 Sift-Down (Heapify) Process — Worked Tree

Initial state (after moving last element to root during Extract-Max):

```mermaid
graph TD
    nA1[1 30]:::problem
    nA2[2 70]:::internal
    nA3[3 80]:::internal
    nA4[4 40]:::leaf
    nA5[5 50]:::leaf
    nA6[6 60]:::leaf
    nA1 --- nA2
    nA1 --- nA3
    nA2 --- nA4
    nA2 --- nA5
    nA3 --- nA6

    classDef problem fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef internal fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

Compare 30 with children 70, 80 → larger child is 80 (at index 3). **Swap** $A[1] \leftrightarrow A[3]$:

```mermaid
graph TD
    nB1[1 80]:::ok
    nB2[2 70]:::internal
    nB3[3 30]:::problem
    nB4[4 40]:::leaf
    nB5[5 50]:::leaf
    nB6[6 60]:::leaf
    nB1 --- nB2
    nB1 --- nB3
    nB2 --- nB4
    nB2 --- nB5
    nB3 --- nB6

    classDef ok fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef problem fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef internal fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

Now at index 3 with value 30. Children: only 60 (left). $60 > 30$ → **Swap** $A[3] \leftrightarrow A[6]$:

```mermaid
graph TD
    nC1[1 80]:::ok
    nC2[2 70]:::internal
    nC3[3 60]:::ok
    nC4[4 40]:::leaf
    nC5[5 50]:::leaf
    nC6[6 30]:::leaf
    nC1 --- nC2
    nC1 --- nC3
    nC2 --- nC4
    nC2 --- nC5
    nC3 --- nC6

    classDef ok fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef internal fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

Heap property restored. **Total swaps performed: 2**; **Total comparisons: 3** (two with children, one between siblings).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q1.** Define a *Priority Queue* Abstract Data Type. How does it differ from a standard FIFO queue? List any two real-world scenarios where a priority queue is the natural data structure choice.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Priority Queue (PQ)** is an Abstract Data Type in which each element is associated with a *priority* (key). The element with the **highest priority** is always removed first, regardless of its insertion order. It differs from a **FIFO queue**, where service order strictly follows arrival order.
>
> Two real-world scenarios:
> 1. **Hospital triage** — critical patients are treated before non-critical ones, even if they arrived later.
> 2. **Operating-system process scheduler** — the CPU is allotted to the process with the highest scheduling priority.
>
> *(Award 1 mark for the formal definition, 1 mark for the FIFO contrast, 1 mark for the two scenarios.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
>
> **Q2.** State the **Max-Heap property** formally. If a max-heap of $n$ elements is stored in a 1-based array `A`, write the formulas for the parent and children of an arbitrary node at index $i$.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Max-Heap property:** For every node $i$ (other than the root), the key of the parent is **greater than or equal to** the key of $i$:
> $$\text{Key}(\text{parent}(i)) \;\geq\; \text{Key}(i)$$
>
> Indexing formulas in a 1-based array `A[1..n]`:
> - Parent of node $i$ : $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor$
> - Left child of node $i$ : $2i$
> - Right child of node $i$ : $2i + 1$
>
> *(1 mark for the property, 1 mark each for parent and one child formula — give both children for full credit.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### ▶ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Max-Heapify** (Sift-Down) algorithm. Write its pseudocode and prove that its worst-case running time on a node of height $h$ is $O(h)$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Build a **max-heap** from the following array using the bottom-up **Build-Heap** procedure. Show *every* intermediate tree state. Given the final heap, perform **one Extract-Max** operation and show the resulting array.
>
> Input array (1-indexed): $A = [\_, 4, 10, 3, 5, 1, 8, 7, 6]$

**Model Solution (a) — Max-Heapify Algorithm [7 marks]**

**Idea:** Max-Heapify assumes that the binary subtrees rooted at the left and right children of node $i$ are already valid max-heaps, but node $i$ itself may violate the max-heap property. The routine *cascades* the offending key down the larger of its two children until the subtree rooted at $i$ becomes a valid max-heap.

**Pseudocode (1-indexed array):**

```
MaxHeapify(A, i):
    left  = 2*i
    right = 2*i + 1
    largest = i
    if left  <= heapSize and A[left]  > A[largest]  then largest = left
    if right <= heapSize and A[right] > A[largest] then largest = right
    if largest != i then
        swap A[i] and A[largest]
        MaxHeapify(A, largest)        // recursive descent
```

**Worst-case running-time analysis (for a node of height $h$):**

Let $T(h)$ denote the worst-case time taken by Max-Heapify on a subtree whose root has height $h$. In the worst case, the largest of the three keys (root, left, right) is one of the children, forcing a swap and a recursive call on a subtree whose height is at most $h - 1$. All other operations (comparisons, assignments, the swap) are $O(1)$. Therefore:

$$
T(h) \;\leq\; T(h - 1) + \Theta(1)
$$

Unrolling the recurrence:

$$
T(h) \;\leq\; T(h - 2) + 2\Theta(1) \;\leq\; \ldots \;\leq\; T(0) + h \cdot \Theta(1)
$$

Since $T(0) = \Theta(1)$ (a leaf has no children to recurse on):

$$
\boxed{\,T(h) \;=\; O(h)\,}
$$

> *(Award 1 mark for the idea, 2 marks for pseudocode, 2 marks for the recurrence setup, 1 mark for the unrolling, 1 mark for the final $O(h)$ conclusion.)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — Build-Heap and Extract-Max [7 marks]**

**Initial array** (1-indexed, with index 0 unused):

$$
A = [\_,\; 4,\; 10,\; 3,\; 5,\; 1,\; 8,\; 7,\; 6], \quad n = 8
$$

**Build-Heap loop:** Call `MaxHeapify` on indices $i = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor = 4$ down to $i = 1$. Nodes at indices 5, 6, 7, 8 are leaves, hence already valid max-heaps of size 1.

**Step 1 — MaxHeapify at $i = 4$ (value 5):**
- Children: indices 8 (value 6) and 9 (out of range).
- Largest child = 6 > 5 → swap $A[4] \leftrightarrow A[8]$.
- Array becomes: $[\_, 4, 10, 3, 6, 1, 8, 7, 5]$.

**Step 2 — MaxHeapify at $i = 3$ (value 3):**
- Children: indices 6 (value 8) and 7 (value 7).
- Largest child = 8 > 3 → swap $A[3] \leftrightarrow A[6]$.
- Recurse on index 6 (value 3): no children within heap size (index 12 > 8). Stop.
- Array becomes: $[\_, 4, 10, 8, 6, 1, 3, 7, 5]$.

**Step 3 — MaxHeapify at $i = 2$ (value 10):**
- Children: indices 4 (value 6) and 5 (value 1).
- Largest child = 6 < 10 → no swap needed. Heap already valid here.

**Step 4 — MaxHeapify at $i = 1$ (value 4):**
- Children: indices 2 (value 10) and 3 (value 8).
- Largest child = 10 > 4 → swap $A[1] \leftrightarrow A[2]$.
- Recurse on index 2 (value 4): children 4 (value 6) and 5 (value 1). Largest child = 6 > 4 → swap $A[2] \leftrightarrow A[4]$.
- Recurse on index 4 (value 4): children 8 (value 5) and 9 (out of range). Largest child = 5 > 4 → swap $A[4] \leftrightarrow A[8]$.
- Recurse on index 8 (value 4): no children. Stop.
- Final array: $[\_, 10, 6, 8, 5, 1, 3, 7, 4]$.

**Final max-heap (in tree form):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    m1[10]:::root
    m2[6]:::internal
    m3[8]:::internal
    m4[5]:::internal
    m5[1]:::leaf
    m6[3]:::leaf
    m7[7]:::leaf
    m8[4]:::leaf
    m1 --- m2
    m1 --- m3
    m2 --- m4
    m2 --- m5
    m3 --- m6
    m3 --- m7
    m4 --- m8

    classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef internal fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

**Extract-Max (root = 10):**

1. Save `max = A[1] = 10`.
2. Move last element $A[8] = 4$ to root: $A[1] = 4$. Decrement `heapSize` to 7.
3. Call `MaxHeapify(A, 1)` on the array $[\_, 4, 6, 8, 5, 1, 3, 7]$. Children of root: 6, 8. Swap with 8. Recurse on index 3 with value 4: children 6, 7. Swap with 7. Recurse on index 7 with value 4: no children. Stop.

**Array after Extract-Max:** $[\_, 8, 6, 7, 5, 1, 3, 4]$, `heapSize = 7`.

> *(Award 2 marks for the four MaxHeapify steps in build-heap, 2 marks for the final heap correctness, 2 marks for the extract-max step, 1 mark for clean presentation.)*

---

#### ▶ Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **unsorted array**, **sorted array**, and **binary heap** implementations of a priority queue. Construct a table of worst-case time complexities for the operations *Insert*, *Extract-Min*, *Find-Min*, and *Build-Heap*. State with justification which is preferred in practice.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Trace the **Heapsort** algorithm on the following array. Show the array state *after each* Extract-Max step. State the final sorted array and the overall time complexity.
>
> Input array: $[5, 13, 2, 25, 7, 17, 20, 8, 4]$

**Model Solution (a) — Implementation Comparison [7 marks]**

| Implementation | Insert | Extract-Min | Find-Min | Build-Heap | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unsorted Array** | $O(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ (just store) | Cheap insert, expensive extract |
| **Sorted Array** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Cheap extract, expensive insert |
| **Binary Heap** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | **Best overall balance** |

**Justification for preferring Binary Heap:**

The unsorted array's $\Theta(n)$ Extract-Min is unacceptable when many extractions are required (e.g., in Dijkstra's algorithm). The sorted array's $O(n)$ Insert is equally painful for the same reason — every insertion shifts elements. The binary heap offers $O(\log n)$ for both Insert and Extract-Min, and crucially achieves $O(n)$ Build-Heap (better than $n$ sequential inserts which would be $O(n \log n)$). Additionally, the heap's array-based storage provides excellent **cache locality** — sequential memory access during sifting, much better than the pointer-chasing cost in tree-based structures like AVL/Red-Black trees. Hence, the binary heap is the de facto industry standard for general-purpose priority queues.

> *(1 mark per table cell group, 1 mark for the build-heap column, 2 marks for the justification.)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — Heapsort Trace [7 marks]**

**Step 0 — Input array (1-indexed):**
$$A = [\_,\; 5,\; 13,\; 2,\; 25,\; 7,\; 17,\; 20,\; 8,\; 4], \quad n = 9$$

**Step 1 — Build Max-Heap (bottom-up):**

Call `MaxHeapify` from $i = 4$ down to $1$. Carrying out the calls:

- $i=4$: child 8 (val 4), 9 (val —). $4<25$ no swap.
- $i=3$: children 6 (val 17), 7 (val 20). Largest child = 20 > 2 → swap $A[3]\leftrightarrow A[7]$. Recurse at 7: no children. Array: $[\_, 5, 13, 20, 25, 7, 17, 2, 8, 4]$.
- $i=2$: children 4 (val 25), 5 (val 7). $25 > 13$ → swap $A[2]\leftrightarrow A[4]$. Recurse at 4: child 8 (val 8), 9 (val 4). $8 < 25$ no swap. Array: $[\_, 5, 25, 20, 13, 7, 17, 2, 8, 4]$.
- $i=1$: children 2 (val 25), 3 (val 20). $25 > 5$ → swap $A[1]\leftrightarrow A[2]$. Recurse at 2: children 4 (val 13), 5 (val 7). $13 < 25$ no swap. Array: $[\_, 25, 5, 20, 13, 7, 17, 2, 8, 4]$.

Wait — recurse at index 2, where $A[2] = 5$. Children: index 4 ($A[4]=13$), index 5 ($A[5]=7$). Largest child = 13 > 5 → swap $A[2]\leftrightarrow A[4]$. Recurse at index 4, $A[4] = 5$. Children: index 8 ($A[8]=8$), index 9 ($A[9]=4$). Largest child = 8 > 5 → swap $A[4]\leftrightarrow A[8]$. Recurse at index 8, $A[8] = 5$. No children. Stop.

**Final max-heap array:** $[\_, 25, 13, 20, 8, 7, 17, 2, 5, 4]$.

**Step 2 — Heapsort (repeated Extract-Max, growing sorted suffix):**

| Pass | Extract-Max | Swap with last | Heapify result | Sorted suffix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 25 | $A[1]\leftrightarrow A[9]$ | $[\_, 20, 13, 17, 8, 7, 4, 2, 5]$ | $[\ldots, 25]$ |
| 2 | 20 | $A[1]\leftrightarrow A[8]$ | $[\_, 17, 13, 5, 8, 7, 4, 2]$ | $[\ldots, 20, 25]$ |
| 3 | 17 | $A[1]\leftrightarrow A[7]$ | $[\_, 13, 8, 5, 2, 7, 4]$ | $[\ldots, 17, 20, 25]$ |
| 4 | 13 | $A[1]\leftrightarrow A[6]$ | $[\_, 8, 7, 5, 2, 4]$ | $[\ldots, 13, 17, 20, 25]$ |
| 5 | 8 | $A[1]\leftrightarrow A[5]$ | $[\_, 7, 4, 5, 2]$ | $[\ldots, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]$ |
| 6 | 7 | $A[1]\leftrightarrow A[4]$ | $[\_, 5, 4, 2]$ | $[\ldots, 7, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]$ |
| 7 | 5 | $A[1]\leftrightarrow A[3]$ | $[\_, 4, 2]$ | $[\ldots, 5, 7, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]$ |
| 8 | 4 | $A[1]\leftrightarrow A[2]$ | $[\_, 2]$ | $[\ldots, 4, 5, 7, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]$ |

**Final sorted array (1-indexed):** $[\_, 2, 4, 5, 7, 8, 13, 17, 20, 25]$.

**Time complexity:** $T(n) = \underbrace{O(n)}_{\text{build}} + \underbrace{O(n \log n)}_{\text{$n-1$ extractions}} = O(n \log n)$.
**Space complexity:** $O(1)$ — in-place.

> *(2 marks for build-heap, 4 marks for the eight extract-max passes, 1 mark for time complexity.)*

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls where KTU students lose easy marks on Priority Queue questions:**
> 1. **Mixing 0-based and 1-based indexing** in the same answer. Pick one convention at the start and stick to it.
> 2. **Forgetting to decrement heap-size** after Extract-Max. This causes the algorithm to "see" the old root as still present and the sorted suffix to be corrupted. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks specifically for this.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate tree states** in Build-Heap/Extract-Max traces. The KTU valuation key mandates **every** swap to be shown. Half-mark per missed intermediate state.
> 4. **Writing $O(n \log n)$ for Build-Heap** — this is the most common error. The correct answer is $O(n)$ because most nodes are near the bottom and the geometric-series sum converges. Examiners *love* testing this misconception.
> 5. **Confusing max-heap and min-heap** indexing — students sometimes write `right = 2i - 1` for max-heap, mixing up the convention. Remember: in 1-based arrays, `left = 2i`, `right = 2i + 1` for **both** min and max heaps; only the comparison direction changes.
> 6. **Not stating space complexity** alongside time complexity. A KTU full-mark answer always mentions both.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Priority Queue** is an ADT ordered by key (priority), not insertion time.
- A **Binary Heap** is a Complete Binary Tree satisfying the heap-order property (max or min) and is the standard implementation.
- Array representation (1-based): `parent(i) = ⌊i/2⌋`, `left(i) = 2i`, `right(i) = 2i + 1`; 0-based: `parent = ⌊(i-1)/2⌋`, `left = 2i+1`, `right = 2i+2`.
- **Find-Min/Max** is $O(1)$ — always the root.
- **Insert** uses Sift-Up; **Extract-Min/Max** uses Sift-Down; both are $O(\log n)$.
- **Build-Heap** is $O(n)$, **not** $O(n \log n)$ — proven by geometric-series bound.
- **Heapsort** = Build-Heap + $n-1$ Extracts → $O(n \log n)$ time, $O(1)$ extra space.
- Default applications: **Huffman coding, Dijkstra, Prim, event-driven simulation, OS scheduling**.
- The heap is **not** a sorted structure — only the root is guaranteed to be the min/max; the rest is in heap order, not linear order.
- For KTU 2024: always present **time + space** complexity, **pseudocode**, and **intermediate states** for full marks on trace questions.
- Master the recurrence $T(h) \leq T(h-1) + \Theta(1) \Rightarrow T(h) = O(h)$ for Max-Heapify — this is a perennial favorite.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Ended Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Double Ended Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Double Ended Queue (Deque)**, pronounced as *"deck"*, is a generalized abstract data type (ADT) that generalizes a queue by allowing **insertion and deletion of elements from both the front (head) and the rear (tail)** ends. Unlike a standard queue which strictly follows the **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** discipline, a deque does not enforce any specific ordering constraint on element removal, making it a versatile sequential container.

Mathematically, a deque $D$ over an element set $E$ supports the following primitive operations:

$$D = \langle E, \{\text{insertFront}, \text{insertRear}, \text{deleteFront}, \text{deleteRear}, \text{getFront}, \text{getRear}, \text{isEmpty}, \text{isFull}\} \rangle$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A deque is a **homogeneous** data structure where all elements are of the same type. The 2024 scheme emphasizes that deque is the most generalized form of linear data structures — it can simulate a stack, queue, or even a combination of both depending on the restricted operations used.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Compartment with Doors on Both Sides"

To build intuition, imagine a **passenger train compartment with doors on both ends**:

- Passengers can **board from the front door** (insertFront) or the **rear door** (insertRear).
- Passengers can **alight from the front door** (deleteFront) or the **rear door** (deleteRear).
- The compartment has a **fixed capacity** (isFull check) just like a real train has limited seats.
- The compartment can be **empty** (isEmpty check) when no passengers are present.

This real-world metaphor captures the **bidirectional nature** of a deque perfectly — you are never restricted to accessing only one end.

## 1.3 Classification of Deques

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly categorizes deques into two restricted variants:

| Variant | Insertion | Deletion | Discipline Enforced |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input-Restricted Deque** | Only at **rear** | Both front & rear | One-sided input, two-sided output |
| **Output-Restricted Deque** | Both front & rear | Only at **front** | Two-sided input, one-sided output |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid (Examiner's Tip):** A *general deque* allows all 4 operations. Think of an **I**nput-restricted deque as a *Queue with a side-exit*, and an **O**utput-restricted deque as a *Stack with a side-entry*.

## 1.4 Why Deque? — Engineering Motivation

The deque was introduced to overcome the rigidity of pure FIFO queues. In real systems:

- **Operating System Job Scheduling** uses a *work-stealing deque* where idle cores "steal" from the rear of busy cores' task lists.
- **Browser History** allows navigation forward (insertFront) and backward (insertRear) — both ends manipulated.
- **Undo/Redo functionality** in editors uses two deques — one for undo, one for redo.

The **standard metric** for all deque operations is a time complexity of **$\mathcal{O}(1)$** per operation, which is a critical design requirement highlighted in the KTU 2024 outcome **CO1: Apply linear data structures to model real-world problems.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "How" Behind Each Operation

A deque is implemented over a **circular array** of capacity $N$ to efficiently utilize memory and avoid the $\mathcal{O}(N)$ shifting cost of a linear array. Let the circular array be denoted $A[0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1]$, and let two integer pointers manage the boundaries:

- **front**: Index of the first element (where the next deleteFront occurs).
- **rear**: Index of the last element (where the next insertRear occurs).
- **size**: Current number of elements (a counter, not derived from pointer arithmetic, to disambiguate empty vs. full).

### 2.1.1 Initialization Step

$$\text{front} \leftarrow 0, \quad \text{rear} \leftarrow N-1, \quad \text{size} \leftarrow 0$$

The `rear` starts at $N-1$ because the first `insertRear` will perform `rear = (rear + 1) % N`, making it land at index 0.

### 2.1.2 The Eight Core Operations

**Step 1 — `isEmpty()`:** Returns $\text{true}$ if and only if $\text{size} = 0$. The "Why": A simple counter comparison avoids the ambiguity problem in circular arrays where `front == rear` could mean either empty or full.

**Step 2 — `isFull()`:** Returns $\text{true}$ if and only if $\text{size} = N$. The "How": Same reasoning — we don't rely on pointer coincidence.

**Step 3 — `insertFront(x)`:** 
- Check `isFull()`; abort with overflow error if true.
- Update: $\text{front} \leftarrow (\text{front} - 1 + N) \bmod N$. The `+ N` ensures the modulo result is non-negative.
- Store: $A[\text{front}] \leftarrow x$.
- Increment: $\text{size} \leftarrow \text{size} + 1$.

**Step 4 — `insertRear(x)`:** 
- Check `isFull()`.
- Update: $\text{rear} \leftarrow (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N$.
- Store: $A[\text{rear}] \leftarrow x$.
- Increment: $\text{size} \leftarrow \text{size} + 1$.

**Step 5 — `deleteFront()`:** 
- Check `isEmpty()`; abort with underflow if true.
- Retrieve: $x \leftarrow A[\text{front}]$.
- Update: $\text{front} \leftarrow (\text{front} + 1) \bmod N$.
- Decrement: $\text{size} \leftarrow \text{size} - 1$.
- Return $x$.

**Step 6 — `deleteRear()`:** 
- Check `isEmpty()`.
- Retrieve: $x \leftarrow A[\text{rear}]$.
- Update: $\text{rear} \leftarrow (\text{rear} - 1 + N) \bmod N$.
- Decrement: $\text{size} \leftarrow \text{size} - 1$.
- Return $x$.

**Step 7 — `getFront()`:** Returns $A[\text{front}]$ if not empty; signals error otherwise.

**Step 8 — `getRear()`:** Returns $A[\text{rear}]$ if not empty; signals error otherwise.

## 2.2 Why Circular Array? — The Engineering Rationale

A linear array implementation of a deque would suffer from the **"drift problem"**: after a series of front deletions and rear insertions, both pointers would march toward the end of the array, eventually triggering spurious overflow even when free space exists at the front. The **modulo arithmetic** of a circular array ensures that pointer positions "wrap around," making every cell reusable indefinitely. This is exactly the property exploited in production-grade systems like **Linux kernel's `kfifo`** and **C++ STL `std::deque`**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Operation | Formula / Condition | Time Complexity | Space |
|---|---|---|---|
| `isEmpty()` | $\text{size} = 0$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| `isFull()` | $\text{size} = N$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| `insertFront(x)` | $\text{front} \leftarrow (\text{front} - 1 + N) \bmod N$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| `insertRear(x)` | $\text{rear} \leftarrow (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| `deleteFront()` | $\text{front} \leftarrow (\text{front} + 1) \bmod N$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| `deleteRear()` | $\text{rear} \leftarrow (\text{rear} - 1 + N) \bmod N$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| `getFront()` | Returns $A[\text{front}]$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| `getRear()` | Returns $A[\text{rear}]$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | — |
| Pointer count check | $\text{size} \in [0, N]$ | — | $N$ slots |
| **Total memory** | $\Theta(N)$ array + $\Theta(1)$ pointers | — | $\Theta(N)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Warning:** The expression $(\text{rear} - 1 + N) \bmod N$ is the **only** correct formulation in C. A naive `rear - 1` in a language that allows negative modulo would give $-1$, which is an invalid array index. Always add $N$ before the modulo.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility Summary

| Engineering Domain | Use Case of Deque |
|---|---|
| **Operating Systems** | A-Steal work scheduling in parallel runtimes (e.g., Cilk, TBB) |
| **Text Editors** | Undo/Redo stacks (two deques facing opposite directions) |
| **Compiler Design** | Palindrome-checking intermediate representation |
| **Networking** | Sliding window protocol packet buffers |
| **Game Development** | Card decks where you draw from top or bottom |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Trace Worked Example — Manual Deque Operations

**Problem Setup:** Let $N = 5$ (capacity of circular array). All indices start at $\text{front}=0$, $\text{rear}=4$, $\text{size}=0$. Array $A[5]$ initialized to all empty.

We will execute the following 8 operations in strict order and trace the state after each one. This is the **exact question type** KTU examiners love for 7-mark sub-questions.

### Operation 1: `insertRear(10)`

- `isFull()`: $\text{size}=0 \neq 5$, so proceed.
- $\text{rear} = (4 + 1) \bmod 5 = 0$.
- $A[0] = 10$.
- $\text{size} = 1$.

**State:** $\text{front}=0$, $\text{rear}=0$, $\text{size}=1$, $A = [10, -, -, -, -]$.

### Operation 2: `insertRear(20)`

- `isFull()`: $\text{size}=1 \neq 5$, proceed.
- $\text{rear} = (0 + 1) \bmod 5 = 1$.
- $A[1] = 20$.
- $\text{size} = 2$.

**State:** $\text{front}=0$, $\text{rear}=1$, $\text{size}=2$, $A = [10, 20, -, -, -]$.

### Operation 3: `insertFront(5)`

- `isFull()`: $\text{size}=2 \neq 5$, proceed.
- $\text{front} = (0 - 1 + 5) \bmod 5 = 4$.
- $A[4] = 5$.
- $\text{size} = 3$.

**State:** $\text{front}=4$, $\text{rear}=1$, $\text{size}=3$, $A = [10, 20, -, -, 5]$.

### Operation 4: `getFront()`

- `isEmpty()`: $\text{size}=3 \neq 0$, proceed.
- Return $A[\text{front}] = A[4] = 5$.

### Operation 5: `deleteRear()`

- `isEmpty()`: $\text{size}=3 \neq 0$, proceed.
- $x = A[\text{rear}] = A[1] = 20$.
- $\text{rear} = (1 - 1 + 5) \bmod 5 = 0$.
- $\text{size} = 2$.

**State:** $\text{front}=4$, $\text{rear}=0$, $\text{size}=2$, $A = [10, -, -, -, 5]$. Returned: **20**.

### Operation 6: `insertRear(30)`

- `isFull()`: $\text{size}=2 \neq 5$, proceed.
- $\text{rear} = (0 + 1) \bmod 5 = 1$.
- $A[1] = 30$.
- $\text{size} = 3$.

**State:** $\text{front}=4$, $\text{rear}=1$, $\text{size}=3$, $A = [10, 30, -, -, 5]$.

### Operation 7: `insertRear(40)` and `insertRear(50)` and `insertRear(60)`

Following the same pattern: $\text{rear}$ moves to $2, 3, 0$ (wrapping), but $\text{size}$ stops at $N=5$ when full.

**State:** $\text{front}=4$, $\text{rear}=0$, $\text{size}=5$, $A = [10, 30, 40, 50, 60]$. Now $\text{front}=4$, $\text{rear}=0$ — note this is the **same pointer coincidence** as the empty case, but $\text{size}=5$ disambiguates.

### Operation 8: `deleteFront()`

- $x = A[4] = 5$, $\text{front} = (4+1) \bmod 5 = 0$, $\text{size} = 4$.

**State:** $\text{front}=0$, $\text{rear}=0$, $\text{size}=4$, $A = [10, 30, 40, 50, 60]$. Returned: **5**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — State After Each Operation

| Step | Operation | Returned | front | rear | size | A[0] | A[1] | A[2] | A[3] | A[4] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Init | — | 0 | 4 | 0 | — | — | — | — | — |
| 1 | insertRear(10) | — | 0 | 0 | 1 | 10 | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | insertRear(20) | — | 0 | 1 | 2 | 10 | 20 | — | — | — |
| 3 | insertFront(5) | — | 4 | 1 | 3 | 10 | 20 | — | — | 5 |
| 4 | getFront() | 5 | 4 | 1 | 3 | 10 | 20 | — | — | 5 |
| 5 | deleteRear() | 20 | 4 | 0 | 2 | 10 | 20 | — | — | 5 |
| 6 | insertRear(30) | — | 4 | 1 | 3 | 10 | 30 | — | — | 5 |
| 7 | insertRear(40) | — | 4 | 2 | 4 | 10 | 30 | 40 | — | 5 |
| 8 | insertRear(50) | — | 4 | 3 | 5 | 10 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 5 |
| 9 | insertRear(60) | **OVERFLOW** | 4 | 3 | 5 | 10 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 5 |
| 10 | deleteFront() | 5 | 0 | 3 | 4 | 10 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 5 |

## 3.3 Derivation — Why `size` Counter is Mandatory

Consider the scenario after step 8: $\text{front}=4$, $\text{rear}=3$, $\text{size}=5$. If we tried step 9 (`insertRear(60)`), we would compute $\text{rear} = (3+1) \bmod 5 = 4$. Now $\text{front}=4$ and $\text{rear}=4$ — the same as a fresh empty deque! Without the `size` counter, we could not distinguish "full" from "empty." This is the **canonical pitfall** that KTU examiners test in the 14-mark questions.

The disambiguation condition is formally:

$$(\text{front} = \text{rear}) \iff (\text{size} = 0) \lor (\text{size} = N)$$

This is why every robust deque implementation maintains an **explicit size counter**.

## 3.4 Deque as a Simulator — A Useful Reformulation

A deque can simulate any of the following data structures with **no overhead**, which makes it the most "complete" linear structure:

$$
\text{Deque} \supseteq \text{Queue} \supseteq \text{Stack}
$$

Specifically:
- **As a Stack:** Use only `insertRear` and `deleteRear` (LIFO discipline on the right).
- **As a Queue:** Use only `insertRear` and `deleteFront` (FIFO discipline).
- **As a Mixed Mode:** Use `insertFront` and `deleteRear` (reverse FIFO), or any combination.

This is why the C++ STL `std::deque` and Java `ArrayDeque` are recommended as default sequential containers — they subsume both `std::stack` and `std::queue` interfaces.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Circular Array Deque

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the internal architecture, control flow, and data path of a deque implementation. This is the **exact schematic style** KTU examiners expect for 14-mark "explain with diagram" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client Application]
    interface[Deque API Layer]
    
    subgraph control[Control and Pointer Module]
        fPtr[front pointer]
        rPtr[rear pointer]
        sCnt[size counter]
    end
    
    subgraph checks[Boundary Check Submodule]
        chkEmpty[isEmpty size eq 0]
        chkFull[isFull size eq N]
    end
    
    subgraph ops[Operation Submodule]
        iF[insertFront]
        iR[insertRear]
        dF[deleteFront]
        dR[deleteRear]
        gF[getFront]
        gR[getRear]
    end
    
    subgraph storage[Circular Array Storage N slots]
        a0[Slot 0]
        a1[Slot 1]
        a2[Slot 2]
        aN[Slot N minus 1]
    end
    
    client --> interface
    interface --> control
    interface --> checks
    interface --> ops
    
    iF --> fPtr
    iR --> rPtr
    dF --> fPtr
    dR --> rPtr
    gF --> fPtr
    gR --> rPtr
    
    iF --> a0
    iR --> a0
    dF --> a0
    dR --> a0
    gF --> a0
    gR --> a0
    
    fPtr -.modulo wrap.-> aN
    rPtr -.modulo wrap.-> aN
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Deque Lifecycle and Overflow Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Empty : Initialize front 0, rear N-1, size 0
    
    Empty --> Partial : insertFront or insertRear
    Partial --> Partial : Any valid operation
    Partial --> Full : size equals N
    Full --> Partial : deleteFront or deleteRear
    Partial --> Empty : size returns to 0
    Full --> FullError : Attempted insert on full
    Empty --> EmptyError : Attempted delete on empty
    FullError --> Full : State unchanged
    EmptyError --> Empty : State unchanged
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapped Diagram — Logical View of Bidirectional Operations

The following diagram shows a deque as a logical double-arrow container, illustrating how data flows at both ends. Each end supports both **incoming** (insert) and **outgoing** (delete) traffic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph front_end[Front End]
        iF2[insertFront] --> container2
        container2 --> dF2[deleteFront]
    end
    
    subgraph core[Deque Container of size N]
        container2[Data Slots]
    end
    
    subgraph rear_end[Rear End]
        iR2[insertRear] --> container2
        container2 --> dR2[deleteRear]
    end
    
    observer[External Observer] -. getFront getRear .-> container2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read-Only Accessors:** The `getFront` and `getRear` operations are drawn as dotted lines because they **inspect** the boundary elements without modifying the structure — a key distinction tested in KTU 2024 module questions on "classification of operations" (mutators vs. observers).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B, Q3a]**

**Q1 (3 Marks):** Define a Double Ended Queue. List any four operations supported by a deque.

**Model Answer:**
A **Double Ended Queue (Deque)** is a linear data structure that allows insertion and deletion of elements from **both** the front and rear ends, without enforcing a strict FIFO or LIFO discipline. The four primary operations are: **(i) `insertFront(x)`** — adds $x$ at the front, **(ii) `insertRear(x)`** — adds $x$ at the rear, **(iii) `deleteFront()`** — removes and returns the front element, and **(iv) `deleteRear()`** — removes and returns the rear element. (Any 4 of the 8 standard operations may be given.)
