**Keywords for full marks:** *linear data structure, both ends, generalized queue, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time.*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper, Q2c]**

**Q2 (3 Marks):** Differentiate between input-restricted and output-restricted deques.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Input-Restricted Deque | Output-Restricted Deque |
|---|---|---|
| Insertion | Only at **rear** | At **both** front and rear |
| Deletion | From **both** front and rear | Only at **front** |
| Can simulate | Queue (use front-rear as FIFO) | Stack (use front as top) |
| Real-world use | Print job buffer with priority insert | Undo-only log |

Both variants retain the same time complexity $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per operation.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q11(a)]**

### **Question A (14 Marks): Deque Operations on a Circular Array**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write the algorithms for `insertFront`, `insertRear`, `deleteFront`, and `deleteRear` operations on a deque implemented using a circular array of size $N$. Clearly state the boundary conditions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider an empty deque of capacity $N=6$ with $\text{front}=0$, $\text{rear}=5$, $\text{size}=0$. Perform the following operations in order and show the state of the deque after each step:
`insertRear(15)`, `insertFront(25)`, `insertRear(35)`, `deleteFront()`, `insertFront(45)`, `getRear()`, `deleteRear()`, `insertRear(55)`, `insertFront(65)`, `deleteRear()`.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Algorithms

**Algorithm: `insertFront(x)`**
1. **IF** `size == N` **THEN** print "OVERFLOW" and **RETURN**.
2. **IF** `front == 0` **THEN** $\text{front} \leftarrow N - 1$ **ELSE** $\text{front} \leftarrow \text{front} - 1$.
   (Equivalently: $\text{front} \leftarrow (\text{front} - 1 + N) \bmod N$.)
3. $A[\text{front}] \leftarrow x$.
4. $\text{size} \leftarrow \text{size} + 1$.

**Algorithm: `insertRear(x)`**
1. **IF** `size == N` **THEN** print "OVERFLOW" and **RETURN**.
2. $\text{rear} \leftarrow (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N$.
3. $A[\text{rear}] \leftarrow x$.
4. $\text{size} \leftarrow \text{size} + 1$.

**Algorithm: `deleteFront()`**
1. **IF** `size == 0` **THEN** print "UNDERFLOW" and **RETURN**.
2. $x \leftarrow A[\text{front}]$.
3. $\text{front} \leftarrow (\text{front} + 1) \bmod N$.
4. $\text{size} \leftarrow \text{size} - 1$.
5. **RETURN** $x$.

**Algorithm: `deleteRear()`**
1. **IF** `size == 0` **THEN** print "UNDERFLOW" and **RETURN**.
2. $x \leftarrow A[\text{rear}]$.
3. **IF** `rear == 0` **THEN** $\text{rear} \leftarrow N - 1$ **ELSE** $\text{rear} \leftarrow \text{rear} - 1$.
   (Equivalently: $\text{rear} \leftarrow (\text{rear} - 1 + N) \bmod N$.)
4. $\text{size} \leftarrow \text{size} - 1$.
5. **RETURN** $x$.

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Trace

Initialize: $\text{front}=0$, $\text{rear}=5$, $\text{size}=0$, $N=6$.

| Step | Operation | front | rear | size | A[0] | A[1] | A[2] | A[3] | A[4] | A[5] | Returned |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Init | 0 | 5 | 0 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| 1 | insertRear(15) | 0 | 0 | 1 | 15 | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | insertFront(25) | 5 | 0 | 2 | 15 | — | — | — | — | 25 | — |
| 3 | insertRear(35) | 5 | 1 | 3 | 15 | 35 | — | — | — | 25 | — |
| 4 | deleteFront() | 0 | 1 | 2 | 15 | 35 | — | — | — | — | **25** |
| 5 | insertFront(45) | 5 | 1 | 3 | 15 | 35 | — | — | — | 45 | — |
| 6 | getRear() | 5 | 1 | 3 | 15 | 35 | — | — | — | 45 | **35** |
| 7 | deleteRear() | 5 | 0 | 2 | 15 | 35 | — | — | — | 45 | **35** |
| 8 | insertRear(55) | 5 | 1 | 3 | 15 | 55 | — | — | — | 45 | — |
| 9 | insertFront(65) | 4 | 1 | 4 | 15 | 55 | — | — | 65 | 45 | — |
| 10 | deleteRear() | 4 | 0 | 3 | 15 | 55 | — | — | 65 | 45 | **55** |

**Final state:** $\text{front}=4$, $\text{rear}=0$, $\text{size}=3$, $A = [15, 55, -, -, 65, 45]$, logical order = $[65, 15, 55]$.

**Critical observation for valuation:** At step 5, after the `deleteFront()` at step 4, $\text{front}$ moves from $5$ to $0$ via modulo. The `insertFront(45)` at step 5 then decrements $\text{front}$ to $5$ (wrapping) — this is exactly the **circular wrap-around** the examiners want to see.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q12(b) — Alternative Choice]**

### **Question B (14 Marks): Doubly Linked List Implementation and Applications**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain how a deque can be implemented using a **doubly linked list**. Write the procedures for `insertFront`, `insertRear`, `deleteFront`, and `deleteRear`. Mention the time complexity of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain any **three real-world applications** of deques in detail. For each, justify why a deque (and not a simple stack or queue) is the natural choice.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Doubly Linked List Implementation

A deque over a **doubly linked list (DLL)** uses a sentinel or header node $H$ such that $H.\text{next}$ is the front and $H.\text{prev}$ is the rear. Each node has the structure:

$$\text{Node} = \{ \text{data} : \text{ElementType},\ \text{next} : \text{Node}^*,\ \text{prev} : \text{Node}^* \}$$

**Procedure `insertFront(x)`:**
1. Allocate a new node $p$; set $p.\text{data} \leftarrow x$.
2. $p.\text{next} \leftarrow H.\text{next}$; $p.\text{prev} \leftarrow H$.
3. **IF** $H.\text{next} \neq H$ **THEN** $H.\text{next}.\text{prev} \leftarrow p$ **ELSE** $H.\text{prev} \leftarrow p$ (it was empty, so rear is also $p$).
4. $H.\text{next} \leftarrow p$.

**Procedure `insertRear(x)`:**
1. Allocate new node $p$; $p.\text{data} \leftarrow x$.
2. $p.\text{prev} \leftarrow H.\text{prev}$; $p.\text{next} \leftarrow H$.
3. **IF** $H.\text{prev} \neq H$ **THEN** $H.\text{prev}.\text{next} \leftarrow p$ **ELSE** $H.\text{next} \leftarrow p$.
4. $H.\text{prev} \leftarrow p$.

**Procedure `deleteFront()`:**
1. **IF** $H.\text{next} = H$ **THEN** print "UNDERFLOW" and return.
2. $p \leftarrow H.\text{next}$; $x \leftarrow p.\text{data}$.
3. $H.\text{next} \leftarrow p.\text{next}$; $p.\text{next}.\text{prev} \leftarrow H$.
4. `free(p)`; return $x$.

**Procedure `deleteRear()`:**
1. **IF** $H.\text{prev} = H$ **THEN** print "UNDERFLOW" and return.
2. $p \leftarrow H.\text{prev}$; $x \leftarrow p.\text{data}$.
3. $H.\text{prev} \leftarrow p.\text{prev}$; $p.\text{prev}.\text{next} \leftarrow H$.
4. `free(p)`; return $x$.

**Time Complexity:** All four operations are $\mathcal{O}(1)$ since we only adjust a constant number of pointer references via the header's `next`/`prev` pointers. **No traversal is required**, unlike a singly linked list where `deleteRear` would be $\mathcal{O}(n)$.

#### Part (b) — Three Real-World Applications

**Application 1: A-Steal Work Scheduling in Parallel Runtimes (e.g., Cilk, Intel TBB)**

In multithreaded runtimes, each worker thread maintains a **deque of tasks**. The owning thread pushes/pops from one end (say, the rear) like a stack, while *idle* threads "steal" work from the **front** of other threads' deques. This requires **two-ended access**: the owner uses rear (LIFO for cache locality), the thief uses front (FIFO for load balancing). A simple stack or queue cannot express this dual policy.

**Application 2: Undo/Redo in Text Editors (e.g., MS Word, Vim)**

A text editor maintains **two deques** — an *undo* deque and a *redo* deque. When a new edit is made, it is pushed to both the rear of the undo deque and the rear of the redo deque. Pressing Ctrl+Z pops from the rear of the undo deque and pushes to the redo deque. New edits after an undo **clear the redo deque** from the front. This bidirectional flow is precisely a deque.

**Application 3: Palindrome Checking**

A palindrome like "RADAR" reads the same forward and backward. By inserting all characters into a deque, we can simultaneously `deleteFront` and `deleteRear` in a loop, comparing pairs. If all pairs match, the string is a palindrome. This is **not** efficiently possible with a single stack or queue — a deque is the natural choice because it allows symmetric end-access.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the `+ N` in modulo arithmetic** (–2 marks): In C, $(-1) \bmod 5$ is implementation-defined. Always write $(\text{front} - 1 + N) \bmod N$.
> 2. **Confusing "full" with "empty"** (–3 marks): When `front == rear`, you must consult `size` to disambiguate. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Not showing the array state** in trace questions (–2 marks): A textual answer like "front becomes 5" without showing the array $A$ loses half the marks allocated to the trace.
> 4. **Drawing a singly linked list for `deleteRear`** (–2 marks): A singly linked list `deleteRear` is $\mathcal{O}(n)$ and is **not** a valid deque implementation per the KTU 2024 syllabus. Use a **doubly linked list**.
> 5. **Omitting boundary checks** `isEmpty`/`isFull` in algorithm questions (–1 mark each): Always start every mutator algorithm with the appropriate check.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Deque Module**

- **Definition:** A deque is a linear data structure allowing **insertion and deletion at both ends**.
- **Full Form:** *Double Ended Queue*.
- **Eight Core Operations:** `insertFront`, `insertRear`, `deleteFront`, `deleteRear`, `getFront`, `getRear`, `isEmpty`, `isFull`.
- **Time Complexity:** Every operation is **$\mathcal{O}(1)$** — this is a *defining property* and frequently tested.
- **Two Restricted Variants:** Input-restricted (insert only at rear) and Output-restricted (delete only at front).
- **Implementation 1 — Circular Array:** Uses modulo arithmetic `(index ± 1 + N) % N`. Requires a `size` counter to disambiguate empty vs. full when `front == rear`. Memory: $\Theta(N)$.
- **Implementation 2 — Doubly Linked List:** Uses header/trailer sentinels. All operations are pointer rewiring, $\mathcal{O}(1)$. Memory: $\Theta(n)$ where $n$ is current size (no wasted slots, unlike array).
- **Polymorphism:** A deque can simulate a stack, queue, or any mixed policy — it is the **most general** linear ADT.
- **Standard Pitfall:** Pointer coincidence `front == rear` means either *empty* or *full* — must use `size` to distinguish.
- **Standard Modulo Formula:** For "move backward," use $(\text{idx} - 1 + N) \bmod N$. For "move forward," use $(\text{idx} + 1) \bmod N$.
- **Key Applications to Memorize:** (1) Work-stealing schedulers, (2) Undo/Redo, (3) Palindrome checking, (4) Sliding-window network buffers, (5) Deque-based BFS for 0–1 graphs.
- **Examiner Buzzwords that Score Marks:** *Generalized queue*, *bidirectional access*, *$\mathcal{O}(1)$ operations*, *FIFO and LIFO simulation*, *circular wraparound*.
- **C++ STL Equivalent:** `std::deque<T>` (pronounced "deck") — backed by a segmented array, supports random access $\mathcal{O}(1)$.
- **Java Equivalent:** `java.util.ArrayDeque<T>` — resizable array implementation, **faster than `Stack` and `LinkedList`** as a stack/queue.
- **One-Line Memory Hook:** *"A deque is to a queue what a power drill is to a screwdriver — same function, both ends available."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Expressions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In computer science, an **arithmetic expression** is a finite combination of operands (operands) and operators (operators) that is syntactically valid according to the rules of the language grammar. The **Evaluation of Expressions** is the systematic process of computing the numerical value of such an expression by processing its constituent tokens using well-defined precedence and associativity rules, typically assisted by a **Stack** abstract data type.

Expressions can be written in three distinct notational forms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Standard Notations for Expressions**
> 1. **Infix Notation** — Operator is placed *between* two operands.
>    *Example:* `A + B`
> 2. **Prefix Notation** (Polish Notation) — Operator is placed *before* its two operands.
>    *Example:* `+ A B`
> 3. **Postfix Notation** (Reverse Polish Notation / RPN) — Operator is placed *after* its two operands.
>    *Example:* `A B +`

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Restaurant Order Slip" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a waiter taking your order verbally. If you say "I want a burger **and** a coke **or** a lemonade", the cook gets confused — *what comes first?* That's infix notation: the human brain uses precedence to resolve it, but machines don't naturally do that.
>
> Now imagine you write the order on a slip in a strict, unambiguous order: `burger coke + lemonade` (postfix). The cook reads left-to-right, sees two items, an operator, performs the action, and proceeds. **No ambiguity, no backtracking, no parentheses** — that's why postfix/prefix are loved by compilers and CPU instruction sets (e.g., the Java Virtual Machine stack).

## 1.3 Why We Need Multiple Notations

| Notation | Parser Complexity | Parentheses Required? | Hardware Friendly |
|---|---|---|---|
| Infix | High (needs precedence parsing) | Yes | No |
| Prefix | Moderate | No | Yes (LISP family) |
| Postfix | Low (single left-to-right scan) | No | Yes (HP calculators, JVM) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Compilers universally convert human-written **infix** expressions into **postfix** (or a parse tree) before generating machine code. Mastering this conversion is foundational for compiler design, expression evaluators, and stack-based virtual machines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operator Precedence & Associativity

The behaviour of every conversion/evaluation algorithm is governed by a **precedence table**. The KTU board examiner expects you to know this table verbatim.

### 2.1.1 Precedence Hierarchy (Highest to Lowest)

| Precedence Level | Operators | Associativity | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **3 (Highest)** | Exponentiation `^` | Right-to-Left | `2^3^2 = 2^(3^2) = 512` |
| **2 (Middle)** | Multiplication `*`, Division `/`, Modulo `%` | Left-to-Right | `8/4*2 = 4` |
| **1 (Lowest)** | Addition `+`, Subtraction `-` | Left-to-Right | `8-4+2 = 6` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Parentheses `(` `)`** are *not operators* — they are grouping symbols with the **highest precedence** during *conversion*, but are *stripped out* in postfix/prefix form because their effect is baked into the operand order.

### 2.1.2 The Three Comparison Rules Used Inside the Stack Algorithm

When converting infix to postfix, for any two operators `op1` (top of stack) and `op2` (incoming scanner character), the decision to *pop* `op1` is governed by:

> **Rule 1 — Precedence:** If `precedence(op1) >= precedence(op2)`, pop `op1`.
>
> **Rule 2 — Right-Associative Exception:** If `op1` and `op2` have *equal* precedence AND `op2` is right-associative (e.g., `^`), *do not* pop `op1` — push `op2` instead.
>
> **Rule 3 — Left-Parentheses Override:** A `(` on the stack is **never** popped based on precedence alone; it is popped only when a matching `)` is encountered.

## 2.2 The Infix-to-Postfix Algorithm (Shunting-Yard by Dijkstra)

### 2.2.1 Algorithmic Steps (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Initialize** an empty operator stack `S` and an empty output queue/string `Q`.
2. **Scan** the infix expression token by token from **left to right**.
3. **If token is an operand (letter/digit):** Append it directly to `Q`.
4. **If token is `(`:** Push it onto `S`.
5. **If token is `)`:**
   - Repeatedly pop from `S` and append to `Q` until the top of `S` is `(`.
   - Pop and discard the `(`.
6. **If token is an operator `op`:**
   - While `S` is not empty AND `top(S) ≠ '('` AND `(precedence(top(S)) > precedence(op)` OR `(precedence(top(S)) == precedence(op) AND associativity(op) == Left))`:
     - Pop the top of `S` and append to `Q`.
   - Push `op` onto `S`.
7. **After scanning all tokens:** Pop and append all remaining operators from `S` to `Q`.
8. **Output:** The contents of `Q` is the postfix expression.

### 2.2.2 Why This Works (The "Why" Behind the "How")

- Operands emerge in `Q` in the **exact order** they will be consumed at evaluation time (left-to-right).
- Operators emerge in `Q` in the **exact order** they will be applied, which is the reverse of the stack's internal ordering — guaranteeing precedence is respected.
- The stack serves as a **delayed-action buffer** holding higher-precedence operators until their operands are committed.

## 2.3 Postfix Evaluation Algorithm

1. **Initialize** an empty operand stack `S`.
2. **Scan** the postfix expression left to right.
3. **If token is an operand:** Push its value onto `S`.
4. **If token is an operator `op`:** Pop the **top** element as `operand2`, pop the **next** element as `operand1`, compute `result = operand1 op operand2`, push `result` onto `S`.
5. **Result:** The final value remaining on `S` is the expression's value.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Bug Trap:** The order of popping matters! For non-commutative operators like `-` and `/`, the *first* pop is `operand2` (the right-hand side), the *second* pop is `operand1` (the left-hand side). Reversing this yields `5 - 3 = 2` instead of the correct `3 - 5 = -2`.

## 2.4 Infix-to-Prefix Algorithm (Reverse-Polish Trick)

Prefix conversion uses a mirror strategy:

1. **Reverse** the infix expression string and **swap every `(` with `)`** and vice versa.
2. **Apply the infix-to-postfix algorithm** on this modified string.
3. **Reverse** the resulting postfix string to obtain the prefix expression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the swap?** Reversing reverses the order of evaluation. Swapping parentheses compensates so that the originally-opening bracket (now on the right of its sub-expression) acts as a closer during the modified scan, and the originally-closing bracket (now on the left) acts as an opener.

## 2.5 Prefix Evaluation Algorithm

1. **Initialize** an empty operand stack `S`.
2. **Scan** the prefix expression from **right to left** (this is the key difference).
3. **If token is an operand:** Push its value onto `S`.
4. **If token is an operator `op`:** Pop the **top** as `operand1`, pop the **next** as `operand2`, compute `result = operand1 op operand2`, push `result`.
5. **Result:** The final value on `S` is the answer.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Stack depth bound (postfix eval) | $D_{max} \leq n$ where $n$ is operand count | Memory budgeting in embedded JVMs |
| Algorithm time complexity (all four) | $T(n) = O(n)$ | Real-time calculator firmware |
| Algorithm space complexity | $S(n) = O(n)$ | Stack-overflow risk assessment |
| Required precedence comparisons | $n - 1$ for $n$ operators | Branch-prediction analysis |
| Operand-to-operator ratio (valid expr) | $\text{operands} = \text{operators} + 1$ | Input validation / parser sanity |
| Conversion correctness invariant | In-order traversal of parse tree | Compiler AST verification |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Front-ends (lexers/parsers) convert infix to AST/postfix; back-ends generate stack machine code.
- **Spreadsheet Engines:** Excel/Google Sheets internally RPN-evaluate formulas.
- **3D Graphics Pipelines:** Shader compilers and GLSL optimizers heavily use RPN for constant folding.
- **Embedded Calculators:** HP-12C, HP-48 use pure RPN — faster, fewer keystrokes, no parentheses.
- **Database Query Optimizers:** SQL `WHERE` expressions are evaluated using stack-based expression trees.
- **Blockchain Smart Contracts:** Solidity/EVM uses a stack machine (similar to JVM) — every opcode is an RPN operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Infix to Postfix Conversion

**Infix Expression:** `(A + B) * C - D / E + F`

### Token-by-Token Walkthrough

| Step | Token Scanned | Stack (top→bottom) | Output (Postfix) | Action / Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `A` | `(empty)` | `A` | Operand → append to output |
| 2 | `+` | `+` | `A` | Stack empty → push operator |
| 3 | `B` | `+` | `A B` | Operand → append |
| 4 | `)` | `(empty)` | `A B +` | Pop `+`, append, then discard `(` |
| 5 | `*` | `*` | `A B +` | Stack empty → push |
| 6 | `C` | `*` | `A B + C` | Operand → append |
| 7 | `-` | `* -` | `A B + C` | Top `*` has precedence ≥ `-` → pop `*`, append |
| 8 | `D` | `-` | `A B + C *` | Operand → append |
| 9 | `/` | `- /` | `A B + C * D` | Top `-` (prec 1) < `/` (prec 2) → do not pop, push `/` |
| 10 | `E` | `- /` | `A B + C * D E` | Operand → append |
| 11 | `+` | `(empty)` | `A B + C * D E / -` | Pop `/` (prec 2 ≥ 1), pop `-` (prec 1 ≥ 1), then push `+` |
| 12 | `F` | `+` | `A B + C * D E / - F` | Operand → append |
| 13 | `(end)` | `(empty)` | `A B + C * D E / - F +` | Pop all remaining; final `+` appended |

### Final Postfix Expression

$$
\boxed{\texttt{A B + C * D E / - F +}}
$$

### Step-by-Step Algorithmic Pseudocode (C-style, KTU Standard)

```c
void infixToPostfix(char infix[], char postfix[]) {
    Stack S; init(&S);
    int i = 0, j = 0;
    char ch, op;

    while ((ch = infix[i++]) != '\0') {
        if (isalnum(ch)) {
            postfix[j++] = ch;            // Rule: operand → output
        }
        else if (ch == '(') {
            push(&S, ch);                  // Rule: ( → stack
        }
        else if (ch == ')') {
            while (peek(&S) != '(')
                postfix[j++] = pop(&S);
            pop(&S);                       // discard '('
        }
        else {                             // ch is an operator
            while (!isEmpty(&S) && peek(&S) != '(' &&
                   (precedence(peek(&S)) > precedence(ch) ||
                   (precedence(peek(&S)) == precedence(ch) && associativity(ch) == LEFT))) {
                postfix[j++] = pop(&S);
            }
            push(&S, ch);
        }
    }
    while (!isEmpty(&S))
        postfix[j++] = pop(&S);
    postfix[j] = '\0';
}
```

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Postfix Evaluation

**Evaluate the postfix expression:**
$$
\texttt{5 3 + 8 * 4 2 / -}
$$

Let `A = 5`, `B = 3`, `C = 8`, `D = 4`, `E = 2`. Equivalent infix: `(5+3) * 8 - 4/2`.

### Detailed Walkthrough

| Step | Token | Stack (top→bottom) | Operation Performed |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `5` | `5` | Push 5 |
| 2 | `3` | `5 3` | Push 3 |
| 3 | `+` | `8` | Pop 3 (op2), Pop 5 (op1) → `5 + 3 = 8` → Push 8 |
| 4 | `8` | `8 8` | Push 8 |
| 5 | `*` | `64` | Pop 8 (op2), Pop 8 (op1) → `8 * 8 = 64` → Push 64 |
| 6 | `4` | `64 4` | Push 4 |
| 7 | `2` | `64 4 2` | Push 2 |
| 8 | `/` | `64 2` | Pop 2 (op2), Pop 4 (op1) → `4 / 2 = 2` → Push 2 |
| 9 | `-` | `62` | Pop 2 (op2), Pop 64 (op1) → `64 - 2 = 62` → Push 62 |

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\text{Result} = 62}
$$

### Evaluation Pseudocode (KTU Standard)

```c
int evaluatePostfix(char postfix[]) {
    Stack S; init(&S);
    int i = 0, op1, op2, result;
    char ch;

    while ((ch = postfix[i++]) != '\0') {
        if (isdigit(ch)) {
            push(&S, ch - '0');            // convert char to int
        }
        else {
            op2 = pop(&S);                 // RIGHT operand first
            op1 = pop(&S);                 // LEFT operand second
            switch (ch) {
                case '+': result = op1 + op2; break;
                case '-': result = op1 - op2; break;
                case '*': result = op1 * op2; break;
                case '/': result = op1 / op2; break;
                case '^': result = pow(op1, op2); break;
            }
            push(&S, result);
        }
    }
    return pop(&S);
}
```

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Infix to Prefix Conversion

**Infix Expression:** `(A + B) * C - D / E`

### Step 1: Reverse the infix string and swap parentheses

$$
\texttt{Original:}\quad (A + B) * C - D / E
$$

$$
\texttt{Reversed + swapped:}\quad E / D - C * (B + A)
$$

### Step 2: Apply infix-to-postfix on the modified string

| Step | Token | Stack | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `E` | ` ` | `E` |
| 2 | `/` | `/` | `E` |
| 3 | `D` | `/` | `E D` |
| 4 | `-` | `(empty)` | `E D /` (pop `/`, then push `-`) |
| 5 | `C` | `-` | `E D / C` |
| 6 | `*` | `- *` | `E D / C` (pop `-`, then push `*`) |
| 7 | `(` | `*` | `E D / C` (push `(`) |
| 8 | `B` | `* (` | `E D / C B` |
| 9 | `+` | `* ( +` | `E D / C B` |
| 10 | `A` | `* ( +` | `E D / C B A` |
| 11 | `)` | `*` | `E D / C B A +` (pop `+`, pop `(`) |
| 12 | end | `(empty)` | `E D / C B A + * -` (pop `*`, pop `-`) |

### Step 3: Reverse the postfix output to get prefix

$$
\boxed{\texttt{- * + A B C / D E}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Example 4 — Prefix Evaluation

**Prefix Expression:** `- * + A B C / D E` with $A=2, B=3, C=4, D=8, E=2$

Equivalent infix: $(2+3) \times 4 - 8/2 = 20 - 4 = 16$.

### Right-to-Left Walkthrough

| Step | Token (right→left) | Stack | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `E=2` | `2` | Push |
| 2 | `D=8` | `2 8` | Push |
| 3 | `/` | `4` | Pop 8 (op2), Pop 2 (op1) → `2/8`? No — `8/2=4` (op1 was 2 popped first, op2 was 8). Wait — in prefix, **op1 = first pop = first operand read R→L = 2**, **op2 = second pop = 8** → `2/8`? |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall — Operand Order in Prefix Eval**
>
> In **prefix**, scanning goes **right-to-left**. The *first* popped value is the **LEFT operand** (op1) and the *second* popped value is the **RIGHT operand** (op2). This is the **mirror** of postfix evaluation, where first-pop = right operand.

Correcting the table:

| Step | Token (R→L) | Stack | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `E=2` | `2` | Push |
| 2 | `D=8` | `2 8` | Push |
| 3 | `/` | `4` | Pop 8 (op2), Pop 2 (op1) → `2/8`? No — first pop is the *last* read R→L, which is `D=8`. First pop = op1 = 8, second pop = op2 = 2. So `op1/op2 = 8/2 = 4`. Push 4 |
| 4 | `C=4` | `4 4` | Push |
| 5 | `B=3` | `4 4 3` | Push |
| 6 | `A=2` | `4 4 3 2` | Push |
| 7 | `+` | `4 4 5` | First pop=2 (op1), second pop=3 (op2) → `2+3=5` |
| 8 | `*` | `4 20` | First pop=5 (op1), second pop=4 (op2) → `5*4=20` |
| 9 | `-` | `16` | First pop=20 (op1), second pop=4 (op2) → `20-4=16` |

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{16}
$$

---

## 3.5 Example 5 — Right-Associative Operator (`^`)

**Convert:** `A ^ B ^ C` (infix)

| Step | Token | Stack | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `A` | ` ` | `A` |
| 2 | `^` | `^` | `A` |
| 3 | `B` | `^` | `A B` |
| 4 | `^` | `^ ^` | `A B` (Equal precedence, but `^` is **RIGHT-associative** → do NOT pop) |
| 5 | `C` | `^ ^` | `A B C` |
| 6 | end | `(empty)` | `A B C ^ ^` |

### Postfix

$$
\boxed{\texttt{A B C \^{} \^{}}}
$$

### Verification

This correctly evaluates to `A ^ (B ^ C)`, matching the right-associative rule. Had we used a left-associative rule blindly, we would have produced `A B ^ C ^` which evaluates to `(A ^ B) ^ C` — **a fundamentally different and wrong answer**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Expression Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infix Expression Input] --> B[Tokenizer Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C{Token Type}
    C -- Operand --> D[Append to Output Queue]
    C -- Operator --> E[Precedence and Associativity Resolver]
    C -- Left Paren --> F[Push to Operator Stack]
    C -- Right Paren --> G[Pop Stack until Left Paren]
    E --> H[Operator Stack S]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    D --> I[Postfix or Prefix String]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Stack Based Evaluator]
    J --> K[Final Numerical Result]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Infix-to-Postfix Conversion Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Scan] --> chkToken{Is Token Operand}
    chkToken -- Yes --> addOut[Append to Postfix Output]
    chkToken -- No --> chkOp{Is Token Operator}
    chkOp -- Yes --> chkStack{Stack Empty or Top is Left Paren}
    chkStack -- Yes --> pushOp[Push Operator onto Stack]
    chkStack -- No --> chkPrec{Top Prec >= Token Prec AND Left Associative}
    chkPrec -- Yes --> popOp[Pop Stack to Output]
    popOp --> chkStack
    chkPrec -- No --> pushOp
    chkOp -- No --> chkL{Is Token Left Paren}
    chkL -- Yes --> pushL[Push Left Paren onto Stack]
    chkL -- No --> chkR{Is Token Right Paren]
    chkR -- Yes --> popUntilL[Pop until Left Paren found, discard it]
    chkR -- No --> endX[Invalid Token]
    addOut --> nextToken[Read Next Token]
    pushOp --> nextToken
    pushL --> nextToken
    popUntilL --> nextToken
    nextToken --> moreTokens{More Tokens}
    moreTokens -- Yes --> startA
    moreTokens -- No --> emptyStack[Pop all remaining operators to output]
    emptyStack --> result[Final Postfix Expression]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stack Evolution During Postfix Evaluation of `5 3 + 8 *`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Step 1: Read 5, Stack contains 5]
    s2[Step 2: Read 3, Stack contains 5 then 3]
    s3[Step 3: Read Plus operator, Pop 3 and 5, Compute 5+3 equal 8, Push 8]
    s4[Step 4: Read 8, Stack contains 8 then 8]
    s5[Step 5: Read Multiply operator, Pop 8 and 8, Compute 8 times 8 equal 64, Push 64]
    s6[Final Result on Stack Top equal 64]
    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Infix to Prefix Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Step 1: Read Original Infix String] --> p2[Step 2: Reverse the String Character by Character]
    p2 --> p3[Step 3: Swap every Left Paren with Right Paren]
    p3 --> p4[Step 4: Apply Infix to Postfix Algorithm on Modified String]
    p4 --> p5[Step 5: Reverse the Resulting Postfix String]
    p5 --> p6[Step 6: Output is the Final Prefix Expression]
```

## 4.5 Comparative State Diagram — Three Notations of the Same Expression

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    infixA[Infix: A plus B times C] --> conv1[Shunting Yard Algorithm]
    conv1 --> postA[Postfix: A B plus C times]
    infixA --> conv2[Reverse Polish Trick]
    conv2 --> preA[Prefix: times plus A B C]
    postA --> eval1[Stack Evaluation L to R]
    preA --> eval2[Stack Evaluation R to L]
    eval1 --> resA[Result equal ABC evaluated]
    eval2 --> resA
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. Define Infix, Prefix, and Postfix notations. Write the postfix form of the expression `A + B * C`.**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Infix Notation:** The operator is written *between* its two operands. This is the standard mathematical notation humans use. *Example:* `X + Y`
- **Prefix Notation (Polish Notation):** The operator is written *before* its two operands. *Example:* `+ X Y`
- **Postfix Notation (Reverse Polish Notation):** The operator is written *after* its two operands. *Example:* `X Y +`

**Conversion of `A + B * C`:**

| Step | Token | Stack | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | — | A |
| 2 | + | + | A |
| 3 | B | + | A B |
| 4 | * | + * | A B (precedence `+` < `*`, do not pop) |
| 5 | C | + * | A B C |
| 6 | end | — | A B C * + |

**Postfix form:** $\texttt{A B C * +}$

---

### **Q2. Explain operator precedence and associativity. How do they affect expression evaluation?**  `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Operator Precedence** defines the order in which operators are evaluated in an expression when no parentheses are present. Higher precedence operators are evaluated first.
  - *Example:* In `A + B * C`, `*` has higher precedence than `+`, so `B * C` is computed first.

- **Associativity** resolves ambiguity when two operators of the *same* precedence appear consecutively.
  - **Left-to-Right Associative:** `A - B - C` is evaluated as `(A - B) - C`. Applies to `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`.
  - **Right-to-Left Associative:** `A ^ B ^ C` is evaluated as `A ^ (B ^ C)`. Applies to `^` (exponentiation).

- **Impact on Evaluation:**
  - The **infix-to-postfix conversion algorithm** explicitly uses precedence (with associativity as a tie-breaker for equal-precedence operators) to decide when to pop operators from the stack.
  - Without these rules, expressions like `8 - 4 + 2` could be ambiguously evaluated as `2` or `6`. The standard rule guarantees `6`.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Conversion + Evaluation `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Convert the following infix expression to postfix form and hence evaluate it using stack. Show all intermediate steps clearly.**

**Infix Expression:** `((A + B) * C - D) ^ (E + F / G)`

Given values: $A=2, B=3, C=4, D=6, E=1, F=8, G=2$

#### **Part (a)** — Convert to Postfix (7 Marks)

| Step | Token | Stack (top→bottom) | Postfix Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `(` | `(` | — |
| 2 | `(` | `((` | — |
| 3 | `A` | `((` | `A` |
| 4 | `+` | `(( +` | `A` |
| 5 | `B` | `(( +` | `A B` |
| 6 | `)` | `(` | `A B +` (pop `+`, discard `(`) |
| 7 | `*` | `(*` | `A B +` |
| 8 | `C` | `(*` | `A B + C` |
| 9 | `-` | `(* -` | `A B + C *` (pop `*` ≥ `-`) |
| 10 | `D` | `(* -` | `A B + C * D` |
| 11 | `)` | `(empty)` | `A B + C * D -` (pop `-`, discard `(`) |
| 12 | `^` | `^` | `A B + C * D -` |
| 13 | `(` | `^ (` | `A B + C * D -` |
| 14 | `E` | `^ (` | `A B + C * D - E` |
| 15 | `+` | `^ ( +` | `A B + C * D - E` |
| 16 | `F` | `^ ( +` | `A B + C * D - E F` |
| 17 | `/` | `^ ( + /` | `A B + C * D - E F` (precedence `+` < `/`, do not pop) |
| 18 | `G` | `^ ( + /` | `A B + C * D - E F G` |
| 19 | `)` | `^` | `A B + C * D - E F G / +` (pop `/`, pop `+`, discard `(`) |
| 20 | end | — | `A B + C * D - E F G / + ^` (pop `^`) |

**Final Postfix:** $\boxed{\texttt{A B + C * D - E F G / + \^{}}}$

#### **Part (b)** — Evaluate the Postfix (7 Marks)

Substituting values: $A=2, B=3, C=4, D=6, E=1, F=8, G=2$

**Postfix:** `2 3 + 4 * 6 - 1 8 2 / + ^`

| Step | Token | Stack | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | `2` | Push |
| 2 | 3 | `2 3` | Push |
| 3 | + | `5` | $2+3=5$ |
| 4 | 4 | `5 4` | Push |
| 5 | * | `20` | $5 \times 4 = 20$ |
| 6 | 6 | `20 6` | Push |
| 7 | - | `14` | $20 - 6 = 14$ |
| 8 | 1 | `14 1` | Push |
| 9 | 8 | `14 1 8` | Push |
| 10 | 2 | `14 1 8 2` | Push |
| 11 | / | `14 1 4` | $8/2 = 4$ |
| 12 | + | `14 5` | $1 + 4 = 5$ |
| 13 | ^ | `537824` | $14^5 = 537824$ |

**Final Result:** $\boxed{537824}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Don't forget to POP and DISCARD `(`** when `)` is encountered — students often forget the final `pop()` to remove the matched parenthesis from the stack.
> 2. **Equal-precedence + left-associative operators must be popped** (Rule 1 from Section 2). Only `^` (right-associative) is exempt.
> 3. **Order of popping matters**: For `5 3 -`, popping 3 first gives `op2=3`, popping 5 gives `op1=5`, computing `5 - 3 = 2`. Reverse it and you get `3 - 5 = -2`. **Examiner deducts 1 mark for sign errors.**
> 4. **End-of-expression cleanup**: Always pop residual operators from the stack. Missing this step is the #1 reason students get the final postfix string wrong by 1 operator.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative: Infix to Prefix + Evaluation `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Convert the following infix expression to prefix form and hence evaluate it using stack. Show all intermediate steps clearly.**

**Infix Expression:** `(A + B * C) - (D / E + F) ^ G`

Given values: $A=5, B=2, C=3, D=8, E=2, F=1, G=2$

#### **Part (a)** — Convert to Prefix (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Reverse the infix string and swap parentheses.**

- Original: `(A + B * C) - (D / E + F) ^ G`
- Reversed: `G ^ (F + E / D) - (C * B + A)`
- Swap parens: `G ^ )F + E / D( - )C * B + A(`

**Step 2: Apply infix-to-postfix on the modified string.**

| Step | Token | Stack | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | G | — | `G` |
| 2 | ^ | `^` | `G` |
| 3 | ( | `^ (` | `G` |
| 4 | F | `^ (` | `G F` |
| 5 | + | `^ ( +` | `G F` |
| 6 | E | `^ ( +` | `G F E` |
| 7 | / | `^ ( + /` | `G F E` |
| 8 | D | `^ ( + /` | `G F E D` |
| 9 | ) | `^` | `G F E D / +` (pop `/`, pop `+`, discard `(`) |
| 10 | - | (empty) | `G F E D / + ^ -` (pop `^`, pop `-`... wait, push `-`) |
| 11 | ( | `- (` | `G F E D / + ^` |
| 12 | C | `- (` | `G F E D / + ^ C` |
| 13 | * | `- ( *` | `G F E D / + ^ C` |
| 14 | B | `- ( *` | `G F E D / + ^ C B` |
| 15 | + | `- ( * +` | `G F E D / + ^ C B` |
| 16 | A | `- ( * +` | `G F E D / + ^ C B A` |
| 17 | ) | `-` | `G F E D / + ^ C B A + *` (pop `+`, pop `*`, discard `(`) |
| 18 | end | — | `G F E D / + ^ C B A + * -` (pop `-`) |

**Step 3: Reverse the postfix output to obtain prefix.**

Reverse of `G F E D / + ^ C B A + * -` is:

**Final Prefix:** $\boxed{\texttt{- * + A B C \^{} + / D E F G}}$

#### **Part (b)** — Evaluate the Prefix (7 Marks)

Values: $A=5, B=2, C=3, D=8, E=2, F=1, G=2$

**Prefix:** `- * + A B C ^ + / D E F G`

**Scan from RIGHT to LEFT:**

| Step | Token (R→L) | Stack | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | G=2 | `2` | Push |
| 2 | F=1 | `2 1` | Push |
| 3 | E=2 | `2 1 2` | Push |
| 4 | D=8 | `2 1 2 8` | Push |
| 5 | / | `2 1 4` | First pop=8 (op1), second pop=2 (op2) → $8/2=4$ |
| 6 | + | `2 5` | First pop=4 (op1), second pop=1 (op2) → $4+1=5$ |
| 7 | ^ | `32` | First pop=5 (op1), second pop=2 (op2) → $5^2=25$... correction: $2^5=32$ (op1=2 popped first, op2=5) → $2^5=32$ |
| 8 | C=3 | `32 3` | Push |
| 9 | B=2 | `32 3 2` | Push |
| 10 | A=5 | `32 3 2 5` | Push |
| 11 | + | `32 3 7` | First pop=5 (op1), second pop=2 (op2) → $5+2=7$ |
| 12 | * | `32 21` | First pop=7 (op1), second pop=3 (op2) → $7 \times 3 = 21$ |
| 13 | - | `11` | First pop=21 (op1), second pop=32 (op2) → $21 - 32 = -11$ |

**Final Result:** $\boxed{-11}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Prefix evaluation scans RIGHT to LEFT** — students who scan left-to-right produce garbage. **2-mark penalty.**
> 2. **The `^` operator in prefix scan**: the *first popped* value is the **base** (left operand of the original infix), the *second popped* is the **exponent** (right operand). Students frequently invert this, getting $5^2 = 25$ instead of $2^5 = 32$.
> 3. **Infix-to-prefix via reverse-trick**: forgetting the **parenthesis swap** step is the most common error; it shifts every token's binding context.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Evaluation of Expressions**

- ✅ **Three notations exist**: Infix (operator between), Prefix (operator before), Postfix (operator after).
- ✅ **Postfix is the easiest to evaluate**: single left-to-right scan, no parentheses, uses one stack.
- ✅ **Prefix evaluation scans RIGHT to LEFT** (mirror of postfix).
- ✅ **Infix-to-Postfix Algorithm (Dijkstra's Shunting Yard)**:
  - Operands go directly to output.
  - `(` always goes to stack.
  - `)` triggers popping until matching `(` is found and discarded.
  - Operators: pop while top-of-stack has *higher* precedence, OR *equal* precedence *and* incoming is left-associative.
  - At the end, flush the entire stack to output.
- ✅ **Right-associative exception**: `^` does **not** pop an equal-precedence `^` from the stack.
- ✅ **Operand pop order in evaluation**:
  - **Postfix:** First pop = `op2` (RHS), second pop = `op1` (LHS).
  - **Prefix:** First pop = `op1` (LHS), second pop = `op2` (RHS).
- ✅ **Infix-to-Prefix trick**: Reverse string → Swap `(` with `)` → Apply infix-to-postfix → Reverse output.
- ✅ **Time complexity of all four algorithms is O(n)**; space complexity is O(n) for the stack.
- ✅ **Valid expression invariant**: `number of operands = number of operators + 1` (use this for input validation).
- ✅ **Common KTU mistake**: Forgetting to pop residual operators at end of conversion — leads to **one operator short** in the postfix output.
- ✅ **Common KTU mistake**: Forgetting to discard `(` when `)` is encountered — leaves a stray parenthesis on the stack that corrupts subsequent pops.
- ✅ **Stack-based evaluation is the foundation** of stack-based virtual machines (JVM, CLR, EVM) and is tested every semester without fail.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Self-Referential Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Self-Referential Structures # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Self-Referential Structure (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > A **self-referential structure** is a user-defined composite data type in the C programming language in which **one or more of its members are pointers pointing to instances of the same structure type itself**. It is the foundational building block for dynamic, non-contiguous, pointer-based data structures such as **Linked Lists, Stacks, Queues, Trees, and Graphs**. The formal C-syntax prototype of a self-referential structure is as follows: ```c struct TagName { data_type member1; data_type member2; /* ... other members ... */ struct TagName *pointer_member; /* Pointer to same struct type */ }; ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why C specifically?** While the concept exists in C++ (via `this` and class references) and other languages, KTU 2024 Scheme PEECT522 (Data Structures) explicitly teaches self-referential structures in the context of **C programming**, because C gives the programmer *raw, explicit control* over memory layout, pointer arithmetic, and dynamic heap allocation — which are essential to understand how data structures actually work in memory. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **treasure hunt** where you are standing at the first location. On this location, there is a **clue** and a **map that points to the next location**. When you reach the next location, you find another clue and another map pointing to the location after that, and so on. Each location "knows" where the next one is. In this analogy: | Treasure Hunt Element | Self-Referential Structure Member | | :--- | :--- | | Location (the box itself) | The `struct` instance in memory | | The clue (information) | A data field like `int data` | | The map pointing to the next location | The pointer `struct Node *next` | So, a self-referential structure is essentially a **"box that contains data and the address of another similar box."** When chained together, these boxes form a **linked data structure**. --- ## 1.3 Why Self-Referential Structures Matter in Data Structures > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Self-referential structures are the **literal syntactic and semantic foundation** upon which the entire non-contiguous memory family of data structures is built. Without them, **dynamic linked lists, trees, and graphs cannot exist in C**. The three core properties they provide are: 1. **Dynamic Size:** Unlike arrays, the collection can grow or shrink at runtime (no fixed compile-time bound). 2. **Non-Contiguous Memory Allocation:** Nodes can reside anywhere in the heap; they are connected only by pointers. 3. **Efficient Insertion/Deletion:** No shifting of elements is required, unlike arrays. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Memory Metric:** On a typical 32-bit architecture, a pointer occupies **4 bytes**; on a 64-bit architecture, a pointer occupies **8 bytes**. This is the overhead per node beyond the actual data payload. --- ## 1.4 Minimum Prerequisite Concepts Before proceeding, the student **must be fluent** in the following: - **`struct` keyword** — for declaring structure types. - **Pointer declaration** — `struct Node *p;` - **Dynamic memory allocation** — `malloc()`, `calloc()`, `free()`. - **The `->` (arrow) operator** — used to access a member *through* a pointer to a struct. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Self-Referential Structure A self-referential structure typically has **two categories of members**: ### Category 1: Data Members (Payload) These store the actual information. Examples: - `int data;` - `char name[20];` - `float marks;` ### Category 2: Link Member(s) (Pointer) These store the address of another node of the **same** structure type. The most common patterns are: | Pattern | Link Members | Used In | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Singly Linked** | One pointer: `struct Node *next` | Singly Linked List, Stack, Queue | | **Doubly Linked** | Two pointers: `*prev` and `*next` | Doubly Linked List, Deque | | **Tree Node** | Multiple child pointers | Binary Tree, N-ary Tree | | **Graph Node** | List of adjacent node pointers | Adjacency-list representation | --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes all critical mathematical and logical relationships involving self-referential structures. **Memorize these for the ESE (End Semester Examination).** | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Memory size of a node | $S_{node} = S_{data} + n \cdot S_{ptr}$ | Bytes; $S_{ptr}$ = **4 B (32-bit)** or **8 B (64-bit)** | | 2 | Total heap memory for $N$ nodes | $M_{total} = N \cdot S_{node} + N \cdot S_{overhead}$ | Includes malloc metadata (~8–16 B per allocation) | | 3 | Number of pointers in a $k$-ary tree node | $n_{ptr} = k$ | E.g., binary tree: $n_{ptr}=2$ | | 4 | Reachability test | If $p \rightarrow next == NULL$, then $p$ is the **last node** | Termination condition for traversal | | 5 | Head pointer | $head == NULL \implies$ **empty structure** | Initial state check | | 6 | Time to traverse $N$ nodes | $T_{traverse} = O(N)$ | Sequential pointer-chasing | | 7 | Address arithmetic | $p = (struct\ Node *)\ malloc(sizeof(struct\ Node))$ | $p$ is the **return address** from heap | | 8 | Dereferencing | $\text{(*}p\text{).data} \equiv p \rightarrow data$ | Both notations are equivalent | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vertical Bar Rule:** Throughout this document, the notation $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value will be written as `\vert x\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown tables. --- ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Pointer A common student question is: *"Why use a pointer to the same struct and not the struct itself?"* > [!WARNING] > If you write `struct Node next;` (a value, not a pointer), the structure becomes **infinite in size**, because each `Node` would contain a complete `Node`, which contains a complete `Node`, and so on **forever**. The compiler will reject this with the error: *"Field 'next' has incomplete type."* By using a **pointer**, we store only the *address* (4 or 8 bytes) of the next node, allowing unlimited chaining. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Self-referential structures underpin: - **Operating Systems:** Process control blocks, linked free-block lists in memory managers. - **Compilers:** Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs), symbol tables. - **Databases:** B+ Tree indexes, hash table chains. - **Networking:** Adjacency lists for routing graphs. - **AI / Machine Learning:** Decision trees, linked-list based sparse representations. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step: From `struct` Definition to a Live Node in Memory > [!NOTE] > This walkthrough is exhaustive and deliberately slow. **Every algebraic / notational step is shown** because this is the exact sequence of cognitive steps a KTU examiner expects in an ESE answer. ### Step 1: Declare the Structure Type ```c struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; ``` - This declaration does **not** allocate any memory. - It only tells the compiler: *"There exists a blueprint called `Node` containing an `int` and a pointer to another `Node`."* ### Step 2: Compute the Size of the Structure The size of `struct Node` is the sum of its members' sizes (plus possible padding, but we ignore padding for theoretical analysis): $$S_{Node} = \text{sizeof}(int) + \text{sizeof}(struct\ Node*)$$ For a **64-bit system**: $$S_{Node} = 4\ \text{bytes} + 8\ \text{bytes} = 12\ \text{bytes}$$ (For a **32-bit system**: $S_{Node} = 4 + 4 = 8$ bytes.) > [!IMPORTANT] > Note that even though `next` *refers* to a `Node`, it does **not** include the size of the pointed-to node. The pointer only stores an *address*. ### Step 3: Declare a Head Pointer ```c struct Node *head = NULL; ``` - `head` is a pointer variable (typically on the stack). - Initialized to `NULL` to signify an **empty list**. - $S_{head} = 8$ bytes (on 64-bit), regardless of list length. ### Step 4: Allocate the First Node Dynamically ```c head = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); ``` - `malloc(sizeof(struct Node))` requests a **contiguous block of 12 bytes** from the heap. - The cast `(struct Node *)` is for type safety. - The returned address is stored in `head`. ### Step 5: Populate the Node ```c head->data = 10; head->next = NULL; ``` - `head->data` is shorthand for `(*head).data`, accessing the `int` member via the pointer. - `head->next = NULL` marks this node as the **last** in the (currently 1-node) chain. ### Step 6: Add a Second Node ```c struct Node *second = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); second->data = 20; second->next = NULL; head->next = second; /* Link first node to second */ ``` - A second 12-byte block is allocated. - The first node's `next` pointer is set to the address of the second node. - The chain is now: $\text{Node}_1 \rightarrow \text{Node}_2 \rightarrow \text{NULL}$. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Notation Equivalence > [!NOTE] > This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU 2024 theory questions where examiners ask: *"Compare the `.` and `->` operators."* | Operation | Using Object (`struct Node n;`) | Using Pointer (`struct Node *p;`) | Equivalent? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Access data field | $n.data$ | $p \rightarrow data$ | $n.data \equiv (\text{*}p).data \equiv p \rightarrow data$ | | Access link field | $n.next$ | $p \rightarrow next$ | $n.next \equiv (\text{*}p).next \equiv p \rightarrow next$ | | Pointer to object | `&n` | `p` (already a pointer) | N/A | | Change link | `n.next = q;` | `p \rightarrow next = q;` | Both update the same logical link | | Compile-time legality | Always | Always | Both are valid C | The arrow operator `->` is **defined** by the C standard as syntactic sugar: $$p \rightarrow m \;\equiv\; (\text{*}p).m$$ --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Traversing an N-Node Linked List We derive the loop that visits every node exactly once. **Goal:** Starting from `head`, print `data` of every node until `NULL` is reached. **Initialization:** - Let $p_0 = head$. - Loop counter $i$ starts at $0$. **Iterative Step:** - At step $i$, we are at node pointed to by $p_i$. - Print $p_i \rightarrow data$. - Advance: $p_{i+1} = p_i \rightarrow next$. **Termination Condition:** - Loop terminates when $p_i = NULL$, i.e., $p_i \rightarrow next$ was the last node and its `next` field is $NULL$. **Loop Body in C:** ```c struct Node *p = head; /* p_0 = head */ while (p != NULL) { printf("%d ", p->data); /* Visit p_i */ p = p->next; /* p_{i+1} = p_i -> next */ } ``` **Total number of iterations** is exactly $N$, the number of nodes, giving: $$T(N) = O(N)$$ --- ## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Memory Footprint of an N-Node Singly Linked List Each node occupies $S_{node}$ bytes of heap memory, and there is a one-time stack allocation of the head pointer. $$M_{heap}(N) = N \cdot S_{node}$$ $$M_{stack} = S_{head} = 8\ \text{bytes (64-bit)}$$ $$M_{total}(N) = N \cdot (S_{data} + S_{ptr}) + S_{head}$$ For $N$ nodes, each containing an `int` (4 B) and one pointer (8 B), on a 64-bit system: $$M_{total}(N) = N \cdot (4 + 8) + 8 = 12N + 8 \quad \text{bytes}$$ In the limit of large $N$: $$M_{total}(N) \approx 12N \quad \text{bytes}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Compare with a static array** of $N$ integers: $M_{array}(N) = 4N$ bytes. The linked list uses **3× more memory** due to pointer overhead, but gains flexibility of dynamic resizing. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Anatomy of a Self-Referential Structure Node > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block depicts a single `Node` in memory. The `next` member is a pointer; the dashed line represents the indirection to a *future* node (which may or may not exist). ```mermaid graph LR subgraph node1["struct Node Instance in Heap"] dataField["data : int (4 bytes)"] nextField["next : struct Node* (8 bytes)"] end nextField -. "points to address of" .-> node2["Next Node or NULL"] style dataField fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style nextField fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#804000 style node2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Chain of Three Nodes in a Singly Linked List ```mermaid graph LR headPtr["head (stack)"] -->|address of| n1["Node 1
data=10
next=*"] n1 -->|next| n2["Node 2
data=20
next=*"] n2 -->|next| n3["Node 3
data=30
next=NULL"] nullPtr["NULL
(end of list)"] style headPtr fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style n1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style n2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style n3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style nullPtr fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The `head` pointer is stored on the **stack** (yellow). - Each `Node` is a separate heap allocation (blue). - The arrow from each `next` field to the next box represents the **pointer** (an address). - The final node's `next` field points to `NULL` (red), terminating the chain. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture > [!NOTE] > This diagram models the *lifecycle* of a self-referential node from declaration to insertion, showing how the C runtime, heap, and pointer fields interact. ```mermaid graph TD A["Step 1: Define struct Node
(Compile-time blueprint)"] --> B["Step 2: Declare head pointer
struct Node *head = NULL"] B --> C["Step 3: malloc sizeof(struct Node)
Returns heap address"] C --> D["Step 4: Type-cast and store in head
head = (struct Node*)ptr"] D --> E["Step 5: Populate data field
head->data = value"] E --> F["Step 6: Set link field
head->next = NULL or another node"] F --> G{"Is more data
to be added?"} G -- Yes --> H["Allocate new node
and link via ->next"] H --> F G -- No --> I["Traversal using
while(p != NULL)"] style A fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41 style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style C fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460 style D fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460 style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style G fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style H fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Why `struct Node next;` (Without Pointer) Fails ```mermaid graph TD wrong["struct Node { int data; struct Node next; };"] wrong --> X["Compiler Error:
'next' has incomplete type
(infinite recursion)"] wrong2["struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; };"] wrong2 --> Y["Valid: pointer stores address
(fixed 8 bytes, no recursion)"] style wrong fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style wrong2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style X fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24 style Y fill:#c3e6cb,stroke:#155724 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** This is one of the most common 3-mark conceptual questions. Always emphasize the keyword **"pointer to the same structure"** — never just "the same structure." # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions are **direct, definition-based, and require a crisp 3–4 sentence answer**. Marks are awarded for keyword usage, so model answers are pre-tuned to the **valuation key pattern**. --- ### Q1. Define a self-referential structure. Give a suitable C example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **self-referential structure** is a structure in which one or more members are pointers pointing to instances of the same structure type. It is the fundamental building block for dynamic data structures such as linked lists, trees, and graphs. ```c struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; ``` Here, the member `next` is a pointer to a `struct Node`, i.e., a pointer to the structure type that contains it. This allows nodes to be linked together dynamically at runtime. --- ### Q2. Why can't a structure contain a member that is the *complete* structure of the same type (without using a pointer)? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** If a structure contains a member of the same type by value (not by pointer), it leads to **infinite recursion** in memory layout. For example, `struct Node { int data; struct Node next; };` would imply that each `Node` contains a full `Node`, which contains another full `Node`, ad infinitum, making the size **infinite** and **uncompilable**. A **pointer member** is used instead because a pointer has a **fixed, finite size** (typically 4 or 8 bytes) and merely stores the *address* of another node, not the node's full contents. This breaks the recursion and allows the C compiler to determine the structure's size at compile time. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every 14-mark question has **internal choice**. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as two fully independent alternatives. Each is split into (a) and (b) at **7 marks each**. --- ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model — Modified from Past Pattern]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a self-referential structure in C with a suitable code example. Discuss the difference between the `.` (dot) operator and the `->` (arrow) operator with examples. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following C structure declaration: ```c struct Student { int roll; char name[20]; struct Student *next; }; ``` Write a complete C program to: 1. Create a singly linked list of **3 students** by dynamically allocating nodes. 2. Traverse the list and print the `roll` and `name` of each student. 3. Compute and display the total memory (in bytes) consumed by the list on a **64-bit system**. Show the output of your program for sample data: `{(1, "Anu"), (2, "Bala"), (3, "Chitra")}`. --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks] **Definition [2 marks]:** A self-referential structure is a structure in which at least one member is a pointer to the same structure type. It is the syntactic prerequisite for implementing dynamic, non-contiguous data structures like linked lists, stacks, queues, and trees. **Code Example [2 marks]:** ```c struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; /* pointer to same struct type */ }; ``` **Difference between `.` and `->` operators [3 marks]:** | Aspect | Dot (`.`) Operator | Arrow (`->`) Operator | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Used with | A **struct object** (not pointer) | A **pointer** to a struct | | Syntax | `object.member` | `pointer->member` | | Internal form | Direct member access | Equivalent to `(*pointer).member` | | Example | `n.data = 5;` (where `n` is `struct Node n;`) | `p->data = 5;` (where `p` is `struct Node *p;`) | | Indirection | None — direct memory offset | Yes — dereferences pointer first | **Sample usage illustrating both:** ```c struct Node n1; /* struct object */ struct Node *p = &n1; /* pointer to it */ n1.data = 10; /* Dot operator */ p->data = 20; /* Arrow operator */ printf("%d %d", n1.data, p->data); /* Prints: 20 20 */ ``` Both `n1.data` and `p->data` refer to the **same memory location**. --- #### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks] **Complete C Program [5 marks]:** ```c #include #include #include struct Student { int roll; char name[20]; struct Student *next; }; int main(void) { /* Step 1: Create three nodes dynamically */ struct Student *head = (struct Student *)malloc(sizeof(struct Student)); struct Student *second = (struct Student *)malloc(sizeof(struct Student)); struct Student *third = (struct Student *)malloc(sizeof(struct Student)); /* Step 2: Populate the nodes */ head->roll = 1; strcpy(head->name, "Anu"); head->next = second; second->roll = 2; strcpy(second->name, "Bala"); second->next = third; third->roll = 3; strcpy(third->name, "Chitra"); third->next = NULL; /* Step 3: Traverse and print */ struct Student *p = head; printf("Roll\tName\n----\t----\n"); while (p != NULL) { printf("%d\t%s\n", p->roll, p->name); p = p->next; } /* Step 4: Compute and display memory */ /* On 64-bit: int=4, pointer=8, char[20]=20 => per node = 4+20+8 = 32 bytes */ long bytes_per_node = sizeof(struct Student); long total = 3L * bytes_per_node; printf("\nMemory per node : %ld bytes\n", bytes_per_node); printf("Total heap usage: %ld bytes (3 nodes)\n", total); /* Free the allocated memory */ free(head); free(second); free(third); return 0; } ``` **Sample Output [2 marks]:** ``` Roll Name ---- ---- 1 Anu 2 Bala 3 Chitra Memory per node : 32 bytes Total heap usage: 96 bytes (3 nodes) ``` **Memory calculation derivation (shown explicitly for marks):** $$S_{student} = S_{int} + S_{char[20]} + S_{ptr} = 4 + 20 + 8 = 32\ \text{bytes (64-bit)}$$ $$M_{total} = 3 \times 32 = 96\ \text{bytes}$$ --- ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model — Modified]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, illustrate the memory representation of a self-referential structure used in a singly linked list of 4 nodes containing integers 5, 15, 25, 35. Show the head pointer, all node fields, and the `NULL` terminator. Also explain how a traversal loop visits all nodes. **(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between the following with C code examples: 1. A structure containing itself by value vs. containing a pointer to itself. 2. Stack allocation (`struct Node n;`) vs. heap allocation (`malloc`) of a structure. --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks] **Memory Diagram [3 marks]:** ``` STACK HEAP +-----------+ +-------------------+ | head | -----------> | roll: 5 | +-----------+ | next: 0xA2 ------+--+ +-------------------+ | v +-------------------+ | roll: 15 | | next: 0xB7 ------+--+ +-------------------+ | v +-------------------+ | roll: 25 | | next: 0xC4 ------+--+ +-------------------+ | v +-------------------+ | roll: 35 | | next: NULL | +-------------------+ ``` **Description of the diagram [2 marks]:** - `head` is a pointer stored on the stack. - Each node in the heap contains an integer `roll` and a pointer `next`. - The `next` pointer of the last node is `NULL`, signalling the end of the list. **Traversal logic [2 marks]:** ```c struct Node *p = head; /* Start at the first node */ int count = 0; while (p != NULL) { /* Continue until NULL is reached */ printf("%d ", p->roll); /* Visit the current node */ p = p->next; /* Advance to the next node */ count++; } /* After the loop, count == 4 */ ``` The loop runs exactly $N = 4$ times. Each iteration advances `p` to the next node via the `->next` pointer. When `p->next == NULL` in the last node, the next iteration sets `p = NULL`, terminating the loop. The time complexity is $O(N)$. --- #### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks] **1. Value vs. Pointer [4 marks]:** ```c /* (i) INVALID: structure contains itself by value */ struct BadNode { int data; struct BadNode bad; /* ERROR: infinite size */ }; /* (ii) VALID: structure contains a pointer to itself */ struct GoodNode { int data; struct GoodNode *next; /* OK: pointer has fixed size */ }; ``` | Aspect | By Value (Invalid) | By Pointer (Valid) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Compiler response | Error: *"Field has incomplete type"* | Compiles successfully | | Memory size | Infinite (recursive) | Finite: $S_{data} + S_{ptr}$ | | Usage | Never used | Foundation of all linked structures | **2. Stack vs. Heap Allocation [3 marks]:** ```c /* Stack allocation — automatic, scoped, fixed at compile time */ struct Node n; n.data = 10; n.next = NULL; /* Heap allocation — dynamic, persists until free() */ struct Node *p = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); p->data = 10; p->next = NULL; /* ... use p ... */ free(p); ``` | Aspect | Stack (`struct Node n;`) | Heap (`malloc`) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Lifetime | Until function returns | Until explicitly `free()`-d | | Size known at | Compile time | Run time | | Address | Fixed stack frame | Returned by `malloc` | | Suitable for | Single temporary objects | Linked list nodes, dynamic structures | | Deallocation | Automatic | Manual via `free()` | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to free heap memory** — results in *memory leaks*. Always pair every `malloc` with a `free` in production code. KTU deducts **1 mark** if memory cleanup is absent. > 2. **Confusing `.` and `->`** — using `n->data` on a non-pointer, or `p.data` on a pointer, will cause a **compilation error**. Examiners award partial marks only if the intent is correct. > 3. **Omitting `NULL` termination** — forgetting to set the last node's `next` to `NULL` results in an **infinite loop** during traversal. This is the single most common runtime bug in KTU practical exams. > 4. **Mixing the value and pointer forms** — writing `struct Node n, *p;` and then using `p = n;` instead of `p = &n;` is a classic 1-mark deduction error. > 5. **Saying "structure contains itself" instead of "structure contains a pointer to itself"** — the most common definitional inaccuracy. Always specify the **pointer**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the night before the exam.** - ✅ A **self-referential structure** contains a **pointer** (not a value) to a structure of the same type. - ✅ The pointer member is **mandatory**; without it, the structure is **infinitely sized** and uncompilable. - ✅ Memory size of a node = sum of data members + $N_{ptr} \times S_{ptr}$ (typically **8 bytes** per pointer on 64-bit). - ✅ Use `.` for **objects**, `->` for **pointers**. They are equivalent via `p->m ≡ (*p).m`. - ✅ Always **initialize** head pointer to `NULL` to represent an empty list. - ✅ Always set the **last node's next to NULL** to terminate traversal. - ✅ Use `malloc(sizeof(struct Node))` for dynamic allocation; **always `free()`** after use. - ✅ Type-cast the `malloc` return: `(struct Node *)malloc(...)`. - ✅ Traversal pattern: `for (p = head; p != NULL; p = p->next) { ... }`. - ✅ Time to traverse $N$ nodes: $T(N) = O(N)$ (sequential pointer-chasing). - ✅ Self-referential structures enable **dynamic, non-contiguous, growable** data structures. - ✅ They are the **foundation** of linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, and graphs. - ✅ They trade **memory overhead** (extra pointer bytes) for **flexibility** (dynamic size, easy insertion/deletion). - ✅ Common KTU keywords to use in answers: *dynamic*, *non-contiguous*, *pointer to the same structure*, *heap allocation*, *traversal*, *link field*.

topics.title: Dynamic Memory Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Memory Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Memory Allocation (DMA)** is a memory management mechanism in C (and other programming languages) in which the size of the data structure is determined and memory is allocated **at runtime** from a region of memory known as the **Heap** (or **Free Store**), as opposed to static allocation where memory is reserved on the **Stack** at compile time.

In the context of **KTU PEECT522 – Data Structures**, DMA is the foundational mechanism that enables the construction of flexible, variable-sized data structures such as **linked lists, trees, graphs, hash tables, and dynamic arrays**, whose size cannot be predicted at compile time.

The C standard library provides four primary DMA functions, all declared in `<stdlib.h>`:

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `malloc()` | Allocates a block of uninitialized memory |
| `calloc()` | Allocates a block of zero-initialized memory |
| `realloc()` | Resizes an already-allocated memory block |
| `free()` | Deallocates (releases) a previously allocated block |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Dynamic Memory Allocation is the prerequisite building block for **Linked Lists, Stacks using Linked Lists, Queues using Linked Lists, Trees, and Graphs**. Every non-linear, non-array data structure in this course depends fundamentally on DMA. Marks are frequently allotted (typically 7–14 marks) in university exams for code involving `malloc()`/`calloc()` and node creation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are moving to a new city and need a place to stay:

- **Static Allocation** is like **buying a house before you arrive**. You must decide the size (1BHK, 2BHK, 3BHK) *before* you know how many family members will live with you. If you bought a 1BHK and your parents visit, there is no room. If you bought a 3BHK and you live alone, you wasted money and space.

- **Dynamic Allocation** is like **renting a serviced apartment on arrival**. You walk in, see how many people are with you, and rent *exactly* the right number of rooms — not one square foot more, not one square foot less. And when your family leaves, you **vacate** (free) the extra rooms and stop paying rent.

The **"rooms"** are bytes of memory, the **"landlord"** is the Operating System (via `malloc`), and **"vacating the rooms"** is calling `free()`.

## 1.3 Why Dynamic Memory Allocation is Required

| Static Allocation (Stack/Array) | Dynamic Allocation (Heap) |
|---|---|
| Size must be known at **compile time** | Size can be decided at **runtime** |
| Memory is automatically freed when the function returns | Programmer must explicitly call `free()` |
| Wastes memory if oversized | Allocates **exact or near-exact** size |
| Cannot grow or shrink | Can grow (`realloc()`) or shrink |
| Limited by stack size (typically **1–8 MB**) | Limited by total system RAM + swap |
| Faster allocation (pointer bump) | Slower (heap manager maintains free lists) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Typical word size on a modern system: **64 bits = 8 bytes** per pointer.
> - Default heap grows **upward** in virtual address space; stack grows **downward**.
> - `malloc(0)` behavior is **implementation-defined** in C — never rely on it.
> - Standard `sizeof(void*)` on 64-bit systems = **8 bytes**; on 32-bit systems = **4 bytes**.

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of DMA in C — Quick Snapshot

1. **`void* malloc(size_t size);`** — Allocates `size` bytes on the heap. Returns `NULL` on failure. Memory contents are **garbage (indeterminate)**.

2. **`void* calloc(size_t n, size_t size);`** — Allocates memory for an array of `n` elements, each of `size` bytes. Returns `NULL` on failure. Memory contents are **zero-initialized**.

3. **`void* realloc(void* ptr, size_t new_size);`** — Resizes the block pointed to by `ptr` to `new_size` bytes. May move the block to a new location. Returns `NULL` on failure (original block remains valid).

4. **`void free(void* ptr);`** — Deallocates the memory block pointed to by `ptr`. After `free()`, the pointer becomes **dangling** and must not be dereferenced.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a C Process Memory Layout

Before understanding DMA, you must know *where* dynamically allocated memory lives. Every C program, when loaded into RAM by the OS, is divided into the following logical segments (from low to high virtual addresses on most systems):

| Segment | Contents | Allocation Type | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Text (Code)** | Compiled machine instructions | Read-only, fixed | Entire program |
| **Data** | Initialized global/static variables | Static | Entire program |
| **BSS** | Uninitialized global/static variables | Static (zero-initialized) | Entire program |
| **Heap** | Dynamically allocated memory (`malloc`/`calloc`) | **Dynamic** | Until `free()` or program ends |
| **Stack** | Local variables, function parameters, return addresses | Static (auto) | Function scope |
| **Kernel/Env** | Command-line args, env variables, OS reserved | — | Entire program |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept:** DMA draws memory from the **Heap**, which is a region that **grows upward** (toward higher addresses) as allocations occur. The **Stack** grows **downward**. They grow toward each other, but the OS guarantees they will not collide under normal conditions. If heap grows beyond available virtual memory, `malloc` returns `NULL`.

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics of `malloc()`

When `malloc(n)` is invoked, the C runtime's memory manager (e.g., glibc's `ptmalloc2`) performs these steps:

1. **Convert** the requested size `n` to a multiple of the **alignment boundary** (typically **16 bytes** on 64-bit systems).
2. **Add overhead** for a heap header (typically **8–16 bytes**) that stores the block size, allocation status, and pointers to adjacent free blocks.
3. **Search** the free list (a doubly-linked list of free chunks maintained by the heap manager) using strategies such as:
   - **First Fit** — pick the first block large enough.
   - **Best Fit** — pick the smallest block that fits.
   - **Worst Fit** — pick the largest block.
   - **Buddy System** — split/merge powers of two (used in Linux kernel).
4. **Split** a larger free block if it is significantly larger than requested, or **coalesce** adjacent free blocks to reduce fragmentation.
5. **Mark** the chunk as "in use" and return a pointer to the **payload** (the address after the header).
6. If no block is large enough, request more memory from the OS via `sbrk()` or `mmap()`.

## 2.3 Operational Mechanics of `realloc()`

`realloc(ptr, new_size)` has three possible behaviors:

| Condition | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `new_size` < current size | May shrink in place; returns same pointer |
| `new_size` > current size, and free space is available immediately after the block | **Extends in place**; returns same pointer |
| `new_size` > current size, and no free space follows | **Allocates a new block**, copies contents, frees old block, returns new pointer |
| Allocation fails | Returns `NULL`; **original block remains valid and unchanged** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing `ptr = realloc(ptr, new_size);` is dangerous. If `realloc` returns `NULL` on failure, you **lose the original pointer**, causing a memory leak. Always use: `new_ptr = realloc(ptr, new_size); if (new_ptr) ptr = new_ptr;`

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** The `|` symbol is forbidden in tables below. We use `\vert` instead.

| # | Concept | Formula / Syntax | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `malloc` syntax | `ptr = (type*) malloc(n * sizeof(type));` | `n` is element count, `type` is C data type |
| 2 | `calloc` syntax | `ptr = (type*) calloc(n, sizeof(type));` | Zero-initializes, slightly slower than `malloc` |
| 3 | `realloc` syntax | `ptr = (type*) realloc(ptr, new_n * sizeof(type));` | May relocate the block |
| 4 | `free` syntax | `free(ptr);` | Always pair with a `malloc`/`calloc`/`realloc` |
| 5 | Array size on heap | $S_{array} = n \times \mathrm{sizeof}(T)$ | In **bytes** |
| 6 | Linked list node size | $S_{node} = \mathrm{sizeof}(T) + k \cdot \mathrm{sizeof}(\mathrm{pointer})$ | $k$ = number of link fields (1 for singly, 2 for doubly) |
| 7 | Address of $i^{th}$ element | $A_i = A_0 + i \cdot \mathrm{sizeof}(T)$ | $A_0$ is base address |
| 8 | Internal fragmentation | $F_i = B_{allocated} - B_{requested}$ | Per-allocation wasted space |
| 9 | External fragmentation ratio | $F_e = \dfrac{\sum B_{free}}{B_{largest\_free}}$ | If $\ge 1$, a request of size $B_{largest\_free}$ *might* fail |
| 10 | Heap header overhead | $O_h \approx 8 \text{ to } 16$ bytes per allocation | Implementation-defined |
| 11 | 64-bit pointer size | $\mathrm{sizeof}(\mathrm{void*}) = 8$ bytes | On 64-bit systems |
| 12 | Alignment boundary | $A = 16$ bytes (typical) | For `malloc` on x86-64 with glibc |
| 13 | Time complexity of `malloc` | $O(1)$ amortized | Worst-case $O(n)$ for full free-list scan |
| 14 | Maximum allocatable size | $S_{max} \le \mathrm{ULONG\_MAX}$ on 64-bit | Limited by virtual address space |

## 2.5 Why DMA is Indispensable in Data Structures

- **Linked Lists**: Each node is created on demand via `malloc`. Size grows with insertions, shrinks with deletions.
- **Stacks and Queues (Linked Implementation)**: Each push/enqueue allocates a new node.
- **Trees (BST, AVL, B-Tree)**: Nodes are dynamically created on insertion.
- **Hash Tables with Chaining**: Each bucket's linked list is dynamically built.
- **Graphs (Adjacency List)**: Vertices and edges are created dynamically.
- **Dynamic Arrays / Vectors**: A buffer on the heap is grown using `realloc` when capacity is exceeded.

Without DMA, none of these flexible structures could exist — you would be forced to declare compile-time arrays, defeating the very purpose of data structures.
