The KTU checker explicitly tests these edge cases. > > 4. **Forgetting to free deleted nodes (Loss: 0.5 mark):** In C-based answers, every `delete` operation must end with a `free()` call on the removed node. In Java/Python, mention "garbage collected" or "de-referenced". > > 5. **Writing only "O(1)" without justification (Loss: 0.5 mark):** KTU requires you to state *why* an operation is O(1) — specifically, that the number of pointer operations is **constant and independent of n**. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **Doubly Linked List node** has **3 fields**: `prev`, `data`, `next`. - ✅ A DLL with `n` nodes has **exactly 2 NULL pointers** (`HEAD.prev` and `TAIL.next`). - ✅ **InsertAtBeginning** requires **3 pointer updates** (if list is non-empty). - ✅ **InsertAfter(p)** requires **4 pointer updates** (the order of these updates is critical). - ✅ **Delete a given node** (pointer provided) is the **defining advantage of DLL** — it is **O(1)** versus O(n) for SLL. - ✅ DLL uses **50% more memory** than SLL (12 bytes vs 8 bytes per node on 32-bit systems). - ✅ DLL enables **reverse traversal** in O(n) time — impossible in SLL without auxiliary memory. - ✅ Real-world uses: **Browser back/forward, LRU Cache, Music playlists, Undo/Redo**. - ✅ Always handle **boundary cases**: empty list, single node, HEAD deletion, TAIL deletion. - ✅ **Order of pointer operations matters** in insertion — never overwrite a pointer before saving its value. - ✅ The KTU exam frequently tests the **time complexity table** — memorize the 10-row cheat sheet in Section 2.3. --- > **End of Module 2 — Doubly Linked List Notes** > *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | Aligned with 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus & NEP 2020 Outcomes*

topics.title: Circular Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Circular Linked List # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Circular Linked List (CLL)** is a linear data structure variant of the linked list in which the last node does not point to a `NULL` terminator. Instead, the `next` pointer (or `prev` pointer in the doubly linked variant) of the terminal node is redirected to point back to the **head** (or first) node, thereby forming a closed circular chain of nodes. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Circular Linked List is a linked list where the last node's link field points back to the first node (head), creating a circular structure. The traversal can begin at any node and follow the chain until it loops back to the starting node. ## 1.2 Variants of Circular Linked Lists | Variant | Full Name | Pointer Structure | |---------|-----------|-------------------| | **SCLL** | Singly Circular Linked List | `last -> next = head` | | **DCLL** | Doubly Circular Linked List | `head -> prev = last`, `last -> next = head` | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Roundabout Imagine a **circular roundabout (traffic circle)** with vehicles parked along its perimeter. Unlike a one-way street (singly linked list) or a two-way street (doubly linked list) that ends at a dead-end (NULL), the roundabout loops continuously back to its starting point. - You can **enter the circle at any vehicle** (any node can be a starting point for traversal). - You keep moving forward to the **next vehicle** (traversal via `next` pointer). - Eventually, you **return to the vehicle you started with** (circular closure). - The "last" vehicle is connected to the "first" vehicle — there is no end. This analogy perfectly captures the essence of a CLL: **no beginning, no end, only a continuous loop.** ## 1.4 Why We Need Circular Linked Lists > [!IMPORTANT] > Circular Linked Lists are the **fundamental building block** for advanced data structures like the **Round Robin CPU Scheduling Algorithm**, **Circular Buffers (Ring Buffers)** in operating systems, **Josephus Problem**, and **multiplayer game turn management**. The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests your ability to implement insertion, deletion, and traversal in CLLs. ## 1.5 Key Structural Notation Let us define the node structure for a **Singly Circular Linked List** (C-style declaration as per KTU curriculum): ```c struct Node { int data; struct Node* next; }; ``` For a **Doubly Circular Linked List**: ```c struct Node { int data; struct Node* next; struct Node* prev; }; ``` Here, the `next` pointer of the **tail (last)** node stores the address of the **head (first)** node, rather than `NULL`. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How a CLL Works A Circular Linked List is governed by a single critical invariant rule: > **The Golden Rule of CLL:** If `last` is the pointer to the last node, then `last -> next = head`. Equivalently, after a complete traversal, you must return to the starting node. ### 2.1.1 Traversal Logic Unlike a standard singly linked list (where traversal ends when `current -> next == NULL`), in a CLL, traversal continues until: ``` current -> next == start_node ``` where `start_node` is the node from which traversal began. ### 2.1.2 Insertion Logic (Singly Circular) To insert a new node `newNode` at the **end** of a Singly Circular Linked List: 1. Traverse the list to find the **last** node (whose `next` points to `head`). 2. Update `last -> next = newNode`. 3. Update `newNode -> next = head`. To insert at the **beginning**: 1. Traverse to the last node. 2. Update `last -> next = newNode`. 3. Update `newNode -> next = head`. 4. Move the `head` pointer to `newNode`. ### 2.1.3 Deletion Logic (Singly Circular) To delete the **last** node: 1. Traverse to find the second-to-last node (`secondLast`). 2. Free the memory of `last`. 3. Update `secondLast -> next = head`. To delete the **head** node: 1. Traverse to the last node. 2. Update `last -> next = head -> next`. 3. Free `head`. 4. Update `head = head -> next`. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Operation | Time Complexity (SCLL) | Time Complexity (DCLL) | Key Formula / Condition | |-----------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Traversal of all nodes** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | Loop until `current == start` | | **Insertion at head** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Update `last -> next` and `head` | | **Insertion at tail** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ (with `last` ptr) | `newNode -> next = head` | | **Insertion at position $k$** | $O(k)$ | $O(\min(k, n-k))$ | Traverse to node $k-1$ | | **Deletion at head** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Update `last -> next` and `head` | | **Deletion at tail** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ (with `last` ptr) | Find second-last node | | **Deletion at position $k$** | $O(k)$ | $O(\min(k, n-k))$ | Traverse to node $k-1$ | | **Search for element $x$** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | Linear scan | > [!NOTE] > **Key Boundary Condition:** A CLL with only **one node** has `node -> next = node` (the node points to itself). This is the most common edge case tested in KTU exams. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Operating Systems:** Round-Robin CPU scheduling uses a CLL to cycle through processes. 2. **Embedded Systems:** Circular buffers (ring buffers) for data streams (audio/video). 3. **Multiplayer Games:** Turn-based player rotation in card/board games. 4. **Networking:** Token ring topology in LANs. 5. **Josephus Problem:** Classical competitive programming problem solved efficiently with CLL. ## 2.4 Memory Visualization Formula If a CLL has $n$ nodes, each node occupies: $$\text{Total Memory} = n \times (\text{sizeof}(\text{data}) + \text{sizeof}(\text{pointer}))$$ For a typical 64-bit system with `int` data: $$\text{Total Memory} = n \times (4 + 8) = 12n \text{ bytes}$$ # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Algorithm: Insertion at End in Singly Circular Linked List Let us derive the exact algorithm for inserting a new node at the end of a Singly Circular Linked List. ### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Procedure **Input:** A Singly Circular Linked List with `head` pointer, and a value `data` to insert. **Output:** The updated CLL with the new node appended at the end. **Step 1: Allocate memory for the new node.** $$\text{newNode} \leftarrow \text{malloc}(\text{sizeof}(\text{Node}))$$ $$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{data} \leftarrow \text{data}$$ $$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} \leftarrow \text{NULL}$$ *Logic:* Reserve heap memory and initialize the data field with the user-provided value. **Step 2: Check if the list is empty.** $$\text{IF } \text{head} = \text{NULL} \text{ THEN}$$ $$\quad \text{head} \leftarrow \text{newNode}$$ $$\quad \text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} \leftarrow \text{head}$$ $$\quad \text{RETURN}$$ *Logic:* A CLL with one node must point to itself. This is the **critical edge case** that KTU examiners frequently test. **Step 3: Traverse to the last node.** Let `temp` be a temporary pointer initialized to `head`. $$\text{temp} \leftarrow \text{head}$$ $$\text{WHILE } \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \neq \text{head} \text{ DO}$$ $$\quad \text{temp} \leftarrow \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next}$$ *Logic:* The loop terminates when `temp` points to the last node (whose `next` field points to `head`). **Step 4: Insert the new node after the last node.** $$\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \leftarrow \text{newNode}$$ $$\text{newNode} \rightarrow \text{next} \leftarrow \text{head}$$ *Logic:* We break the circular link from the last node to `head`, insert the new node, and reconnect the circle through the new node. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Algorithm: Deletion of Last Node in Singly Circular Linked List ### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Procedure **Step 1: Handle edge case — single node list.** $$\text{IF } \text{head} \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{head} \text{ THEN}$$ $$\quad \text{free}(\text{head})$$ $$\quad \text{head} \leftarrow \text{NULL}$$ $$\quad \text{RETURN}$$ *Logic:* If the list has only one node (which points to itself), we free it and set `head` to `NULL`. **Step 2: Traverse to find the second-to-last node.** $$\text{temp} \leftarrow \text{head}$$ $$\text{WHILE } \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \rightarrow \text{next} \neq \text{head} \text{ DO}$$ $$\quad \text{temp} \leftarrow \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next}$$ *Logic:* After this loop, `temp` points to the **second-to-last** node, and `temp -> next` points to the last node. **Step 3: Free the last node and update the link.** $$\text{lastNode} \leftarrow \text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next}$$ $$\text{temp} \rightarrow \text{next} \leftarrow \text{head}$$ $$\text{free}(\text{lastNode})$$ *Logic:* We redirect the second-to-last node's `next` to `head`, breaking the circle through the old last node, then deallocate the old last node. ## 3.3 Worked Example: Insertion Trace Consider an existing CLL: `10 -> 20 -> 30 -> (back to 10)` with `head = 10`. **Insert 40 at the end:** | Iteration | `temp` value | `temp -> next` value | Action | |-----------|--------------|----------------------|--------| | Initial | `10` | `20` | Begin traversal | | 1 | `20` | `30` | Continue | | 2 | `30` | `10` (head) | Stop — this is the last node | After Step 4: - `30 -> next = 40` (newNode) - `40 -> next = 10` (head) **Final List:** `10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40 -> (back to 10)` ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Other Linked List Types | Property | Singly LL | Doubly LL | Singly Circular LL | Doubly Circular LL | |----------|-----------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------| | **NULL termination** | Yes | Yes | No | No | | **Last node points to** | `NULL` | `NULL` | `head` | `head` | | **Head node's prev** | N/A | `NULL` | N/A | `last` | | **Memory per node** | 1 pointer | 2 pointers | 1 pointer | 2 pointers | | **Reverse traversal** | Not possible | Possible | Not possible | Possible | | **Use case** | Simple lists | Browser history | Round-robin | LRU cache | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Basic Structure of a Singly Circular Linked List The following Mermaid diagram illustrates a Singly Circular Linked List with four nodes. The arrow from the last node `40` loops back to the head node `10`, signifying the circular closure. ```mermaid graph LR node10["Node 10
data=10
next=Node20"] node20["Node 20
data=20
next=Node30"] node30["Node 30
data=30
next=Node40"] node40["Node 40
data=40
next=Node10"] headPtr(("head
ptr")) headPtr --> node10 node10 --> node20 node20 --> node30 node30 --> node40 node40 -.-> node10 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Insertion Operation This block diagram maps the complete functional flow for inserting a new node at the end of a Singly Circular Linked List. Each block represents a distinct operational stage, and the arrows denote the control flow and data dependencies. ```mermaid graph TD startBlock(["START
InsertAtEnd"]) initBlock["Initialize newNode
newNode.data = data
newNode.next = NULL"] checkEmpty{"Is head == NULL?"} emptyCase["Set head = newNode
Set newNode.next = head"] traverseInit["Set temp = head"] traverseLoop{"Is temp.next
!= head?"} traverseStep["Move temp to
temp = temp.next"] insertAction["Set temp.next = newNode
Set newNode.next = head"] stopBlock(["END"]) startBlock --> initBlock initBlock --> checkEmpty checkEmpty -- Yes --> emptyCase checkEmpty -- No --> traverseInit emptyCase --> stopBlock traverseInit --> traverseLoop traverseLoop -- Yes --> traverseStep traverseStep --> traverseLoop traverseLoop -- No --> insertAction insertAction --> stopBlock ``` ## 4.3 Structure of a Doubly Circular Linked List The following diagram shows a Doubly Circular Linked List, where each node has both `next` and `prev` pointers. The `prev` of the head points to the last node, and the `next` of the last node points to the head. ```mermaid graph LR dnode10["Node 10
prev=Node40
next=Node20"] dnode20["Node 20
prev=Node10
next=Node30"] dnode30["Node 30
prev=Node20
next=Node40"] dnode40["Node 40
prev=Node30
next=Node10"] dheadPtr(("head
ptr")) dheadPtr --> dnode10 dnode10 <--> dnode20 dnode20 <--> dnode30 dnode30 <--> dnode40 dnode40 -.next.-> dnode10 dnode10 -.prev.-> dnode40 ``` ## 4.4 Visual Comparison — Standard LL vs Circular LL This architecture diagram contrasts the pointer topology of a standard singly linked list (which terminates at `NULL`) with that of a singly circular linked list (which loops back to `head`). ```mermaid graph TD subgraph SLL ["Standard Singly Linked List"] sllA["Node A"] --> sllB["Node B"] sllB --> sllC["Node C"] sllC --> sllNull(("NULL")) end subgraph CLL ["Circular Linked List"] cllA["Node A"] --> cllB["Node B"] cllB --> cllC["Node C"] cllC -.-> cllA end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The dashed line in the Mermaid diagrams represents the **circular back-link** (the defining feature of CLL). Solid lines represent standard forward links. The absence of a `NULL` terminator is what fundamentally distinguishes a CLL from a standard linked list. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a Circular Linked List. How does it differ from a standard Singly Linked List?** **Model Answer:** A **Circular Linked List (CLL)** is a linked list data structure in which the last node's `next` pointer does not point to `NULL` but instead points back to the **head** (first) node, forming a continuous circular chain. **Differences from a Standard Singly Linked List:** | Feature | Singly LL | Circular LL | |---------|-----------|-------------| | Last node points to | `NULL` | `head` | | Traversal termination | `next == NULL` | `next == start` | | No `NULL` reference | No | Yes | | Can start from any node | Yes | Yes (inherently) | | Risk of infinite loop | No | Yes (if not careful) | --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the time complexity of inserting a node at the head of a Doubly Circular Linked List? Justify.** **Model Answer:** The time complexity of inserting a node at the head of a **Doubly Circular Linked List** is $O(1)$ (constant time). **Justification:** 1. If we maintain a `tail` pointer (pointing to the last node), we can directly access the last node in $O(1)$. 2. The new node's `next` is set to `head` and `prev` is set to `tail` — both $O(1)$. 3. The old head's `prev` is updated to the new node — $O(1)$. 4. The tail's `next` is updated to the new node — $O(1)$. 5. The `head` pointer is moved to the new node — $O(1)$. All operations are direct pointer manipulations without any traversal, hence the time complexity is $O(1)$. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the structure of a Singly Circular Linked List with a suitable diagram. Write the algorithm to insert a new node at the **end** of a Singly Circular Linked List. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write the algorithm to **delete a node from the end** of a Singly Circular Linked List. Trace the algorithm with an example list containing 4 nodes. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Structure:** A Singly Circular Linked List consists of nodes where each node contains a `data` field and a `next` pointer. The `next` pointer of the last node points to the head node. **Diagram:** ``` head --> [10|next] --> [20|next] --> [30|next] --> [40|next] ^ | |____________________________________________| ``` **Algorithm: Insert at End** ``` INSERT_AT_END(head, data): 1. newNode = (struct Node*) malloc(sizeof(struct Node)) 2. newNode -> data = data 3. newNode -> next = NULL 4. IF head == NULL THEN 5. head = newNode 6. newNode -> next = head 7. RETURN head 8. ENDIF 9. temp = head 10. WHILE temp -> next != head DO 11. temp = temp -> next 12. ENDWHILE 13. temp -> next = newNode 14. newNode -> next = head 15. RETURN head ``` #### Model Solution for (b): **Algorithm: Delete from End** ``` DELETE_FROM_END(head): 1. IF head == NULL THEN 2. PRINT "List is empty" 3. RETURN NULL 4. ENDIF 5. IF head -> next == head THEN 6. free(head) 7. head = NULL 8. RETURN NULL 9. ENDIF 10. temp = head 11. WHILE temp -> next -> next != head DO 12. temp = temp -> next 13. ENDWHILE 14. lastNode = temp -> next 15. temp -> next = head 16. free(lastNode) 17. RETURN head ``` **Trace with list: 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40 -> (back to 10)** | Step | Action | State of list | |------|--------|---------------| | Initial | List has 4 nodes | `10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40 -> 10` | | Line 10 | `temp = head = node(10)` | `temp` at `10` | | Iter 1 | `temp->next->next = 30`, not head | `temp` at `10`, continue | | Line 12 | `temp = temp->next = node(20)` | `temp` at `20` | | Iter 2 | `temp->next->next = 40`, not head | `temp` at `20`, continue | | Line 12 | `temp = temp->next = node(30)` | `temp` at `30` | | Iter 3 | `temp->next->next = 10 = head` | Loop exits | | Line 14 | `lastNode = node(40)` | Identify target | | Line 15 | `temp->next = head` | `30 -> next = 10` | | Line 16 | `free(node(40))` | Deallocate `40` | | Final | List updated | `10 -> 20 -> 30 -> (back to 10)` | --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the structure of a **Doubly Circular Linked List** with a neat diagram. Compare it with a Singly Circular Linked List. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write algorithms for **inserting a node at the beginning** and **deleting a node from the beginning** of a Doubly Circular Linked List. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Structure:** A Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL) is a linked list where each node contains three fields: `prev` (pointer to previous node), `data` (the value), and `next` (pointer to next node). The `next` of the last node points to the head, and the `prev` of the head points to the last node, forming a bidirectional circular chain. **Diagram:** ``` prev next prev next head --> [NULL]<-->[10]<-->[20]<-->[30]<-->[NULL] | ^ v | +--- [40] <------------------------------+ ^ | |________________________________________| ``` **Comparison Table:** | Property | Singly Circular LL | Doubly Circular LL | |----------|--------------------|--------------------| | Pointers per node | 1 (`next`) | 2 (`next`, `prev`) | | Memory per node | Smaller | Larger | | Reverse traversal | Not possible | Possible | | Insert at head | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | | Delete from end | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ with `tail` ptr | | Complexity of code | Simpler | More complex | #### Model Solution for (b): **Algorithm: Insert at Beginning (DCLL)** ``` INSERT_AT_BEGIN(head, data): 1. newNode = (struct Node*) malloc(sizeof(struct Node)) 2. newNode -> data = data 3. IF head == NULL THEN 4. head = newNode 5. newNode -> next = head 6. newNode -> prev = head 7. RETURN head 8. ENDIF 9. tail = head -> prev 10. newNode -> next = head 11. newNode -> prev = tail 12. head -> prev = newNode 13. tail -> next = newNode 14. head = newNode 15. RETURN head ``` **Algorithm: Delete from Beginning (DCLL)** ``` DELETE_FROM_BEGIN(head): 1. IF head == NULL THEN 2. PRINT "List is empty" 3. RETURN NULL 4. ENDIF 5. IF head -> next == head THEN 6. free(head) 7. head = NULL 8. RETURN NULL 9. ENDIF 10. tail = head -> prev 11. temp = head 12. head = head -> next 13. head -> prev = tail 14. tail -> next = head 15. free(temp) 16. RETURN head ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Infinite Loop Trap:** When traversing a Circular Linked List, students often use the condition `temp != NULL` instead of `temp -> next != head`. This causes an **infinite loop** and results in **zero marks** for the algorithm. > 2. **Single Node Edge Case:** Forgetting to handle the case when the list has only one node (`head -> next == head`) is the **most common reason** for losing 2-3 marks in KTU exams. > 3. **Pointer Order in Deletion:** Always save the pointer to the node being deleted **before** freeing it, and update all surrounding links **before** calling `free()`. Reversing this order leads to dangling pointer errors. > 4. **Memory Leak:** In CLL operations, failing to update the `last -> next` link after deletion causes a broken circular chain — the list is no longer circular and subsequent traversals will be incorrect. > 5. **Confusing SCLL and DCLL:** Students frequently mix up the operations of Singly and Doubly Circular Linked Lists. Ensure you clearly state which variant your algorithm is for. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** A Circular Linked List is a linked list where the last node's pointer links back to the head, forming a circle. - **Two Variants:** **Singly Circular Linked List (SCLL)** — one pointer per node; **Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL)** — two pointers per node. - **No NULL Terminator:** The defining feature of CLL is the **absence of `NULL`** at the end of the list. - **Single Node Rule:** In a CLL with one node, `node -> next = node` (points to itself). - **Traversal Condition:** Loop until `current -> next == start_node`, **never** until `current == NULL`. - **Time Complexity:** - SCLL insertion at head/tail: $O(n)$ (requires traversal to find last node) - DCLL insertion at head/tail: $O(1)$ (with `tail` pointer maintained) - Traversal in both: $O(n)$ - **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$ for $n$ nodes. - **Common Applications:** Round-robin scheduling, circular buffers, Josephus problem, token ring networks. - **Memory per Node:** SCLL uses $O(1)$ extra pointer; DCLL uses $O(2)$ extra pointers. - **Edge Cases to Always Handle:** 1. Empty list (`head == NULL`) 2. Single node list (`head -> next == head`) 3. Two node list (boundary for traversal loops) 4. Deleting the only node (must set `head = NULL` after freeing) - **Key Advantage:** Any node can serve as a starting point for traversal — no need to maintain explicit head/tail distinction in some algorithms. - **Key Disadvantage:** Risk of infinite loops if termination conditions are not carefully designed; no direct access to the previous node in SCLL. - **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the diagram first, then write the algorithm, then trace with an example. This sequence maximizes partial marks even if your final answer has a minor error.

topics.title: Stacks and Queues using Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Stacks and Queues using Linked List # MODULE 2: STACKS AND QUEUES USING LINKED LIST ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Linked List Based Stack (LIFO Discipline):** A dynamic linear data structure that follows the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle, implemented using a singly linked list where all `PUSH` and `POP` operations are performed at the **head** node. The structure grows and shrinks dynamically with **O(1)** time complexity for core operations and consumes memory proportional to the number of elements, denoted as **O(n)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Linked List Based Queue (FIFO Discipline):** A dynamic linear data structure that follows the **First-In-First-Out (FIFO)** principle, implemented using a singly linked list where `ENQUEUE` (insertion) occurs at the **rear (tail)** and `DEQUEUE` (deletion) occurs at the **front (head)**. It maintains two pointers — `FRONT` and `REAR` — to achieve **O(1)** insertion and deletion. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Stack using Linked List — "A Stack of Plates in a Spring-Loaded Dish Rack"** Imagine a dish rack where you keep adding plates on top. The last plate you place is always the first one you take out. The key insight: you don't have a fixed number of plates — the rack can hold 5 or 500 plates because the spring expands. Similarly, a linked-list stack is "spring-loaded" — it grows with each push and shrinks with each pop, never running out of space until your system's RAM is exhausted. The **head pointer** is like the topmost plate; you always touch the head. **Queue using Linked List — "A Train Entering a Single-Track Tunnel"** Picture passengers boarding a train. The first passenger to enter the queue is the first to enter the train. The queue has a clear **entry point (rear)** where new passengers join, and an **exit point (front)** where the longest-waiting passenger leaves. With a linked list, the tunnel can be infinitely long, and the train station never has to declare a fixed capacity in advance. --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Conditions - **Memory per node:** Each node occupies **24 bytes** on a 64-bit system (8 bytes for data pointer + 8 bytes for next pointer + 8 bytes for overhead). - **Stack underflow condition:** Attempting to `POP` from an empty stack when `TOP = NULL`. - **Stack overflow condition:** In linked list, only when **system memory (heap)** is exhausted — practically never. - **Queue underflow condition:** Attempting to `DEQUEUE` from an empty queue when `FRONT = NULL`. - **Empty queue invariant:** `FRONT = REAR = NULL`. - **Single-element queue invariant:** `FRONT = REAR` (both point to the same node). --- ### 1.4 Node Structure > [!NOTE] > Every node in a stack/queue linked list is a self-referential structure containing a **DATA field** and a **LINK (next pointer) field**. $$ \text{Node} = \langle \text{Data} \rangle + \langle \text{Next Pointer} \rangle $$ $$ \text{Memory per node} = \text{sizeof}(Data) + \text{sizeof}(Pointer) + \text{Padding} $$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stack using Linked List — Operational Mechanics ### 2.1.1 PUSH Operation (Insertion at Head) The PUSH operation inserts a new element at the **beginning (head)** of the linked list. This choice is deliberate because it gives **O(1)** insertion — we don't need to traverse the list. **Step-by-step logic:** - **Step 1:** Allocate a new node dynamically using `malloc()` or `new`. - **Step 2:** Assign the user-supplied data to the new node's data field. - **Step 3:** Set the new node's `next` pointer to the current `TOP`. - **Step 4:** Update `TOP` to point to the newly created node. - **Step 5:** Increment the element counter (optional but useful for O(1) size retrieval). ### 2.1.2 POP Operation (Deletion from Head) - **Step 1:** Check for **underflow** (i.e., is `TOP == NULL`?). - **Step 2:** Store the data of the current `TOP` node in a temporary variable. - **Step 3:** Move `TOP` to `TOP->next`. - **Step 4:** Free the memory of the old `TOP` node. - **Step 5:** Decrement the counter and return the stored data. ### 2.1.3 PEEK Operation - **Step 1:** Check for underflow. - **Step 2:** Return `TOP->data` without modifying any pointer. --- ## 2.2 Queue using Linked List — Operational Mechanics ### 2.2.1 ENQUEUE Operation (Insertion at Rear) - **Step 1:** Allocate a new node. - **Step 2:** Assign the data to the new node. - **Step 3:** Set the new node's `next` to `NULL`. - **Step 4:** If `REAR` is `NULL` (empty queue), set both `FRONT` and `REAR` to the new node. - **Step 5:** Otherwise, link the current `REAR->next` to the new node and update `REAR` to the new node. ### 2.2.2 DEQUEUE Operation (Deletion from Front) - **Step 1:** Check for underflow (`FRONT == NULL`). - **Step 2:** Store `FRONT->data` in a temporary variable. - **Step 3:** Move `FRONT` to `FRONT->next`. - **Step 4:** If `FRONT` becomes `NULL` after deletion, set `REAR` to `NULL` (queue is now empty). - **Step 5:** Free the old front node and return the stored data. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these complexity values — they appear in nearly every KTU exam.** | Operation | Array Stack | Linked Stack | Array Queue (Linear) | Linked Queue | Circular Queue | |-----------|-------------|--------------|----------------------|--------------|----------------| | **PUSH / ENQUEUE** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | | **POP / DEQUEUE** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(n)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | | **PEEK / FRONT** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | | **isEmpty** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** | | **Space (n elements)** | $(n+1) \cdot k$ bytes | $n \cdot (k + p)$ bytes | $(n+1) \cdot k$ bytes | $n \cdot (k + p) + 2p$ bytes | $(n+1) \cdot k$ bytes | | **Memory Wastage** | None | None | $\vert n - \text{capacity} \vert$ | None | $\vert \text{slot} - n \vert$ | | **Overflow Trigger** | `TOP == MAX-1` | `malloc fails` | `REAR == MAX-1` | `malloc fails` | `(REAR+1) \mod N == FRONT` | Where: - $k$ = size of data type (e.g., 4 bytes for `int`) - $p$ = size of pointer (8 bytes on 64-bit systems) - $n$ = number of elements currently stored - $N$ = maximum capacity of the array-based structure --- ## 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Choosing Linked List > [!NOTE] > **Why use a linked list instead of an array for stacks/queues?** - **Dynamic sizing:** Arrays require a fixed `MAX` declared at compile time. Linked lists adapt to runtime memory. - **No memory wastage:** Linear queue using array wastes $\vert MAX - n \vert$ slots when front pointer advances. Linked list queues have **zero wastage**. - **Theoretical O(1):** Array-based linear queue gives O(n) for dequeue because you must shift the front pointer or shift all elements — linked list maintains true O(1). - **Trade-off:** Linked list incurs an extra **8 bytes per node** for the pointer and suffers from **poor cache locality** (cache misses) compared to contiguous arrays. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Function call stack** in compilers (activations records). - **Undo/Redo operations** in text editors like Microsoft Word. - **Browser history** navigation (back/forward buttons). - **Process scheduling** in operating systems (Round-Robin queue). - **Disk scheduling** in operating systems (FCFS, SSTF). - **CPU pipeline registers** in processor design. - **Printer spooling** and **network packet buffering**. - **Recursion implementation** (each recursive call is a stack frame). - **BFS traversal** in graph algorithms (uses queue). - **DFS traversal** in graph algorithms (uses stack). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Time Complexity Derivation — Linked List Stack PUSH > [!NOTE] > **Theorem:** PUSH operation on a linked list stack runs in **constant time**, i.e., $\mathcal{O}(1)$. **Derivation:** Let $T_{push}(n)$ denote the time taken to push an element into a stack of current size $n$. - **Step 1 — Memory Allocation:** $t_1 = C_1$ (constant time of `malloc`) - **Step 2 — Data Assignment:** $t_2 = C_2$ (constant, independent of $n$) - **Step 3 — Pointer Update:** $t_3 = C_3$ (single assignment `new->next = TOP`) - **Step 4 — Top Update:** $t_4 = C_4$ (single assignment `TOP = new`) $$ T_{push}(n) = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 $$ $$ T_{push}(n) = C_1 + C_2 + C_3 + C_4 $$ $$ T_{push}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} C_i = \text{constant} $$ $$ \therefore T_{push}(n) = \mathcal{O}(1) $$ **Conclusion:** Since no loop or recursion is involved and every operation is a constant-time pointer manipulation, the PUSH operation does not depend on $n$. --- ## 3.2 Time Complexity Derivation — Linked List Queue DEQUEUE > [!NOTE] > **Theorem:** DEQUEUE operation on a linked list queue runs in **constant time**, i.e., $\mathcal{O}(1)$. **Derivation:** Let $T_{dequeue}(n)$ denote the time taken to remove the front element from a queue of size $n$. - **Step 1 — Underflow Check:** $T_1 = C_1$ (single comparison `FRONT == NULL`) - **Step 2 — Data Retrieval:** $T_2 = C_2$ (single access `FRONT->data`) - **Step 3 — Pointer Move:** $T_3 = C_3$ (single assignment `FRONT = FRONT->next`) - **Step 4 — Rear Update (if needed):** $T_4 = C_4 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\{FRONT = NULL\}}$ $$ T_{dequeue}(n) = C_1 + C_2 + C_3 + C_4 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\{FRONT = NULL\}} $$ $$ \text{Taking worst case: } T_{dequeue}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{4} C_i $$ $$ \therefore T_{dequeue}(n) = \mathcal{O}(1) $$ --- ## 3.3 Space Complexity Derivation — Linked Stack vs Array Stack **Linked Stack Space:** For $n$ elements, memory consumed is: $$ S_{linked}(n) = n \cdot (S_{data} + S_{pointer}) + S_{top\_pointer} $$ $$ S_{linked}(n) = n \cdot (k + p) + p $$ Where: - $k$ = size of data field (e.g., $4$ bytes for `int`) - $p$ = size of pointer field ($8$ bytes on 64-bit system) **Array Stack Space:** $$ S_{array}(n) = N \cdot k $$ Where $N$ is the maximum capacity (declared size). **Comparison Ratio:** $$ R(n) = \frac{S_{linked}(n)}{S_{array}(n)} = \frac{n(k+p) + p}{N \cdot k} $$ For typical values $k = 4$, $p = 8$, $n = N$: $$ R(N) = \frac{N \cdot 12 + 8}{N \cdot 4} = 3 + \frac{2}{N} $$ $$ \therefore R(N) \approx 3 \text{ for large } N $$ **Conclusion:** A linked list stack consumes approximately **3x more memory** than an array stack of equivalent capacity, due to pointer overhead. This is the fundamental memory trade-off. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operation Behavior Table | Scenario | TOP before | Action | TOP after | List State | |----------|------------|--------|-----------|------------| | **PUSH 10** | NULL | Insert at head | Node(10) | 10 | | **PUSH 20** | Node(10) | Insert at head | Node(20)->10 | 20, 10 | | **PUSH 30** | Node(20) | Insert at head | Node(30)->20 | 30, 20, 10 | | **POP** | Node(30) | Delete head | Node(20) | 20, 10 | | **POP** | Node(20) | Delete head | Node(10) | 10 | | **POP** | Node(10) | Delete head | NULL | (empty) | | **POP** | NULL | **UNDERFLOW** | NULL | Error raised | --- ## 3.5 Queue Trace Example — Detailed Working **Operations:** `ENQUEUE 5, ENQUEUE 10, ENQUEUE 15, DEQUEUE, ENQUEUE 20, DEQUEUE, DEQUEUE` After `ENQUEUE 5`: $$ \text{FRONT} \to [5 \vert \circ] \leftarrow \text{REAR} $$ After `ENQUEUE 10`: $$ \text{FRONT} \to [5 \vert \bullet] \to [10 \vert \circ] \leftarrow \text{REAR} $$ After `ENQUEUE 15`: $$ \text{FRONT} \to [5 \vert \bullet] \to [10 \vert \bullet] \to [15 \vert \circ] \leftarrow \text{REAR} $$ After `DEQUEUE` (returns 5): $$ \text{FRONT} \to [10 \vert \bullet] \to [15 \vert \circ] \leftarrow \text{REAR} $$ After `ENQUEUE 20`: $$ \text{FRONT} \to [10 \vert \bullet] \to [15 \vert \bullet] \to [20 \vert \circ] \leftarrow \text{REAR} $$ After `DEQUEUE` (returns 10): $$ \text{FRONT} \to [15 \vert \bullet] \to [20 \vert \circ] \leftarrow \text{REAR} $$ After `DEQUEUE` (returns 15): $$ \text{FRONT} \to [20 \vert \circ] \leftarrow \text{REAR} $$ **Note:** When only one element remains, both `FRONT` and `REAR` point to the same node — a critical boundary case in implementation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Singly Linked List Node — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[DATA FIELD
Stores the value] --> B[LINK FIELD
Stores address of next node] B --> C[Memory allocated
on HEAP] ``` --- ## 4.2 Stack using Linked List — Full Architectural Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD TOP[TOP POINTER
global reference] --> N3[Node 3
Data: 30] N3 --> N2[Node 2
Data: 20] N2 --> N1[Node 1
Data: 10] N1 --> NULL1[NULL
end of list] PUSH[PUSH Operation] -.-> N3 POP[POP Operation] -.-> N3 PEEK[PEEK Operation] -.-> N3 ``` --- ## 4.3 Queue using Linked List — Full Architectural Flow ```mermaid flowmermaidflowchart LR FRONT[FRONT POINTER] --> Q1[Node 1
Data: A] Q1 --> Q2[Node 2
Data: B] Q2 --> Q3[Node 3
Data: C] Q3 --> Q4[Node 4
Data: D] Q4 --> NULL2[NULL] NULL2 -.-> REAR[REAR POINTER] ENQ[ENQUEUE
insert at REAR] -.-> Q4 DEQ[DEQUEUE
delete from FRONT] -.-> Q1 ``` --- ## 4.4 PUSH Operation — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start PUSH x] --> A1[Allocate newNode
on HEAP] A1 --> A2[Set newNode data = x] A2 --> A3[newNode next = TOP] A3 --> A4[TOP = newNode] A4 --> A5[Increment count] A5 --> END[End PUSH] ``` --- ## 4.5 POP Operation — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start POP] --> B1{TOP == NULL ?} B1 -->|Yes| B2[Print UNDERFLOW
Return error] B1 -->|No| B3[temp = TOP data] B3 --> B4[TOP = TOP next] B4 --> B5[Free old TOP node] B5 --> B6[Decrement count] B6 --> B7[Return temp] B7 --> END[End POP] ``` --- ## 4.6 ENQUEUE Operation — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start ENQUEUE x] --> C1[Allocate newNode] C1 --> C2[Set newNode data = x] C2 --> C3[newNode next = NULL] C3 --> C4{REAR == NULL ?} C4 -->|Yes Empty Queue| C5[FRONT = newNode] C5 --> C6[REAR = newNode] C4 -->|No Not Empty| C7[REAR next = newNode] C7 --> C8[REAR = newNode] C6 --> END[End ENQUEUE] C8 --> END[End ENQUEUE] ``` --- ## 4.7 DEQUEUE Operation — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start DEQUEUE] --> D1{FRONT == NULL ?} D1 -->|Yes| D2[Print UNDERFLOW
Return error] D1 -->|No| D3[temp = FRONT data] D3 --> D4[FRONT = FRONT next] D4 --> D5{FRONT == NULL ?} D5 -->|Yes| D6[REAR = NULL] D5 -->|No| D7[Continue] D6 --> D8[Free old FRONT node] D7 --> D8 D8 --> D9[Return temp] D9 --> END[End DEQUEUE] ``` --- ## 4.8 Circular Queue using Linked List — Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR CF[FRONT] --> CN1[Node 1
Data: P] CN1 --> CN2[Node 2
Data: Q] CN2 --> CN3[Node 3
Data: R] CN3 --> CR[REAR] CR -.-> CN1 ``` In a **circular queue using linked list**, the `REAR->next` always points back to the `FRONT`. This eliminates the NULL termination and enables continuous rotation — useful in Round-Robin CPU scheduling. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]` **Explain the concept of a stack implemented using a linked list. Mention its advantages over an array-based implementation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A stack implemented using a linked list is a dynamic data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** principle, where insertion (PUSH) and deletion (POP) are performed at the **head** of the linked list in **O(1)** time. **Advantages over array-based stack:** 1. **Dynamic sizing** — no fixed `MAX` limit, grows until heap memory is exhausted. **(1 mark)** 2. **No memory wastage** — unlike an array where unused slots are wasted. **(1 mark)** 3. **No overflow (practical)** — overflow occurs only on `malloc` failure, not at a predefined size. **(1 mark)** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]` **Compare linear queue using an array and queue using a linked list in terms of memory utilization and dequeue operation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Criterion | Array Queue | Linked List Queue | |-----------|-------------|-------------------| | **Memory Wastage** | Wastes $\vert MAX - n \vert$ slots when FRONT advances | **Zero wastage** — allocates exactly $n$ nodes | | **DEQUEUE Time** | **O(n)** — needs shifting of all elements | **O(1)** — single pointer move | The linked list queue is superior in both dynamic memory efficiency and asymptotic dequeue complexity. **(1 mark for conclusion)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice 1]`** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Write the algorithm for PUSH and POP operations on a stack implemented using a linked list. Explain with neat diagrams.** **Model Solution:** **Node Structure:** A node contains two fields: `data` and `next`. **PUSH Operation Algorithm:** 1. **START** — Begin PUSH procedure. 2. Create a new node `newNode`. 3. If memory is not available, print `"Stack Overflow"` and return. 4. Set `newNode->data = item` (the element to be inserted). 5. Set `newNode->next = TOP` (link the new node to the existing top). 6. Set `TOP = newNode` (update top pointer to new node). 7. **END** — PUSH complete. **POP Operation Algorithm:** 1. **START** — Begin POP procedure. 2. If `TOP == NULL`, print `"Stack Underflow"` and return. 3. Store `item = TOP->data` in a temporary variable. 4. Set `temp = TOP` (save current top for freeing). 5. Set `TOP = TOP->next` (move top to the next node). 6. Free the memory of `temp` (deallocate old top). 7. Return `item`. 8. **END** — POP complete. **Diagram:** ``` Initial State: After PUSH(30): After POP(): TOP TOP TOP | | | [10|*] [30|*]-->[20|*]-->[10|*] [20|*]-->[10|*] | | | NULL NULL NULL ``` #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Implement a queue using a linked list with FRONT and REAR pointers. Write algorithms for ENQUEUE and DEQUEUE operations and trace them with an example.** **Model Solution:** **Structure:** Two global pointers `FRONT` and `REAR` are maintained. **ENQUEUE Operation Algorithm:** 1. **START** — Begin ENQUEUE procedure. 2. Allocate a new node `newNode`. 3. Set `newNode->data = item`. 4. Set `newNode->next = NULL`. 5. If `REAR == NULL` (queue is empty): - Set `FRONT = newNode`. - Set `REAR = newNode`. 6. Else (queue is not empty): - Set `REAR->next = newNode`. - Set `REAR = newNode`. 7. **END** — ENQUEUE complete. **DEQUEUE Operation Algorithm:** 1. **START** — Begin DEQUEUE procedure. 2. If `FRONT == NULL`, print `"Queue Underflow"` and return. 3. Store `item = FRONT->data`. 4. Set `temp = FRONT`. 5. Set `FRONT = FRONT->next`. 6. If `FRONT == NULL` (queue became empty), set `REAR = NULL`. 7. Free `temp`. 8. Return `item`. 9. **END** — DEQUEUE complete. **Trace Example:** Operations: `ENQUEUE(5), ENQUEUE(10), ENQUEUE(15), DEQUEUE, ENQUEUE(20)` | Step | Operation | FRONT | REAR | Queue Contents | |------|-----------|-------|------|----------------| | 1 | ENQUEUE(5) | Node(5) | Node(5) | 5 | | 2 | ENQUEUE(10) | Node(5) | Node(10) | 5, 10 | | 3 | ENQUEUE(15) | Node(5) | Node(15) | 5, 10, 15 | | 4 | DEQUEUE() returns 5 | Node(10) | Node(15) | 10, 15 | | 5 | ENQUEUE(20) | Node(10) | Node(20) | 10, 15, 20 | --- ### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice 2]`** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the advantages and disadvantages of implementing a stack using a linked list over an array. Compare their time and space complexities.** **Model Solution:** **Advantages of Linked List Stack over Array Stack:** 1. **Dynamic Memory Allocation** — Size is determined at runtime; no need to pre-declare `MAX`. **(2 marks)** 2. **No Overflow (practical)** — Only fails when system memory is exhausted, not at a hard limit. **(1 mark)** 3. **Efficient Memory Utilization** — No pre-allocated but unused slots. **(1 mark)** **Disadvantages of Linked List Stack over Array Stack:** 1. **Extra Memory per Node** — Each node requires an additional pointer (8 bytes on 64-bit systems), leading to ~3x memory overhead. **(1.5 marks)** 2. **Poor Cache Locality** — Nodes are scattered in heap, causing cache misses and slower real-world performance. **(1 mark)** 3. **Memory Allocation Overhead** — `malloc` and `free` are slower than direct array indexing. **(0.5 marks)** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Write an algorithm to reverse a queue using only stack operations. Implement it using linked list-based stack and queue. Provide the trace.** **Model Solution:** **Algorithm: Reverse Queue using Stack** 1. Create an empty linked list stack `S`. 2. Create an empty linked list queue `Q`. 3. **Step 1:** While `Q` is not empty: - `item = DEQUEUE(Q)`. - `PUSH(S, item)`. 4. **Step 2:** While `S` is not empty: - `item = POP(S)`. - `ENQUEUE(Q, item)`. 5. The queue `Q` is now reversed. **Time Complexity Analysis:** $$ T(n) = n \cdot T_{dequeue} + n \cdot T_{push} + n \cdot T_{pop} + n \cdot T_{enqueue} $$ $$ T(n) = n \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) + n \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) + n \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) + n \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) $$ $$ T(n) = 4n \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(n) $$ **Space Complexity:** $$ S(n) = n \cdot (k + p) + 2p = \mathcal{O}(n) $$ **Trace Example:** Initial Queue: `[1, 2, 3, 4]` | Phase | Operation | Stack | Queue | |-------|-----------|-------|-------| | Start | — | Empty | 1, 2, 3, 4 | | Phase 1 Step 1 | DEQUEUE(1), PUSH(1) | 1 | 2, 3, 4 | | Phase 1 Step 2 | DEQUEUE(2), PUSH(2) | 2, 1 | 3, 4 | | Phase 1 Step 3 | DEQUEUE(3), PUSH(3) | 3, 2, 1 | 4 | | Phase 1 Step 4 | DEQUEUE(4), PUSH(4) | 4, 3, 2, 1 | Empty | | Phase 2 Step 1 | POP(4), ENQUEUE(4) | 3, 2, 1 | 4 | | Phase 2 Step 2 | POP(3), ENQUEUE(3) | 2, 1 | 4, 3 | | Phase 2 Step 3 | POP(2), ENQUEUE(2) | 1 | 4, 3, 2 | | Phase 2 Step 4 | POP(1), ENQUEUE(1) | Empty | 4, 3, 2, 1 | **Final Queue:** `[4, 3, 2, 1]` — successfully reversed in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout** > > 1. **Forgetting to set `REAR = NULL` after dequeue empties the queue** — This causes dangling pointer issues and undefined behavior. Always reset `REAR` when the last element is removed. **(Common 2-mark deduction)** > 2. **Using `TOP->next = NULL` after POP instead of freeing the node** — Memory leak. Examiners specifically look for `free(temp)` calls. **(1.5-mark deduction)** > 3. **Confusing PUSH direction in stack** — Some students implement PUSH at the tail, which gives **O(n)** complexity. Always push at the **head** to maintain **O(1)**. **(Critical conceptual error — 3 marks)** > 4. **Not handling the single-element case in DEQUEUE** — When `FRONT == REAR`, after deletion, both must be set to `NULL`. Missing this causes segmentation faults. **(2-mark deduction)** > 5. **Forgetting to check underflow before POP/DEQUEUE** — Leads to dereferencing NULL pointer. **(1-mark deduction for missing guard)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme** - ✅ **Stack PUSH/POP is always at HEAD** for O(1) complexity — never at tail. - ✅ **Queue ENQUEUE is at REAR, DEQUEUE is at FRONT** — both are O(1) with two pointers. - ✅ **Empty stack invariant:** `TOP = NULL`. - ✅ **Empty queue invariant:** `FRONT = REAR = NULL`. - ✅ **Single-element queue invariant:** `FRONT = REAR` (both point to the same node). - ✅ **Underflow conditions** must be checked **before** every POP and DEQUEUE. - ✅ **Memory for nodes** is allocated on the **HEAP** using `malloc()` / `new`. - ✅ **Free every deleted node** to prevent memory leaks — examiners deduct marks for missing `free()`. - ✅ **Linked list stack has NO practical overflow** — only fails when `malloc` returns `NULL`. - ✅ **Time complexity** for PUSH, POP, ENQUEUE, DEQUEUE, PEEK, isEmpty — all are **O(1)** for linked implementation. - ✅ **Space complexity** of linked stack/queue is **O(n)** — proportional to elements. - ✅ **Array linear queue dequeue is O(n)** due to shifting — linked list fixes this. - ✅ **Circular queue** connects `REAR->next` back to `FRONT` — useful in Round-Robin scheduling. - ✅ **Node size on 64-bit systems:** 8 (pointer) + data size + padding bytes. - ✅ **LIFO** = Last-In-First-Out = Stack; **FIFO** = First-In-First-Out = Queue. - ✅ **Recursion uses stack** internally; **BFS uses queue**; **DFS uses stack**. - ✅ **Reverse a queue using a stack** — the classic KTU favorite question, runs in **O(n)** time and space. - ✅ **Real-world examples:** Function calls (stack), Printer spooling (queue), Undo/Redo (stack), CPU scheduling (queue). - ✅ **In KTU exams, always draw neat diagrams** showing pointer movements — it earns easy 2-3 marks. - ✅ **Time complexity proofs** require showing that the operation does NOT depend on $n$ (no loops, no traversal). - ✅ **Space complexity proofs** use the formula $S(n) = n \cdot (k + p) + \text{pointer\_overhead}$.

topics.title: Polynomial Representation using Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polynomial Representation using Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Polynomial Representation using Linked List

## 1. Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEECT522 – Data Structures, Module 2)**, a **Polynomial** is a mathematical expression of the form:

$$P(x) = a_n x^n + a_{n-1} x^{n-1} + \dots + a_1 x + a_0$$

where each $a_i$ is called the **coefficient** of the term, and the integer $i$ is called the **power** (or **exponent** / **degree**) of $x$ in that term. A **Polynomial Representation using a Linked List** is a **dynamic, sparse-memory storage technique** in which each non-zero term of the polynomial is stored as a separate **node** containing three fields — the coefficient, the exponent, and a pointer to the next term.

A typical polynomial node is declared as:

$$\text{Node} = \big\langle \text{coeff}, \; \text{exp}, \; \text{next} \big\rangle$$

The list is conventionally maintained in **descending order of exponents**, so the head node holds the highest-degree term, and the last node (tail) holds the constant term.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The concept belongs to the broader unit *"Linked List Applications"*. Students must be able to **represent, create, add, and multiply sparse polynomials** using singly linked lists, and analyze their **time and space complexity**.

---

## 2. Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a polynomial as a **train of wagons**. Each wagon is a *term* of the polynomial. The first wagon carries the highest-degree cargo, the second wagon carries the next-highest, and so on. Each wagon has a label showing **how much** (the *coefficient*) and **what power** (the *exponent*) of the variable it represents, and is connected to the next wagon by a coupling (the *next pointer*).

- If you want to **add another wagon** (a new term), you simply insert it at the correct position in the line and reconnect the couplings.
- If a wagon becomes **empty** (coefficient becomes zero after cancellation), you detach it.
- Unlike a railway yard where wagons are stored in **fixed-length parallel platforms** (an array), the train route is **flexible** — you only build wagons for the terms that actually exist.

This "flexible train" is exactly what a **linked-list representation of a polynomial** gives us: a *sparse-friendly*, *dynamic*, and *growable* storage model.

> [!NOTE]
> A polynomial like $5x^{1000} + 7$ uses only **2 terms out of a possible 1001** — making a linked list dramatically more memory-efficient than a fixed-size array of 1001 slots.

---

## 3. Why Linked List Instead of Array?

| Aspect | Array Representation | Linked List Representation |
|---|---|---|
| Memory allocation | **Static / contiguous** | **Dynamic / non-contiguous** |
| Wastage for sparse polynomial | **High** (zero slots still reserved) | **Negligible** (one node per term) |
| Insertion of new term | **Costly** ($O(n)$ shifts) | **Cheaper** ($O(1)$ to $O(n)$ pointer rewiring) |
| Max degree | Fixed at compile time | **Unbounded** at runtime |
| Random access by degree | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ traversal |

Hence, linked lists dominate when the polynomial is **sparse** or when the **maximum degree is unknown in advance**.

---

## 4. Structure of a Polynomial Node

A polynomial term is encapsulated in a node with the following three logical fields:

1. **Coefficient (`coeff`)** — a real (or integer) value, can be positive, negative, or zero.
2. **Exponent (`exp`)** — a non-negative integer, conventionally **unique** within a single polynomial (no two terms share the same power).
3. **Next (`link`)** — pointer to the next node in the list, or `NULL` if it is the tail.

The head pointer of the list is the address of the first (highest-degree) node, and traversal proceeds along the `next` field until `NULL` is encountered.

> [!TIP]
> A **circular doubly linked list** is sometimes used in advanced applications to allow backward traversal and easier deletion at the tail, but for KTU 2024 evaluations, the **singly linked list** is the default expectation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Construction of a Polynomial Linked List

A polynomial is built by **repeatedly inserting terms in descending order of exponent**. The general algorithm is:

1. **Read** the number of terms $n$.
2. For each of the $n$ terms:
   - Read `coeff` and `exp`.
   - If `coeff == 0`, skip the term (a polynomial never stores zero-coefficient terms).
   - Otherwise, **insert** a new node at the correct position so that the list remains sorted by descending exponent.
3. After all insertions, the head pointer is returned as the polynomial handle.

The insertion itself can occur in three places:

- **At the head** — when the new term has an exponent higher than every existing term.
- **In the middle** — when the new term's exponent lies between two existing exponents.
- **At the tail** — when the new term has the smallest exponent.

> [!NOTE]
> Maintaining the **sorted-by-exponent invariant** is essential. Without it, the addition and multiplication algorithms will produce incorrect results.

---

## 2. The Three Core Operations

### (A) Polynomial Addition
Given two polynomials $P_1(x)$ and $P_2(x)$, the sum is $P_3(x) = P_1(x) + P_2(x)$.

The algorithm uses **two parallel traversers** (`p1` on $P_1$ and `p2` on $P_2$) and compares exponents at each step:

- If $\text{exp}(p1) = \text{exp}(p2)$ → create a term with exponent $e = \text{exp}(p1)$ and coefficient $\text{coeff}(p1) + \text{coeff}(p2)$. **Skip** this term if the sum is zero.
- If $\text{exp}(p1) > \text{exp}(p2)$ → copy the term from $p1$ into $P_3$.
- If $\text{exp}(p1) < \text{exp}(p2)$ → copy the term from $p2$ into $P_3$.
- When one list is exhausted, **append** the rest of the other list.

**Time complexity:** $O(m + n)$, where $m$ and $n$ are the number of terms in $P_1$ and $P_2$.
**Space complexity:** $O(m + n)$ for the resulting list.

### (B) Polynomial Multiplication
Given $P_1(x)$ and $P_2(x)$, the product is $P_3(x) = P_1(x) \cdot P_2(x)$.

There are two classical strategies:

- **Brute force (nested loop):** For every term $t_1$ in $P_1$ and every term $t_2$ in $P_2$, compute a new term with exponent $\text{exp}(t_1) + \text{exp}(t_2)$ and coefficient $\text{coeff}(t_1) \cdot \text{coeff}(t_2)$, then **add** it to the running accumulator $P_3$. The accumulator itself must support a *like-term-merge* operation (typically implemented via sorted insertion).  
  **Time complexity:** $O(m \cdot n \cdot k)$ where $k$ is the number of terms in the accumulator, simplifying to **$O(m^2 \cdot n)$** in the worst case for sparse polynomials.

- **Smart multiplication (with auxiliary array):** If the maximum degree is bounded, an array of size `(deg1 + deg2 + 1)` is used as a temporary buffer, then converted back into a linked list. This is **$O(m \cdot n)$** and is the preferred answer for KTU problems with bounded degree.

### (C) Polynomial Evaluation
Substitute a value $x = v$ and compute the result using **Horner's rule** for efficiency:

$$P(v) = a_n v^n + a_{n-1} v^{n-1} + \dots + a_1 v + a_0$$

$$\text{Horner form: } P(v) = (\dots((a_n v + a_{n-1})v + a_{n-2})v + \dots )v + a_0$$

**Time complexity:** $O(n)$ instead of the naive $O(n^2)$ term-by-term exponentiation.

---

## 3. KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below — it is the single most-tested artifact for this topic in KTU 2024 End-Semester Examinations.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Complexity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polynomial form | $P(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i x^i$ | — | Each $a_i \neq 0$ in stored form |
| Node tuple | $\langle \text{coeff}, \text{exp}, \text{next} \rangle$ | — | 3 fields per term |
| Memory per term | **3 units** (2 values + 1 pointer) | — | vs. array's 1 unit + zero-wastage |
| Addition (2 polynomials of $m, n$ terms) | Term-by-term merge | $O(m + n)$ | Both lists sorted descending |
| Multiplication (brute force) | Nested loop + accumulator merge | $O(m \cdot n \cdot k)$ | $k$ is the running term count |
| Multiplication (array-buffered) | $P_3[i+j] \mathrel{+}= a_i \cdot b_j$ | $O(m \cdot n)$ | Bounded max-degree required |
| Evaluation (naive) | Sum of $a_i \cdot v^i$ per term | $O(n^2)$ | Includes exponentiation |
| Evaluation (Horner) | Iterative nested multiply-add | $O(n)$ | KTU-preferred method |
| Sorted insertion of a term | Locate $p$ with $\text{exp}(p) \geq e$ | $O(n)$ | Keep descending order |
| Resulting term count (addition) | $\leq m + n$ | — | Many terms cancel via zero |

> Real-world utility: Sparse polynomial linked lists are the **backbone of symbolic algebra systems** (Mathematica, Maple, SymPy), **signal-processing filter design** (FIR filter coefficients), and **machine-learning polynomial regression kernels** in high dimensions where most coefficients are zero.

---

## 4. The "Why" Behind Sorted Storage

The descending-exponent invariant is not cosmetic — it is the **algorithmic enabler**. If the list is unsorted, addition degenerates from $O(m+n)$ to $O(m \cdot n)$ because we would have to scan both lists fully for every term to find a possible match. Sorted storage therefore converts a *search problem* into a *synchronized traversal problem*, which is exponentially cheaper.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners frequently award 2 bonus marks for an explicit statement of the **descending-order invariant** at the start of your answer — never skip it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## Example 1 — Representing a Polynomial as a Linked List

**Given:** $P(x) = 7x^4 + 3x^3 - 2x^2 + 9x - 5$

**Step 1 — Identify the terms (skip zero coefficients):**

$$\big\{(7, 4),\; (3, 3),\; (-2, 2),\; (9, 1),\; (-5, 0)\big\}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the descending-order invariant:**

$$4 > 3 > 2 > 1 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Construct the linked list node by node:**

$$\text{Head} \rightarrow \langle 7, 4 \rangle \rightarrow \langle 3, 3 \rangle \rightarrow \langle -2, 2 \rangle \rightarrow \langle 9, 1 \rangle \rightarrow \langle -5, 0 \rangle \rightarrow \text{NULL}$$

**Step 4 — Memory used:** $5 \text{ terms} \times 3 \text{ fields} = 15 \text{ memory units}$ (versus $5$ units for an array of size $5$, but with the array, we lose dynamic growth).

---

## Example 2 — Polynomial Addition (Full Walkthrough)

**Given:**

$$P_1(x) = 5x^4 + 3x^2 + 1$$

$$P_2(x) = 4x^3 + 2x^2 + 6x + 7$$

**Goal:** Compute $P_3(x) = P_1(x) + P_2(x)$.

### Step-by-Step Synchronized Traversal

Initialize two pointers $p_1$ at the head of $P_1$ and $p_2$ at the head of $P_2$.

**Step 1 — Compare exponents $4$ vs $3$:**  
Since $4 > 3$, copy $P_1$'s term $(5, 4)$ into $P_3$. Advance $p_1$.

$$P_3 \text{ so far} = \langle 5, 4 \rangle$$

**Step 2 — Compare exponents $2$ vs $3$:**  
Since $2 < 3$, copy $P_2$'s term $(4, 3)$ into $P_3$. Advance $p_2$.

$$P_3 \text{ so far} = \langle 5, 4 \rangle \rightarrow \langle 4, 3 \rangle$$

**Step 3 — Compare exponents $2$ vs $2$:**  
Equal exponents ⇒ combine coefficients.

$$3 + 2 = 5$$

Insert the term $(5, 2)$ into $P_3$. Advance **both** $p_1$ and $p_2$.

$$P_3 \text{ so far} = \langle 5, 4 \rangle \rightarrow \langle 4, 3 \rangle \rightarrow \langle 5, 2 \rangle$$

**Step 4 — $P_1$ is exhausted (its last term was exponent $2$).** Append the remainder of $P_2$ verbatim:

$$P_2 \text{ remainder} = \langle 6, 1 \rangle \rightarrow \langle 7, 0 \rangle$$

$$P_3 = \langle 5, 4 \rangle \rightarrow \langle 4, 3 \rangle \rightarrow \langle 5, 2 \rangle \rightarrow \langle 6, 1 \rangle \rightarrow \langle 7, 0 \rangle$$

### Final Algebraic Result

$$P_3(x) = 5x^4 + 4x^3 + 5x^2 + 6x + 7$$

### Verification by Direct Algebra

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1(x) + P_2(x) &= (5x^4 + 3x^2 + 1) + (4x^3 + 2x^2 + 6x + 7) \\
&= 5x^4 + 4x^3 + (3x^2 + 2x^2) + 6x + (1 + 7) \\
&= 5x^4 + 4x^3 + 5x^2 + 6x + 8 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Discrepancy noticed?** The direct algebra gives constant term $1 + 7 = 8$, while the trace above wrote $7$. Let us correct Step 4 — the tail of $P_2$ is actually **$\langle 6, 1\rangle \rightarrow \langle 7, 0\rangle$**, so when appended, the constant $7$ is correct (the running list still has $P_1$'s term $(1, 0)$ from earlier? No — $P_1$'s last term was the constant $1$ with exponent $0$, but we already advanced past it. We must recheck: $P_1$ has three terms $(5,4), (3,2), (1,0)$ and $P_2$ has four terms $(4,3), (2,2), (6,1), (7,0)$.)

**Corrected trace:**

- Step 1: $p_1=(5,4)$, $p_2=(4,3)$ → $4>3$ → emit $(5,4)$. $p_1 \to (3,2)$.
- Step 2: $p_1=(3,2)$, $p_2=(4,3)$ → $2<3$ → emit $(4,3)$. $p_2 \to (2,2)$.
- Step 3: equal → emit $(3+2, 2) = (5,2)$. $p_1 \to (1,0)$, $p_2 \to (6,1)$.
- Step 4: $0<1$ → emit $(6,1)$. $p_2 \to (7,0)$.
- Step 5: $0=0$ → emit $(1+7, 0) = (8,0)$.

$$P_3(x) = 5x^4 + 4x^3 + 5x^2 + 6x + 8 \quad \checkmark$$

**Time complexity used:** $m + n = 3 + 4 = 7$ comparison steps, matching the theoretical $O(m + n)$.

---

## Example 3 — Polynomial Multiplication (Brute-Force Walkthrough)

**Given:**

$$P_1(x) = 3x^2 + 5x + 6 \quad (m = 3 \text{ terms})$$

$$P_2(x) = 4x^2 + 2x + 3 \quad (n = 3 \text{ terms})$$

**Goal:** Compute $P_3(x) = P_1(x) \cdot P_2(x)$.

### Iteration Trace

For each term $t_1$ in $P_1$, multiply it by **every** term $t_2$ in $P_2$ and accumulate.

**Round 1 — Multiply $t_1 = (3, 2)$ by all of $P_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(3,2) \times (4,2) &= (12, 4) \\
(3,2) \times (2,1) &= (6, 3) \\
(3,2) \times (3,0) &= (9, 2) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round 2 — Multiply $t_1 = (5, 1)$ by all of $P_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(5,1) \times (4,2) &= (20, 3) \\
(5,1) \times (2,1) &= (10, 2) \\
(5,1) \times (3,0) &= (15, 1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round 3 — Multiply $t_1 = (6, 0)$ by all of $P_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(6,0) \times (4,2) &= (24, 2) \\
(6,0) \times (2,1) &= (12, 1) \\
(6,0) \times (3,0) &= (18, 0) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Accumulator (Merge Like Terms)

Collecting all 9 products and grouping by exponent:

| Exponent | Contributing Coefficients | Sum |
|---|---|---|
| $x^4$ | $12$ | $12$ |
| $x^3$ | $6 + 20$ | $26$ |
| $x^2$ | $9 + 10 + 24$ | $43$ |
| $x^1$ | $15 + 12$ | $27$ |
| $x^0$ | $18$ | $18$ |

### Final Result

$$P_3(x) = 12x^4 + 26x^3 + 43x^2 + 27x + 18$$

### Verification by Direct Algebra

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 \cdot P_2 &= (3x^2 + 5x + 6)(4x^2 + 2x + 3) \\
&= 3x^2(4x^2 + 2x + 3) + 5x(4x^2 + 2x + 3) + 6(4x^2 + 2x + 3) \\
&= (12x^4 + 6x^3 + 9x^2) + (20x^3 + 10x^2 + 15x) + (24x^2 + 12x + 18) \\
&= 12x^4 + (6+20)x^3 + (9+10+24)x^2 + (15+12)x + 18 \\
&= 12x^4 + 26x^3 + 43x^2 + 27x + 18 \quad \checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of elementary multiplications performed:** $m \times n = 9$, matching the theoretical $O(m \cdot n)$ for the array-buffered method.

---

## Analytical Matrix — Memory Footprint

| Representation | Memory for a $d$-degree polynomial with $k$ non-zero terms | Insertion of new term | Deletion of a term |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static array of size $(d+1)$ | $(d + 1)$ coefficients | $O(d)$ shift | $O(d)$ shift + set to $0$ |
| Dynamic array | $k$ coefficients (amortized) | $O(k)$ shift on growth | $O(k)$ shift |
| **Linked list (our topic)** | $3k$ units (coeff + exp + pointer) | $O(k)$ traversal + $O(1)$ rewire | $O(k)$ traversal + $O(1)$ rewire |

The linked list is **optimal for sparse polynomials** ($k \ll d$) at the cost of **extra pointer overhead**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — A Polynomial Node and a Concrete Linked List

This diagram shows the **logical memory layout** of a single term and a fully constructed list for $P(x) = 7x^4 + 3x^3 - 2x^2 + 9x - 5$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD(["HEAD pointer"]) --> N1["coeff = 7  |  exp = 4"]
    N1 --> N2["coeff = 3  |  exp = 3"]
    N2 --> N3["coeff = -2  |  exp = 2"]
    N3 --> N4["coeff = 9  |  exp = 1"]
    N4 --> N5["coeff = -5  |  exp = 0"]
    N5 --> NIL(["NULL / End"])
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each rectangle represents one **node** in dynamic memory. The arrow from one node to the next is the `next` pointer. The list is **strictly sorted in descending order of `exp`**.

---

## Diagram 2 — Block-Level Architecture of the Polynomial ADT

This diagram captures the **modular architecture** of a complete polynomial handling module — the way a typical KTU lab question would expect you to design it.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT["User Input: coeff and exp pairs"] --> CREATE["CREATE_POLY Function"]
    CREATE --> INSERT["SORTED_INSERT Function"]
    INSERT --> STORE["Linked List in Heap Memory"]
    STORE --> DISPLAY["DISPLAY_POLY Function"]
    STORE --> ADD["ADD_POLY Function: P1 + P2"]
    STORE --> MULT["MULTIPLY_POLY Function: P1 times P2"]
    STORE --> EVAL["EVAL_POLY Function: Horner Rule"]
    ADD --> RESULT["Result Polynomial P3"]
    MULT --> RESULT
    EVAL --> VALUE["Numeric Result at x = v"]
```

---

## Diagram 3 — Decision Logic of Polynomial Addition

The synchronized-traversal algorithm can be visualized as the following decision flow, where $e_1$ and $e_2$ are the current exponents of the two pointers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["Start: p1 at P1 head, p2 at P2 head"]) --> CHECK{"p1 NULL OR p2 NULL?"}
    CHECK -- "Yes, both non-null" --> COMPARE{"Compare e1 and e2"}
    CHECK -- "p1 is NULL" --> COPY2["Append remaining P2 to P3"]
    CHECK -- "p2 is NULL" --> COPY1["Append remaining P1 to P3"]
    COMPARE -- "e1 equals e2" --> EQUAL["Create term with coeff = c1 + c2, exp = e1. Skip if sum is zero. Advance both pointers."]
    COMPARE -- "e1 greater than e2" --> HIGHER["Copy term from P1 to P3. Advance p1."]
    COMPARE -- "e1 less than e2" --> LOWER["Copy term from P2 to P3. Advance p2."]
    EQUAL --> CHECK
    HIGHER --> CHECK
    LOWER --> CHECK
    COPY1 --> END(["End: Return P3 head"])
    COPY2 --> END
```

---

## Diagram 4 — Smart Multiplication Using an Auxiliary Array

When the maximum degree $D = \deg(P_1) + \deg(P_2)$ is bounded, an auxiliary array of size $D+1$ is used as a temporary buffer. After accumulation, the array is scanned and converted back into a linked list, dropping any zero entries.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1IN["P1 List"] --> OUTER{"For each term t1 in P1"}
    OUTER --> INNER{"For each term t2 in P2"}
    INNER --> PROD["Compute product: coeff = c1 times c2, exp = e1 plus e2"]
    PROD --> ACC["Accumulate into buffer of size D plus 1"]
    ACC --> NEXT2["Advance t2 pointer"]
    NEXT2 --> INNER
    INNER -- "t2 exhausted" --> NEXT1["Advance t1 pointer"]
    NEXT1 --> OUTER
    OUTER -- "t1 exhausted" --> SCAN["Scan buffer, skip zeros"]
    SCAN --> P3OUT["Build P3 list, sorted descending"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q:** Define a polynomial abstract data type. Explain how a polynomial can be represented using a singly linked list, with a neat diagram.

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

A *polynomial* $P(x) = a_n x^n + a_{n-1}x^{n-1} + \dots + a_0$ is an algebraic expression consisting of terms. The **Polynomial ADT** is an abstract container that supports operations such as *creation, addition, multiplication, evaluation, and display*.

In a **singly linked list representation**, every non-zero term is encapsulated in a node with three fields: `coeff`, `exp`, and `next`. The list is maintained in **descending order of exponent**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD(["Head"]) --> A["coeff = a_n | exp = n"] --> B["coeff = a_{n-1} | exp = n-1"] --> C["..."] --> LAST["coeff = a_0 | exp = 0"] --> NIL(["NULL"])
```

The first node points to the term with the **highest degree**, and the last node points to the **constant term**, terminating in `NULL`. Only non-zero terms are stored, which makes this representation ideal for **sparse polynomials**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (1 mark lost here annually):** Students often draw a doubly linked list when the question clearly asks for a *singly* linked list, or they store zero-coefficient terms. Both are marked down.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]

**Q:** Compare polynomial representation using arrays and linked lists. State one scenario where each is preferred.

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

| Criterion | Array | Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Static, contiguous, size = `max_degree + 1` | Dynamic, non-contiguous, one node per term |
| Wastage | High for sparse polynomials | Negligible |
| Access by index | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Insertion | $O(n)$ shifting | $O(1)$ to $O(n)$ pointer rewiring |
| Max degree | Fixed at compile time | Unbounded at runtime |

- **Array preferred** when the polynomial is **dense** (most coefficients non-zero) and the maximum degree is known and small.
- **Linked list preferred** when the polynomial is **sparse** (very few non-zero terms) or the maximum degree is unknown or very large.

> [!TIP]
> Always end with one crisp, scenario-specific sentence — it is the deciding factor between a 2-mark and a 3-mark answer.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Part B questions in the End-Semester Exam carry **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7** in two sub-parts. **You must attempt only ONE full question** out of the choice provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Polynomial Addition & Display

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected]`**

**(a)** Write the algorithm to create a polynomial using a singly linked list. Show the data structure definition and the insertion procedure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given $P_1(x) = 8x^5 + 4x^3 + 2x^2 + 5$ and $P_2(x) = 6x^4 + 7x^3 + 2x^2 + 3x + 1$, write the algorithm to add these two polynomials and trace it step by step to obtain the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Data structure definition:**

A node is defined with three logical members: `coeff` (the coefficient, a real or integer value), `exp` (the exponent, a non-negative integer), and `next` (a pointer to the next node of the same type).

**Algorithm — `CREATE_POLYNOMIAL`:**

1. Read the integer $n$ = number of terms.
2. Set `head = NULL` (empty list).
3. **Repeat** for $i = 1$ to $n$:
   1. Read `c` (coefficient) and `e` (exponent).
   2. **If** $c = 0$, continue to the next iteration (do not store zero terms).
   3. Allocate a new node `temp` with `temp->coeff = c` and `temp->exp = e`.
   4. Call `SORTED_INSERT(head, temp)` to insert the term at the correct sorted position.
4. Return `head` as the polynomial handle.

**Algorithm — `SORTED_INSERT(head, temp)`:**

1. **If** `head == NULL` **or** `temp->exp > head->exp`, insert `temp` at the head. Return.
2. Otherwise, traverse the list with a pointer `cur` starting at `head` until `cur->next != NULL` and `cur->next->exp > temp->exp`.
3. Insert `temp` between `cur` and `cur->next` by rewiring the pointers.

> This maintains the descending-order invariant and prevents duplicate exponents.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

$$P_1(x) = 8x^5 + 4x^3 + 2x^2 + 5 \quad (m = 4 \text{ terms})$$

$$P_2(x) = 6x^4 + 7x^3 + 2x^2 + 3x + 1 \quad (n = 5 \text{ terms})$$

**Step-by-step trace** using two synchronized pointers $p_1$ and $p_2$:

| Step | $p_1$ term | $p_2$ term | Comparison | Action | $P_3$ term emitted |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(8, 5)$ | $(6, 4)$ | $5 > 4$ | Copy $p_1$, advance $p_1$ | $(8, 5)$ |
| 2 | $(4, 3)$ | $(6, 4)$ | $3 < 4$ | Copy $p_2$, advance $p_2$ | $(6, 4)$ |
| 3 | $(4, 3)$ | $(7, 3)$ | $3 = 3$ | Combine: $4 + 7 = 11$ | $(11, 3)$ |
| 4 | $(2, 2)$ | $(2, 2)$ | $2 = 2$ | Combine: $2 + 2 = 4$ | $(4, 2)$ |
| 5 | $(5, 0)$ | $(3, 1)$ | $0 < 1$ | Copy $p_2$, advance $p_2$ | $(3, 1)$ |
| 6 | $(5, 0)$ | $(1, 0)$ | $0 = 0$ | Combine: $5 + 1 = 6$ | $(6, 0)$ |
| 7 | NULL | — | — | $P_1$ exhausted, stop | — |

**Resulting polynomial:**

$$P_3(x) = 8x^5 + 6x^4 + 11x^3 + 4x^2 + 3x + 6$$

**Verification by direct algebra:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 + P_2 &= (8x^5 + 4x^3 + 2x^2 + 5) + (6x^4 + 7x^3 + 2x^2 + 3x + 1) \\
&= 8x^5 + 6x^4 + (4+7)x^3 + (2+2)x^2 + 3x + (5+1) \\
&= 8x^5 + 6x^4 + 11x^3 + 4x^2 + 3x + 6 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Time complexity:** $O(m + n) = O(9)$ operations.  
**Space complexity:** $O(m + n)$ for the new list.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Polynomial Multiplication

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]`**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram how two polynomials can be multiplied using linked lists. State the time complexity of the algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Multiply $P_1(x) = 2x^3 + 4x + 1$ and $P_2(x) = 5x^2 + 3x + 2$ using the linked-list method, and write the final polynomial in standard form. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Strategy (Brute-Force Method):**

1. Initialize an empty result list $P_3$ (head = `NULL`).
2. For **every** term $t_1$ of $P_1$ (outer loop):
   - For **every** term $t_2$ of $P_2$ (inner loop):
     - Compute a new term with `coeff = $t_1$.coeff × $t_2$.coeff` and `exp = $t_1$.exp + $t_2$.exp`.
     - **Insert this term into $P_3$ using `SORTED_INSERT`** — this automatically merges like terms because if the new term shares an exponent with an existing term, the existing node is updated (or the new term is merged into it) rather than inserted as a duplicate.

**Diagram of nested loops:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OUTER["For each t1 in P1"] --> INNER["For each t2 in P2"]
    INNER --> CALC["Compute product term: coeff = c1 * c2, exp = e1 + e2"]
    CALC --> MERGE["SORTED_INSERT into P3: if exp already exists, add coefficients; else insert as new node"]
    MERGE --> ADV2["Advance t2"]
    ADV2 --> INNER
    INNER -- "End of P2" --> ADV1["Advance t1"]
    ADV1 --> OUTER
    OUTER -- "End of P1" --> DONE["Return P3 head"]
```

**Time complexity:** $O(m \cdot n \cdot k)$, where $m$ and $n$ are the term counts of $P_1$ and $P_2$ and $k$ is the running term count of the accumulator $P_3$. In the **array-buffered variant**, this reduces to $O(m \cdot n)$ because merging into an array is $O(1)$ per cell.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

$$P_1(x) = 2x^3 + 4x + 1 \quad (m = 3 \text{ terms})$$

$$P_2(x) = 5x^2 + 3x + 2 \quad (n = 3 \text{ terms})$$

**Inner-product trace — multiply each term of $P_1$ by all of $P_2$:**

**Round 1 — $t_1 = (2, 3)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(2,3) \times (5,2) &= (10, 5) \\
(2,3) \times (3,1) &= (6, 4) \\
(2,3) \times (2,0) &= (4, 3) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round 2 — $t_1 = (4, 1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(4,1) \times (5,2) &= (20, 3) \\
(4,1) \times (3,1) &= (12, 2) \\
(4,1) \times (2,0) &= (8, 1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Round 3 — $t_1 = (1, 0)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1,0) \times (5,2) &= (5, 2) \\
(1,0) \times (3,1) &= (3, 1) \\
(1,0) \times (2,0) &= (2, 0) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Accumulator — group by exponent and sum coefficients:**

| Exponent | Contributing terms | Sum |
|---|---|---|
| $x^5$ | $10$ | $10$ |
| $x^4$ | $6$ | $6$ |
| $x^3$ | $4 + 20$ | $24$ |
| $x^2$ | $12 + 5$ | $17$ |
| $x^1$ | $8 + 3$ | $11$ |
| $x^0$ | $2$ | $2$ |

**Final result:**

$$P_3(x) = 10x^5 + 6x^4 + 24x^3 + 17x^2 + 11x + 2$$

**Algebraic verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 \cdot P_2 &= (2x^3 + 4x + 1)(5x^2 + 3x + 2) \\
&= 2x^3(5x^2 + 3x + 2) + 4x(5x^2 + 3x + 2) + 1(5x^2 + 3x + 2) \\
&= (10x^5 + 6x^4 + 4x^3) + (20x^3 + 12x^2 + 8x) + (5x^2 + 3x + 2) \\
&= 10x^5 + 6x^4 + (4 + 20)x^3 + (12 + 5)x^2 + (8 + 3)x + 2 \\
&= 10x^5 + 6x^4 + 24x^3 + 17x^2 + 11x + 2 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls (Mark-Loss Hotspots):**
> 1. **Forgetting to declare the `exp` field as `int` and `coeff` as `float`** — students often use `int` for both, losing 1 mark when fractional coefficients appear.
> 2. **Storing zero-coefficient terms** after cancellation in addition. Always explicitly write *"if the sum of coefficients is zero, skip emitting this term."*
> 3. **Not maintaining the descending-order invariant** in the result list of multiplication. Marks are docked if the final list is unsorted.
> 4. **Confusing polynomial addition with polynomial multiplication** — the addition algorithm has *one* synchronized traversal, the multiplication has *two nested* traversals. Examiners often test this distinction.
> 5. **Failing to state the time complexity** at the end of a 14-mark answer. It is a guaranteed 1–2 mark differentiator.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Node structure:** Every polynomial term is stored as $\langle \text{coeff}, \text{exp}, \text{next} \rangle$ with `next` being a pointer to the next node.
- **Sorting invariant:** The list is **always** maintained in **descending order of exponent** — this is the foundation of efficient addition.
- **Skip zero coefficients:** Polynomial linked lists **never** store zero-coefficient terms.
- **Addition algorithm:** Two synchronized pointers, $O(m + n)$ time, handles three cases (equal, greater, lesser exponent) plus the append-remainder phase.
- **Multiplication algorithm:** Nested loops over both lists, with sorted insertion into a result accumulator, $O(m \cdot n \cdot k)$ brute-force or $O(m \cdot n)$ array-buffered.
- **Horner's rule for evaluation:** $O(n)$ time using nested multiply-and-add; preferred over the naive $O(n^2)$ term-by-term approach.
- **Memory overhead:** Linked lists spend **3 memory units per term** (2 values + 1 pointer) versus 1 unit for an array, so they shine only when the polynomial is **sparse**.
- **KTU-favorite trick questions:** (i) "What if the polynomial is dense?" → array is better. (ii) "What if degrees are duplicates?" → merge them, do not insert twice. (iii) "What if coefficients cancel to zero?" → do not store them.
- **Real-world relevance:** Symbolic algebra engines (Mathematica, SymPy), FIR filter design in DSP, and polynomial kernel methods in machine learning all rely on this exact data structure.
- **Default fallback for the examiner:** Always end every long answer with the **time and space complexity** statement and a **one-line summary of the algorithm's principle** (e.g., *"synchronized merge of two sorted streams"*).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Trees # 1.
