Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Tree (Data Structure):** A *Tree* is a non-linear, hierarchical data structure consisting of a finite set of one or more **nodes** such that there is a designated node called the **root**, and the remaining nodes are partitioned into $n \geq 0$ disjoint subsets $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$, each of which is itself a tree. These subsets are called **subtrees** of the root. In graph-theoretic terms, a tree is a **connected, acyclic, undirected graph**. It is the simplest connected graph and forms the structural backbone of numerous computer science algorithms. ## 1.2 Key Terminology (Standard KTU Vocabulary) - **Root** – The topmost node with no parent. - **Node (Vertex)** – A fundamental unit that stores data and links to children. - **Edge** – The connecting link between a parent and a child. - **Parent / Child** – The node above (ancestor) / below (descendant) a given node. - **Leaf (External / Terminal Node)** – A node with **zero children**. - **Internal Node** – A node with at least one child. - **Degree of a Node** – The number of children the node possesses. - **Degree of a Tree** – The maximum degree among all its nodes. - **Depth of a Node** – The length of the path from the root to that node (root has depth $0$). - **Height of a Node** – The length of the longest path from that node down to a leaf. - **Height of a Tree** – The height of the root node. - **Sibling** – Nodes that share the same parent. - **Ancestor / Descendant** – Any node on the path from root to that node / any node in its subtree. - **Subtree** – A tree formed by a node and all its descendants. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy – The Family Tree:** > Imagine a *family tree* starting from a great-grandparent at the top. The root represents the eldest ancestor, and the branches represent the offspring. Each ancestor can have multiple children, who in turn can have their own children, forming an inverted, branching structure. The most recent generation (those with no children) represents the **leaves**. The *height* of the family tree is the number of generations, and the *depth* of a particular person is their number of generations removed from the ancestor. This hierarchical inheritance of properties is exactly how computational trees operate. A file system on a computer is another perfect example: the root is the `/` (root directory), folders are internal nodes, and files are leaves. ## 1.4 Why Trees? – Motivation Trees provide: - **Hierarchical data representation** (file systems, organizational charts, XML/HTML DOM). - **Efficient searching** (Binary Search Tree: $O(\log n)$ average). - **Efficient insertion/deletion** compared to linear structures. - **Foundation for advanced structures** (Heaps, Tries, B-Trees, Red-Black Trees). - **Algorithm building blocks** (Decision trees, parse trees, expression trees). > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever defining a tree, always mention the *recursive* nature ("a tree is a collection of nodes where each node holds a value and references to subtrees"). Examiners award bonus marks for emphasizing recursion. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 General Properties of a Tree For any tree $T$ with $n$ nodes: 1. There is exactly **one path** between any two nodes. 2. The tree is **connected** and has **no cycles**. 3. The number of **edges** in a tree with $n$ nodes is always $n - 1$. 4. Removing any edge disconnects the tree; adding any edge creates a cycle. 5. There are no self-loops or multiple edges (simple graph). ### Why These Properties Matter These properties are the foundation of many proofs in graph theory. The *unique path* property is what makes trees perfect for representing precedence, ancestry, and hierarchical search. The *edge = node - 1* relationship is critical for KTU numerical problems. ## 2.2 Binary Tree – KTU Focus Area > [!NOTE] > **Binary Tree:** A tree in which each node has at most **two children**, referred to as the *left child* and the *right child*. ### Special Forms of Binary Trees | Form | Definition | | :--- | :--- | | **Full (Strict) Binary Tree** | Every node has either **0 or 2 children**. | | **Complete Binary Tree** | All levels are completely filled **except possibly the last**, which is filled from left to right. | | **Perfect Binary Tree** | All internal nodes have 2 children and all leaves are at the **same depth**. | | **Degenerate (Skewed) Tree** | Every internal node has only **one child** (resembles a linked list). | | **Balanced Binary Tree** | Height difference between left and right subtrees of every node is at most **1**. | ## 2.3 Binary Search Tree (BST) – Property For every node $N$ with key $k$: - All keys in the **left subtree** of $N$ are $< k$. - All keys in the **right subtree** of $N$ are $> k$. - Both left and right subtrees are themselves BSTs. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use `\vert` instead of `\vertvert` in all tables below to keep markdown syntax intact.** | # | Property | Formula | Applicable To | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Max nodes at level $L$ (root at level 0) | $N(L) = 2^{L}$ | Binary Tree | | 2 | Max nodes in tree of height $h$ | $N_{max} = 2^{(h+1)} - 1$ | Binary Tree | | 3 | Min nodes in tree of height $h$ | $N_{min} = h + 1$ | Binary Tree | | 4 | Number of edges in any tree with $n$ nodes | $E = n - 1$ | General Tree | | 5 | Relation: leaf $L$, internal nodes $I$, total $n$ | $n = L + I$ | Any Tree | | 6 | For a full binary tree: leaves & internal nodes | $L = I + 1$ | Full Binary Tree | | 7 | For any binary tree: nodes with 2 children $= L - 1$ | $n_2 = n_0 - 1$ | Full Binary Tree | | 8 | Total degree sum = $2 \cdot (n - 1)$ | $\sum \deg = 2(n-1)$ | General Tree | | 9 | Height of a complete binary tree with $n$ nodes | $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Complete BT | | 10 | Min height of BST with $n$ nodes | $h = \lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil - 1$ | BST | ## 2.5 Tree Traversals – The Four Standard Methods ### (a) Depth-First Traversals (Recursive) Let $T$ be a tree with root $r$, left subtree $L$, and right subtree $R$. - **Preorder (NLR):** Visit root $\rightarrow$ Left $\rightarrow$ Right - **Inorder (LNR):** Left $\rightarrow$ Visit root $\rightarrow$ Right *(gives sorted order in BST)* - **Postorder (LRN):** Left $\rightarrow$ Right $\rightarrow$ Visit root ### (b) Breadth-First Traversal - **Level Order (BFS):** Visit nodes level by level, left to right, using a **queue**. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications | Application | Tree Variant Used | | :--- | :--- | | Database indexing | B-Tree / B+ Tree | | Compiler syntax analysis | Parse Tree / Abstract Syntax Tree | | Network routing | Spanning Tree (Kruskal / Prim) | | File system (NTFS, ext4) | B+ Tree | | Decision support systems | Decision Tree | | Memory management | Heap (Complete Binary Tree) | | Auto-complete / IP routing | Trie (Prefix Tree) | | Game AI (chess) | Game Tree with alpha-beta pruning | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Number of Nodes in a Binary Tree of Height $h$ **Statement:** A binary tree of height $h$ contains at most $2^{(h+1)} - 1$ nodes. **Proof by Mathematical Induction on $h$:** **Base Case ($h = 0$):** A tree of height $0$ is just the root node. $$ N_{max}(0) = 2^{(0+1)} - 1 = 2^{1} - 1 = 1 $$ The root is the only node, and the count is **1**. The base case holds. **Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume that for some $k \geq 0$, a binary tree of height $k$ has at most $2^{(k+1)} - 1$ nodes. **Inductive Step:** Consider a binary tree $T$ of height $k+1$. The root has two subtrees, each of height at most $k$. By the inductive hypothesis, the maximum number of nodes in each subtree is $2^{(k+1)} - 1$. $$ N_{max}(k+1) = 1 + N_{max}^{left} + N_{max}^{right} $$ Substituting the inductive hypothesis: $$ N_{max}(k+1) = 1 + \big(2^{(k+1)} - 1\big) + \big(2^{(k+1)} - 1\big) $$ $$ N_{max}(k+1) = 1 + 2 \cdot \big(2^{(k+1)} - 1\big) $$ $$ N_{max}(k+1) = 1 + 2^{(k+2)} - 2 $$ $$ N_{max}(k+1) = 2^{(k+2)} - 1 $$ This is exactly the formula for height $h = k+1$. Hence proved by induction. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic Summary:** A perfect binary tree doubles its node count at every level. After $h$ levels below the root, you accumulate $1 + 2 + 4 + \dots + 2^{h}$ nodes, which is the geometric series sum. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Edges in a Tree ($E = n - 1$) **Statement:** Any tree with $n$ nodes has exactly $n - 1$ edges. **Proof:** We use the handshaking lemma on the node degrees. Let $d_i$ denote the degree of node $i$. The sum of all node degrees equals twice the number of edges (since each edge contributes $1$ to the degree of each of its two endpoints): $$ \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i = 2E $$ Now, we count the number of non-root nodes. Every non-root node has exactly one parent edge leading into it. The root has no parent. Therefore, the number of edges equals the number of non-root nodes: $$ E = n - 1 $$ Equivalently, using the degree identity: $$ 2E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i = 2(n - 1) \quad\Rightarrow\quad E = n - 1 $$ $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relation Between Leaves and Internal Nodes in a Full Binary Tree **Statement:** In a full binary tree, $L = I + 1$, where $L$ = number of leaves, $I$ = number of internal nodes. **Proof:** Let $n_0$ be the number of leaves (degree $0$), $n_1$ the number of nodes with exactly one child, and $n_2$ the number of nodes with exactly two children. Total number of nodes: $$ n = n_0 + n_1 + n_2 $$ In a **full binary tree**, $n_1 = 0$ by definition. So: $$ n = n_0 + n_2 $$ By the degree-sum identity: $$ \sum d_i = 0 \cdot n_0 + 1 \cdot n_1 + 2 \cdot n_2 = 2E = 2(n - 1) $$ Substituting $n_1 = 0$: $$ 2n_2 = 2(n_0 + n_2 - 1) $$ $$ 2n_2 = 2n_0 + 2n_2 - 2 $$ $$ 0 = 2n_0 - 2 $$ $$ n_0 = 1 + n_2 $$ Since leaves $= n_0$ and internal nodes $= n_2$, we obtain: $$ L = I + 1 $$ $\blacksquare$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for KTU Problems | Given Quantity | Formula to Use | Compute | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Height $h$ of full BT | $n = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | Plug $h$ | | Number of nodes $n$ in full BT | Height $h = \log_2(n+1) - 1$ | Apply log | | Number of leaves in full BT | $L = (n + 1) / 2$ | Plug $n$ | | Number of internal nodes in full BT | $I = (n - 1) / 2$ | Plug $n$ | | Edges in any tree with $n$ nodes | $E = n - 1$ | Plug $n$ | | Max nodes at level $L$ | $2^{L}$ | Plug $L$ | | Height of skewed BT with $n$ nodes | $h = n - 1$ | Plug $n$ | ## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU Style **Problem:** A binary tree has $25$ nodes. If the height of the tree is $4$, determine whether the tree is: - (i) Complete - (ii) Full - (iii) Skewed **Solution:** **Step 1:** Calculate the maximum number of nodes possible for height $4$: $$ N_{max} = 2^{(4+1)} - 1 = 2^{5} - 1 = 31 $$ **Step 2:** Calculate the minimum number of nodes for height $4$: $$ N_{min} = 4 + 1 = 5 $$ **Step 3:** Since $5 \leq 25 \leq 31$, the configuration is feasible in principle. **Step 4:** Check full binary tree condition: $n$ must be odd because $n = 2n_2 + 1$ (from $L = I + 1$). Since $25$ is odd, it **could be** a full binary tree with $12$ internal nodes and $13$ leaves. **Step 5:** Check skewed tree: height $4$ would have $5$ nodes, but we have $25$, so it is **not skewed**. **Step 6:** Conclusion: The tree is most likely a *complete* or *full* binary tree — but it cannot be uniquely determined from the given information alone. $\blacksquare$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Tree Node Anatomy ```mermaid graph TD A["NODE X
data: 50
left ptr
right ptr"] --> B["LEFT CHILD
data: 30
..."] A --> C["RIGHT CHILD
data: 70
..."] B --> D["LEAF
data: 20"] B --> E["LEAF
data: 40"] C --> F["LEAF
data: 60"] C --> G["LEAF
data: 80"] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The gold node is the **root**, blue nodes are **internal**, and green nodes are **leaves**. ## 4.2 Tree Terminology Map (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD R["ROOT NODE
No parent"] --> P["PARENT NODE
Has one or more children"] P --> C["CHILD NODE
Has exactly one parent"] C --> S["SIBLING
Same parent as another child"] C --> L["LEAF NODE
Zero children"] P --> IN["INTERNAL NODE
At least one child"] D1["DEPTH"] -.-> R D2["HEIGHT"] -.-> L D3["DEGREE"] -.-> P D4["SUBTREE"] -.-> C D5["ANCESTOR"] -.-> R D6["DESCENDANT"] -.-> L style R fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style L fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style IN fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style P fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Binary Search Tree — Insertion Workflow ```mermaid graph TD Start["START
Insert key K"] --> RootCheck{"Is tree empty?"} RootCheck -- YES --> MakeRoot["Create root node with K"] RootCheck -- NO --> Compare{"Compare K with current node"} Compare -- "K less" --> GoLeft["Go to LEFT subtree"] Compare -- "K greater" --> GoRight["Go to RIGHT subtree"] GoLeft --> NullLeft{"Left child NULL?"} NullLeft -- YES --> InsertL["Insert K as left child"] NullLeft -- NO --> Compare GoRight --> NullRight{"Right child NULL?"} NullRight -- YES --> InsertR["Insert K as right child"] NullRight -- NO --> Compare InsertL --> Done["DONE"] InsertR --> Done MakeRoot --> Done style Start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style Done fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Tree Traversal Sequences — Visual Map ```mermaid graph TD T1["A
Root"] --> T2["B
Left"] T1 --> T3["C
Right"] T2 --> T4["D"] T2 --> T5["E"] T3 --> T6["F"] T3 --> T7["G"] style T1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style T2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style T3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style T4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style T5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style T6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style T7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Traversal Results for the tree above:** > - **Preorder (NLR):** A, B, D, E, C, F, G > - **Inorder (LNR):** D, B, E, A, F, C, G > - **Postorder (LRN):** D, E, B, F, G, C, A > - **Level Order (BFS):** A, B, C, D, E, F, G ## 4.5 Complete vs Full vs Perfect Binary Tree Comparison ```mermaid graph TD subA["COMPLETE BT
All levels filled
except last left-justified"] subB["FULL BT
Every node has
0 or 2 children"] subC["PERFECT BT
All internal nodes have 2 children
All leaves same level"] subA --> SA1["A -> B -> C -> D"] subB --> SB1["A -> B, A -> C, B -> D, B -> E"] subC --> SC1["A -> B, A -> C, B -> D, B -> E, C -> F, C -> G"] style subA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style subB fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style subC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the following terms with respect to a binary tree:** **(a)** Depth of a node **(b)** Height of a tree **(c)** Degree of a node. **Model Answer:** **(a) Depth of a Node:** The depth of a node $N$ is the length of the unique path from the **root** to $N$. The root has depth $0$, its children have depth $1$, and so on. If a node is at level $L$, then its depth is $L$. **(b) Height of a Tree:** The height of a tree is the depth of its **deepest leaf** (equivalently, the length of the longest path from the root to any leaf). A tree with a single node has height $0$, and an empty tree has height $-1$. **(c) Degree of a Node:** The degree of a node is the number of **children** it has. For example, in a binary tree, a node can have a degree of $0$ (leaf), $1$ (one child), or $2$ (two children). The degree of a tree is the maximum degree among all its nodes. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the difference between a full binary tree and a complete binary tree? Illustrate with an example.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Full Binary Tree | Complete Binary Tree | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | Every node has either $0$ or $2$ children | All levels fully filled, last level filled **left to right** | | **Constraint on Leaves** | All leaves need not be at the same level | All leaves at the last level or second-last level | | **Example Use** | Expression trees | Heaps (priority queues) | **Example:** **Full Binary Tree:** Root $A$ has children $B$ and $C$. $B$ has children $D$ and $E$. $C$ is a leaf. Every node has either $0$ or $2$ children ✓ **Complete Binary Tree (not full):** Root $A$ has children $B$ and $C$. $B$ has only one child $D$. Levels are filled left-to-right ✓ A **perfect** binary tree is both full and complete, but a complete tree need not be full, and a full tree need not be complete. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question must provide internal choice. Two full alternative questions are given below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the different types of binary trees with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a binary tree with $n$ nodes, prove that the maximum number of nodes is $2^{h+1} - 1$ where $h$ is the height. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **1. Strict (Full) Binary Tree:** Every node has $0$ or $2$ children. No node has exactly $1$ child. **2. Complete Binary Tree:** All levels except possibly the last are completely filled, and the last level has all nodes as far left as possible. **3. Perfect Binary Tree:** All internal nodes have $2$ children and all leaves are at the same depth. A perfect tree of height $h$ has exactly $2^{h+1} - 1$ nodes. **4. Degenerate (Skewed) Binary Tree:** Each parent has only one child, effectively reducing the tree to a linked list. Height = number of nodes $- 1$. **5. Balanced Binary Tree:** For every node, the heights of the left and right subtrees differ by at most $1$. AVL and Red-Black trees are self-balancing variants. *(Diagrams for each are mandatory — refer to Section 4.5 of these notes.)* #### Model Solution for (b): *Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the exhaustive proof.* The proof uses **mathematical induction**: **Base Case** $h = 0$: $N_{max} = 2^{1} - 1 = 1$ (only the root). ✓ **Inductive Hypothesis:** For height $k$, $N_{max}(k) = 2^{k+1} - 1$. **Inductive Step:** For height $k+1$: $$ N_{max}(k+1) = 1 + 2 \cdot N_{max}(k) = 1 + 2(2^{k+1} - 1) = 2^{k+2} - 1 $$ Hence, the result holds for all $h \geq 0$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Define a Binary Search Tree (BST). Write the algorithm to insert a node into a BST and illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Perform **Preorder**, **Inorder**, **Postorder**, and **Level Order** traversals on the following binary tree: **(7 marks)** Tree structure used: ``` 50 / \ 30 70 / \ / \ 20 40 60 80 ``` #### Model Solution for (a): **Definition:** A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a binary tree $T$ such that for every node $N$ with key $k$: - All keys in the **left subtree** of $N$ are strictly **less than** $k$. - All keys in the **right subtree** of $N$ are strictly **greater than** $k$. - Both subtrees are themselves BSTs. **Insertion Algorithm (Conceptual Steps):** 1. Start at the root. 2. If the tree is empty, the new node becomes the root. 3. Compare the new key $K$ with the current node's key. 4. If $K <$ current, move to the left child (recursively insert). 5. If $K >$ current, move to the right child (recursively insert). 6. Insert $K$ as a leaf at the first NULL position encountered. **Example:** Insert $35$ into the BST above. Compare $35$ with $50 \rightarrow$ go left. Compare $35$ with $30 \rightarrow$ go right. Right child of $30$ is NULL $\rightarrow$ insert $35$ as the right child of $30$. #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1: Preorder (Root → Left → Right):** Visit $50$, then recursively traverse left subtree ($30, 20, 40$), then right subtree ($70, 60, 80$). **Preorder:** $50, 30, 20, 40, 70, 60, 80$ **Step 2: Inorder (Left → Root → Right):** Recursively traverse left subtree ($20, 30, 40$), visit $50$, then right subtree ($60, 70, 80$). **Inorder:** $20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80$ *(sorted — characteristic of BSTs)* **Step 3: Postorder (Left → Right → Root):** Recursively traverse left subtree ($20, 40, 30$), then right subtree ($60, 80, 70$), then visit $50$. **Postorder:** $20, 40, 30, 60, 80, 70, 50$ **Step 4: Level Order (BFS using a queue):** Level $0$: $50$ Level $1$: $30, 70$ Level $2$: $20, 40, 60, 80$ **Level Order:** $50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing height and depth:** Depth is measured *from the root downward*; height is measured *from the node to the deepest leaf*. Drawing a diagram is mandatory — verbal explanation alone loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the base case** in induction proofs: $h = 0$ must be explicitly shown, or the proof is incomplete. > 3. **Mixing up traversals:** A very common error is writing the inorder as preorder. Memorize: *Pre* = *Pre*-fix = root first; *Post* = root *last*; *In* = root *middle*. > 4. **Skipping the queue mention** in level-order traversal: Examiners expect you to state that BFS uses a *queue* (FIFO). Forgetting this loses 1 mark. > 5. **BST insertion at wrong position:** Always insert as a **leaf** — never in the middle of an existing subtree. > 6. **In BST, allowing duplicates:** The standard BST definition disallows equal keys in left/right subtrees (or places them by consistent convention). State your assumption clearly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam Day!** - A **tree** is a connected acyclic graph with $n$ nodes and $n - 1$ edges. - A **binary tree** has at most $2$ children per node. - **Root** has no parent; **leaf** has no children; **internal node** has $\geq 1$ child. - **Depth** = distance from root; **Height** = distance to deepest leaf. - **Max nodes** in BT of height $h$: $2^{h+1} - 1$ *(memorize this!)* - **Min nodes** in BT of height $h$: $h + 1$. - **Full BT property:** $L = I + 1$ (leaves = internal nodes + 1). - **Perfect BT:** All leaves at same level, all internal nodes have $2$ children. - **Complete BT:** Last level filled left-to-right. - **Skewed BT:** Effectively a linked list; height $= n - 1$. - **BST Property:** Left $<$ Node $<$ Right (inorder traversal yields sorted order). - **Three DFS Traversals:** - **Preorder** = NLR (root first) - **Inorder** = LNR (root middle) — sorted in BST - **Postorder** = LRN (root last) — used in tree deletion - **Level Order** = BFS using a **queue** (FIFO discipline). - **Edges in a tree** with $n$ nodes: $E = n - 1$ (always). - **Number of leaves in a full BT** with $n$ nodes: $L = (n+1)/2$. - **Time complexity of BST search/insert/delete:** $O(h)$ in worst case, $O(\log n)$ when balanced. - **Common applications:** File systems, databases (B-Tree), compilers (parse trees), AI (game trees), routing (spanning trees).

topics.title: Binary Trees - Tree Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Binary Trees - Tree Operations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Binary Tree (BT)** is a finite, non-linear, hierarchical data structure consisting of a collection of nodes, where each node contains a **data element** and at most **two distinct child references**, conventionally named the **Left Child** and the **Right Child**. The topmost node is called the **Root**, and nodes with zero children are termed **Leaf Nodes** (or external nodes). Formally, a binary tree $T$ is defined as a finite set of nodes such that: $$T = \emptyset \quad \text{or} \quad T = \{ \text{Root}, T_L, T_R \}$$ where $T_L$ and $T_R$ are themselves disjoint binary trees (the left and right subtrees). This recursive definition is fundamental to writing recursive algorithms on trees. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A tree with $N$ nodes has exactly $N-1$ edges. This property is frequently tested in KTU examinations. ## 1.2 Essential Binary Tree Terminology | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Root** | The topmost node with no parent | | **Parent** | A node having one or more children | | **Child** | A node descending from another node | | **Leaf / External Node** | A node with no children (degree = 0) | | **Internal Node** | A node with at least one child | | **Sibling** | Nodes sharing the same parent | | **Degree of a Node** | Number of children of that node | | **Degree of a Tree** | Maximum degree among all nodes (≤ 2 for binary tree) | | **Depth of a Node** | Length of the path from root to that node (root depth = 0) | | **Height of a Node** | Length of the longest path from that node to a leaf (leaf height = 0) | | **Height of a Tree** | Height of the root node | | **Level** | All nodes at the same depth | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Family Tree:** > Think of a binary tree as a **family genealogy chart** where every person can have at most two direct descendants. The oldest ancestor is the **root**, the eldest child becomes the **left subtree**, and the younger child becomes the **right subtree**. A person with no children represents a **leaf node**. The "generation gap" between the oldest ancestor and a newborn is the **height of the tree**, while the lineage path from the ancestor to a specific descendant defines the **depth of a node**. Other real-world analogies: - **Organizational Hierarchy:** CEO → Managers → Team Leads → Employees (at most 2 direct reports per level for binary structure) - **Decision Trees:** Yes/No decisions branching out - **File System Directories:** Folders can contain at most 2 sub-folders in a binary file system ## 1.4 Types of Binary Trees (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following classifications are **repeatedly asked** in KTU Module 3 examinations. Memorize the distinctions. 1. **Full (Strict) Binary Tree:** Every node has either 0 or 2 children. 2. **Complete Binary Tree (CBT):** All levels are completely filled except possibly the last, which is filled from **left to right**. 3. **Perfect Binary Tree:** All internal nodes have 2 children AND all leaves are at the same level. 4. **Skewed Binary Tree:** Every node has exactly one child (Left-skewed or Right-skewed). Height = $N-1$. 5. **Balanced Binary Tree:** Height difference between left and right subtrees of every node is at most **1**. **Standard Metrics to Remember (in Bold):** - Minimum number of nodes for height $h$ = $h + 1$ (skewed tree) - Maximum number of nodes for height $h$ = $2^{h+1} - 1$ (perfect tree) - For a tree with $N$ nodes: **Minimum height = $\lfloor \log_2 N \rfloor$**, **Maximum height = $N - 1$** - Number of leaf nodes in a full binary tree = number of internal nodes + 1 # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Core Tree Operations — The "Why" and "How" Binary tree operations can be classified into four functional categories. Each has a precise algorithmic purpose and a KTU-expected complexity. ### 2.1.1 Traversal Operations (Most Frequently Tested) Traversal means visiting every node **exactly once** in a specific order. There are exactly **4 standard traversals** in KTU syllabus: **(A) Preorder Traversal: Root → Left → Right (NLR)** - **Why:** Used to create a copy of the tree, prefix expression evaluation. - **How:** Visit root, recursively traverse left subtree, recursively traverse right subtree. **(B) Inorder Traversal: Left → Root → Right (LNR)** - **Why:** Produces sorted sequence in a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**. Infix expression evaluation. - **How:** Recursively traverse left subtree, visit root, recursively traverse right subtree. **(C) Postorder Traversal: Left → Right → Root (LRN)** - **Why:** Used for tree deletion (children before parent), postfix expression evaluation. - **How:** Recursively traverse left subtree, recursively traverse right subtree, visit root. **(D) Level Order Traversal: Level by level, left to right (BFS)** - **Why:** Level-by-level processing, shortest path in unweighted tree. - **How:** Uses a **Queue (FIFO)** data structure. ### 2.1.2 Insertion Operation - **Why:** To add a new node while preserving the tree's structural property (e.g., CBT → level order insertion). - **How:** In a CBT, new nodes are inserted at the first available position scanning **left to right** at the last level. ### 2.1.3 Deletion Operation - **Why:** To remove a node and maintain tree properties. - **How (3 Cases):** - **Case 1: Leaf node** → Simply remove. - **Case 2: Node with one child** → Replace node with its child. - **Case 3: Node with two children** → Replace node's value with **(a) inorder successor** (smallest in right subtree) or **(b) inorder predecessor** (largest in left subtree), then delete that successor/predecessor. ### 2.1.4 Search Operation - **Why:** Locate a specific key. - **How:** Linear scan via any traversal — $O(N)$ for general binary tree, $O(\log N)$ for BST. ### 2.1.5 Height, Count & Mirror Operations - **Height of Tree:** $h = \max(h_L, h_R) + 1$ - **Count of Nodes:** $N = N_L + N_R + 1$ - **Count of Leaves:** $L = L_L + L_R$ (leaf if both children null) - **Mirror Image:** Swap left and right subtrees recursively. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is your **exam-day reference**. Every formula here is a confirmed KTU high-yield item. ### Table 2.1 — Property Formulas for Binary Trees | # | Property | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Max nodes at level $i$ | $2^i$ | $i$ starts from 0 | | 2 | Max nodes in tree of height $h$ | $2^{h+1} - 1$ | Perfect tree | | 3 | Min nodes in tree of height $h$ | $h + 1$ | Skewed tree | | 4 | Min height for $N$ nodes | $\lfloor \log_2 N \rfloor$ | Balanced minimum | | 5 | Max height for $N$ nodes | $N - 1$ | Skewed (worst case) | | 6 | Leaves in full binary tree | $L = I + 1$ | $I$ = internal nodes | | 7 | Total nodes $N$ | $N = L + I$ | Always holds | | 8 | Number of NULL pointers | $N + 1$ | Critical KTU property | | 9 | Internal path length | $I(N) = I(N-1) + N - 1$ | Recurrence | ### Table 2.2 — Time Complexity of Operations | Operation | Average Case | Worst Case (Skewed) | Best Case (Balanced) | |---|---|---|---| | Search | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | | Insertion | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | | Deletion | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | | Traversal (all 4) | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | | Height calculation | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | | Space (recursion stack) | $O(h)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications > [!TIP] > **Why study Tree Operations?** They are the backbone of: > - **Compiler Design:** Expression trees (parse trees) for arithmetic/logical expressions > - **Databases:** B-trees and B+ trees for indexing > - **AI/Machine Learning:** Decision trees for classification/regression > - **Computer Graphics:** BSP trees for 3D rendering > - **Networking:** Spanning tree protocol, routing trees > - **Operating Systems:** Process trees, file system hierarchies # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: All Four Traversals on a Single Tree > [!NOTE] > **Master this exact problem — it appears in nearly every KTU exam.** Consider the following binary tree: ``` 1 / \ 2 3 / \ \ 4 5 6 ``` We will derive each traversal **step by step** using the recursive call stack. ### 3.1.1 Preorder Traversal (NLR) The algorithm visits **N**ode first, then **L**eft, then **R**ight. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Preorder}(T) &= \text{Visit}(\text{Root}) + \text{Preorder}(T_L) + \text{Preorder}(T_R) \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step call sequence:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Preorder}(1) \rightarrow \text{Visit } 1 \\ &\quad \text{Preorder}(2) \rightarrow \text{Visit } 2 \\ &\quad\quad \text{Preorder}(4) \rightarrow \text{Visit } 4 \quad (\text{left and right null, return}) \\ &\quad\quad \text{Preorder}(5) \rightarrow \text{Visit } 5 \quad (\text{return}) \\ &\quad \text{Preorder}(3) \rightarrow \text{Visit } 3 \\ &\quad\quad \text{Preorder}(\text{null}) \rightarrow \text{return} \\ &\quad\quad \text{Preorder}(6) \rightarrow \text{Visit } 6 \quad (\text{return}) \end{aligned} $$ **Final Preorder Output: `1, 2, 4, 5, 3, 6`** ### 3.1.2 Inorder Traversal (LNR) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Inorder}(T) &= \text{Inorder}(T_L) + \text{Visit}(\text{Root}) + \text{Inorder}(T_R) \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step call sequence:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Inorder}(1) \rightarrow \\ &\quad \text{Inorder}(2) \rightarrow \\ &\quad\quad \text{Inorder}(4) \rightarrow \text{Visit } 4 \\ &\quad\quad \text{Visit } 2 \\ &\quad\quad \text{Inorder}(5) \rightarrow \text{Visit } 5 \\ &\quad \text{Visit } 1 \\ &\quad \text{Inorder}(3) \rightarrow \\ &\quad\quad \text{Inorder}(\text{null}) \rightarrow \text{return} \\ &\quad\quad \text{Visit } 3 \\ &\quad\quad \text{Inorder}(6) \rightarrow \text{Visit } 6 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Inorder Output: `4, 2, 5, 1, 3, 6`** ### 3.1.3 Postorder Traversal (LRN) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Postorder}(T) &= \text{Postorder}(T_L) + \text{Postorder}(T_R) + \text{Visit}(\text{Root}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step derivation:** - Process left subtree of 1: $\text{Postorder}(2)$ - Process left subtree of 2: $\text{Postorder}(4)$ → Visit 4 - Process right subtree of 2: $\text{Postorder}(5)$ → Visit 5 - Visit 2 - Process right subtree of 1: $\text{Postorder}(3)$ - Process left subtree of 3: empty → return - Process right subtree of 3: $\text{Postorder}(6)$ → Visit 6 - Visit 3 - Visit 1 **Final Postorder Output: `4, 5, 2, 6, 3, 1`** ### 3.1.4 Level Order Traversal (BFS) Uses a Queue. Step-by-step: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Enqueue}(1) ; \text{Queue: } [1] \\ &\text{Dequeue } 1 \rightarrow \text{Print } 1 ; \text{Enqueue}(2, 3) ; \text{Queue: } [2, 3] \\ &\text{Dequeue } 2 \rightarrow \text{Print } 2 ; \text{Enqueue}(4, 5) ; \text{Queue: } [3, 4, 5] \\ &\text{Dequeue } 3 \rightarrow \text{Print } 3 ; \text{Enqueue}(6) ; \text{Queue: } [4, 5, 6] \\ &\text{Dequeue } 4 \rightarrow \text{Print } 4 ; \text{Queue: } [5, 6] \\ &\text{Dequeue } 5 \rightarrow \text{Print } 5 ; \text{Queue: } [6] \\ &\text{Dequeue } 6 \rightarrow \text{Print } 6 ; \text{Queue: } [] \\ &\text{Queue empty → STOP} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Level Order Output: `1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6`** ## 3.2 Algorithm Derivations (C-style Pseudocode) ### 3.2.1 Recursive Traversal Function $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{void preorder(struct Node* root)} \{ \\ &\quad \text{if (root == NULL) return;} \\ &\quad \text{printf("\%d ", root->data);} \quad // \text{N} \\ &\quad \text{preorder(root->left);} \quad\quad\quad // \text{L} \\ &\quad \text{preorder(root->right);} \quad\quad // \text{R} \\ &\} \end{aligned} $$ > Replace the print statement position to obtain Inorder (between L and R) and Postorder (after R). ### 3.2.2 Height of a Tree — Recurrence Derivation The height of an empty tree is $0$ (or $-1$, depending on convention). For a non-empty tree: $$ \begin{aligned} H(\text{root}) = \begin{cases} -1 & \text{if root == NULL} \\ \max\big(H(\text{root.left}),\ H(\text{root.right})\big) + 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ **Derivation for our example tree:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(4) &= \max(-1, -1) + 1 = 0 \\ H(5) &= 0 \\ H(6) &= 0 \\ H(2) &= \max(0, 0) + 1 = 1 \\ H(3) &= \max(-1, 0) + 1 = 1 \\ H(1) &= \max(1, 1) + 1 = 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Height of the tree = 2 ✓** ### 3.2.3 Total Node Count — Recurrence $$ \begin{aligned} C(\text{root}) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if root == NULL} \\ C(\text{root.left}) + C(\text{root.right}) + 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification for the example:** $$ \begin{aligned} C(4) &= 1,\ C(5) = 1,\ C(6) = 1 \\ C(2) &= 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \\ C(3) &= 0 + 1 + 1 = 2 \\ C(1) &= 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 \ \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study | Operation | Recursion Strategy | Auxiliary Structure | Typical KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | Preorder | Root first | Stack (implicit recursion) | Tree copy / prefix expression | | Inorder | Root middle | Stack (implicit recursion) | BST sorting / infix expression | | Postorder | Root last | Stack (implicit recursion) | Tree deletion / postfix expression | | Level Order | BFS | Queue (explicit) | Level-wise printing | | Insertion (CBT) | BFS-based | Queue | Heap construction | | Deletion | Inorder successor swap | Stack | BST maintenance | | Height | Postorder (bottom-up) | Stack | Tree balancing | | Mirror | Recursive swap | Stack | Tree isomorphism | ## 3.4 Construction Problem — Building a Tree from Traversals > [!NOTE] > **KTU Favorite:** Given Preorder + Inorder → Construct the unique binary tree. **Given:** Preorder = `{1, 2, 4, 5, 3, 6}` and Inorder = `{4, 2, 5, 1, 3, 6}` **Algorithm:** 1. First element of Preorder = **Root** → `1` 2. Locate `1` in Inorder → splits inorder into `{4, 2, 5}` (left) and `{3, 6}` (right) 3. First element of remaining Preorder in left part = `2` (root of left subtree) 4. Locate `2` in inorder left part → `{4}` and `{5}` 5. First element of remaining Preorder in right part = `3` (root of right subtree) 6. Locate `3` in inorder right part → `{}` and `{6}` **Resulting tree matches our example above.** ✓ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Binary Tree Structure with Traversal Annotation ```mermaid graph TD node1["1
Root | L0"] node2["2
L-Child of 1"] node3["3
R-Child of 1"] node4["4
Leaf | L2"] node5["5
Leaf | L2"] node6["6
Leaf | L2"] node1 --> node2 node1 --> node3 node2 --> node4 node2 --> node5 node3 --> node6 ``` ## 4.2 Traversal Flow Architecture (Block-Level Functional Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD startA["Start: root != NULL"] --> checkA{"root NULL?"} checkA -- YES --> returnA["Return to Caller"] checkA -- NO --> visitA["VISIT root.data"] visitA --> recurLA["recurse LEFT subtree"] recurLA --> recurRA["recurse RIGHT subtree"] recurRA --> returnA ``` ## 4.3 Deletion Operation State Machine ```mermaid graph TD startB["Find Node to Delete"] --> checkB{"How many children?"} checkB -- ZERO --> caseB1["Case 1: Leaf - Simply Remove"] checkB -- ONE --> caseB2["Case 2: One Child - Replace with Child"] checkB -- TWO --> caseB3["Case 3: Two Children - Find Inorder Successor"] caseB3 --> caseB3a["Copy Successor Value to Node"] caseB3a --> caseB3b["Recursively Delete Successor"] caseB1 --> endB["Tree Updated"] caseB2 --> endB caseB3b --> endB ``` ## 4.4 Level Order Traversal (BFS with Queue) ```mermaid graph LR qInit["Enqueue Root: Q=[1]"] --> qStep1["Dequeue 1, Print 1, Enqueue 2,3: Q=[2,3]"] qStep1 --> qStep2["Dequeue 2, Print 2, Enqueue 4,5: Q=[3,4,5]"] qStep2 --> qStep3["Dequeue 3, Print 3, Enqueue 6: Q=[4,5,6]"] qStep3 --> qStep4["Dequeue 4, Print 4: Q=[5,6]"] qStep4 --> qStep5["Dequeue 5, Print 5: Q=[6]"] qStep5 --> qStep6["Dequeue 6, Print 6: Q=[]"] qStep6 --> qStop["Queue Empty: STOP"] ``` ## 4.5 Binary Tree Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD rootBT["Binary Tree"] --> strict["Full / Strict BT"] rootBT --> complete["Complete BT"] rootBT --> perfect["Perfect BT"] rootBT --> skewed["Skewed BT"] rootBT --> balanced["Balanced BT"] perfect --> complete strict --> perfect ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each node label is plain alphanumeric (no bold, no markdown). Mermaid parses these blocks directly. The flow arrows indicate the order of execution. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define a binary tree. List and briefly explain the four standard tree traversal techniques.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **binary tree** is a non-linear hierarchical data structure in which each node has at most two children, referred to as the **left child** and **right child**. It is formally defined recursively as a finite set of nodes that is either empty or consists of a root node with two disjoint binary subtrees (left and right). **The four standard traversals are:** 1. **Preorder (NLR):** Visit the root node first, then traverse the left subtree, then the right subtree. **Use:** Tree copy, prefix expression. 2. **Inorder (LNR):** Traverse the left subtree, visit the root, then traverse the right subtree. **Use:** Produces sorted output in a BST; infix expression. 3. **Postorder (LRN):** Traverse left subtree, right subtree, then visit root. **Use:** Tree deletion, postfix expression evaluation. 4. **Level Order (BFS):** Visit nodes level by level, left to right. **Use:** Implemented using a **queue**; shortest path in unweighted tree. > [!TIP] > **Valuation Tip (3-Mark Split):** Definition = 1 mark, listing 4 traversals = 1 mark, brief explanation/use = 1 mark. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is the difference between a complete binary tree and a full binary tree? Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Complete Binary Tree | Full (Strict) Binary Tree | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | All levels completely filled except possibly the last, which is filled **left to right** | Every node has **0 or 2 children** (no node has exactly 1 child) | | **Constraint on last level** | May be partially filled (left to right) | All leaves must be at the same level (if also perfect) | | **Example** | A heap structure | An expression tree with binary operators | **Example — Complete Binary Tree:** ``` 10 / \ 20 30 / \ 40 50 ``` **Example — Full Binary Tree:** ``` A / \ B C / \ D E ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "Complete" with "Full." Remember: A **Perfect** tree is both Complete AND Full. A Full tree is not necessarily Complete. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **one** full question (either OR choice), subdivided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]** **For the following binary tree, write the (i) Preorder, (ii) Inorder, (iii) Postorder, and (iv) Level Order traversals. Also compute the height of the tree.** ``` 50 / \ 30 70 / \ / \ 20 40 60 80 / 75 ``` **Model Solution:** **(i) Preorder (NLR):** Root, then left, then right at every node. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Trace:} \quad & 50 \to 30 \to 20 \to 40 \to 70 \to 60 \to 80 \to 75 \\ \therefore \quad \text{Preorder} &= \{50, 30, 20, 40, 70, 60, 80, 75\} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Inorder (LNR):** Left, root, right. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Trace:} \quad & 20 \to 30 \to 40 \to 50 \to 60 \to 70 \to 75 \to 80 \\ \therefore \quad \text{Inorder} &= \{20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 75, 80\} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Postorder (LRN):** Left, right, root. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Trace:} \quad & 20 \to 40 \to 30 \to 60 \to 75 \to 80 \to 70 \to 50 \\ \therefore \quad \text{Postorder} &= \{20, 40, 30, 60, 75, 80, 70, 50\} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Level Order (BFS with Queue):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Level 0:} \quad & \{50\} \\ \text{Level 1:} \quad & \{30, 70\} \\ \text{Level 2:} \quad & \{20, 40, 60, 80\} \\ \text{Level 3:} \quad & \{75\} \\ \therefore \quad \text{Level Order} &= \{50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80, 75\} \end{aligned} $$ **Height of the Tree:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(75) &= 0, \quad H(20) = H(40) = H(60) = 0 \\ H(80) &= \max(H(75), 0) + 1 = 1 \\ H(30) &= \max(0, 0) + 1 = 1 \\ H(70) &= \max(0, 1) + 1 = 2 \\ H(50) &= \max(1, 2) + 1 = 3 \\ \therefore \quad \text{Height} &= 3 \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall (Lose up to 2 marks):** Students often confuse **height** with **depth**. Height of the tree = 3 (number of edges on longest path from root to leaf). Number of levels = 4. Don't write height = 4 unless your convention defines height as number of levels. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]** **Explain the deletion operation in a Binary Search Tree. Handle all three cases with a suitable example.** **Model Solution:** Deletion in a Binary Search Tree (BST) is the most complex tree operation because it must preserve the **BST property**: for every node, all values in the left subtree are **less than** the node's value, and all values in the right subtree are **greater than** the node's value. **Three Cases of Deletion:** **Case 1: Deleting a Leaf Node (Node has 0 children)** - **Action:** Simply remove the node and set the parent's corresponding child pointer to `NULL`. - **Why simple:** No subtree restructuring needed. **Example:** Delete `40` from the tree above. Result: Node 30's right child becomes `NULL`. **Case 2: Deleting a Node with One Child** - **Action:** Replace the deleted node with its only child (link the node's parent directly to the child). - **Why:** The child already satisfies the BST property relative to the grandparent. **Example:** Delete `30` (which has only left child `20` after deleting 40). The parent `50` now points to `20` directly. **Case 3: Deleting a Node with Two Children (Most Complex)** - **Action:** 1. Find the **Inorder Successor** of the node (smallest value in the right subtree) **OR** the **Inorder Predecessor** (largest value in the left subtree). 2. **Copy** the successor/predecessor's value into the node to be deleted. 3. **Recursively delete** the successor/predecessor (which will be a Case 1 or Case 2 deletion). **Example:** Delete `70` from the original tree. - Inorder successor of `70` = `75` (smallest in right subtree). - Copy `75` into node `70`. - Delete original `75` node (Case 1 — leaf). **Resulting Tree:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{After Deleting 70:} \quad \text{Tree} = \{50, 30, 80, 20, 40, 60, 75\} \end{aligned} $$ **Why use Inorder Successor?** Because it is the **next larger value** in the BST, replacing the deleted node with it keeps the inorder sequence still sorted, preserving the BST invariant. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. Forgetting to **re-link** the parent pointer correctly in Case 2. > 2. Choosing the **wrong successor** (it must be the *leftmost* node of the right subtree, not just any right child). > 3. Not explaining **why** the BST property is maintained. --- ### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3]** **Write the recursive algorithm (pseudocode) to perform Preorder, Inorder, and Postorder traversals of a binary tree. Explain with a recursive call tree for the example tree used above.** **Model Solution:** **Pseudocode (C-style):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Algorithm: Recursive Traversals} \\ &\text{Input: root — pointer to the root node of the binary tree} \\ &\text{Output: Sequence of node values} \\ &--- \\ &\text{Procedure } \text{PREORDER}(root): \\ &\quad \text{if } root = \text{NULL} \text{ then return} \\ &\quad \text{print } root \to \text{data} \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \text{// N} \\ &\quad \text{PREORDER}(root \to \text{left}) \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{// L} \\ &\quad \text{PREORDER}(root \to \text{right}) \quad\quad\quad \text{// R} \\ &--- \\ &\text{Procedure } \text{INORDER}(root): \\ &\quad \text{if } root = \text{NULL} \text{ then return} \\ &\quad \text{INORDER}(root \to \text{left}) \quad\quad\quad\quad \text{// L} \\ &\quad \text{print } root \to \text{data} \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \text{// N} \\ &\quad \text{INORDER}(root \to \text{right}) \quad\quad\quad \text{// R} \\ &--- \\ &\text{Procedure } \text{POSTORDER}(root): \\ &\quad \text{if } root = \text{NULL} \text{ then return} \\ &\quad \text{POSTORDER}(root \to \text{left}) \quad\quad\quad \text{// L} \\ &\quad \text{POSTORDER}(root \to \text{right}) \quad\quad \text{// R} \\ &\quad \text{print } root \to \text{data} \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \text{// N} \end{aligned} $$ **Recursive Call Tree for Preorder of our example tree (root = 50):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{PREORDER}(50) \\ &\quad \rightarrow \text{print } 50 \\ &\quad \rightarrow \text{PREORDER}(30) \\ &\quad\quad \rightarrow \text{print } 30 \\ &\quad\quad \rightarrow \text{PREORDER}(20) \rightarrow \text{print } 20 \rightarrow \text{return} \\ &\quad\quad \rightarrow \text{PREORDER}(40) \rightarrow \text{print } 40 \rightarrow \text{return} \\ &\quad \rightarrow \text{PREORDER}(70) \\ &\quad\quad \rightarrow \text{print } 70 \\ &\quad\quad \rightarrow \text{PREORDER}(60) \rightarrow \text{print } 60 \rightarrow \text{return} \\ &\quad\quad \rightarrow \text{PREORDER}(80) \\ &\quad\quad\quad \rightarrow \text{print } 80 \\ &\quad\quad\quad \rightarrow \text{PREORDER}(75) \rightarrow \text{print } 75 \rightarrow \text{return} \\ &\quad\quad\quad \rightarrow \text{return} \\ &\quad \rightarrow \text{return} \\ &\text{Final Output: } 50, 30, 20, 40, 70, 60, 80, 75 \end{aligned} $$ **Time Complexity Analysis:** Each node is visited exactly once, and the work done at each node is $O(1)$. Therefore, $T(N) = 2T(N/2) + O(1)$ in a balanced tree. By the Master Theorem: $$ \begin{aligned} T(N) &= O(N) \quad \text{for all three traversals} \end{aligned} $$ **Space Complexity:** $O(h)$ due to recursion stack, where $h$ is the height of the tree. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3]** **Construct a binary tree from the given traversals. Preorder: `{1, 2, 4, 5, 3, 6, 7}`, Inorder: `{4, 2, 5, 1, 6, 3, 7}`. Show every step.** **Model Solution:** **Algorithm Used:** First element of Preorder is always the **root** of the (sub)tree. Locating the root in Inorder splits the tree into left and right subtrees. Then repeat recursively. **Step 1:** - Preorder[0] = `1` → **Root of entire tree = 1** - Locate `1` in Inorder at index 3 → Left inorder = `{4, 2, 5}`, Right inorder = `{6, 3, 7}` **Step 2 (Left Subtree of 1):** - Inorder left = `{4, 2, 5}` has 3 elements → next 3 preorder elements are `{2, 4, 5}` - Preorder[1] = `2` → **Left child of 1 = 2** - Locate `2` in `{4, 2, 5}` at index 1 → Left = `{4}`, Right = `{5}` **Step 3 (Subtrees of 2):** - `4` has no left/right subtree members → becomes **leaf** (left child of 2) - `5` has no left/right subtree members → becomes **leaf** (right child of 2) **Step 4 (Right Subtree of 1):** - Inorder right = `{6, 3, 7}` has 3 elements → next 3 preorder elements are `{3, 6, 7}` - Preorder[4] = `3` → **Right child of 1 = 3** - Locate `3` in `{6, 3, 7}` at index 1 → Left = `{6}`, Right = `{7}` **Step 5 (Subtrees of 3):** - `6` → **left child of 3** (leaf) - `7` → **right child of 3** (leaf) **Final Constructed Tree:** ``` 1 / \ 2 3 / \ / \ 4 5 6 7 ``` **Verification:** - Preorder: 1, 2, 4, 5, 3, 6, 7 ✓ - Inorder: 4, 2, 5, 1, 6, 3, 7 ✓ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall (Lose up to 3 marks):** > 1. Writing the tree without **showing the construction steps** — KTU examiners require step-by-step derivation. > 2. Confusing the **left and right child placement** based on inorder position. > 3. Stating that two traversals are *sufficient* when they are not — **Postorder + Inorder** also works, but **Preorder + Postorder** is **ambiguous** and cannot uniquely determine the tree. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3** - ⭐ **Definition:** Binary tree is recursive: $T = \emptyset$ or $T = \{Root, T_L, T_R\}$ - ⭐ **Tree with $N$ nodes has $N-1$ edges** and **$N+1$ NULL pointers** (including the children of leaves) - ⭐ **Maximum nodes** at level $i$ = $2^i$; **maximum total** = $2^{h+1} - 1$ - ⭐ **Minimum height** of tree with $N$ nodes = $\lfloor \log_2 N \rfloor$; **maximum height** = $N - 1$ - ⭐ **4 Traversals:** Preorder (NLR), Inorder (LNR), Postorder (LRN), Level Order (BFS using Queue) - ⭐ **Pre/In/Postorder use recursion** (implicit stack); **Level Order uses an explicit Queue** - ⭐ **Time complexity** of all traversals = $O(N)$ — each node visited exactly once - ⭐ **Inorder of a BST gives sorted output** — most-asked property - ⭐ **Deletion has 3 cases:** Leaf, One child, Two children (use Inorder Successor/Predecessor) - ⭐ **Tree construction requires Preorder+Inorder OR Postorder+Inorder** — never Preorder+Postorder alone - ⭐ **Height recurrence:** $H = \max(H_L, H_R) + 1$, with $H(\text{null}) = -1$ - ⭐ **Node count recurrence:** $C = C_L + C_R + 1$ - ⭐ **Mirror operation:** swap left and right child pointers recursively at every node - ⭐ **Real-world uses:** Expression parsing, decision trees, file systems, B-trees in DB indexing - ⭐ **KTU exam tip:** Always show **step-by-step recursion trace** for traversal problems; drawing the recursion call tree fetches full marks - ⭐ **Memory:** Recursion stack space = $O(h)$, worst case $O(N)$ for skewed tree

topics.title: Binary Tree Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Binary Tree Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Binary Tree** is a hierarchical, non-linear data structure in which each node has **at most two children**, designated as the **left child** and the **right child**. **Binary Tree Representation** refers to the systematic method of storing the nodes of a binary tree in the computer's memory so that parent-child relationships and traversal operations can be executed efficiently.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, binary trees are formally classified under two principal storage paradigms:

1. **Sequential (Array-Based) Representation** — Stores tree nodes in contiguous memory locations using index-based mapping rules.
2. **Linked (Pointer-Based) Representation** — Stores tree nodes as dynamically allocated objects connected via pointer references.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT522 / Module 3):** Students must be able to *represent* a binary tree using **both array and linked structures**, derive the index mapping formulae, and analyze the **memory trade-offs** between the two schemes. This is a **high-weightage topic** for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Family Tree**
> Imagine a family lineage chart of royalty. The **king (or queen)** sits at the top — this is the **root node**. The king has at most **two direct heirs** — the **left heir** and the **right heir** (binary constraint). Each heir, in turn, may have up to two of *their own* heirs, forming branches. A person with no heirs is a **leaf node**. The generations below the root represent **levels**, and the longest chain of descent from a node to a leaf is its **height**.
>
> Now, to record this family tree in a register, you could either:
> - Write names in a **numbered ledger** (each name gets a row number, and the children of the person in row *i* must always go into specific rows 2*i* and 2*i*+1) — this is the **array representation**.
> - Or give each person a **card** with their name, a pointer to their left heir, and a pointer to their right heir — this is the **linked representation**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Root** | The topmost node of the tree with no parent. |
| **Node** | A fundamental unit holding data and links to children. |
| **Parent** | A node that has one or two child nodes below it. |
| **Child** | A node that descends from a parent. |
| **Leaf / Terminal Node** | A node with **zero** children. |
| **Internal Node** | A node with **at least one** child. |
| **Degree of a Node** | Number of children the node has (0, 1, or 2). |
| **Degree of a Tree** | Maximum degree among all nodes (for binary tree = 2). |
| **Depth of a Node** | Length of the path from the **root** to that node. |
| **Height of a Tree** | Length of the longest path from the **root** to a **leaf**. |
| **Level** | All nodes at the same depth belong to the same level. |
| **Subtree** | A tree formed by a node and all its descendants. |
| **Edge** | A connecting line between a parent and a child. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric — Maximum Nodes:** A binary tree of height *h* (root at height 0) can contain at most **2^(h+1) − 1** nodes (the *perfect* or *complete* tree configuration). This is one of the most frequently tested identities in KTU exams.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Method 1 — Sequential (Array-Based) Representation

In the array representation, all nodes of the binary tree are stored in a **one-dimensional array** in a *level-order* (breadth-first) sequence. The position of each node is **not arbitrary**; it is governed by a deterministic mapping rule that allows us to compute the indices of a node's parent and children directly from its own index.

#### 2.1.1 The Indexing Rule (1-Based Indexing)

Let the root of the tree be stored at index **1** of the array. Then for any node stored at index *i* (where *i* ≥ 1):

- The **left child** of the node at index *i* is stored at index **2*i*.
- The **right child** of the node at index *i* is stored at index **2*i + 1**.
- The **parent** of the node at index *i* is stored at index **⌊i / 2⌋** (the floor of *i*/2).

#### 2.1.2 The Indexing Rule (0-Based Indexing)

Some C/C++ implementations store the root at index **0** of the array. In that convention:

- The **left child** of the node at index *i* is stored at index **2*i + 1**.
- The **right child** of the node at index *i* is stored at index **2*i + 2**.
- The **parent** of the node at index *i* is stored at index **⌊(i − 1) / 2⌋**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always explicitly mention which indexing convention (0-based or 1-based) you are using in your answer. Failing to do so often costs **1 mark** in the University Exam.

#### 2.1.3 Why Does the Indexing Rule Work? (The "Why" Behind It)

The mapping arises from the **natural binary enumeration of tree levels**:

- The **root** occupies position 1 (level 0 — 2⁰ = 1 node).
- **Level 1** has 2¹ = 2 nodes, occupying positions 2 and 3.
- **Level 2** has 2² = 4 nodes, occupying positions 4, 5, 6, and 7.
- **Level *k*** has 2^*k* nodes, occupying positions 2^*k* through 2^(*k*+1) − 1.

Because every level is contiguous, the children of a node at index *i* simply occupy the next two available slots (2*i* and 2*i*+1) in the natural left-to-right numbering.

#### 2.1.4 Drawbacks of Array Representation

- **Memory Wastage in Skewed Trees:** A right-skewed (or left-skewed) tree of *n* nodes still requires an array of size approximately 2^*n* (since the array indices stretch all the way to 2^*n* − 1). For *n* = 10, a skewed tree wastes roughly **1014 of 1023 slots (≈ 99%)**.
- **Fixed Size:** The maximum tree size must be known in advance; resizing is expensive.
- **Insertion / Deletion Overhead:** Adding or removing nodes in the middle requires shifting array elements.

> [!WARNING]
> A right-skewed binary tree stored in array representation exhibits the **worst-case space complexity of O(2^n)**, while holding only *n* actual data items. This is the canonical KTU exam question to test your understanding of array-representation trade-offs.

---

### 2.2 Method 2 — Linked (Pointer-Based) Representation

The linked representation models the tree as a collection of **dynamically allocated nodes**, where each node contains three fields:

1. **DATA field** — stores the actual element/value.
2. **LCHILD (Left Pointer)** — points to the left subtree (or NULL if absent).
3. **RCHILD (Right Pointer)** — points to the right subtree (or NULL if absent).

A separate **pointer variable ROOT** stores the address of the root node. If the tree is empty, ROOT = NULL.

#### 2.2.1 C-Style Structure Declaration

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* lchild;
    struct Node* rchild;
};
```

A pointer variable of type `struct Node*` called `ROOT` is maintained globally to anchor the tree in memory.

#### 2.2.2 Advantages of Linked Representation

- **No Memory Wastage:** Memory is allocated only for nodes that exist — efficient even for skewed trees (uses O(n) space).
- **Dynamic Size:** The tree can grow or shrink at runtime without pre-declaration of size.
- **Natural Fit for Recursive Operations:** Traversal, insertion, and deletion algorithms are cleaner.

#### 2.2.3 Disadvantages of Linked Representation

- **Extra Memory for Pointers:** Each node carries two pointer fields (8 bytes on a 64-bit system, plus the data field). For small data types, this is significant overhead.
- **No Random Access:** To find the *i*-th node, you must traverse from the root; there is no direct indexing.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **condensed cheat sheet** of all indexing, count, and complexity formulae tested in KTU exams for the binary-tree representation topic. Memorize these before every test.

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max nodes at level *k* (root = level 0) | $N_{\text{level}}(k) = 2^{k}$ | Each level doubles in capacity |
| 2 | Max nodes in tree of height *h* | $N_{\text{max}}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1$ | Total slots in a perfect tree |
| 3 | Max height for *n* nodes | $H_{\text{max}}(n) = n - 1$ | Achieved by a skewed tree |
| 4 | Min height for *n* nodes | $H_{\text{min}}(n) = \lfloor \log_{2} n \rfloor$ | Achieved by a complete tree |
| 5 | Left child index (1-based) | $\text{LC}(i) = 2i$ | Where to find left child |
| 6 | Right child index (1-based) | $\text{RC}(i) = 2i + 1$ | Where to find right child |
| 7 | Parent index (1-based) | $\text{Parent}(i) = \lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$ | Reverse mapping |
| 8 | Left child index (0-based) | $\text{LC}_{0}(i) = 2i + 1$ | C/C++ array convention |
| 9 | Right child index (0-based) | $\text{RC}_{0}(i) = 2i + 2$ | C/C++ array convention |
| 10 | Parent index (0-based) | $\text{Parent}_{0}(i) = \lfloor (i-1) / 2 \rfloor$ | Reverse mapping in 0-based |
| 11 | Array size needed for *n*-node tree | $S = 2^{n}$ (skewed worst case) | Memory budget for sequential storage |
| 12 | Space complexity — Array (skewed) | $O(2^{n})$ | Catastrophic in worst case |
| 13 | Space complexity — Array (complete) | $O(n)$ | Optimal for balanced trees |
| 14 | Space complexity — Linked | $O(n)$ | Always optimal in node count |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Array representation** is the foundation of the **Binary Heap** data structure used in **priority queues, Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm, and Heap Sort**.
- **Linked representation** underpins the **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** in compilers, **DOM tree** in web browsers, **decision trees** in machine learning, and **B-tree variants** in database indexing.
- Choosing the right representation is a recurring **systems design decision** in operating-system kernels (process scheduling), embedded systems (fixed memory budgets), and game engines (scene-graph rendering).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: From Binary Tree to Array (1-Based Indexing)

Consider the following binary tree, with **root = A**, where A has a left child **B** and a right child **C**. Node B has a right child **D**. Node C has a left child **E**.

We will map this tree step by step into an array.

**Step 1 — Identify the root.** The root is A. By the 1-based indexing rule, the root occupies **index 1**.

**Step 2 — Identify the children of the root (index 1).**
- Left child of A is at index 2*1 = **2** → store B.
- Right child of A is at index 2*1 + 1 = **3** → store C.

**Step 3 — Identify the children of B (index 2).**
- Left child of B is at index 2*2 = **4** → B has no left child, so this slot remains **empty / NULL**.
- Right child of B is at index 2*2 + 1 = **5** → store D.

**Step 4 — Identify the children of C (index 3).**
- Left child of C is at index 2*3 = **6** → store E.
- Right child of C is at index 2*3 + 1 = **7** → C has no right child, so this slot remains **empty / NULL**.

**Step 5 — Identify the children of D (index 5) and E (index 6).**
- D (index 5) has no children, so indices 10 and 11 are empty.
- E (index 6) has no children, so indices 12 and 13 are empty.

The final array (1-based, using '-' to denote empty slots) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Array}[1..13] \;=\; [\,A,\; B,\; C,\; -,\; D,\; E,\; -\,]
\end{aligned}
$$

Explicitly, slot by slot:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Array}[1] &= A \\
\text{Array}[2] &= B \\
\text{Array}[3] &= C \\
\text{Array}[4] &= \text{NULL} \\
\text{Array}[5] &= D \\
\text{Array}[6] &= E \\
\text{Array}[7] &= \text{NULL}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are indices 4 and 7 empty?** Even though B has no left child and C has no right child, the *array slots* reserved for them at indices 4 and 7 must still be allocated. This is precisely the "memory wastage" issue we discussed — for sparse or skewed trees, many slots stay empty.

---

### 3.2 Reverse Mapping: From Array to Binary Tree

Given the array $\text{Array}[1..7] = [A, B, C, -, D, E, -]$, we can reconstruct the tree as follows.

**Step 1** — Place the root at **index 1** → node A.

**Step 2** — For index 1, look at indices 2*1 = 2 and 2*1+1 = 3 → place B as A's left child and C as A's right child.

**Step 3** — For index 2 (B), look at indices 4 and 5 → index 4 is empty (so B has no left child); index 5 is D → make D the right child of B.

**Step 4** — For index 3 (C), look at indices 6 and 7 → index 6 is E → make E the left child of C; index 7 is empty (so C has no right child).

**Step 5** — For index 5 (D) and index 6 (E), their children indices (10, 11 and 12, 13) are out of the valid range, so they are leaves.

This reproduces exactly the original tree.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Parent-Index Formula

**Claim:** The parent of the node at index *i* (for *i* ≥ 2) is at index $\lfloor i / 2 \rfloor$.

**Proof by Case Analysis:**

**Case 1: *i* is even.** Suppose *i* = 2*k* for some positive integer *k*. Then *i* = 2*k* is the **left child** of the node at index *k* (by the left-child rule). Therefore the parent of *i* is *k* = *i*/2. Since *i* is even, $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor = i/2 = k$. The formula holds.

**Case 2: *i* is odd.** Suppose *i* = 2*k* + 1 for some positive integer *k*. Then *i* = 2*k* + 1 is the **right child** of the node at index *k* (by the right-child rule). Therefore the parent of *i* is *k* = (*i* − 1)/2. Since *i* is odd, $(i-1)/2$ is an integer and $\lfloor i/2 \rfloor = \lfloor (2k+1)/2 \rfloor = k$. The formula holds.

Since these two cases cover all integers *i* ≥ 2, the formula $\text{Parent}(i) = \lfloor i/2 \rfloor$ is **proven**.

---

### 3.4 Memory-Wastage Calculation for a Skewed Tree

Consider a **right-skewed** binary tree of *n* nodes. Each node has a right child but no left child, forming a chain. We want to compute the array size required.

**Step 1** — The root is at index 1. The first (and only) right child is at index 2*1 + 1 = **3**. Its right child is at index 2*3 + 1 = **7**. Its right child is at index 2*7 + 1 = **15**.

**Step 2** — Generalizing, the *j*-th node (1-indexed) of the right-skewed chain occupies index $2^{j} - 1$.

**Step 3** — Therefore, the *n*-th node occupies index $2^{n} - 1$, which means the array must have at least $2^{n} - 1$ slots.

**Step 4** — Of these $2^{n} - 1$ slots, only *n* contain actual data. The number of **empty slots** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Empty slots} &= (2^{n} - 1) - n = 2^{n} - n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5** — The **wastage ratio** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Wastage} &= \frac{2^{n} - n - 1}{2^{n} - 1} \times 100\% \;\xrightarrow{\;n \to \infty\;}\; 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This means that for large skewed trees, the array representation wastes **nearly all** allocated memory — a striking demonstration of why linked representation is preferred for sparse or unbalanced trees.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The Original Binary Tree (Logical Structure)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the example tree from Section 3 (A is root, with B and C as children; B has right child D; C has left child E).

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["A - ROOT"]
    nodeB["B - Left of A"]
    nodeC["C - Right of A"]
    nodeD["D - Right child of B"]
    nodeE["E - Left child of C"]
    emptyL["NULL - Left of B"]
    emptyR["NULL - Right of C"]

    rootA --- nodeB
    rootA --- nodeC
    nodeB --- emptyL
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeE
    nodeC --- emptyR
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Array Mapping Block Diagram (1-Based Indexing)

This block diagram visualizes how the same tree is flattened into a contiguous array. The *Index Block* holds addresses 1 through 7, and the *Value Block* stores the corresponding node data (or NULL for empty slots).

```mermaid
graph LR
    idx1["Index 1"] --- val1["A - ROOT NODE"]
    idx2["Index 2"] --- val2["B - Left of A"]
    idx3["Index 3"] --- val3["C - Right of A"]
    idx4["Index 4"] --- val4["NULL - Slot Empty"]
    idx5["Index 5"] --- val5["D - Right of B"]
    idx6["Index 6"] --- val6["E - Left of C"]
    idx7["Index 7"] --- val7["NULL - Slot Empty"]

    idx1 -. "Parent of 2 and 3" .-> idx2
    idx1 -. "Parent of 2 and 3" .-> idx3
    idx2 -. "Parent of 4 and 5" .-> idx4
    idx2 -. "Parent of 4 and 5" .-> idx5
    idx3 -. "Parent of 6 and 7" .-> idx6
    idx3 -. "Parent of 6 and 7" .-> idx7
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Linked Representation — Node Architecture

This block diagram shows the structure of a single linked node and how multiple nodes interlock to form a tree.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeField["DATA FIELD - Stores the value"]
    lptrField["LCHILD POINTER - Address of left subtree"]
    rptrField["RCHILD POINTER - Address of right subtree"]

    nodeField --- lptrField
    lptrField --- rptrField

    note1["Root pointer variable ROOT points to the topmost node"]
    note2["Each lchild or rchild is NULL if that child does not exist"]
    note3["Memory is allocated dynamically using malloc in C or new in C plus plus"]

    rptrField --- note1
    rptrField --- note2
    rptrField --- note3
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Comparison Flowchart — Array vs Linked Representation

```mermaid
graph TD
    startQ{"Is the tree balanced and its maximum size known at compile time?"}
    pathArray["Use ARRAY REPRESENTATION - O of n space, O of 1 random access, fast level order traversal"]
    pathLinked["Use LINKED REPRESENTATION - O of n space, O of n access by position, no size limit, dynamic insertion deletion"]

    startQ -- YES --> pathArray
    startQ -- NO --> pathLinked
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5: Indexing Formula Application Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    rootI["Node at Index i"]
    lcI["Left Child at Index 2i"]
    rcI["Right Child at Index 2i plus 1"]
    pI["Parent at Index floor of i divided by 2"]

    rootI -- "Apply left child rule" --> lcI
    rootI -- "Apply right child rule" --> rcI
    rootI -- "Apply parent rule" --> pI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams?** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **visual-spatial understanding** under Outcome-Based Education (OBE). In the End Semester Exam, a well-drawn tree + array diagram typically earns **2 to 3 extra marks** over a purely textual answer. The above diagrams are designed to be directly reproducible in your answer script.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

Define a binary tree. List and briefly explain any **two** methods of representing a binary tree in memory.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **binary tree** is a non-linear hierarchical data structure in which each node has at most two children, referred to as the **left child** and the **right child**.
>
> The two principal methods of representing a binary tree in memory are:
>
> **1. Sequential (Array) Representation:** The nodes of the binary tree are stored in a one-dimensional array using level-order numbering. For a node stored at index *i*, the left child is at **2*i*, the right child is at **2*i* + 1**, and the parent is at **⌊*i* / 2⌋**. This representation is memory-efficient only for **complete or nearly-complete** trees.
>
> **2. Linked (Pointer) Representation:** Each node is a structure containing three fields — a **DATA** field, a pointer to the **left child (LCHILD)**, and a pointer to the **right child (RCHILD)**. A pointer variable `ROOT` anchors the tree. This representation is dynamic, wastes no memory for skewed trees, and supports arbitrary insertion and deletion.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two methods with formula/structure — total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]**

For a binary tree of height *h* (root at height 0), what is the **maximum number of nodes**? Justify your answer.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height *h* is **2^(h+1) − 1**.
>
> **Justification:** At level *k* (where *k* = 0, 1, 2, ..., *h*), the maximum number of nodes is **2^*k***. This is because each node at level *k* can have at most 2 children, doubling the node count at each subsequent level. Summing across all levels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{max}}(h) &= \sum_{k=0}^{h} 2^{k} = 2^{0} + 2^{1} + 2^{2} + \cdots + 2^{h} = 2^{h+1} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> The summation uses the geometric-series identity $\sum_{k=0}^{h} r^{k} = \dfrac{r^{h+1} - 1}{r - 1}$ with $r = 2$.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for stating the formula, 1 mark for the derivation/summation, 1 mark for the geometric-series substitution — total 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Draw a binary tree for the expression $\;((A + B) \;\times\; C) - (D / (E - F))\;$ and represent it using an **array**. Show the array indices and explain the indexing rule used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the **array representation** and the **linked representation** of a binary tree under the following heads: (i) Memory utilization, (ii) Access time for a node, (iii) Suitability for skewed trees, (iv) Ease of insertion/deletion.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Parse the expression into a binary expression tree.**

The fully-parenthesized infix expression is parsed such that:
- Each **operator** becomes an **internal node**.
- Each **operand** becomes a **leaf node**.
- The **root** is the operator with the **lowest precedence** that is the principal binding operator.

Parsing $((A + B) \times C) - (D / (E - F))$:
- The outermost operator is **subtraction (−)**, which is the root.
- The left subtree of root represents $((A + B) \times C)$:
  - Its root is the **multiplication (×)** operator.
  - Left child: subtree of $(A + B)$, rooted at **addition (+)** with children A and B.
  - Right child: leaf **C**.
- The right subtree of root represents $(D / (E - F))$:
  - Its root is the **division (/)** operator.
  - Left child: leaf **D**.
  - Right child: subtree of $(E - F)$, rooted at **subtraction (−)** with children E and F.

**Step 2 — Apply level-order indexing (1-based).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Index 1} &= - \quad \text{(root, subtraction)} \\
\text{Index 2} &= \times \quad \text{(left child of root)} \\
\text{Index 3} &= / \quad \text{(right child of root)} \\
\text{Index 4} &= + \quad \text{(left child of ×)} \\
\text{Index 5} &= C \quad \text{(right child of ×)} \\
\text{Index 6} &= D \quad \text{(left child of /)} \\
\text{Index 7} &= - \quad \text{(right child of /)} \\
\text{Index 8} &= A \quad \text{(left child of +)} \\
\text{Index 9} &= B \quad \text{(right child of +)} \\
\text{Index 10} &= \text{NULL} \quad \text{(left child of C)} \\
\text{Index 11} &= \text{NULL} \quad \text{(right child of C)} \\
\text{Index 12} &= \text{NULL} \quad \text{(left child of D)} \\
\text{Index 13} &= \text{NULL} \quad \text{(right child of D)} \\
\text{Index 14} &= E \quad \text{(left child of inner −)} \\
\text{Index 15} &= F \quad \text{(right child of inner −)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — State the indexing rule used.**

> "For any node at index *i*, the left child is at index 2*i* and the right child is at index 2*i* + 1. The parent of the node at index *i* is at index ⌊*i*/2⌋."

*(Mark Distribution: 3 marks for correct expression tree, 2 marks for array mapping with all indices, 1 mark for state-of-the-rule, 1 mark for clarity/presentation — total 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

| Comparison Head | Array Representation | Linked Representation |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Memory Utilization | Wastes memory for skewed trees (O(2^n) slots for an *n*-node skewed tree); efficient for complete trees. | Uses exactly *n* nodes with two pointers each; no wastage regardless of tree shape. |
| (ii) Access Time for a Node | **O(1)** random access by index — you can directly go to the *i*-th slot. | **O(h)** — must traverse from the root down the path to the desired node. |
| (iii) Suitability for Skewed Trees | **Highly unsuitable** — wastes up to 99% of allocated slots. | **Highly suitable** — memory is O(n). |
| (iv) Ease of Insertion / Deletion | **Difficult** — requires shifting array elements and may need resizing. | **Easy** — just adjust the relevant LCHILD / RCHILD pointers. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Array representation is preferred for **static, complete binary trees** (e.g., binary heaps). Linked representation is preferred for **dynamic, sparse, or skewed trees** (e.g., BSTs, ASTs).

*(Mark Distribution: Roughly 1.5 to 2 marks per comparison head, plus 1 mark for the conclusion — total 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Consider the binary tree whose **level-order traversal** yields the sequence: **50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80**. (i) Draw the tree. (ii) Represent the tree using an **array** and write down the array contents with indices. (iii) State the index of the right child of the node with data 30 and the parent of the node with data 60.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Define a **node structure** for the linked representation of a binary tree in C. Write the algorithm/pseudocode to perform **preorder traversal** of a binary tree using this linked structure and explain with a small example.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Reconstruct the tree from level-order.**

The level-order sequence gives us nodes level by level, left to right:
- Level 0: **50** (root)
- Level 1: **30** (left of 50), **70** (right of 50)
- Level 2: **20** (left of 30), **40** (right of 30), **60** (left of 70), **80** (right of 70)

**Step 2 — Draw the tree.**

```
                  50
                /    \
              30      70
             /  \    /  \
           20   40  60   80
```

**Step 3 — Build the array (1-based indexing).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Array}[1] &= 50 \\
\text{Array}[2] &= 30 \\
\text{Array}[3] &= 70 \\
\text{Array}[4] &= 20 \\
\text{Array}[5] &= 40 \\
\text{Array}[6] &= 60 \\
\text{Array}[7] &= 80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Answer the specific sub-questions.**

- **Right child of node 30 (index 2):** Right child is at index 2*2 + 1 = **5**, which holds **40**.
- **Parent of node 60 (index 6):** Parent is at index ⌊6/2⌋ = **3**, which holds **70**.
