*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks tree drawing, 2 marks array mapping, 1.5 marks for each specific sub-question — total 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the node structure in C.**

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* lchild;
    struct Node* rchild;
};
```

A global pointer `struct Node* ROOT` anchors the tree.

**Step 2 — Preorder Traversal Algorithm.**

Preorder traversal visits nodes in the order: **Root → Left → Right**.

```c
void preorder(struct Node* root) {
    if (root == NULL)
        return;
    printf("%d ", root->data);   // Visit root
    preorder(root->lchild);      // Traverse left subtree
    preorder(root->rchild);      // Traverse right subtree
}
```

**Step 3 — Worked example.**

Using the tree from part (a):

```
                  50
                /    \
              30      70
             /  \    /  \
           20   40  60   80
```

**Trace of preorder(root):**
- Visit **50** → output: 50
- Recurse into left subtree (rooted at 30):
  - Visit **30** → output: 50, 30
  - Recurse into left subtree (rooted at 20):
    - Visit **20** → output: 50, 30, 20
    - Both children NULL, return.
  - Recurse into right subtree (rooted at 40):
    - Visit **40** → output: 50, 30, 20, 40
    - Both children NULL, return.
- Recurse into right subtree (rooted at 70):
  - Visit **70** → output: 50, 30, 20, 40, 70
  - Recurse into left subtree (rooted at 60):
    - Visit **60** → output: 50, 30, 20, 40, 70, 60
  - Recurse into right subtree (rooted at 80):
    - Visit **80** → output: 50, 30, 20, 40, 70, 60, 80

**Final preorder output:** 50, 30, 20, 40, 70, 60, 80.

*(Mark Distribution: 1.5 marks for structure definition, 2 marks for correct algorithm, 1.5 marks for trace mechanics, 2 marks for the worked example — total 7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Before You Write the Exam):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the indexing convention** — Always write *either* "1-based" or "0-based" before quoting a formula. Generic statements like "left child is at 2*i*" lose **1 mark** if the convention is not declared.
>
> 2. **Confusing the level of the root** — Some textbooks set the root at level 0, others at level 1. The KTU 2024 convention is **root at level 0**, height measured as the number of edges from the root to the deepest leaf. Writing inconsistent level numbers in different sub-parts confuses the examiner.
>
> 3. **Not drawing the NULL slots in the array diagram** — When drawing the array, examiners expect you to *explicitly mark* which indices are empty (write "−" or "NULL" inside the box). An array showing only the populated cells looks incomplete.
>
> 4. **Mixing up array-index and linked-pointer conventions** — Students sometimes write "left child at 2*i* + 1" while simultaneously citing 1-based indexing (where it should be 2*i*). This is a very common error that costs **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to mention "root pointer variable ROOT"** in linked representation — A complete answer must declare that `ROOT` is a global pointer pointing to the topmost node.
>
> 6. **Not justifying memory wastage calculation** — If the question asks why the array representation is unsuitable for skewed trees, you *must* include a numerical example (e.g., "for *n* = 10, array size = 2¹⁰ − 1 = 1023 slots, of which 1013 are empty") to earn full marks.
>
> 7. **Recursion base case omission** — In traversal algorithms, always write the base case (`if (root == NULL) return;`) explicitly. Examiners deduct marks for missing it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**
>
> ✅ **Core Definition:** Binary tree = each node has at most 2 children (left and right).
>
> ✅ **Two Representations:** (1) Sequential / Array (2) Linked / Pointer.
>
> ✅ **1-Based Indexing Rules:** LC = 2*i*, RC = 2*i* + 1, Parent = ⌊*i*/2⌋.
>
> ✅ **0-Based Indexing Rules:** LC = 2*i* + 1, RC = 2*i* + 2, Parent = ⌊(*i* − 1)/2⌋.
>
> ✅ **Max Nodes Formula:** $N_{\text{max}}(h) = 2^{h+1} - 1$ for a tree of height *h* (root at height 0).
>
> ✅ **Max Nodes at Level *k*:** $2^{k}$ (root at level 0).
>
> ✅ **Max Height for *n* nodes:** *n* − 1 (achieved by skewed tree).
>
> ✅ **Min Height for *n* nodes:** ⌊log₂ *n*⌋ (achieved by complete tree).
>
> ✅ **Array Wastage:** Skewed tree of *n* nodes needs $2^{n} - 1$ array slots → ~99% waste.
>
> ✅ **Linked Node Structure:** 3 fields → DATA, LCHILD pointer, RCHILD pointer.
>
> ✅ **Root Anchor:** A pointer variable `ROOT` points to the topmost node; if `ROOT == NULL`, tree is empty.
>
> ✅ **Trade-off Summary:** Array = fast random access, wastes memory for skewed trees. Linked = no wastage, no random access, dynamic size.
>
> ✅ **Cautions:** Always declare indexing convention, draw NULL slots, justify numerical examples, and include recursion base case in traversal code.
>
> ✅ **Real-world uses:** Heaps (array), BSTs / ASTs / DOM (linked).
>
> ✅ **Tree Traversals (Next Module Hook):** Preorder (Root-L-R), Inorder (L-Root-R), Postorder (L-R-Root), Level-order (BFS).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tree Traversals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tree Traversals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tree Traversal** is the process of visiting each node in a tree data structure exactly once in a systematic, well-defined order. For a **Binary Tree**, the four canonical traversal strategies mandated by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are:
> 1. **Inorder Traversal** (Left $\rightarrow$ Root $\rightarrow$ Right)
> 2. **Preorder Traversal** (Root $\rightarrow$ Left $\rightarrow$ Right)
> 3. **Postorder Traversal** (Left $\rightarrow$ Right $\rightarrow$ Root)
> 4. **Level Order Traversal** (Breadth-First / Top-to-Bottom, Left-to-Right)

A traversal is classified as a **Depth-First Search (DFS)** if it explores as deep as possible down one branch before backtracking (Inorder, Preorder, Postorder), and as a **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** if it explores all nodes at the current depth before moving deeper (Level Order).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **postal delivery person** in a city where houses are arranged in a **tree-like neighborhood**:

- **Preorder** = You visit a house, **drop the parcel**, then visit the **left neighbor first**, then the **right neighbor**. *(Visit yourself first → "Pre" = before children)*
- **Inorder** = You go all the way **left**, drop the parcel, come back to **yourself**, drop, then go **right**. *(Visit yourself "in between" left and right children)*
- **Postorder** = You tell your **left neighbor** to deliver first, then your **right neighbor**, and you **deliver your own parcel last**. *(Visit yourself "after" children → "Post")*
- **Level Order** = You deliver to **everyone on the same floor/street first**, then move to the **next floor**. *(Layer-by-layer, like flood-fill)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Tip:** The position of the keyword **"Root"** in the rule (Left-Root-Right, Root-Left-Right, Left-Right-Root) tells you *when* the root node is processed — first, middle, or last — giving you the traversal's name.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Bounds

- **Time Complexity:** $\Theta(n)$ — every node is visited exactly once.
- **Space Complexity (Recursive):** $\Theta(h)$ where $h$ is the height of the tree (call stack depth).
- **Space Complexity (Level Order):** $\Theta(w)$ where $w$ is the maximum width of the tree (queue size).
- **Worst-case height** for a skewed tree: $h = n - 1$.
- **Balanced tree height:** $h = \Theta(\log n)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Traversals — Operational Logic

### A. Inorder Traversal (LNR — Left, Node, Right)
1. Recursively traverse the **left subtree**.
2. **Visit the root node** (process / print its data).
3. Recursively traverse the **right subtree**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters:** For a **Binary Search Tree (BST)**, Inorder traversal produces nodes in **strictly ascending sorted order**. This property is heavily tested in KTU exams.

### B. Preorder Traversal (NLR — Node, Left, Right)
1. **Visit the root node** first.
2. Recursively traverse the **left subtree**.
3. Recursively traverse the **right subtree**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters:** Preorder is used to **create a copy of the tree** and to **serialize** a tree structure (e.g., to disk). The root always appears first, making it ideal for prefix-style expression trees (Polish notation).

### C. Postorder Traversal (LRN — Left, Right, Node)
1. Recursively traverse the **left subtree**.
2. Recursively traverse the **right subtree**.
3. **Visit the root node** last.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters:** Postorder is used to **delete an entire tree safely** (children must be freed before the parent). It is also used to evaluate **postfix expressions** and compute directory sizes.

### D. Level Order Traversal (BFS)
1. Enqueue the **root** into a queue.
2. **Dequeue** a node, **visit it**, then enqueue its **left child** (if any), then its **right child** (if any).
3. Repeat step 2 until the queue is empty.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Properties

| Property / Metric | Inorder (LNR) | Preorder (NLR) | Postorder (LRN) | Level Order (BFS) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Order Keyword Position** | Middle | First | Last | By Depth |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Auxiliary Space** | $O(h)$ | $O(h)$ | $O(h)$ | $O(w)$ |
| **Data Structure Used** | Recursion / Stack | Recursion / Stack | Recursion / Stack | **Queue** (FIFO) |
| **BST Output** | **Sorted ascending** | Root-first copy | Child-first deletion | Layer-wise snapshot |
| **Application** | BST sort, K-th smallest | Tree copy, serialization | Tree deletion, postfix eval | Shortest path, BFS |
| **Stack Frame / Queue Size (Worst)** | $h = n - 1$ (skewed) | $h = n - 1$ (skewed) | $h = n - 1$ (skewed) | $w = n$ (complete tree) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition:** For a tree with $n$ nodes, the total number of recursive calls (push + pop) across any DFS traversal is exactly $2n$ — one "arrival" push and one "departure" pop per node. This is a frequently-asked KTU question.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compilers:** Inorder yields infix expressions; Preorder yields prefix (Polish); Postorder yields postfix (Reverse Polish) — all used in **expression evaluation** and **code generation**.
- **Databases (B-Trees / B+ Trees):** Inorder traversal gives sorted record output for range queries.
- **File Systems:** Postorder deletion prevents memory leaks (children freed before parents).
- **Network Routing / AI:** Level Order underpins BFS for shortest path and puzzle-solving (e.g., 8-puzzle).
- **HTML / XML DOM:** Tree traversals power rendering engines and XPath queries.

## 2.4 Reconstruction Theorems (High-Yield KTU Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem 1 (Unique Reconstruction):** A binary tree can be **uniquely reconstructed** from its **Inorder + Preorder** traversals, or from its **Inorder + Postorder** traversals.
> **Theorem 2 (Impossibility):** A binary tree **cannot** be uniquely reconstructed from **Preorder + Postorder alone** (ambiguity remains).

The **first element of Preorder** is always the **root**. The **last element of Postorder** is always the **root**. The position of this root in the **Inorder** sequence splits the tree uniquely into left and right subtrees.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Tracing All Four Traversals on a Single Tree

Let us consider the following **binary tree** with **7 nodes**, which is a classic KTU exam sample:

```
              A              (Level 0)
            /   \
           B     C            (Level 1)
          / \     \
         D   E     F          (Level 2)
            /
           G                  (Level 3)
```

**Node inventory:** $\{A, B, C, D, E, F, G\}$
**Edge inventory:** $A-B,\ A-C,\ B-D,\ B-E,\ C-F,\ E-G$

### Step-by-Step Trace: INORDER (L-N-R)

We apply the rule **(Left, Node, Right)** recursively.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inorder}(A) &= \text{Inorder}(B) \;\cup\; \{A\} \;\cup\; \text{Inorder}(C) \\[4pt]
\text{Inorder}(B) &= \text{Inorder}(D) \;\cup\; \{B\} \;\cup\; \text{Inorder}(E) \\[4pt]
\text{Inorder}(D) &= \emptyset \;\cup\; \{D\} \;\cup\; \emptyset = \{D\} \\[4pt]
\text{Inorder}(E) &= \text{Inorder}(G) \;\cup\; \{E\} \;\cup\; \emptyset \\[4pt]
\text{Inorder}(G) &= \emptyset \;\cup\; \{G\} \;\cup\; \emptyset = \{G\} \\[4pt]
\text{Inorder}(C) &= \emptyset \;\cup\; \{C\} \;\cup\; \text{Inorder}(F) \\[4pt]
\text{Inorder}(F) &= \emptyset \;\cup\; \{F\} \;\cup\; \emptyset = \{F\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Back-substituting** (combining from the bottom up):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inorder}(E) &= \{G, E\} \\
\text{Inorder}(B) &= \{D, B, G, E\} \\
\text{Inorder}(C) &= \{C, F\} \\
\text{Inorder}(A) &= \{D, B, G, E, A, C, F\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Inorder Sequence: D $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ G $\rightarrow$ E $\rightarrow$ A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ F**

### Step-by-Step Trace: PREORDER (N-L-R)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Preorder}(A) &= \{A\} \;\cup\; \text{Preorder}(B) \;\cup\; \text{Preorder}(C) \\[4pt]
\text{Preorder}(B) &= \{B\} \;\cup\; \text{Preorder}(D) \;\cup\; \text{Preorder}(E) = \{B, D, E\} \\[4pt]
\text{Preorder}(E) &= \{E, G\} \\[4pt]
\text{Preorder}(C) &= \{C, F\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Preorder Sequence: A $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ D $\rightarrow$ E $\rightarrow$ G $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ F**

### Step-by-Step Trace: POSTORDER (L-R-N)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Postorder}(A) &= \text{Postorder}(B) \;\cup\; \text{Postorder}(C) \;\cup\; \{A\} \\[4pt]
\text{Postorder}(B) &= \{D\} \;\cup\; \{G, E\} \;\cup\; \{B\} = \{D, G, E, B\} \\[4pt]
\text{Postorder}(C) &= \{F\} \;\cup\; \{C\} = \{F, C\} \\[4pt]
\text{Postorder}(A) &= \{D, G, E, B, F, C, A\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Postorder Sequence: D $\rightarrow$ G $\rightarrow$ E $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ F $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ A**

### Step-by-Step Trace: LEVEL ORDER (BFS)

We use a queue, starting with the root.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Queue start:} \quad & [A] \\
\text{Dequeue } A \to \text{visit } A; \text{ enqueue } B, C: \quad & [B, C] \\
\text{Dequeue } B \to \text{visit } B; \text{ enqueue } D, E: \quad & [C, D, E] \\
\text{Dequeue } C \to \text{visit } C; \text{ enqueue } F: \quad & [D, E, F] \\
\text{Dequeue } D \to \text{visit } D: \quad & [E, F] \\
\text{Dequeue } E \to \text{visit } E; \text{ enqueue } G: \quad & [F, G] \\
\text{Dequeue } F \to \text{visit } F: \quad & [G] \\
\text{Dequeue } G \to \text{visit } G: \quad & [\ ]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Level Order Sequence: A $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ D $\rightarrow$ E $\rightarrow$ F $\rightarrow$ G**

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix (for the Worked Example)

| Traversal | Output Sequence | Verify: First Node | Verify: Last Node | Recursion Depth Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inorder (LNR)** | D, B, G, E, A, C, F | Leftmost leaf = **D** ✓ | Rightmost leaf = **F** ✓ | $h = 3$ (skewed at E) |
| **Preorder (NLR)** | A, B, D, E, G, C, F | **Root = A** ✓ | Rightmost-deepest = **F** ✓ | $h = 3$ |
| **Postorder (LRN)** | D, G, E, B, F, C, A | Leftmost leaf = **D** ✓ | **Root = A** ✓ | $h = 3$ |
| **Level Order (BFS)** | A, B, C, D, E, F, G | **Root = A** ✓ | Last node = **G** (deepest, rightmost) | Queue max = **3** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity-check pattern for exams:** For any DFS traversal, the **first node in the output** is always the leftmost node (Inorder/Postorder) or the root (Preorder). The **last node** is always the rightmost leaf (Inorder/Preorder) or the root (Postorder).

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Tree Reconstruction from Traversals

**Given:**
- Inorder: D, B, G, E, A, C, F
- Preorder: A, B, D, E, G, C, F

**Reconstruct the tree step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{First element of Preorder} = A \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Root} = A. \\
& \text{Find } A \text{ in Inorder at position 4 (0-indexed).} \\
& \text{Inorder left of } A: \{D, B, G, E\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Left Subtree}. \\
& \text{Inorder right of } A: \{C, F\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Right Subtree}. \\[6pt]
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Next Preorder in left set} = B \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Left Subtree Root} = B. \\
& \text{Inorder left of } B: \{D\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{B-Left} = D. \\
& \text{Inorder right of } B: \{G, E\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{B-Right subtree}. \\[6pt]
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Next Preorder} = D \;\Rightarrow\; \text{leaf} = D. \\
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Next Preorder} = E \;\Rightarrow\; \text{B-Right Root} = E. \\
& \text{Inorder left of } E: \{G\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{E-Left} = G. \\
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Next Preorder} = C \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Right Subtree Root} = C. \\
& \text{Inorder right of } C: \{F\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{C-Right} = F. \\[6pt]
\textbf{Result:} \quad & \text{Tree matches Section 3.1 exactly. Reconstruction is unique. } \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Iterative Traversal (Stack-Based) — Algorithm

To convert the **recursive** Inorder into an **iterative** version (a frequent KTU question):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Algorithm IterativeInorder}(root): \\
1. & \quad \text{Initialize an empty stack } S \text{ and set } curr = root. \\
2. & \quad \textbf{while } curr \neq NULL \text{ OR } S \text{ is not empty:} \\
3. & \quad\quad \textbf{while } curr \neq NULL: \quad \text{push } curr \text{ onto } S; \quad curr = curr.left. \\
4. & \quad\quad curr = \text{pop}(S); \quad \text{visit}(curr); \quad curr = curr.right. \\
5. & \quad \text{End while.} \\
6. & \quad \textbf{return}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Complexity:** Same $O(n)$ time, $O(h)$ space — but with explicit stack control, making it suitable for **deeply skewed trees** that may overflow the system's call stack.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Reference Binary Tree (Mermaid Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["A"] --> B["B"]
    A --> C["C"]
    B --> D["D"]
    B --> E["E"]
    C --> F["F"]
    E --> G["G"]

    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Color legend:** Pink = Root, Blue = Level 1, Green = Level 2, Gold = Level 3.

## 4.2 Traversal Order Flow Diagrams

### 4.2.1 Inorder (L $\rightarrow$ N $\rightarrow$ R)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1["Step 1: D"] --> L2["Step 2: B"]
    L2 --> L3["Step 3: G"]
    L3 --> L4["Step 4: E"]
    L4 --> L5["Step 5: A (root)"]
    L5 --> L6["Step 6: C"]
    L6 --> L7["Step 7: F"]
```

### 4.2.2 Preorder (N $\rightarrow$ L $\rightarrow$ R)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Step 1: A (root)"] --> P2["Step 2: B"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: D"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: E"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5: G"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6: C"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7: F"]
```

### 4.2.3 Postorder (L $\rightarrow$ R $\rightarrow$ N)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q1["Step 1: D"] --> Q2["Step 2: G"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Step 3: E"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Step 4: B"]
    Q4 --> Q5["Step 5: F"]
    Q5 --> Q6["Step 6: C"]
    Q6 --> Q7["Step 7: A (root)"]
```

### 4.2.4 Level Order (BFS by Depth)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1["L0: A"] --> R2["L1: B, C"]
    R2 --> R3["L2: D, E, F"]
    R3 --> R4["L3: G"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Iterative Traversal Engine

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input["Input: Tree Root Pointer"] --> Init["Initialize Stack S (empty)"]
    Init --> CheckA{"curr = NULL?"}
    CheckA -- "No" --> PushS["Push curr onto S; curr = curr.left"]
    PushS --> CheckA
    CheckA -- "Yes" --> CheckB{"Stack S empty?"}
    CheckB -- "No" --> PopS["Pop top node into curr; Visit curr; curr = curr.right"]
    PopS --> CheckA
    CheckB -- "Yes" --> Output["Output: Traversal Sequence"]
    Output --> Done["Terminate"]

    style Input fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333
    style Output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style Done fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 BST Inorder Sortedness — A Visual Proof

For a **Binary Search Tree** (left child < parent < right child), the inorder traversal **always** produces a sorted sequence — a fact repeatedly tested in KTU board exams:

```mermaid
graph TD
    X["50 (root)"] --> Y["30"]
    X --> Z["70"]
    Y --> YL["20"]
    Y --> YR["40"]
    Z --> ZL["60"]
    Z --> ZR["80"]

    style X fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style Y fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style Z fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style YL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style YR fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style ZL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style ZR fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

**Inorder result:** $20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 40 \rightarrow 50 \rightarrow 60 \rightarrow 70 \rightarrow 80$ — **strictly ascending**, confirming the BST + Inorder = Sorting theorem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Preorder, Inorder, and Postorder traversal of a binary tree. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Traversal | Order | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Preorder** (NLR) | Root $\rightarrow$ Left $\rightarrow$ Right | **Tree copy / serialization** — root visited first, ideal for cloning |
| **Inorder** (LNR) | Left $\rightarrow$ Root $\rightarrow$ Right | **BST sorting** — produces sorted output in ascending order |
| **Postorder** (LRN) | Left $\rightarrow$ Right $\rightarrow$ Root | **Safe tree deletion** — children deleted before parent |

*(1 mark for correct order, 1 mark for distinguishing feature, 1 mark for valid application. Total = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is a Queue used in Level Order Traversal, while a Stack is used in Inorder, Preorder, and Postorder traversals? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Level Order Traversal follows a **First-In-First-Out (FIFO)** discipline — we must process nodes in the exact order they were discovered (the parent's children are processed *only after* the parent, and within the same level, the left child is processed before the right child). A **Queue** naturally implements FIFO behavior. *(1 mark)*

Inorder, Preorder, and Postorder are **Depth-First** — we go as deep as possible down one branch before backtracking. This **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** backtracking behavior matches a **Stack** (or the system's recursive call stack). *(2 marks)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, you must answer **ONE full question of 14 marks**, with internal choice. Below are two fully solved alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Construct a binary tree from the following traversals. Show all intermediate steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

- **Inorder:** $5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35$
- **Preorder:** $20, 10, 5, 15, 30, 25, 35$

**(b)** Write the Inorder, Preorder, and Postorder traversals of the constructed tree. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Reconstruction (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Preorder[0]} = 20 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Root} = 20. \\
& \text{Find 20 in Inorder at index 3.} \\
& \text{Inorder-Left} = \{5, 10, 15\}; \quad \text{Inorder-Right} = \{25, 30, 35\}. \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Preorder[1]} = 10 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Left Root} = 10. \\
& \text{Find 10 in Inorder at index 1.} \\
& \text{10-Left} = \{5\}; \quad \text{10-Right} = \{15\}. \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Preorder[2]} = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{10-Left} = 5 \text{ (leaf)}. \\
\textbf{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Preorder[3]} = 15 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{10-Right} = 15 \text{ (leaf)}. \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Preorder[4]} = 30 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Right Root} = 30. \\
& \text{Find 30 in Inorder at index 5 (within right subtree).} \\
& \text{30-Left} = \{25\}; \quad \text{30-Right} = \{35\}. \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 6:} \quad & 25 \text{ is leaf}; \quad 35 \text{ is leaf.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Constructed Tree:**

```
                 20
               /    \
             10      30
            /  \    /  \
           5   15  25   35
```

*(Award: 1 mark for root identification, 2 marks for left subtree, 2 marks for right subtree, 2 marks for final diagram = 7 marks)*

**Part (b) — Traversals (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inorder: } & 5 \to 10 \to 15 \to 20 \to 25 \to 30 \to 35 \\[2pt]
\text{Preorder: } & 20 \to 10 \to 5 \to 15 \to 30 \to 25 \to 35 \\[2pt]
\text{Postorder: } & 5 \to 15 \to 10 \to 25 \to 35 \to 30 \to 20
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for inorder, 2 marks for preorder, 2 marks for postorder, 1 mark for correct sequencing methodology = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define a binary tree. For the tree given below, write the Inorder, Preorder, Postorder, and Level Order traversals. **(7 marks)**

```
                 1
               /   \
              2     3
             / \   / \
            4   5 6   7
```

**(b)** Construct the binary tree from the following Inorder and Postorder traversals. Show all steps and write its Preorder traversal. **(7 marks)**

- **Inorder:** $H, D, I, B, E, A, F, C, G, J$
- **Postorder:** $H, I, D, E, B, F, J, G, C, A$

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Traversals (7 marks):**

**Binary Tree Definition (1 mark):**
> A binary tree is a hierarchical, non-linear data structure in which each node has **at most two children**, referred to as the **left child** and the **right child**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inorder (L-N-R): } & 4 \to 2 \to 5 \to 1 \to 6 \to 3 \to 7 \\[2pt]
\text{Preorder (N-L-R): } & 1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5 \to 3 \to 6 \to 7 \\[2pt]
\text{Postorder (L-R-N): } & 4 \to 5 \to 2 \to 6 \to 7 \to 3 \to 1 \\[2pt]
\text{Level Order (BFS): } & 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 6 \to 7
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks each for the 4 traversals = 7 marks)*

**Part (b) — Reconstruction (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Postorder[last]} = A \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Root} = A. \\
& \text{Inorder index of A} = 5. \\
& \text{Inorder-Left} = \{H, D, I, B, E\}; \quad \text{Inorder-Right} = \{F, C, G, J\}. \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 2 (Right Subtree First — Postorder goes L-R-Root):} \\
& \text{Next-from-end in Postorder} = C \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Right Root} = C. \\
& \text{Find C in Inorder at index 7. C-Left} = \{F\}; \quad \text{C-Right} = \{G, J\}. \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 3:} \quad & G \text{ is C-Right root}; \quad \text{G-Right} = \{J\}; \quad \text{G-Left} = \emptyset. \\
& F \text{ is C-Left leaf}. \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 4 (Left Subtree):} \\
& \text{Next-from-end} = B \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Left Root} = B. \\
& \text{Find B in Inorder at index 3. B-Left} = \{H, D, I\}; \quad \text{B-Right} = \{E\}. \\[4pt]
\textbf{Step 5:} \quad & D \text{ is B-Left root}; \quad \text{D-Left} = \{H\}; \quad \text{D-Right} = \{I\}. \\
& E \text{ is B-Right leaf}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Constructed Tree:**

```
                  A
                /   \
               B     C
              / \   / \
             D   E F   G
            / \         \
           H   I         J
```

**Preorder Traversal (1 mark):**
$$
A \to B \to D \to H \to I \to E \to C \to F \to G \to J
$$

*(2 marks for tree diagram, 4 marks for step-by-step reconstruction, 1 mark for preorder = 7 marks)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Avoid these to secure full marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Preorder and Postorder root position:** Students often write the **first** element of postorder as the root. **The LAST element of Postorder is the root**, not the first.
> 2. **Forgetting to split the Inorder sequence:** When reconstructing a tree, students sometimes identify the root but fail to **recursively split the Inorder array** into left and right partitions. The position of the root in Inorder is the *key* that determines the split.
> 3. **Mixing up Inorder output for a BST:** Always explicitly state that **Inorder of a BST gives ascending sorted output** — examiners award a separate mark for this property statement.
> 4. **Level Order Traversal confusion:** Students sometimes use a **Stack** instead of a **Queue** for level order. This is a 1-mark deduction but a conceptual error.
> 5. **Skipping recursion-base-case justification:** When asked to "explain" a traversal algorithm, stating the **base case** (`if (root == NULL) return;`) carries weight — many students omit it.
> 6. **Time Complexity omissions:** Always explicitly write **$T(n) = O(n)$** and justify via the recurrence $T(n) = T(k) + T(n-k-1) + O(1)$ for any DFS traversal.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **4 Traversal Types:** Inorder (LNR), Preorder (NLR), Postorder (LRN), Level Order (BFS).
- ✅ **Time Complexity** for all four: $O(n)$ — every node visited once.
- ✅ **Space Complexity:** DFS = $O(h)$, BFS = $O(w)$.
- ✅ **Root Identification:** First of Preorder = Root; Last of Postorder = Root.
- ✅ **Reconstruction Rule:** Need **Inorder + (Preorder OR Postorder)** — never just Pre + Post.
- ✅ **BST + Inorder = Sorted Ascending Output** (most-tested single property).
- ✅ **Postorder is mandatory for safe tree deletion** (children freed before parent).
- ✅ **Preorder is mandatory for tree copying / serialization** (root first).
- ✅ **Level Order requires a Queue**; all DFS traversals use a Stack (or recursion).
- ✅ **Iterative versions** use an explicit stack to avoid stack overflow on skewed trees.
- ✅ **Inorder of an Expression Tree** = Infix; **Preorder** = Prefix (Polish); **Postorder** = Postfix (Reverse Polish).
- ✅ **Total recursive calls in DFS** = $2n$ (one arrival, one departure per node).
- ✅ **Worst-case height** $h = n - 1$ for a completely skewed tree; $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ for a balanced tree.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binary Search Trees - Binary Search Tree Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Binary Search Trees - Binary Search Tree Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Binary Search Tree (BST)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Search Tree (BST)** — A Binary Search Tree is a node-based binary tree data structure that satisfies the **BST Ordering Property**: for every node `N` in the tree, all keys (values) in the **left subtree** of `N` are **strictly less than** the key of `N`, and all keys in the **right subtree** of `N` are **strictly greater than** the key of `N`. This property must hold **recursively** for every node.

Mathematically, for a node `N` with key `k(N)`:

$$\text{For all nodes } L \in \text{LeftSubtree}(N) : k(L) < k(N)$$

$$\text{For all nodes } R \in \text{RightSubtree}(N) : k(R) > k(N)$$

The tree must also have the **no-duplicate-keys** constraint in the standard definition; however, certain variants allow duplicates by placing them in the right subtree (or via a frequency counter).

## 1.2 Structural Definition

A BST is formally defined as a **5-tuple** in data structure theory:

$$\text{BST} = (K, \text{root}, L, R, \text{key})$$

Where:
- **K** = Set of all keys (data values)
- **root** = Pointer/reference to the topmost node
- **L, R** = Left and Right child pointer functions
- **key** = Extractor function returning the comparable value of a node

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEECT522 Module 3):** Students are expected to master **four core operations** — *Search*, *Insertion*, *Deletion*, and *Traversal* — along with their recursive and iterative implementations and time-complexity derivations.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Staircase"

Imagine a **massive library** where books are arranged on a staircase:
- At the **top step** (root), the librarian asks: *"Is your book on Fiction (left) or Science (right)?"*
- You go down one step (descend one level) based on the answer.
- At each subsequent step, the librarian narrows down: *"Section A through M (left) or N through Z (right)?"*
- Each step **halves** the remaining search space.

This is **exactly** how a BST works. The root acts as a **decision node** that splits the search space into two halves. If the tree is **balanced**, the search space halves at every level, giving us the famous **O(log n)** search time — just like binary search on a sorted array, but on a dynamic structure that supports insertions and deletions.

## 1.4 Key Properties of a BST

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Ordering Property** | Left < Node < Right (for all nodes) |
| **Recursive Structure** | Every subtree is itself a valid BST |
| **Inorder Traversal** | Always produces keys in **sorted ascending order** |
| **No Cycles** | Strict hierarchical tree — each node has at most one parent |
| **Unique Keys** | No two nodes share the same key (standard definition) |
| **Height Bound** | Best case: $\lceil \log_2(n+1) \rceil$; Worst case: $n-1$ (degenerate/skewed) |
| **Root** | The **only** node with no parent; uniquely defined for a non-empty BST |

## 1.5 Why BSTs Matter in Engineering

BSTs are foundational to:
- **Database indexing** (B-trees are generalized BSTs)
- **Compiler symbol tables** and **autocomplete engines**
- **File system directory traversal**
- **Priority scheduling** in operating systems
- **Range queries** and **nearest-neighbor searches** in GIS systems
- **Expression evaluation** in parsing engines

> [!TIP]
> **The most important "exam fact":** The **inorder traversal** of a BST is ALWAYS a **sorted sequence**. If you remember nothing else, remember this — it appears in nearly every KTU exam paper.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Core BST Operations — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 OPERATION 1: SEARCH (Find a Key)

**Why it works:** The BST ordering property guarantees that at every node, we can decide in **O(1)** whether to search the left or right subtree, eliminating half the remaining nodes.

**How it works — Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. Start at the **root** node.
2. If `key == root.key` → **FOUND**. Return node.
3. If `key < root.key` → Recurse/Search in the **left subtree**.
4. If `key > root.key` → Recurse/Search in the **right subtree**.
5. If a `NULL` pointer is reached → **NOT FOUND**. Return failure.

**Recursive formulation:**

$$\text{Search}(N, k) = \begin{cases} \text{return } N & \text{if } N = \text{NULL or } N.key = k \\ \text{Search}(N.left, k) & \text{if } k < N.key \\ \text{Search}(N.right, k) & \text{if } k > N.key \end{cases}$$

### 2.1.2 OPERATION 2: INSERTION

**Why it works:** We must preserve the BST property, so we find the **exact position** where the new key belongs (the NULL leaf position) and attach the new node there.

**How it works — Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. If tree is empty → create new node as **root**.
2. Start at root. Compare `newKey` with current node's key.
3. If `newKey < current.key` → go **left**; if `newKey > current.key` → go **right**.
4. If the child pointer is **NULL** → insert the new node there. **STOP**.
5. If the child is not NULL → recurse/iterate to that child and repeat from Step 2.
6. **Duplicate keys:** Conventionally rejected (or inserted into right subtree).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Insertion always occurs at a **leaf position**. The structure of the existing tree is never rearranged during insertion — we only add a new leaf.

### 2.1.3 OPERATION 3: DELETION (The Trickiest Operation)

**Why it's tricky:** Unlike insertion, deletion may require us to **reconnect** subtrees to preserve the BST property. We must handle **three distinct cases**.

**How it works — Three Cases:**

**Case 1: Node to delete is a LEAF (no children)**
- Simply remove the node. Set parent's corresponding child pointer to `NULL`.
- **Cost: O(1) pointer operation.**

**Case 2: Node to delete has EXACTLY ONE child**
- **Bypass** the node: replace it with its only child (left or right).
- Parent's pointer is updated to point to the grandchild.
- **Cost: O(1) pointer operation.**

**Case 3: Node to delete has TWO CHILDREN**
- Find the **Inorder Successor** (smallest node in the right subtree) OR the **Inorder Predecessor** (largest node in the left subtree).
- **Copy** its key into the node to be deleted.
- **Recursively delete** the inorder successor/predecessor from the right/left subtree.
- The recursive deletion will fall into **Case 1 or Case 2** (since the successor has at most a right child).

**Inorder Successor formula:**

$$\text{Succ}(N) = \text{minimum node of } N.\text{right}$$

**Inorder Predecessor formula:**

$$\text{Pred}(N) = \text{maximum node of } N.\text{left}$$

### 2.1.4 OPERATION 4: TRAVERSAL

Four standard traversals exist; for BSTs, **inorder** is the most significant:

| Traversal | Visit Order | BST-Specific Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Inorder** | Left → Root → Right | Produces **sorted ascending** output |
| **Preorder** | Root → Left → Right | Used to **copy/serialize** the tree |
| **Postorder** | Left → Right → Root | Used to **delete** the entire tree safely |
| **Level-order** | Level by level (BFS) | Uses a **queue**; gives breadth-wise view |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Property** | **Time Complexity** | **Space Complexity** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Search (Average)** | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(h)$ recursion stack |
| **Search (Worst)** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ skewed tree |
| **Insertion (Average)** | Same as search path | $O(\log n)$ | $O(h)$ |
| **Insertion (Worst)** | Skewed chain | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Deletion (Average)** | Find + Fix | $O(\log n)$ | $O(h)$ |
| **Deletion (Worst)** | Skewed chain | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Min / Max element** | Leftmost / Rightmost node | $O(h)$ | $O(1)$ iterative |
| **Height of tree** | $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ (balanced) | — | — |
| **Worst-case height** | $h = n - 1$ (completely skewed) | — | — |
| **Number of nodes** | $n = 2^{h+1} - 1$ (full binary tree) | — | — |
| **Inorder traversal** | $\text{LNR}$ = sorted sequence | $O(n)$ | $O(h)$ |
| **Find Inorder Successor** | $\text{min}(N.\text{right})$ | $O(h)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Find Inorder Predecessor** | $\text{max}(N.\text{left})$ | $O(h)$ | $O(1)$ |

> **Where:** $h$ = height of the tree, $n$ = number of nodes.

## 2.3 Master Theorem Application for BST Operations

The recurrence for the search/insert/delete operations is:

$$T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(1) \quad \text{(balanced case)}$$

Applying the **Master Theorem**: $a = 1$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = O(1)$
- $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 1} = n^0 = 1$
- $f(n) = 1 = n^0$ → **Case 2** of Master Theorem
- Therefore: $T(n) = O(\log n)$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | BST Application |
|---|---|
| **Databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL)** | B-tree indexes (multi-way generalization of BST) |
| **Compilers** | Symbol tables for variable/function lookup |
| **Network Routing** | Longest prefix matching in trie-based routers |
| **Game AI** | Decision trees for move evaluation |
| **3D Graphics** | BSP trees for rendering sorted polygons |
| **Auto-complete** | Tries (n-ary BSTs) in search engines |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: BST Construction via Sequential Insertion

**Problem:** Insert the keys **50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80, 35** into an initially empty BST. Show the tree after each insertion.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Insert 50:**
Tree is empty. Create root node.

```
50
```

**Step 2 — Insert 30:**
$30 < 50$ → go left of 50. Left child is NULL. Insert.

```
    50
   /
  30
```

**Step 3 — Insert 70:**
$70 > 50$ → go right of 50. Right child is NULL. Insert.

```
    50
   /  \
  30   70
```

**Step 4 — Insert 20:**
$20 < 50$ → go left to 30. $20 < 30$ → go left of 30. NULL. Insert.

```
      50
     /  \
    30   70
   /
  20
```

**Step 5 — Insert 40:**
$40 < 50$ → left to 30. $40 > 30$ → right of 30. NULL. Insert.

```
      50
     /  \
    30   70
   /  \
  20   40
```

**Step 6 — Insert 60:**
$60 > 50$ → right to 70. $60 < 70$ → left of 70. NULL. Insert.

```
      50
     /  \
    30   70
   / \   /
  20 40 60
```

**Step 7 — Insert 80:**
$80 > 50$ → right to 70. $80 > 70$ → right of 70. NULL. Insert.

```
      50
     /  \
    30   70
   / \   / \
  20 40 60 80
```

**Step 8 — Insert 35:**
$35 < 50$ → left to 30. $35 > 30$ → right to 40. $35 < 40$ → left of 40. NULL. Insert.

```
        50
       /  \
      30   70
     / \   / \
    20 40 60 80
       /
      35
```

**Verification via Inorder Traversal (LNR):**
$$20 \to 30 \to 35 \to 40 \to 50 \to 60 \to 70 \to 80$$

✅ **Strictly ascending — BST property verified.**

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Deletion — All Three Cases

Using the BST from Example 1, perform the following deletions: **(a) Delete 20** (Case 1: Leaf), **(b) Delete 30** (Case 2: One child), **(c) Delete 50** (Case 3: Two children).

### (a) Delete 20 (CASE 1 — Leaf Node)

**Reasoning:** Node 20 has no children. We simply remove it and set 30's left pointer to NULL.

**Before:**

```
        50
       /  \
      30   70
     / \   / \
    20 40 60 80
       /
      35
```

**After:**

```
        50
       /  \
      30   70
       \   / \
       40 60 80
       /
      35
```

**Step-by-step justification:**
$$\text{Parent}(20) = 30, \quad 30.\text{left} = 20, \quad 20.\text{left} = \text{NULL}, \quad 20.\text{right} = \text{NULL}$$

$$\Rightarrow 30.\text{left} = \text{NULL}$$

**Operation Cost:** $O(1)$ — single pointer modification.

### (b) Delete 30 (CASE 2 — Exactly One Child)

**Reasoning:** Node 30 has only one child: 40 (with 35 as 40's left child). We bypass 30 and connect 50's left pointer directly to 40.

**Before:**

```
        50
       /  \
      30   70
       \   / \
       40 60 80
       /
      35
```

**After:**

```
        50
       /  \
      40   70
     /     / \
    35    60 80
```

**Step-by-step justification:**
$$\text{Parent}(30) = 50, \quad 30.\text{left} = \text{NULL}, \quad 30.\text{right} = 40$$

$$\Rightarrow 50.\text{left} = 30.\text{right} = 40$$

The subtree rooted at 40 (including 35) is preserved intact.

**Operation Cost:** $O(1)$ — single pointer bypass.

### (c) Delete 50 (CASE 3 — Two Children)

**Reasoning:** Node 50 (the root) has two children. We use the **Inorder Successor** strategy:
1. Find the **minimum** node in 50's right subtree.
2. Copy its key to node 50.
3. Delete the original successor node (which is guaranteed to have **no left child** → reduces to Case 1 or Case 2).

**Step 1: Find inorder successor of 50.**

$$\text{Succ}(50) = \min(50.\text{right}) = \min(70)$$

Starting at 70, go **left** as far as possible: $70 \to 60$ (60 has no left child). So:

$$\text{Succ}(50) = 60$$

**Step 2: Copy 60's value to node 50.**

Node 50 now holds key 60.

**Step 3: Delete the original node 60 from the right subtree.**

Node 60 is a leaf → **Case 1** applies. Set 70's left pointer to NULL.

**Final BST after deleting 50:**

```
        60
       /  \
      40   70
     /       \
    35       80
```

**Step-by-step mathematical justification:**

$$\text{Search}(50) \Rightarrow \text{Found at root} \Rightarrow \text{Case 3 applies}$$

$$\text{Succ} = \min(50.\text{right}) = \min(70) = 60$$

$$50.\text{key} \leftarrow 60, \quad \text{then delete}(60) \text{ from subtree of 70}$$

**Inorder Traversal Verification:**
$$35 \to 40 \to 60 \to 70 \to 80$$

✅ **Strictly ascending — BST property preserved.**

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Search Path Tracing

**Problem:** Search for key **35** in the BST from Example 1 (after 50's deletion, i.e., the final tree above).

**Step-by-step trace:**

| **Step** | **Current Node** | **Comparison** | **Decision** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 (root) | $35 < 60$ | Go **left** |
| 2 | 40 | $35 < 40$ | Go **left** |
| 3 | 35 | $35 = 35$ | **FOUND** ✅ |

**Cost:** 3 comparisons = $O(h) = O(\log n)$ where $n = 5$.

**Recursion depth:** 3 frames used on the call stack.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Deletion Strategies

| **Strategy** | **How to Replace** | **Advantage** | **Disadvantage** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inorder Successor** | Replace with min of right subtree | Successor is always ≥ deleted node | May increase right subtree depth |
| **Inorder Predecessor** | Replace with max of left subtree | Predecessor is always ≤ deleted node | May increase left subtree depth |
| **Random** | Choose successor or predecessor randomly | Better expected balance | Non-deterministic — harder to test |
| **Copy by merging** | Merge left and right subtrees | Preserves all nodes | More complex implementation |

## 3.5 Time Complexity Derivation — Worst Case (Skewed Tree)

If keys are inserted in **already-sorted** order (e.g., 10, 20, 30, 40, 50):

```
10
  \
   20
     \
      30
        \
         40
           \
            50
```

The tree degenerates into a **linked list**. The height is:

$$h = n - 1 = O(n)$$

Therefore, all operations degrade to:

$$\text{Search} = \text{Insert} = \text{Delete} = O(n)$$

This is why **self-balancing BSTs** (AVL, Red-Black trees) were invented — to guarantee $h = O(\log n)$ always.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: BST Search Operation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Search key K in BST]) --> rootA[Current = ROOT]
    rootA --> checkA{Is Current NULL?}
    checkA -- YES --> notFoundA[/Return NOT FOUND/]
    checkA -- NO --> cmpA{K compared with Current.key}
    cmpA -- EQUAL --> foundA[/Return Current node FOUND/]
    cmpA -- LESS --> goLeftA[Current = Current.left]
    cmpA -- GREATER --> goRightA[Current = Current.right]
    goLeftA --> checkA
    goRightA --> checkA
    foundA --> stopA([END])
    notFoundA --> stopA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: BST Insertion Operation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([START: Insert key K]) --> emptyB{Tree Empty?}
    emptyB -- YES --> makeRootB[Create new node as ROOT]
    emptyB -- NO --> currB[Current = ROOT]
    currB --> cmpB{K vs Current.key}
    cmpB -- LESS --> leftB{Is Current.left NULL?}
    leftB -- YES --> insLeftB[Insert K as Current.left]
    leftB -- NO --> moveLeftB[Current = Current.left]
    moveLeftB --> cmpB
    cmpB -- GREATER --> rightB{Is Current.right NULL?}
    rightB -- YES --> insRightB[Insert K as Current.right]
    rightB -- NO --> moveRightB[Current = Current.right]
    moveRightB --> cmpB
    cmpB -- EQUAL --> dupB[/Reject duplicate key/]
    makeRootB --> stopB([END])
    insLeftB --> stopB
    insRightB --> stopB
    dupB --> stopB
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: BST Deletion — Three Case Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([START: Delete key K]) --> findC[Locate node N with key K]
    findC --> notFoundC{N found?}
    notFoundC -- NO --> retC[/Return NOT FOUND/]
    notFoundC -- YES --> checkChildC{N has children?}
    checkChildC -- ZERO --> case1C[Case 1: Leaf — Remove N and set parent pointer to NULL]
    checkChildC -- ONE --> case2C[Case 2: One child — Bypass N with its child]
    checkChildC -- TWO --> case3C[Case 3: Two children]
    case3C --> succC[Find Inorder Successor: min of N.right]
    succC --> copyC[Copy successor key into N]
    copyC --> recDelC[Recursively delete successor from right subtree]
    recDelC --> case1C
    recDelC --> case2C
    case1C --> doneC[/Deletion Complete/]
    case2C --> doneC
    retC --> stopC([END])
    doneC --> stopC
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Final BST After All Deletions (Worked Example)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode((60))
    leftA((40))
    rightA((70))
    leafL((35))
    leafR((80))
    rootNode --- leftA
    rootNode --- rightA
    leftA --- leafL
    rightA --- leafR
```

**Tree Representation:**

```
        60
       /  \
      40   70
     /       \
    35       80
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture — BST Operation Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        k1[Key Value K]
        op1[Operation Type]
    end

    subgraph CORE[BST Core Engine]
        search1[Search Module]
        insert1[Insert Module]
        delete1[Delete Module]
        traverse1[Traverse Module]
    end

    subgraph MEM[Memory Layer]
        heap1[Heap Allocated Nodes]
        stack1[Recursion Stack]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        res1[Result Node or Boolean]
        trav1[Sorted Sequence]
    end

    k1 --> search1
    k1 --> insert1
    k1 --> delete1
    op1 --> traverse1
    search1 --> heap1
    insert1 --> heap1
    delete1 --> heap1
    search1 --> stack1
    insert1 --> stack1
    delete1 --> stack1
    search1 --> res1
    insert1 --> res1
    delete1 --> res1
    traverse1 --> trav1
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram: Inorder Traversal Visual Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1((35)) --> n2((40)) --> n3((60)) --> n4((70)) --> n5((80))
```

This horizontal chain represents the **inorder traversal output** of the final BST — a strictly ascending sequence, confirming the BST property.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Binary Search Tree (BST). List any four properties of a BST.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Binary Search Tree (BST)** is a binary tree data structure in which for every node `N`, all keys in the left subtree of `N` are less than the key of `N`, and all keys in the right subtree of `N` are greater than the key of `N`.

**Four Properties:**
1. The left subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys **less than** the node's key.
2. The right subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys **greater than** the node's key.
3. Both the left and right subtrees must also be **binary search trees**.
4. There are **no duplicate nodes** (in the standard definition).

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is an inorder successor? How is it used in BST deletion?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **inorder successor** of a node `N` in a BST is the node with the **smallest key** in `N`'s right subtree. It can be found by going to the right child once and then following left-child pointers as far as possible.

**Role in Deletion:** When a node with **two children** must be deleted, the inorder successor's key is **copied** into the node being deleted, and then the successor itself is deleted from the right subtree. This deletion reduces to a simpler case (Case 1 or Case 2), preserving the BST property.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Construct a Binary Search Tree (BST) by inserting the following keys in the given order: **45, 25, 65, 15, 35, 55, 75, 5, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80**. Show the tree after each insertion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Perform the following operations on the resulting BST and show the tree after each operation: **(i)** Delete 15, **(ii)** Delete 25, **(iii)** Delete 45. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

We insert keys one by one. For brevity, we show the final tree and key intermediate states.

**Insertion Sequence and Key States:**

| Step | Key Inserted | Path Taken | Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 45 | (empty) | Root |
| 2 | 25 | 45 → L | Left child of 45 |
| 3 | 65 | 45 → R | Right child of 45 |
| 4 | 15 | 45 → L → 25 → L | Left child of 25 |
| 5 | 35 | 45 → L → 25 → R | Right child of 25 |
| 6 | 55 | 45 → R → 65 → L | Left child of 65 |
| 7 | 75 | 45 → R → 65 → R | Right child of 65 |
| 8 | 5 | 45 → L → 25 → L → 15 → L | Left child of 15 |
| 9 | 20 | 45 → L → 25 → L → 15 → R | Right child of 15 |
| 10 | 30 | 45 → L → 25 → R → 35 → L | Left child of 35 |
| 11 | 40 | 45 → L → 25 → R → 35 → R | Right child of 35 |
| 12 | 50 | 45 → R → 65 → L → 55 → L | Left child of 55 |
| 13 | 60 | 45 → R → 65 → L → 55 → R | Right child of 55 |
| 14 | 70 | 45 → R → 65 → R → 75 → L | Left child of 75 |
| 15 | 80 | 45 → R → 65 → R → 75 → R | Right child of 75 |

**Final BST after all insertions:**

```
                    45
                  /    \
                25      65
               /  \    /  \
              15  35  55  75
             / \  / \ / \  / \
             5 20 30 40 50 60 70 80
```

**Inorder Traversal Verification (sorted order):**
$$5, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80$$ ✅

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i) Delete 15** — Node 15 has **two children** (5 and 20).
- **Inorder successor** of 15 = smallest in right subtree of 15 = **20**.
- Copy 20 into node 15. Now 15 holds the value 20.
- Delete original 20: node 20 is a **leaf** → simply remove it.

**Resulting BST:**

```
                    45
                  /    \
                25      65
               /  \    /  \
              20  35  55  75
             /   / \ / \  / \
             5  30 40 50 60 70 80
```

**(ii) Delete 25** — Node 25 has **two children** (20 and 35).
- **Inorder successor** of 25 = smallest in right subtree of 25 = **30**.
- Copy 30 into node 25. Now 25 holds the value 30.
- Delete original 30: node 30 is a **leaf** → simply remove it.

**Resulting BST:**

```
                    45
                  /    \
                30      65
               /  \    /  \
              20  35  55  75
             /     \  / \  / \
             5     40 50 60 70 80
```

**(iii) Delete 45 (the root)** — Node 45 has **two children** (30 and 65).
- **Inorder successor** of 45 = smallest in right subtree of 45 = **50**.
- Copy 50 into node 45. Now 45 holds the value 50.
- Delete original 50: node 50 is a **leaf** → simply remove it.

**Final BST after all deletions:**

```
                    50
                  /    \
                30      65
               /  \    /  \
              20  35  55  75
             /     \    \  / \
             5     40   60 70 80
```

**Final Inorder Traversal Verification:**
$$5, 20, 30, 35, 40, 50, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80$$ ✅ Sorted — BST property preserved.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** What is a Binary Search Tree? Write the **recursive algorithm** for the following BST operations: **(i)** Search, **(ii)** Insertion. Analyze the **time complexity** of each in the average and worst case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the BST below, perform the following: **(i)** Find the **inorder successor** and **inorder predecessor** of node 35. **(ii)** Delete the root node 50 and redraw the tree. **(7 Marks)**

**Initial BST for Part (b):**

```
            50
          /    \
        30      70
       /  \    /  \
      20  40  60  80
         /    \
        35    65
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Definition:** *(As given in Section 1.1)*

**Recursive Search Algorithm:**

```
ALGORITHM: BSTSearch(root, key)
BEGIN
    IF root = NULL OR root.key = key THEN
        RETURN root
    END IF
    
    IF key < root.key THEN
        RETURN BSTSearch(root.left, key)
    ELSE
        RETURN BSTSearch(root.right, key)
    END IF
END
```

**Recursive Insertion Algorithm:**

```
ALGORITHM: BSTInsert(root, newNode)
BEGIN
    IF root = NULL THEN
        RETURN newNode
    END IF
    
    IF newNode.key < root.key THEN
        root.left ← BSTInsert(root.left, newNode)
    ELSE IF newNode.key > root.key THEN
        root.right ← BSTInsert(root.right, newNode)
    END IF
    // Else: duplicate key — ignore
    
    RETURN root
END
```

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

| Operation | Average Case | Worst Case (Skewed) |
|---|---|---|
| Search | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Insertion | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ |

**Justification:** Each recursive call descends one level, so the number of calls equals the height $h$. For a balanced tree, $h = O(\log n)$. For a completely skewed (degenerate) tree, $h = O(n)$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i) Inorder Successor and Predecessor of 35:**

- **Inorder Predecessor of 35:** Largest node in the left subtree of 35.
  - Left subtree of 35 has only a leaf (or is empty). 35 has **no left child**.
  - Therefore, the predecessor is the **nearest ancestor** for which 35 lies in its right subtree.
  - Tracing: 35's parent is 40. 35 is 40's **left child** → so 40 is NOT the predecessor.
  - Continue up: 40's parent is 30. 40 is 30's **right child** → predecessor is **30**.

$$\text{Predecessor}(35) = 30$$

- **Inorder Successor of 35:** Smallest node in the right subtree of 35.
  - 35 has no right child.
  - Therefore, the successor is the **nearest ancestor** for which 35 lies in its left subtree.
  - Tracing: 35's parent is 40. 35 is 40's **left child** → successor is **40**.

$$\text{Successor}(35) = 40$$

**(ii) Delete root node 50:**

- Node 50 has two children → **Case 3** applies.
- Find **inorder successor** of 50 = min of right subtree = min of 70's subtree.
- Starting at 70, go left: $70 \to 60$. 60 has no left child. Successor = **60**.
- Copy 60's value into root. Now root holds 60.
- Delete original 60: node 60 is a leaf (Case 1) → simply remove it.

**Resulting BST after deleting 50:**

```
            60
          /    \
        30      70
       /  \       \
      20  40      80
         /
        35
```

**Inorder Verification:**
$$20, 30, 35, 40, 60, 70, 80$$ ✅ Sorted.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing inorder successor with inorder predecessor** — When deleting with two children, students often write "replace with the leftmost node of the right subtree" but then mistakenly use the **rightmost** or some other node. Use **min(right subtree)** for successor, **max(left subtree)** for predecessor.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to update parent pointers** — In Case 2 (one child) deletion, students remove the node but forget to reconnect the grandparent to the child, leaving a **dangling subtree**.
>
> 3. **Not showing intermediate trees** — KTU examiners award **partial marks** for each step. Always draw the tree **after every insertion and every deletion**, even if you think the answer is obvious.
>
> 4. **Inserting duplicates incorrectly** — Standard BSTs **reject duplicates**. If you insert them, state your convention clearly.
>
> 5. **Writing inorder traversal without BST verification** — Always verify your final tree's inorder traversal is **sorted ascending**. This is a 1-mark "bonus" that shows thoroughness.
>
> 6. **Confusing time complexities** — Average case for BST is $O(\log n)$, but worst case is $O(n)$. The KTU exam often asks for both — do not give only one.
>
> 7. **Mixing recursive and iterative logic in pseudo-code** — Pick **one style** and stick to it consistently in your answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — BST Operations**

- ⭐ **BST Property:** Left < Node < Right (strictly, for all nodes recursively).
- ⭐ **Inorder Traversal** of a BST **always produces a sorted sequence** — this is the single most important fact.
- ⭐ **Search:** Descend left if key < current, right if key > current. Cost = height = $O(h)$.
- ⭐ **Insertion:** Find the NULL leaf position where the new key belongs; attach it there. **Never rearrange** existing nodes.
- ⭐ **Deletion has 3 cases:**
  - **Case 1 (Leaf):** Just remove. $O(1)$.
  - **Case 2 (One child):** Bypass the node. $O(1)$.
  - **Case 3 (Two children):** Replace with **inorder successor** (min of right subtree) or **inorder predecessor** (max of left subtree), then recursively delete. $O(h)$.
- ⭐ **Inorder Successor of node N:**
  - If N has a right subtree → successor = min of N's right subtree.
  - Else → successor = the **lowest ancestor** of N for which N lies in its **left** subtree.
- ⭐ **Inorder Predecessor of node N:**
  - If N has a left subtree → predecessor = max of N's left subtree.
  - Else → predecessor = the **lowest ancestor** of N for which N lies in its **right** subtree.
- ⭐ **Time Complexities:**
  - **Average (balanced):** $O(\log n)$ for search, insert, delete.
  - **Worst (skewed/degenerate):** $O(n)$ — same as a linked list.
- ⭐ **Min element** = leftmost node; **Max element** = rightmost node.
- ⭐ **Self-balancing BSTs** (AVL, Red-Black) guarantee $h = O(\log n)$ by performing rotations after insertions/deletions.
- ⭐ **Master Theorem application:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) \Rightarrow O(\log n)$.
- ⭐ **Space complexity** of recursive operations = $O(h)$ due to the recursion call stack.
- ⭐ **Use of Queue** is required only for **level-order traversal** (BFS), not for the other three traversals.
- ⭐ **In a KTU exam, always draw the BST figure clearly** — examiners give 1–2 marks just for a correct, well-labeled diagram even if the explanation has minor errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Graphs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Graph** is a non-linear, hierarchical data structure consisting of a finite set of **vertices** (also called *nodes*) and a finite set of **edges** (also called *arcs*) that connect pairs of vertices. Formally, it is mathematically defined as an ordered pair:

$$G = (V, E)$$

where:
- $V$ is a non-empty finite set of **vertices** or **nodes**: $V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, \ldots, v_n\}$
- $E$ is a finite set of **edges** connecting the vertices: $E = \{e_1, e_2, e_3, \ldots, e_m\}$

Each edge $e_k$ can be represented either as a pair $(v_i, v_j)$ for an **undirected graph** or as an ordered pair $\langle v_i, v_j \rangle$ for a **directed graph (digraph)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Graphs are categorized as a **non-linear data structure** because elements (vertices) are not arranged sequentially, but are instead interconnected through edges forming arbitrary relationships.

## 1.2 Essential Graph Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Vertex (Node)** | A fundamental unit of a graph, denoted by a circle/dot |
| **Edge (Arc)** | A connection between two vertices |
| **Adjacent Vertices** | Two vertices connected directly by an edge |
| **Degree of a Vertex** | Number of edges incident to that vertex |
| **In-degree** | Number of edges coming **into** a vertex (digraph only) |
| **Out-degree** | Number of edges going **out of** a vertex (digraph only) |
| **Path** | A sequence of vertices where each adjacent pair is connected by an edge |
| **Cycle** | A path that starts and ends at the same vertex |
| **Simple Path** | A path with no repeated vertices |
| **Connected Graph** | A graph where every pair of vertices has a path between them |
| **Complete Graph ($K_n$)** | A graph where every pair of distinct vertices is connected by an edge |
| **Weighted Graph** | A graph where each edge has an associated numerical value (weight/cost) |
| **Subgraph** | A graph formed from a subset of vertices and edges of another graph |
| **Bipartite Graph** | A graph whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets with edges only between sets |

## 1.3 Types of Graphs

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification under KTU 2024 Scheme:**

1. **Undirected Graph:** Edges have no direction. Edge $(u, v) = (v, u)$.
2. **Directed Graph (Digraph):** Edges have direction. $\langle u, v \rangle \neq \langle v, u \rangle$.
3. **Weighted Graph:** Each edge carries a weight (cost, distance, time).
4. **Unweighted Graph:** All edges are considered equal.
5. **Cyclic Graph:** Contains at least one cycle.
6. **Acyclic Graph:** Contains no cycles (e.g., a Tree).
7. **Connected Graph:** Path exists between every pair of vertices.
8. **Disconnected Graph:** Has at least one isolated component.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Social Network**
>
> Imagine **Facebook** as a graph. Each **person** is a *vertex*, and each **friendship** is an *edge* connecting two people. If you want to find how many mutual friends you have with someone, you are essentially performing a **graph traversal** (BFS/DFS) starting from your vertex.
>
> - **Directed version:** Think of **Twitter/X** "Followers" — you can follow someone without them following you back (directional relationship).
> - **Weighted version:** Think of **Google Maps** — each road (edge) has a *distance* or *time* associated with it.
>
> This is why graphs are foundational in GPS navigation, social networking, internet routing, and recommendation systems.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Number of edges in a Complete Undirected Graph:** $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ where $n = \vert V \vert$
- **Number of edges in a Complete Directed Graph:** $n(n-1)$
- **Maximum degree of any vertex in a simple undirected graph:** $n-1$
- **Total number of possible simple graphs on $n$ vertices:** $2^{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties and Theorems

### 2.1.1 The Handshaking Lemma (Sum of Degrees Theorem)

This is one of the **most frequently tested theorems** in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** The sum of the degrees of all vertices in an undirected graph is **exactly twice** the number of edges.
>
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \deg(v_i) = 2 \vert E \vert$$

**Why this works (Intuitive Explanation):**
Every edge $e = (u, v)$ contributes exactly **1 to the degree of $u$** AND **1 to the degree of $v$**. So each edge is counted exactly **twice** when we sum the degrees of all vertices. Since there are $\vert E \vert$ edges, the total sum is $2 \vert E \vert$.

### 2.1.2 Number of Edges in Special Graphs

For a graph with $n$ vertices:
- **Tree (connected, acyclic):** exactly $n - 1$ edges
- **Complete Graph $K_n$:** $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ edges
- **Cycle $C_n$:** $n$ edges
- **Forest of $k$ trees with $n$ vertices:** $n - k$ edges

### 2.1.3 Graph Representation Methods

A graph can be stored in memory using one of three primary representations. The choice impacts space and time complexity significantly.

### A) Adjacency Matrix Representation

An adjacency matrix $A$ is a $\vert V \vert \times \vert V \vert$ 2D array where:

$$A[i][j] = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if there is an edge from vertex } i \text{ to vertex } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

For a **weighted graph**:
$$A[i][j] = \begin{cases} w(i,j) & \text{if edge exists} \\ 0 \text{ or } \infty & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

For a **directed graph**, the matrix is **asymmetric**; for an **undirected graph**, it is **symmetric** ($A[i][j] = A[j][i]$).

### B) Adjacency List Representation

Each vertex maintains a list of all its adjacent vertices. Implemented as an array of lists (or linked lists) where $Adj[v]$ contains all vertices $u$ such that edge $(v, u)$ exists.

### C) Incidence Matrix Representation

An incidence matrix is a $\vert V \vert \times \vert E \vert$ matrix $M$ where:

$$M[i][j] = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if vertex } v_i \text{ is incident to edge } e_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU exam paper!**

| # | Property / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Unit/Type |
|---|---------------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Graph Definition | $G = (V, E)$ | Definition |
| 2 | Handshaking Lemma | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$ | Sum of degrees |
| 3 | Max edges (undirected) | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Edge count |
| 4 | Max edges (directed) | $n(n-1)$ | Edge count |
| 5 | Tree edges | $n - 1$ | Edge count |
| 6 | Space — Adjacency Matrix | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | Space complexity |
| 7 | Space — Adjacency List | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Space complexity |
| 8 | Time — BFS/DFS (Matrix) | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | Time complexity |
| 9 | Time — BFS/DFS (List) | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Time complexity |
| 10 | Check edge exists (Matrix) | $O(1)$ | Lookup |
| 11 | Check edge exists (List) | $O(\deg(v))$ | Lookup |
| 12 | Total degree (digraph) | $\sum \text{in-deg} = \sum \text{out-deg} = \vert E \vert$ | Sum property |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Where Graphs are used in Industry:**

- **Google Maps / GPS Navigation:** Vertices = intersections, Edges = roads, Weights = distances/times. Solved using **Dijkstra's Algorithm** (Shortest Path).
- **Social Networks (Facebook, LinkedIn):** Vertices = users, Edges = friendships/connections. **BFS** is used to find people within "N degrees of separation".
- **Internet Routing:** Routers are vertices, links are edges with bandwidth as weights.
- **Compiler Design:** **Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)** represent expressions for optimization.
- **Operating Systems:** **Resource Allocation Graphs** detect deadlocks.
- **AI/Machine Learning:** Decision trees, neural networks, knowledge graphs.
- **Database Systems:** Query optimization using graph joins.
- **Computer Networks:** Topology discovery using **Spanning Tree Protocols (STP)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Handshaking Lemma (Complete Proof)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \deg(v_i) = 2 \vert E \vert$ for any undirected graph $G = (V, E)$.

### Step-by-Step Proof:

**Step 1: Setup**
Let $G = (V, E)$ be an undirected graph with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges, where $m = \vert E \vert$.

**Step 2: Count the contribution of each edge**
Consider an arbitrary edge $e_k = (v_i, v_j) \in E$. By definition of degree, this edge contributes exactly **1** to $\deg(v_i)$ and exactly **1** to $\deg(v_j)$.

$$\text{Contribution of } e_k = \deg_{e_k}(v_i) + \deg_{e_k}(v_j) = 1 + 1 = 2$$

**Step 3: Sum over all edges**
Summing the contribution of every edge in $E$:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{m} \left[ \text{Contribution of } e_k \right] = \sum_{k=1}^{m} 2 = 2m = 2 \vert E \vert$$

**Step 4: Reorganize the sum by vertex**
Equivalently, when we sum the degrees of all vertices, we are counting how many edges are incident to each vertex:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \deg(v_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{e \text{ incident to } v_i} 1$$

**Step 5: Equate both sides**
Since both expressions count the same quantity (edge-vertex incidences):

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \deg(v_i) = 2 \vert E \vert \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Number of Edges in a Simple Undirected Graph

**Statement:** A simple undirected graph on $n$ vertices has at most $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ edges.

**Step 1:** In a simple graph, no self-loops are allowed, and no parallel edges exist. So the maximum number of edges occurs when **every pair of distinct vertices is connected by exactly one edge**.

**Step 2:** The number of ways to choose 2 vertices from $n$ vertices is given by the combination:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2!(n-2)!} = \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)!}{2 \cdot (n-2)!} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the maximum number of edges is:

$$\vert E \vert_{\max} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: BFS Traversal Step-by-Step

**Problem:** Perform BFS on the following graph starting from vertex **1**, and produce the BFS tree.

**Graph:** $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$
**Edges:** $\{(1,2), (1,3), (2,4), (3,4), (4,5)\}$

### Adjacency List Representation:

| Vertex | Adjacent Vertices |
|--------|--------------------|
| 1 | 2, 3 |
| 2 | 1, 4 |
| 3 | 1, 4 |
| 4 | 2, 3, 5 |
| 5 | 4 |

### BFS Algorithm (Queue-based):

```
1. Initialize: Visited[1..5] = false, Queue = empty
2. Enqueue 1, mark Visited[1] = true
3. While Queue not empty:
       Dequeue vertex v
       Process v
       For each neighbor u of v:
           If NOT Visited[u]:
               Mark Visited[u] = true
               Enqueue u
```

### Step-by-Step Trace Table:

| Step | Dequeue (Process) | Queue State (after operation) | Visited Set | Explanation |
|------|--------------------|-------------------------------|--------------|-------------|
| 0 | — | [1] | {1} | Start: Enqueue 1 |
| 1 | **1** | [2, 3] | {1, 2, 3} | Process 1; enqueue unvisited neighbors 2, 3 |
| 2 | **2** | [3, 4] | {1, 2, 3, 4} | Process 2; enqueue unvisited neighbor 4 |
| 3 | **3** | [4] | {1, 2, 3, 4} | Process 3; 1 and 4 already visited |
| 4 | **4** | [5] | {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} | Process 4; enqueue unvisited neighbor 5 |
| 5 | **5** | [ ] | {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} | Process 5; no unvisited neighbors |

### Final Output:

**BFS Traversal Order:** $\boxed{1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5}$

**BFS Spanning Tree Edges:** $\{(1,2), (1,3), (2,4), (4,5)\}$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: DFS Traversal Step-by-Step

**Same graph as above; starting from vertex 1.**

### DFS Algorithm (Stack-based / Recursive):

```
1. Initialize: Visited[1..5] = false, Stack = empty
2. Push 1, mark Visited[1] = true
3. While Stack not empty:
       Peek top v
       If v has unvisited neighbor u:
           Mark Visited[u] = true
           Push u
       Else:
           Pop v
```

### Step-by-Step Trace Table:

| Step | Action | Stack State | Visited Set | Output |
|------|---------|--------------|--------------|---------|
| 0 | Push 1 | [1] | {1} | — |
| 1 | Visit 2 (neighbor of 1) | [1, 2] | {1, 2} | 1, 2 |
| 2 | Visit 4 (neighbor of 2) | [1, 2, 4] | {1, 2, 4} | 1, 2, 4 |
| 3 | Visit 3 (neighbor of 4, 2 visited) | [1, 2, 4, 3] | {1, 2, 3, 4} | 1, 2, 4, 3 |
| 4 | Backtrack: 3 has no unvisited | [1, 2, 4] | {1, 2, 3, 4} | — |
| 5 | Visit 5 (neighbor of 4) | [1, 2, 4, 5] | {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} | 1, 2, 4, 3, 5 |
| 6 | Backtrack all the way out | [ ] | {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} | Done |

### Final Output:

**DFS Traversal Order:** $\boxed{1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 5}$

**DFS Spanning Tree Edges:** $\{(1,2), (2,4), (4,3), (4,5)\}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** BFS uses a **FIFO Queue** (Level-by-level exploration), while DFS uses a **LIFO Stack** (Deep exploration first). Both traversals visit all reachable vertices, but in different orders.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: Adjacency Matrix vs Adjacency List

| Criterion | Adjacency Matrix | Adjacency List |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Space Used** | $O(n^2)$ always | $O(n + m)$ |
| **Best for** | Dense graphs ($m \approx n^2$) | Sparse graphs ($m \ll n^2$) |
| **Edge Lookup** | $O(1)$ | $O(\deg(v))$ |
| **Find all neighbors of $v$** | $O(n)$ | $O(\deg(v))$ |
| **Add an edge** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ with efficient impl. |
| **Remove a vertex** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\deg(v))$ |
| **BFS/DFS time** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n + m)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: A Sample Undirected Graph

The following Mermaid diagram represents a sample graph with 5 vertices and 5 edges, used as the reference example throughout these notes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    v1((1)) --- v2((2))
    v1((1)) --- v3((3))
    v2((2)) --- v4((4))
    v3((3)) --- v4((4))
    v4((4)) --- v5((5))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid lines represent undirected edges. Vertex 4 is a *cut vertex* (its removal disconnects vertex 5 from the rest).

## 4.2 Diagram 2: BFS Traversal Flow

The block-level functional architecture of the **Breadth-First Search** algorithm is depicted below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose source vertex S] --> B[Mark S as VISITED]
    B --> C[Enqueue S into Queue]
    C --> D{Is Queue empty?}
    D -- No --> E[Dequeue vertex V from front]
    E --> F[Process V and add to BFS order]
    F --> G[For each neighbor U of V]
    G --> H{Is U visited?}
    H -- No --> I[Mark U as VISITED]
    I --> J[Enqueue U into Queue]
    J --> G
    H -- Yes --> G
    G -- All neighbors checked --> D
    D -- Yes --> K[End: BFS Complete]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: DFS Traversal Flow

The block-level functional architecture of the **Depth-First Search** algorithm is depicted below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose source vertex S] --> B[Push S onto Stack]
    B --> C[Mark S as VISITED]
    C --> D{Is Stack empty?}
    D -- No --> E[Peek top vertex V]
    E --> F{Does V have unvisited neighbor U?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Mark U as VISITED]
    G --> H[Push U onto Stack]
    H --> F
    F -- No --> I[Pop V from Stack]
    I --> D
    D -- Yes --> J[End: DFS Complete]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Adjacency Matrix & List Side-by-Side Mapping

This block diagram shows how the same graph is represented using two different storage methods.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Graph G with 5 vertices] --> B[Adjacency Matrix 5x5]
    A --> C[Adjacency List Array of Lists]
    B --> B1[Row 1: 0 1 1 0 0]
    B --> B2[Row 2: 1 0 0 1 0]
    B --> B3[Row 3: 1 0 0 1 0]
    B --> B4[Row 4: 0 1 1 0 1]
    B --> B5[Row 5: 0 0 0 1 0]
    C --> C1[List 1: 2 3]
    C --> C2[List 2: 1 4]
    C --> C3[List 3: 1 4]
    C --> C4[List 4: 2 3 5]
    C --> C5[List 5: 4]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Graph Traversal Comparison (BFS vs DFS Order)

This diagram highlights the structural difference between BFS (level-order) and DFS (depth-first) traversals on the same graph.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start((Start: Vertex 1)) --> L1B[Level 1 BFS: 1]
    Start --> L1D[Depth DFS: 1]
    L1B --> L2B[Level 2 BFS: 2, 3]
    L1D --> L2D[Depth DFS: 2]
    L2B --> L3B[Level 3 BFS: 4]
    L2D --> L3D[Depth DFS: 4]
    L3B --> L4B[Level 4 BFS: 5]
    L3D --> L4D[Depth DFS: 3, then 5]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** BFS explores the graph **level by level** (like ripples in a pond), while DFS goes **as deep as possible** before backtracking (like navigating a maze with a single thread).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a graph data structure. Differentiate between a directed and an undirected graph with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Graph** $G$ is a non-linear data structure defined as an ordered pair $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of vertices (nodes) and $E$ is the set of edges connecting pairs of vertices.

| Feature | Directed Graph | Undirected Graph |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| Edge Type | Ordered pair $\langle u, v \rangle$ | Unordered pair $(u, v)$ |
| Symmetry | $A[i][j] \neq A[j][i]$ possible | $A[i][j] = A[j][i]$ |
| Degree | In-degree & Out-degree | Only Degree |
