# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. Forgetting to mark a vertex as **visited** the moment it is enqueued/recursed into (not when dequeued/exiting) — costs 2 marks.
> 2. Reporting only the **visit order** in DFS without showing **disc/finish** timestamps when the question demands them — costs 3–4 marks.
> 3. Confusing *back*, *forward*, and *cross* edges in DFS — examiners award partial credit only if the student names the edge and explains *why*.
> 4. Stating BFS complexity as $O(E)$ *or* $O(V)$ alone instead of $O(V + E)$ — strict loss of 1 mark.
> 5. Using the **wrong initialisation** (e.g. forgetting $\text{dist}[s] = 0$) — costs 1 mark and breaks the shortest-path answer.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Breadth First Search (BFS) and Depth First Search (DFS) traversal techniques on graphs. Mention the data structure used in each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| Traversal strategy | Explores all vertices at the current depth before moving to the next depth (level-by-level). | Explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. |
| Data structure | **Queue (FIFO)** | **Stack (LIFO)** or recursion |
| Use case | Shortest path in unweighted graphs, bipartite test | Cycle detection, topological sort |
| Memory pattern | Wide | Deep |
| Complexity (adjacency list) | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |

**Key points to write for full marks:**
- (1 mark) Data structure used in each.
- (1 mark) Traversal order philosophy (breadth vs depth).
- (1 mark) One application difference (e.g. BFS for shortest path, DFS for cycle detection).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "back edge" in the context of DFS. How is it related to cycle detection in an undirected graph?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** A *back edge* $(u, v)$ in a DFS of an undirected graph is an edge that connects a vertex $u$ to an *ancestor* $v$ already present on the recursion stack (i.e. a vertex that has been discovered but not yet finished).
- **(1 mark) Cycle relation:** In an undirected graph, the presence of **any back edge** implies the existence of a cycle. Conversely, a graph is acyclic (i.e. a *forest*) **iff** DFS discovers no back edge.
- **(1 mark) Justification:** A back edge $(u, v)$ along with the tree-path from $v$ to $u$ forms a closed walk, hence a cycle.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Both **Question A** and **Question B** are full 14-mark independent alternatives — pick one.

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Module 3]`
**Consider the following graph $G$ with 8 vertices and 10 edges:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \{A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H\} \\
E &= \{(A,B), (A,C), (B,D), (C,E), (D,F), (E,F), (F,G), (G,H), (A,H), (B,E)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a)** Perform **Breadth First Search** starting from vertex **A**. Show the queue state at every step, the BFS visit order, and the final BFS tree. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Perform **Depth First Search** starting from vertex **A** using the *recursive* form. Show the **discovery and finishing times** for every vertex and list all back edges (if any). Is the graph cyclic? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Adjacency list (sorted for determinism):**
- $A \to \{B, C, H\}$
- $B \to \{A, D, E\}$
- $C \to \{A, E\}$
- $D \to \{B, F\}$
- $E \to \{B, C, F\}$
- $F \to \{D, E, G\}$
- $G \to \{F, H\}$
- $H \to \{A, G\}$

**Part (a) — BFS from A (7 marks)**

| Step | Dequeue | Queue After | Visited After |
|---|---|---|---|
| Init | – | $[A]$ | $\{A\}$ |
| 1 | $A$ | $[B, C, H]$ | $\{A, B, C, H\}$ |
| 2 | $B$ | $[C, H, D, E]$ | $\{A, B, C, H, D, E\}$ |
| 3 | $C$ | $[H, D, E]$ (E already visited) | – |
| 4 | $H$ | $[D, E, G]$ | $\{A, B, C, H, D, E, G\}$ |
| 5 | $D$ | $[E, G, F]$ | $\{A, B, C, H, D, E, G, F\}$ |
| 6 | $E$ | $[G, F]$ (F already visited) | – |
| 7 | $G$ | $[F]$ (F already visited) | – |
| 8 | $F$ | $[]$ | – |

- **BFS Visit Order:** $A, B, C, H, D, E, G, F$
- **BFS Tree Edges:** $(A,B), (A,C), (A,H), (B,D), (B,E), (H,G), (D,F)$ → exactly $7 = n - 1$ ✓
- **Distance from A:** $A:0, B:1, C:1, H:1, D:2, E:2, G:2, F:3$.

**Part (b) — DFS from A (7 marks)**

Recursive trace (using alphabetical neighbour order):

| Call | Disc | Action | Finish |
|---|---|---|---|
| DFS-VISIT($A$) | 1 | Recurse to $B$ | 16 |
| DFS-VISIT($B$) | 2 | Recurse to $D$ | 13 |
| DFS-VISIT($D$) | 3 | Recurse to $F$ | 10 |
| DFS-VISIT($F$) | 4 | Recurse to $E$ (A,B visited) | 9 |
| DFS-VISIT($E$) | 5 | $C$ unvisited, recurse to $C$ | 8 |
| DFS-VISIT($C$) | 6 | All visited, backtrack | 7 |
| back at $E$ | – | All visited, finish | 8 |
| back at $F$ | – | $G$ unvisited, recurse | – |
| DFS-VISIT($G$) | 9 | $H$ unvisited, recurse | – |
| DFS-VISIT($H$) | 10 | All visited, backtrack | 11 |
| back at $G$ | – | All visited, finish | 12 |
| back at $F$ | – | All visited, finish | 10 → already recorded |
| back at $D$ | – | All visited, finish | 14 |
| back at $B$ | – | $E$ visited, finish | 15 |
| back at $A$ | – | $C, H$ visited, finish | 16 |

**Final Arrays:**
- $\text{disc} = [1, 2, 6, 3, 5, 4, 9, 10]$
- $\text{finish} = [16, 15, 7, 14, 8, 9, 12, 11]$

**DFS Visit Order (Discovery):** $A, B, D, F, E, C, G, H$

**Back Edges Identified:** None — every edge was either a tree edge or skipped because the neighbour was already finished.

**Cycle Determination:** Since **no back edges** were discovered, the graph is **acyclic** under DFS. However, this contradicts the visual presence of cycle $A-B-E-C-A$!

**Crucial point for marks:** For *undirected* graphs, every non-tree edge is a *back edge*. The student must check: edge $(A, C)$ connects vertex $A$ (still on stack) to vertex $C$ (finished). Per the strict definition, this is a **cross edge** in *directed* graphs, but in *undirected* graphs, all non-tree edges are *back* edges. Thus, on the undirected graph, $(A, C)$ is a back edge, and the graph is **cyclic**. **(Student must clarify "undirected vs directed" for full marks.)**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Module 3]`
**(a)** For a graph $G$ with $V$ vertices and $E$ edges, prove that the time complexity of BFS using an **adjacency list** is $O(V + E)$. State the asymptotic complexity when an **adjacency matrix** is used, and explain why it differs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply DFS to the following directed graph. Compute discovery and finish times, classify every edge (tree / back / forward / cross), and determine the **topological ordering** of vertices. **(7 marks)**

**Directed graph edges:**
$$
(A,B), (A,C), (B,D), (C,D), (D,E), (F,A), (F,E), (G,F), (G,H), (H,I), (I,G)
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — BFS Complexity Proof (7 marks)**

For BFS using an adjacency list:
1. **Initialisation loop** iterates over all $V$ vertices → $O(V)$ work to set $\text{visited}, \text{dist}, \text{pred}$.
2. **Queue operations** — each vertex is **enqueued** and **dequeued** at most once → $2 \times V = O(V)$ operations.
3. **Adjacency scan** — when vertex $u$ is dequeued, the entire list $\text{Adj}(u)$ is scanned. Across all dequeue operations:

$$
\sum_{u \in V} \vert \text{Adj}(u) \vert = \sum_{u \in V} \deg(u) = 2 \vert E \vert \quad \text{(Handshake Lemma)}
$$

4. **Total work:**

$$
T_{\text{BFS}} = O(V) + O(V) + O(E) = O(V + E)
$$

For an **adjacency matrix**, locating neighbours of $u$ requires scanning the *entire* $u$-th row, taking $O(V)$ time per vertex, regardless of $\deg(u)$. Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{BFS, matrix}} = \sum_{u \in V} O(V) = O(V \cdot V) = O(V^2)
$$

**Why the difference:** The matrix representation has $O(1)$ edge query but $O(V)$ neighbour enumeration per vertex. The adjacency list has $O(\deg(u))$ neighbour enumeration, summing to $2E$ globally. When $E \ll V^2$ (sparse graphs), the list is asymptotically faster.

**Part (b) — DFS on Directed Graph (7 marks)**

Adjacency (outgoing only):
- $A \to \{B, C\}$
- $B \to \{D\}$
- $C \to \{D\}$
- $D \to \{E\}$
- $E \to \{\}$
- $F \to \{A, E\}$
- $G \to \{F, H\}$
- $H \to \{I\}$
- $I \to \{G\}$

Trace (process isolated component $G$–$H$–$I$ first only if alphabetical — assume we start at $A$):

| Step | Vertex | Disc | Finish | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A$ | 1 | 10 | Recurses $B$ |
| 2 | $B$ | 2 | 5 | Recurses $D$ |
| 3 | $D$ | 3 | 4 | Recurses $E$ |
| 4 | $E$ | – | – | All done, finish 4 |
| 5 | back at $D$ | – | – | Finish 5 |
| 6 | back at $B$ | – | – | Finish 6 (re-check) |
| – | $A$ continues | – | – | Recurses $C$ |
| 7 | $C$ | 7 | 10 | $D$ already finished → **cross edge** $(C, D)$ |
| – | back at $A$ | – | – | Finish 10 |

Now process $F$ (next unvisited alphabetically):
- $F$: disc 11, recurse $A$ (visited, finished → **cross edge** $(F, A)$), recurse $E$ (finished → **cross edge** $(F, E)$), finish 12.

Now process $G$:
- $G$: disc 13, recurse $F$ (finished → **cross edge** $(G, F)$), recurse $H$.
- $H$: disc 14, recurse $I$.
- $I$: disc 15, recurse $G$ (still on stack → **back edge** $(I, G)$). Finish 16.
- Back at $H$, finish 17.
- Back at $G$, finish 18.

**Final arrays:**
- $\text{disc} = [1, 2, 7, 3, 4, 11, 13, 14, 15]$
- $\text{finish} = [10, 6, 10, 5, 4, 12, 18, 17, 16]$

**Edge classification:**
- **Tree edges:** $(A,B), (B,D), (D,E), (A,C), (F,A), (G,F), (G,H), (H,I)$
- **Back edges:** $(I, G)$ — vertex $G$ is an ancestor of $I$.
- **Cross edges:** $(C, D), (F, E)$ (and $(F, A)$ if not already classified).
- **Forward edges:** None.

**Topological order** (vertices sorted by *decreasing* $\text{finish}$):
$$
G, H, I, F, C, A, B, D, E
$$

(Cycle $G \to H \to I \to G$ exists, so a topological order exists only on the **DAG portion** of the graph. Examiner accepts: list the DAG components separately.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Glance at this list 5 minutes before walking into the exam hall.**

- [ ] BFS uses a **Queue (FIFO)**, DFS uses a **Stack / Recursion (LIFO)**.
- [ ] BFS produces the **shortest path** in an unweighted graph; DFS does **not** guarantee shortest path.
- [ ] Both traversals are $\mathbf{O(V + E)}$ on an adjacency list, $\mathbf{O(V^2)}$ on an adjacency matrix.
- [ ] BFS tree edges = $V - 1$ if the graph is connected; same for DFS.
- [ ] In undirected graphs, every non-tree edge encountered by DFS is a **back edge**; a back edge $\Rightarrow$ a cycle exists.
- [ ] In directed graphs, DFS classifies non-tree edges into back, forward, or cross edges.
- [ ] The **Parenthesis Theorem** states that discovery/finish intervals are either *disjoint* or *nested*, never partially overlapping.
- [ ] The **topological order** of a DAG is given by sorting vertices in *decreasing* order of $\text{finish}$ times.
- [ ] BFS is suitable for **flood fill**, **shortest hops**, **bipartite test**; DFS is suitable for **cycle detection**, **topological sort**, **SCC**, **articulation points**.
- [ ] Disc times range from $1$ to $V$; finish times range from $V + 1$ to $2V$. Total increments = $2V$.
- [ ] Always **mark visited on enqueue / recurse**, not on dequeue / finish. This is the #1 reason BFS/DFS go wrong on the exam.
- [ ] Handshake Lemma: $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \vert E \vert$ — vital for time-complexity derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Graphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Applications of Graphs ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of the **PEECT522 – Data Structures** syllabus, **Applications of Graphs** refers to the set of classical algorithmic problems defined on the abstract data type **Graph $G = (V, E)$**, where $V$ is a finite set of **vertices (nodes)** and $E \subseteq V \times V$ is a set of **edges (arcs)**. These applications convert real-world networks (roads, communication lines, dependencies, social ties) into combinatorial optimization problems and provide provably correct polynomial-time procedures to solve them. ### 1.2 Classification of Graph Applications (KTU Syllabus Mapping) | Category | Classical Problem | Industry Use-Case | |---|---|---| | Connectivity | Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) | LAN cabling, pipeline laying, VLSI routing | | Optimization | Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP) | Google Maps, OSPF routing, GPS navigation | | Optimization | All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP) | Network latency matrices, traffic analytics | | Ordering | Topological Sorting | Task scheduling, build systems (Make, Maven), course prerequisite sequencing | | Traversal | BFS / DFS based reachability | Web crawlers, garbage collection, deadlock detection | | Structural | Bipartite Testing, Cycle Detection | Bipartite matching, dependency cycles in code | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-3 Focus:** The Board Examiner typically tests **MST (Prim/Kruskal)**, **Dijkstra**, **Floyd-Warshall**, and **Topological Sort**. Mastery of the iterative trace of these four algorithms is the single highest-weight skill in this module. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are the **Chief Engineer of Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)** asked to connect 7 district headquarters with **minimum total cable length**, ensuring every district is reachable. The cable network is a **graph**; the districts are **vertices**; the possible cable routes are **edges** with weights equal to the length in km. Your mission is the **Minimum Spanning Tree** problem. Now suppose a customer in Kochi wants the **cheapest (shortest) travel time** to every other city — that is the **Single-Source Shortest Path** problem (Dijkstra). Replace "cable" with "road" and "km" with "travel time" and the analogy extends naturally to navigation apps. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** MST minimizes the *total* edge weight to connect *all* vertices. Shortest Path minimizes the *path* weight from a *source* to a *destination*. Confusing these two is the most common KTU valuation error. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) — Theoretical Foundation An MST of a connected, weighted, undirected graph $G = (V, E, w)$ is a subset $T \subseteq E$ such that: - $T$ is **acyclic** (forms a tree). - $T$ spans **all** $n = \vert V \vert$ vertices. - The total weight $w(T) = \sum_{e \in T} w(e)$ is **minimum** among all such spanning subgraphs. Since a spanning tree on $n$ vertices has exactly $n - 1$ edges, an MST has precisely $\boxed{n - 1}$ edges. #### 2.1.1 Fundamental MST Properties (The "Why" behind every algorithm) - **Cut Property:** For any cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ of the graph, the **minimum-weight edge crossing the cut** is *safe* to include in *some* MST. - **Cycle Property:** For any cycle $C$ in the graph, the **maximum-weight edge in $C$** is *never* in *any* MST. - **Uniqueness:** If all edge weights are **distinct**, the MST is **unique**. #### 2.1.2 Prim's Algorithm (Greedy, Vertex-Growth) - Maintains a single tree $T$ that grows by **one vertex at a time**. - Uses a **min-priority queue (min-heap)** keyed on the cheapest edge that connects a new vertex to $T$. - Cut property is invoked at every step on the cut between $T$ and $V \setminus T$. #### 2.1.3 Kruskal's Algorithm (Greedy, Edge-Growth) - Sorts all edges by weight in **non-decreasing order**. - Iteratively adds the next lightest edge $e = (u, v)$ **iff** it does **not** create a cycle. - Cycle test is performed using the **Union-Find / Disjoint Set Union (DSU)** data structure with path compression and union by rank. ### 2.2 Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP) — Dijkstra's Algorithm For a graph with **non-negative** edge weights, Dijkstra's algorithm computes the shortest path distance $d[v]$ from a source $s$ to every other vertex $v \in V$. **Relaxation Operation (the heart of every shortest-path algorithm):** For a directed edge $(u, v)$ with weight $w(u, v)$: $$ d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad d[v] := d[u] + w(u, v); \; \pi[v] := u $$ where $\pi[v]$ is the **predecessor** of $v$ on the shortest path. ### 2.3 All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP) — Floyd-Warshall Algorithm A dynamic-programming algorithm that computes shortest paths between **every pair** of vertices in $O(V^3)$ time using an intermediate-vertex recurrence. ### 2.4 Topological Sort — Theoretical Foundation A **topological ordering** of a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) $G = (V, E)$ is a linear ordering $\sigma : V \to \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ of its vertices such that for every directed edge $(u, v) \in E$, we have $\sigma(u) < \sigma(v)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > A topological order **exists if and only if the graph is a DAG**. If the graph contains a cycle, no topological order exists — this is the standard KTU trick question to test whether the student will detect a back-edge during DFS. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Algorithm | Time Complexity (Adj. List + Heap) | Time Complexity (Adj. Matrix) | Space | Applicable Graph | |---|---|---|---|---| | Prim's MST | $O((V + E) \log V)$ | $O(V^2)$ | $O(V)$ | Undirected, weighted, connected | | Kruskal's MST | $O(E \log E)$ | $O(E \log E)$ | $O(V + E)$ | Undirected, weighted | | Dijkstra (SSSP) | $O((V + E) \log V)$ | $O(V^2)$ | $O(V)$ | Non-negative weights | | Bellman-Ford (SSSP) | $O(V \cdot E)$ | $O(V^3)$ | $O(V)$ | Handles negative weights | | Floyd-Warshall (APSP) | $O(V^3)$ | $O(V^3)$ | $O(V^2)$ | Handles negative (no neg. cycles) | | Topological Sort (Kahn / DFS) | $O(V + E)$ | $O(V^2)$ | $O(V)$ | DAG only | | Property | Formula / Expression | |---|---| | Number of edges in any spanning tree of $n$ vertices | $n - 1$ | | MST total weight | $w(T) = \sum_{e \in T} w(e)$ | | Cycle property (cut max-edge) | $e_{\max} \in C \Rightarrow e_{\max} \notin \text{MST}$ | | Floyd recurrence (intermediate set $\{1, \ldots, k\}$) | $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min \!\left( D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \right)$ | | Dijkstra relaxation invariant | $d[v] = \min_{P : s \rightsquigarrow v} \sum_{e \in P} w(e)$ | | Topological ordering existence | DAG $\iff$ topological sort exists | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **MST:** KSEB minimum cable design, water pipeline network, telecommunication backbone (Spanning Tree Protocol in Ethernet switches). - **Dijkstra:** Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) routing protocol, Google Maps, ride-sharing fare computation. - **Floyd-Warshall:** Computing transitive closure, network diameter, all-pair reachability. - **Topological Sort:** Compiler instruction scheduling, software build systems (Makefile targets), university course prerequisite planning, deadlock detection in operating systems. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Prim's Algorithm (MST) **Graph:** $V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$, $E$ with weights (undirected): | Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight | |---|---|---|---| | (A, B) | 2 | (B, C) | 3 | | (A, C) | 3 | (B, D) | 4 | | (A, D) | 6 | (C, D) | 1 | | (A, E) | 5 | (D, E) | 2 | | (B, E) | 7 | (C, E) | 8 | **Source vertex:** $A$. **Key arrays:** $key[\cdot]$ (cheapest edge to tree), $\pi[\cdot]$ (parent), $T$ (MST edge set). | Step | Vertex Picked | $key[B]$ | $key[C]$ | $key[D]$ | $key[E]$ | $T$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Init | — | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\emptyset$ | | 1 | $A$ | $2_{(A,B)}$ | $3_{(A,C)}$ | $6_{(A,D)}$ | $5_{(A,E)}$ | $\emptyset$ | | 2 | $B$ (key=2) | — | $\min(3,2+3)=3$ | $\min(6,2+4)=6$ | $\min(5,2+7)=5$ | $\{(A,B)\}$ | | 3 | $C$ (key=3) | — | — | $\min(6,3+1)=\mathbf{4}_{(C,D)}$ | $\min(5,3+8)=5$ | $\{(A,B),(A,C)\}$ | | 4 | $D$ (key=4) | — | — | — | $\min(5,4+2)=\mathbf{3}_{(D,E)}$ | $\{(A,B),(A,C),(C,D)\}$ | | 5 | $E$ (key=3) | — | — | — | — | $\{(A,B),(A,C),(C,D),(D,E)\}$ | **MST edges (4 = $n-1$ edges):** $\{(A,B), (A,C), (C,D), (D,E)\}$ **Total MST weight:** $2 + 3 + 1 + 2 = \mathbf{8}$ units. ### 3.2 Worked Example — Kruskal's Algorithm (MST) **Same graph as above.** Sort all edges in non-decreasing order: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Rank} \quad 1: (C,D)=1, \quad 2: (A,B)=2, \quad 3: (D,E)=2, \quad 4: (A,C)=3, \quad 5: (B,C)=3, \\ &\text{Rank} \quad 6: (B,D)=4, \quad 7: (A,E)=5, \quad 8: (A,D)=6, \quad 9: (B,E)=7, \quad 10: (C,E)=8 \end{aligned} $$ | Step | Edge Considered | Weight | Action (DSU) | $T$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | (C, D) | 1 | Accept (C, D form new component) | $\{(C,D)\}$ | | 2 | (A, B) | 2 | Accept | $\{(C,D), (A,B)\}$ | | 3 | (D, E) | 2 | Accept (D, E merge into {C, D, E}) | $\{(C,D), (A,B), (D,E)\}$ | | 4 | (A, C) | 3 | Accept (merges {A, B} and {C, D, E}) | $\{(C,D), (A,B), (D,E), (A,C)\}$ | | 5 | (B, C) | 3 | **Reject** (creates cycle: A-B-C-A) | unchanged | | 6 | (B, D) | 4 | **Reject** (all 5 vertices already connected) | unchanged | Algorithm terminates when $\vert T \vert = n - 1 = 4$. **MST edges:** $\{(C,D), (A,B), (D,E), (A,C)\}$ with total weight $1 + 2 + 2 + 3 = \mathbf{8}$ ✓ (matches Prim's result). ### 3.3 Worked Example — Dijkstra's Algorithm (SSSP) **Directed graph** with source $S$: | Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight | |---|---|---|---| | (S, A) | 7 | (A, C) | 3 | | (S, B) | 2 | (A, D) | 4 | | (S, C) | 3 | (B, D) | 4 | | (B, A) | 3 | (B, E) | 5 | | (C, B) | 2 | (D, E) | 1 | | (C, D) | 5 | (E, A) | 2 | **Initialize:** $d[S] = 0$, $d[\text{others}] = \infty$. Visited set $V' = \emptyset$. | Iter. | Extract-min | Update A | Update B | Update C | Update D | Update E | $V'$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $S$ (0) | $0+7=7$ | $0+2=2$ | $0+3=3$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\{S\}$ | | 2 | $B$ (2) | $\min(7,2+3)=5$ | — | $\min(3,2+2)=\mathbf{3}$ | $2+4=6$ | $2+5=7$ | $\{S,B\}$ | | 3 | $C$ (3) | $\min(5,3+3)=5$ | — | — | $\min(6,3+5)=\mathbf{6}$ | 7 | $\{S,B,C\}$ | | 4 | $A$ (5) | — | — | — | $\min(6,5+4)=\mathbf{6}$ | 7 | $\{S,B,C,A\}$ | | 5 | $D$ (6) | — | — | — | — | $\min(7,6+1)=\mathbf{7}$ | $\{S,B,C,A,D\}$ | | 6 | $E$ (7) | — | — | — | — | — | $\{S,B,C,A,D,E\}$ | **Final shortest distances from $S$:** $d[S]=0,\; d[B]=2,\; d[C]=3,\; d[A]=5,\; d[D]=6,\; d[E]=7$. **Shortest path tree edges** (via $\pi$): $S \to B \to C \to A \to D \to E$. ### 3.4 Worked Example — Floyd-Warshall Algorithm (APSP) Take 4 vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. Initial distance matrix $D^{(0)}$ (∞ for non-edges, 0 on diagonal): $$ D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & \infty & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & \infty & 0 & \infty \\ \infty & 2 & 9 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Recurrence used at every step $k$:** $$ D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min \!\left( D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \right) $$ **Iteration $k=1$ (allow intermediate vertex 1):** Check paths $i \to 1 \to j$ for all $i, j$. Update $D^{(1)}[2][4] = \min(\infty, D[2][1] + D[1][4]) = \min(\infty, \infty + 1) = \infty$. (No change since column 1 has only diagonal.) $$ D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & \infty & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & 5 \\ \infty & 2 & 9 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ > *Updated entries: $D[3][2] = 4 + 8 = 12$, $D[3][4] = 4 + 1 = 5$.* **Iteration $k=2$ (allow intermediate vertex 2):** Key update: $D^{(2)}[1][3] = \min(\infty, D[1][2] + D[2][3]) = \min(\infty, 8 + 1) = 9$. Similarly $D[4][4] = 2 + \infty$ (no change), $D[1][4] = \min(1, 8 + \infty) = 1$. $$ D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & 9 & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & 5 \\ \infty & 2 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Iteration $k=3$ (allow intermediate vertex 3):** $D[4][1] = \min(\infty, 2 + 4) = 6$; $D[4][4] = \min(0, 2 + 5) = 0$ (no change). $D[1][4] = \min(1, 8 + 1) = 1$ (no change). $D[1][2] = \min(8, 8 + 1) = 8$ (no change). $$ D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & 9 & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & 5 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Iteration $k=4$ (allow intermediate vertex 4):** $D[1][2] = \min(8, 1 + 2) = \mathbf{3}$; $D[2][1] = \min(\infty, \infty + 6) = \infty$; $D[3][1] = \min(4, 5 + 6) = 4$ (no change); $D[2][4] = \min(\infty, 1 + 0) = \mathbf{1}$. $$ D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 9 & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & 5 \\ 6 & 2 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Final all-pairs shortest distances** $D^{(4)}$ — read entry $[i][j]$ for shortest path from $i$ to $j$. ### 3.5 Worked Example — Topological Sort (DFS-based) **DAG with edges:** $(5, 2), (5, 0), (4, 0), (4, 1), (2, 3), (3, 1)$. DFS visit order (with finish times marked in reverse): - Start at 5 → 2 → 3 → 1 (finish 1) → (finish 3) → (finish 2) → 0 (finish 0) → 4 → (finish 4) → (finish 5). **Reverse of finish order (post-order stack output):** $\mathbf{5, 4, 2, 3, 1, 0}$ — this is a valid topological ordering. Verification: every edge $(u, v)$ has $u$ appearing before $v$. ✓ ### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Algorithm Selection Guide) | Question in Problem | Wrong Algorithm | Correct Algorithm | |---|---|---| | "Connect all nodes with minimum total cost" | Dijkstra | **Prim / Kruskal** | | "Shortest path from one source to all nodes" | Prim | **Dijkstra** | | "Shortest path between every pair" | Dijkstra $V$ times | **Floyd-Warshall** | | "Graph has negative edge weights" | Dijkstra | **Bellman-Ford / Floyd-Warshall** | | "Order tasks by dependency" | BFS shortest path | **Topological Sort** | | "Dense graph ($E \approx V^2$)" | Adj. list + heap (slow) | **Adj. matrix + $O(V^2)$ Prim** | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Applications of Graphs Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[GRAPH APPLICATIONS]:::root --> B[CONNECTIVITY]:::branch A --> C[SHORTEST PATH]:::branch A --> D[ORDERING]:::branch A --> E[TRAVERSAL]:::branch B --> B1[Prim MST]:::leaf B --> B2[Kruskal MST]:::leaf C --> C1[Dijkstra SSSP]:::leaf C --> C2[Bellman-Ford]:::leaf C --> C3[Floyd-Warshall APSP]:::leaf D --> D1[Topological Sort DFS]:::leaf D --> D2[Kahn BFS Algorithm]:::leaf E --> E1[BFS Levels]:::leaf E --> E2[DFS Cycle Detect]:::leaf E --> E3[Bipartite Test]:::leaf classDef root fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef branch fill:#3a5f8a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef leaf fill:#a8c5e8,stroke:#1f2a44,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.2 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Operational Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A([START]) --> B[Initialize d source = 0, others = infinity] B --> C[Insert all vertices into min-priority queue] C --> D{Queue empty?} D -- YES --> Z([END - distances finalized]) D -- NO --> E[Extract vertex u with minimum d value] E --> F[Mark u as visited final] F --> G[For each neighbor v of u] G --> H{d v greater than d u + w u v ?} H -- YES --> I[Update d v and predecessor] H -- NO --> J[Keep d v unchanged] I --> K[Decrease-key v in priority queue] J --> K K --> D ``` ### 4.3 Floyd-Warshall — Matrix Iteration Process ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph I0["k = 0: Direct Edges Only"] M0["D0 matrix: w i j if edge exists, infinity otherwise"] end subgraph I1["k = 1: Paths via vertex 1"] M1["D1 i j = min D0 i j, D0 i 1 + D0 1 j"] end subgraph I2["k = 2: Paths via vertices 1, 2"] M2["D2 i j = min D1 i j, D1 i 2 + D1 2 j"] end subgraph I3["k = V: Full APSP"] M3["DV i j = shortest path from i to j"] end I0 --> I1 --> I2 --> I3 ``` ### 4.4 MST Algorithm Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Graph is undirected
and weighted?} -->|NO| NA[Not an MST problem] Q1 -->|YES| Q2{Already nearly
complete graph?} Q2 -->|YES, dense| P1[Use Prim with adjacency matrix
Time O V squared] Q2 -->|NO, sparse| Q3{Need to detect cycles
during construction?} Q3 -->|YES| K1[Use Kruskal with DSU
Time O E log E] Q3 -->|NO, growing from one root| P2[Use Prim with min-heap
Time O V + E log V] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Diagram Tip:** In the ESE answer book, always draw the **initial graph** with vertex labels and edge weights BEFORE writing the algorithm trace. Examiners award 1–2 marks purely for a clean, labeled diagram that lets them follow your iteration tables. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between Prim's and Kruskal's algorithms for finding the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST). Mention one scenario where each is preferred.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Prim's Algorithm | Kruskal's Algorithm | |---|---|---| | Growth strategy | Vertex-growth: expands a single connected tree | Edge-growth: builds a forest that merges | | Initial element | Any single root vertex (e.g., vertex 0) | Empty forest; edges sorted by weight | | Data structure | Min-priority queue keyed on cheapest edge to tree | Disjoint Set Union (DSU) for cycle detection | | Complexity (adj. list) | $O((V + E) \log V)$ | $O(E \log E)$ | | Preferred when | Graph is **dense** ($E \approx V^2$); use adjacency matrix form $O(V^2)$ | Graph is **sparse**; edges are given as a list | (3 marks awarded for a clear 3-row table covering strategy, data structure, and complexity.) --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Why does Dijkstra's algorithm fail on graphs with negative edge weights? Illustrate with a counter-example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Dijkstra's algorithm relies on the **greedy assumption** that once a vertex $u$ is extracted from the min-priority queue with distance $d[u]$, that distance is **final** and cannot be improved later. With negative edge weights, this assumption breaks because a later-relaxed path through some other vertex may yield a *smaller* total distance. **Counter-example:** Graph with 3 vertices $A, B, C$. Edges: $w(A, B) = 2$, $w(A, C) = 5$, $w(B, C) = -4$. True shortest path from $A$ to $C$ is $A \to B \to C$ with cost $2 + (-4) = -2$, but Dijkstra finalizes $d[C] = 5$ upon extracting $A$ first, missing the negative improvement. (3 marks: 1 for the assumption, 1 for the counter-example, 1 for the true shortest cost.) --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) **Q3. Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Prim's algorithm** for finding the MST of a connected, weighted, undirected graph. State its time complexity using a binary heap. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):** Prim's algorithm maintains a set $T$ of vertices already in the MST and a min-priority queue $Q$ keyed on the minimum-weight edge connecting each vertex outside $T$ to a vertex inside $T$. **Algorithm Steps (write in the answer book):** 1. Initialize: $T = \emptyset$, $key[v] = \infty$ for all $v \in V$, $key[r] = 0$ for chosen root $r$, $\pi[v] = \text{NIL}$ for all $v$. Build min-heap $Q = V$ keyed on $key[\cdot]$. 2. While $Q \neq \emptyset$: - Extract-min $u$ from $Q$ (this is the next MST vertex). - Add $u$ to $T$; if $\pi[u] \neq \text{NIL}$, add edge $(\pi[u], u)$ to MST edge set $E_{\text{MST}}$. - **For each neighbor $v$ of $u$** in the adjacency list: if $v \in Q$ and $w(u, v) < key[v]$, then set $key[v] = w(u, v)$, $\pi[v] = u$, and call **Decrease-Key**($Q, v, key[v]$). 3. Return the edge set $E_{\text{MST}}$ containing $n - 1$ edges. **Time Complexity (with binary heap):** Build-heap $O(V)$, then $V$ extract-min operations each $O(\log V)$, plus at most $E$ decrease-key operations each $O(\log V)$. Total: $\mathbf{O((V + E) \log V)}$. With **Fibonacci heap**: $O(E + V \log V)$. **(Marks distribution: 2 for the algorithm, 1 for the cut property justification, 2 for complexity derivation, 2 for heap operations.)** --- **(b)** For the graph given below, find the MST using **Kruskal's algorithm**. Show all intermediate steps and the final MST weight. **(7 marks)** ``` 1 A ----- B |\2 /|3 6| \ / | | \ / 4| C---D---E 2\ |5 /1 \ | / \|/ F (Edge weights: A-B=1, A-C=2, A-D=6, B-D=4, B-E=3, C-D=2, C-F=2, D-E=5, D-F=1, E-F=1) ``` **Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):** **Step 1: Sort edges ascending:** (A,B)=1, (D,F)=1, (E,F)=1, (A,C)=2, (C,D)=2, (C,F)=2, (B,E)=3, (B,D)=4, (D,E)=5, (A,D)=6. | Step | Edge | Weight | DSU Check (roots) | Accept? | MST Edges | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | (A, B) | 1 | Different roots | **Yes** | (A,B) | | 2 | (D, F) | 1 | Different roots | **Yes** | (A,B), (D,F) | | 3 | (E, F) | 1 | Different roots | **Yes** | (A,B), (D,F), (E,F) | | 4 | (A, C) | 2 | Different roots | **Yes** | ..., (A,C) | | 5 | (C, D) | 2 | Different roots | **Yes** | ..., (C,D) — 5 edges so far | | 6 | (C, F) | 2 | Same root now | **Reject** (cycle) | unchanged | | 7 | (B, E) | 3 | Same root | **Reject** (cycle) | unchanged | Algorithm stops at $n - 1 = 5$ edges (vertices: A, B, C, D, E, F → $n = 6$, need 5 edges). **Final MST edges:** $\{(A,B), (D,F), (E,F), (A,C), (C,D)\}$ **Total MST weight:** $1 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 = \mathbf{7}$ units. ✓ **(Marks: 2 for sorted edge list, 3 for the DSU trace, 1 for cycle-rejection reasoning, 1 for final weight.)** --- **Q3. Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** Apply **Dijkstra's algorithm** on the following directed graph with source vertex $S$. Show the iteration table and list the final shortest path from $S$ to every other vertex. **(7 marks)** ``` 4 S --------> A | \ | 1| \3 2| | \ v v \ B C \ / | \ > / 2| \ 5 / 1 | \ / v \v D E \ / 3 \ / F (Edges: S-A=4, S-C=1, S-E=5, C-D=2, C-F=10, D-B=7, E-D=3, E-B=1, B-A=2, A-F=1) ``` **Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):** **Initial:** $d[S] = 0$, all others $= \infty$. | Iter. | Extract $u$ | $d[A]$ | $d[B]$ | $d[C]$ | $d[D]$ | $d[E]$ | $d[F]$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $S$ (0) | $0+4=4$ | $\infty$ | $0+1=\mathbf{1}$ | $\infty$ | $0+5=5$ | $\infty$ | | 2 | $C$ (1) | 4 | $\infty$ | — | $1+2=\mathbf{3}$ | 5 | $1+10=11$ | | 3 | $D$ (3) | 4 | $3+7=10$ | — | — | 5 | $\min(11,3+3)=\mathbf{6}$ via $D$ | | 4 | $A$ (4) | — | $\min(10,4+2)=\mathbf{6}$ | — | — | 5 | $\min(6,4+1)=5$ via $A$ | | 5 | $E$ (5) | — | $\min(6,5+1)=6$ | — | — | — | $\min(5,5+3)=5$ (no change) | | 6 | $B$ (6) | — | — | — | — | — | 5 (no change) | | 7 | $F$ (5) | — | — | — | — | — | — | **Final shortest distances from $S$:** $d[S]=0, d[A]=4, d[B]=6, d[C]=1, d[D]=3, d[E]=5, d[F]=5$. **Shortest path tree (using predecessor $\pi$):** - $S \to A$ (cost 4) - $S \to C$ (cost 1) - $S \to C \to D$ (cost 3) - $S \to E$ (cost 5) - $S \to A \to F$ (cost 5) - $S \to A \to B$ (cost 6) **(Marks: 3 for the table, 2 for predecessor tracking, 2 for final paths.)** --- **(b)** Explain the **Floyd-Warshall algorithm** for All-Pairs Shortest Path. Apply it on the given 4-vertex graph and present the final distance matrix. **(7 marks)** ``` Vertices: 1, 2, 3, 4 Edges: (1,2)=3, (1,4)=7, (2,1)=2, (2,4)=2, (3,1)=5, (3,4)=1, (4,3)=4 (All other pairs = infinity, diagonals = 0) ``` **Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):** **Initial matrix $D^{(0)}$:** $$ D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & 2 \\ 5 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\ \infty & \infty & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Recurrence:** $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$. **Iteration $k=1$ (via vertex 1):** $D[2][3] = \min(\infty, 2 + \infty) = \infty$; $D[2][4] = \min(2, 2+7) = 2$; $D[3][2] = \min(\infty, 5+3) = 8$; $D[3][4] = \min(1, 5+7) = 1$ (no change). $D[4][1] = \min(\infty, \infty + 0) = \infty$ — **no meaningful changes.** **Iteration $k=2$ (via vertex 2):** $D[1][4] = \min(7, 3+2) = \mathbf{5}$; $D[3][1] = \min(5, 8+2) = 5$ (no change); $D[3][4] = \min(1, 8+2) = 1$ (no change). $D[4][1] = \min(\infty, \infty + 2) = \infty$. **Iteration $k=3$ (via vertex 3):** $D[1][4] = \min(5, 3+1) = \mathbf{4}$; $D[2][4] = \min(2, 2+1) = 2$ (no change); $D[4][1] = \min(\infty, 4+5) = \mathbf{9}$; $D[4][2] = \min(\infty, 4+\infty) = \infty$. **Iteration $k=4$ (via vertex 4):** $D[1][2] = \min(3, 4+\infty) = 3$; $D[2][1] = \min(2, 2+9) = 2$ (no change); $D[3][1] = \min(5, 1+9) = 5$ (no change). **Final distance matrix $D^{(4)}$:** $$ D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 4 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & 2 \\ 5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ 9 & \infty & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Marks (7 total):** 1 for the recurrence statement, 1 each for $k=1,2,3,4$ iterations (4 marks), 1 for the final matrix. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in Module 3:** > 1. **MST vs Shortest Path confusion (–2 marks):** A student writes Dijkstra when asked for MST. Always check the question: "minimum total cost to connect all" = MST; "shortest route from one to all" = Dijkstra. > 2. **Kruskal cycle check skipped (–1 mark):** When tracing Kruskal, students add edges that form cycles because they "feel" like they should be in the MST. The DSU test must be **explicitly shown** (e.g., "find(A) ≠ find(C) → accept; else reject"). > 3. **Dijkstra with negative weights (–2 marks):** If the question specifies or uses a negative weight, Dijkstra is **invalid**. Use Bellman-Ford. > 4. **Floyd-Warshall intermediate vertex order (–1 mark):** The recurrence uses $D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]$ (the **previous** matrix), not the current one — this is the most common error. > 5. **Topological sort on a non-DAG (–1 mark):** If a back-edge exists, no topological order exists; the algorithm should report failure rather than produce a wrong order. > 6. **Forgetting $n - 1$ edges in MST (–1 mark):** The MST edge set must have exactly $n - 1$ edges; explicitly verify this in the final answer. ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **MST always has $n - 1$ edges**, is acyclic, and spans every vertex exactly once. - **Cut property** ⇒ minimum-weight crossing edge is safe (basis of Prim's). **Cycle property** ⇒ maximum-weight edge in any cycle is rejected (basis of Kruskal's). - **Prim's** = vertex-growth, best for **dense** graphs (use $O(V^2)$ matrix version). - **Kruskal's** = edge-growth with DSU, best for **sparse** graphs, $O(E \log E)$. - **Dijkstra's** assumes **non-negative weights**; otherwise use Bellman-Ford. - **Relaxation** is the core operation: $d[v] := \min(d[v], d[u] + w(u, v))$. - **Floyd-Warshall** recurrence: $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j], D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$, time $O(V^3)$, space $O(V^2)$. - **Topological sort exists $\iff$ graph is a DAG**; the DFS-based version pushes vertices onto a stack in **post-order**. - Always **label the graph diagram** with vertex IDs and edge weights before any trace table — this is a 1–2 mark "diagram insurance" in ESE. - Final answer must include **explicit verification**: MST has $n-1$ edges, Dijkstra has $n-1$ extract-min operations, Floyd matrix satisfies triangle inequality ($D[i][j] \le D[i][k] + D[k][j]$).

topics.title: Sorting Techniques - Selection Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sorting Techniques - Selection Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Sort** is a comparison-based, in-place, non-stable internal sorting algorithm that divides the input list into two logical partitions: a **sorted sub-array** (built from left to right) and an **unsorted sub-array** (occupying the remainder). During each iteration $i$ (for $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-2$), the algorithm scans the unsorted partition to locate the **minimum element**, then performs a single swap to place that element at index $i$ (the boundary of the sorted partition). The boundary of the sorted region advances by one position after every pass.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Selection Sort belongs to the family of **$O(n^2)$ brute-force sorting techniques** alongside Bubble Sort and Insertion Sort. It is classified as a **non-adaptive** algorithm because its running time does not depend on the initial order of the input, and as a **non-stable** algorithm because equal elements may change their relative order after sorting.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Shortest-First" Classroom Lineup:**
> Imagine a class teacher who must line up 50 students in increasing order of height. The teacher scans the entire row, identifies the shortest student, walks them to the far left (position 1), and marks that spot as done. For position 2, the teacher again scans *all remaining* students, picks the shortest among them, places them second from the left, and marks that spot as done. The process repeats until every student is correctly placed.
>
> The teacher does **not** re-examine students already placed. Each pass performs a *full scan* of the remaining unsorted region, but performs only *one exchange*. This trade-off (many comparisons, few swaps) is the defining identity of Selection Sort.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Boundaries

- **Input size range:** Operates correctly for $n \geq 1$. For $n = 0$ (empty list) and $n = 1$ (single element), the array is trivially sorted and zero passes are executed.
- **Number of passes:** $n - 1$ passes for an array of size $n$.
- **Total comparisons (fixed):** $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — independent of input distribution.
- **Total swaps (worst case):** $n - 1$ — even when the array is already sorted (a known inefficiency of the algorithm).
- **Auxiliary memory:** $O(1)$ — only a few scalar variables (`i`, `j`, `min_idx`, `temp`) are required.
- **In-place:** **True** — sorts within the original array.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

The Selection Sort algorithm can be decomposed into the following structured logic steps, which are the same logic KTU examiners expect students to articulate for full marks:

1. **Outer Loop (`i` from 0 to n-2):** Iterates over each boundary position of the unsorted sub-array. After pass `i`, the element at index `i` is in its final, correct position.
2. **Initialize Tracker:** Set `min_idx = i` — assume the first unsorted element is the smallest.
3. **Inner Loop (`j` from i+1 to n-1):** Compares every remaining unsorted element with `A[min_idx]`. If `A[j] < A[min_idx]`, update `min_idx = j`.
4. **Single Swap:** After the inner loop completes, swap `A[i]` and `A[min_idx]`. This places the true minimum at its sorted position.
5. **Repeat:** Increment `i` and repeat until the unsorted sub-array reduces to size 1.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a full inner scan?** The algorithm cannot predict where the minimum lies without examining every candidate, so it must perform $n-i-1$ comparisons on each pass.
- **Why a single swap per pass?** The minimum is *guaranteed* to be unique for the current pass, so only one placement is needed.
- **Why $n-1$ passes, not $n$?** After $n-1$ passes, the last element is automatically the largest, so a final pass would be redundant.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula, boundary condition, and metric that a KTU 2024 examiner can ask for the Selection Sort topic. Memorize this table verbatim.

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Value** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Total number of passes | $n - 1$ | Outer loop iterations |
| Comparisons per pass $i$ | $n - i - 1$ | Inner loop iterations |
| Total comparisons (all cases) | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Fixed, input-independent |
| Best-case swaps | $0$ | Only if the input is already sorted AND a "skip-swap" optimization is used |
| Worst-case swaps | $n - 1$ | Maximum one swap per pass |
| Best-case time complexity | $O(n^2)$ | Even already-sorted input takes $n(n-1)/2$ comparisons |
| Average-case time complexity | $O(n^2)$ | No early-termination mechanism |
| Worst-case time complexity | $O(n^2)$ | Quadratic in all scenarios |
| Auxiliary space | $O(1)$ | In-place; only a constant number of variables |
| Stability | **Not stable** | Equal keys may be reordered |
| Adaptivity | **Not adaptive** | Runtime is independent of input order |
| Recurrence relation (comparison count) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + (n-1)$ | With $T(1) = 0$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although Selection Sort is rarely used in production for large datasets, it has niche applications:

- **Embedded systems with constrained write-cycles** (e.g., EEPROM, Flash memory): Each swap is a write operation. Selection Sort minimizes writes to $O(n)$, far better than Bubble Sort's $O(n^2)$ writes.
- **Teaching tool for algorithm analysis:** It is the canonical example for introducing the *selection* paradigm in divide-and-conquer pedagogy.
- **Small-array sorting kernels:** Used as a base case inside hybrid sorts like **Timsort** or **Introsort** when the sub-array size falls below a threshold (typically 8–16 elements).
- **Predictable timing:** Because the number of comparisons is constant, Selection Sort is useful in **real-time systems** where worst-case timing must be tightly bounded.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> The following exhaustive trace uses the input array $A = [29,\ 10,\ 14,\ 37,\ 13]$ with $n = 5$. Every comparison and every swap is recorded. KTU examiners frequently award full marks (7/7) only when students show *every* intermediate state, not just the final array.

#### Pass 1 ($i = 0$)

- Initialize: `min_idx = 0` (assumes $A[0] = 29$ is the minimum).
- Inner loop scans indices $j = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
j=1 &: \ A[1] = 10 < A[\text{min\_idx}] = 29 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 1 \\
j=2 &: \ A[2] = 14 < A[\text{min\_idx}] = 10 \Rightarrow \text{No update} \\
j=3 &: \ A[3] = 37 < A[\text{min\_idx}] = 10 \Rightarrow \text{No update} \\
j=4 &: \ A[4] = 13 < A[\text{min\_idx}] = 10 \Rightarrow \text{No update} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

- Final `min_idx = 1`. Swap $A[0]$ and $A[1]$.
- Comparisons in Pass 1: **4**. Swaps in Pass 1: **1**.
- Array after Pass 1: $[\mathbf{10},\ 29,\ 14,\ 37,\ 13]$ (sorted region = $\{A[0]\}$).

#### Pass 2 ($i = 1$)

- Initialize: `min_idx = 1` (assumes $A[1] = 29$ is the minimum of the unsorted region).
- Inner loop scans indices $j = 2, 3, 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
j=2 &: \ A[2] = 14 < 29 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 2 \\
j=3 &: \ A[3] = 37 < 14 \Rightarrow \text{No update} \\
j=4 &: \ A[4] = 13 < 14 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 4 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

- Final `min_idx = 4`. Swap $A[1]$ and $A[4]$.
- Comparisons in Pass 2: **3**. Swaps in Pass 2: **1**.
- Array after Pass 2: $[10,\ \mathbf{13},\ 14,\ 37,\ 29]$ (sorted region = $\{A[0], A[1]\}$).

#### Pass 3 ($i = 2$)

- Initialize: `min_idx = 2` (assumes $A[2] = 14$ is the minimum).
- Inner loop scans indices $j = 3, 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
j=3 &: \ A[3] = 37 < 14 \Rightarrow \text{No update} \\
j=4 &: \ A[4] = 29 < 14 \Rightarrow \text{No update} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

- `min_idx` remains 2. No swap needed because the assumed minimum is already the true minimum.
- Comparisons in Pass 3: **2**. Swaps in Pass 3: **0**.
- Array after Pass 3: $[10,\ 13,\ \mathbf{14},\ 37,\ 29]$ (sorted region = $\{A[0], A[1], A[2]\}$).

#### Pass 4 ($i = 3$)

- Initialize: `min_idx = 3` (assumes $A[3] = 37$ is the minimum).
- Inner loop scans index $j = 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
j=4 &: \ A[4] = 29 < 37 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 4 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

- Final `min_idx = 4`. Swap $A[3]$ and $A[4]$.
- Comparisons in Pass 4: **1**. Swaps in Pass 4: **1**.
- Array after Pass 4: $[10,\ 13,\ 14,\ \mathbf{29},\ \mathbf{37}]$ (sorted region = $\{A[0], A[1], A[2], A[3]\}$).

#### Final Sorted Array

$$
A_{\text{sorted}} = [10,\ 13,\ 14,\ 29,\ 37]
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Pass-wise Summary

The following KTU-style table consolidates the entire trace and validates the analytical formulas.

| **Pass $i$** | **Unsorted Region (before)** | **min_idx (before swap)** | **Comparisons** | **Swaps** | **Array (after pass)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $[29, 10, 14, 37, 13]$ | 1 | 4 | 1 | $[10, 29, 14, 37, 13]$ |
| 2 | $[29, 14, 37, 13]$ | 4 | 3 | 1 | $[10, 13, 14, 37, 29]$ |
| 3 | $[14, 37, 29]$ | 2 | 2 | 0 | $[10, 13, 14, 37, 29]$ |
| 4 | $[37, 29]$ | 4 | 1 | 1 | $[10, 13, 14, 29, 37]$ |
| **Total** | — | — | **10** | **3** | **Sorted** |

### 3.3 Validation Against the Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total comparisons} &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n - i - 1) = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Total swaps} &= 3 \quad (\text{which is } \leq n - 1 = 4) \quad \checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Both counts match the table exactly, confirming the theoretical model.

### 3.4 Reference Algorithm (C-style Pseudocode)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this is a CS course (PEECT522 – Data Structures), presenting the algorithm in pseudocode/C is mandatory and *expected* by KTU examiners. For non-CS courses, code is forbidden per protocol.

```c
void selectionSort(int A[], int n) {
    int i, j, min_idx, temp;
    for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        min_idx = i;
        for (j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
            if (A[j] < A[min_idx])
                min_idx = j;
        }
        // Single swap per pass
        temp = A[min_idx];
        A[min_idx] = A[i];
        A[i] = temp;
    }
}
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Control-Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

> [!TIP]
> The following Mermaid flowchart renders the full control flow of Selection Sort, including the outer loop, inner loop, comparison, index update, and the post-pass swap. It maps directly to the C-style pseudocode above.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START]) --> A1[/Read n and array A of size n/]
    A1 --> A2[outer_i = 0]
    A2 --> A3{outer_i less than n minus 1?}
    A3 -- No --> A4([END - Array is Sorted])
    A3 -- Yes --> A5[min_idx = outer_i]
    A5 --> A6[inner_j = outer_i + 1]
    A6 --> A7{inner_j less than n?}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Swap A of outer_i and A of min_idx]
    A7 -- Yes --> A9{A of inner_j less than A of min_idx?}
    A9 -- Yes --> A10[min_idx = inner_j]
    A9 -- No --> A11[inner_j = inner_j + 1]
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A7
    A8 --> A12[outer_i = outer_i + 1]
    A12 --> A3
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram depicts the **two-region partitioning model** used in every pass, emphasizing the role of `min_idx` as the active scanner.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputArray[Input Array of size n]
        direction LR
        S1[Sorted Region - indices 0 to i-1]:::sorted
        U1[Active Index - i]:::active
        U2[Unsorted Region - indices i+1 to n-1]:::unsorted
    end

    S1 --> U1
    U1 --> U2

    subgraph InnerPass[Inner Pass Logic]
        direction TB
        P1[Assume min_idx equals i]:::step
        P2[Scan j from i+1 to n-1]:::step
        P3[Update min_idx if A of j is smaller]:::step
        P4[Swap A of i with A of min_idx]:::step
        P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    end

    U2 -.feeds into.-> P1
    P4 -.advances boundary.-> InputArray

    classDef sorted fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20
    classDef unsorted fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#b71c1c
    classDef active fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825,color:#e65100
    classDef step fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1
```

### 4.3 Pass-wise State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Initial State: A equals 29, 10, 14, 37, 13]:::unsorted
    P1[After Pass 1: 10, 29, 14, 37, 13]:::unsorted
    P2[After Pass 2: 10, 13, 14, 37, 29]:::unsorted
    P3[After Pass 3: 10, 13, 14, 37, 29]:::unsorted
    P4[After Pass 4: 10, 13, 14, 29, 37]:::sorted

    P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4

    classDef sorted fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000
    classDef unsorted fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Color Legend:** Green-shaded cells represent the final sorted array; orange-shaded cells represent intermediate (partially sorted) states. This legend matches the Mermaid class definitions above.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define Selection Sort. State its best-case, average-case, and worst-case time complexities. Comment on its stability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Selection Sort is an in-place comparison-based sorting algorithm that repeatedly selects the minimum element from the unsorted portion of the array and places it at the beginning of the unsorted portion. Its time complexity is $O(n^2)$ in the best, average, and worst cases. It is **not stable** by default because swapping non-adjacent equal elements can change their relative order. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for complexities, 1 mark for stability comment.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **How many comparisons and swaps does Selection Sort perform on an array of size $n$? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Selection Sort performs exactly $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ comparisons regardless of the input, because the inner loop scans all remaining unsorted elements in every pass. The number of swaps is at most $n - 1$ (one per pass), and exactly $n - 1$ in the worst case, but can be **0 in the best case** if a `min_idx == i` check is added before swapping. *(1.5 marks for comparisons derivation, 1.5 marks for swaps justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Walkthrough**

**(a)** Illustrate the working of Selection Sort on the array $A = [40, 10, 50, 20, 30]$. Show all intermediate passes and indicate the number of comparisons and swaps in each pass. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Pass $i$** | **Array before pass** | **min_idx after scan** | **Comparisons** | **Swaps** | **Array after pass** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $[40, 10, 50, 20, 30]$ | 1 | 4 | 1 | $[10, 40, 50, 20, 30]$ |
| 2 | $[40, 50, 20, 30]$ | 3 | 3 | 1 | $[10, 20, 50, 40, 30]$ |
| 3 | $[50, 40, 30]$ | 4 | 2 | 1 | $[10, 20, 30, 40, 50]$ |
| 4 | $[40, 50]$ | 3 | 1 | 1 | $[10, 20, 30, 40, 50]$ |
| **Total** | — | — | **10** | **4** | **Sorted** |

Verification: $\dfrac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10$ comparisons ✓; $n - 1 = 4$ swaps ✓.

**(b)** Write the algorithm for Selection Sort in C. Discuss its time and space complexity. State one practical scenario where Selection Sort is preferred over Bubble Sort. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```c
void selectionSort(int A[], int n) {
    int i, j, min_idx, temp;
    for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        min_idx = i;
        for (j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
            if (A[j] < A[min_idx])
                min_idx = j;
        }
        temp = A[min_idx];
        A[min_idx] = A[i];
        A[i] = temp;
    }
}
```

**Complexity Analysis:**

- **Time:** $T(n) = (n-1) + (n-2) + \ldots + 1 = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)$ in all cases.
- **Space:** $O(1)$ auxiliary — in-place sorting.

**Practical Preference:** Selection Sort is preferred in **memory-constrained or write-limited environments** (e.g., EEPROM-based embedded systems) because it performs at most $n - 1$ swaps, whereas Bubble Sort can perform up to $O(n^2)$ swaps. Each swap corresponds to a write operation, so minimizing writes extends device lifetime.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Prove that the number of comparisons in Selection Sort is always $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$, irrespective of the input distribution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let $C(n)$ denote the total number of comparisons for an array of size $n$.

During pass $i$ (where $i$ ranges from $0$ to $n-2$), the inner loop compares $A[i+1], A[i+2], \ldots, A[n-1]$ against $A[\text{min\_idx}]$, performing exactly $n - i - 1$ comparisons. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n - i - 1) \\
     &= (n-1) + (n-2) + (n-3) + \ldots + 1 \\
     &= \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k \\
     &= \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since this summation contains no terms that depend on the actual values of the array elements, $C(n)$ is **independent of the input distribution**. Hence the number of comparisons is always $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** Is Selection Sort stable? Justify your answer with a counter-example. Suggest one modification that can make it stable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

By default, Selection Sort is **not stable**. Consider the input array:

$$
A = [4_a, 2, 4_b, 1]
$$

Pass 1: minimum = 1 at index 3. Swap with $A[0]$.

$$
A = [1, 2, 4_b, 4_a]
$$

The relative order of the two 4s has been reversed ($4_a$ now appears after $4_b$), even though they are equal in value. This violates stability.

**Modification for Stability:** Instead of swapping, **shift** all elements between index $i$ and `min_idx` one position to the right, and then insert the minimum at index $i`. This shifting preserves the relative order of equal elements. However, this modification increases the number of writes to $O(n^2)$ and removes the algorithm's primary advantage of minimal writes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Before Writing Your Exam:**
> 1. **Skipping intermediate array states** is the #1 reason students lose 3–5 marks. Always show the array *after every pass*, not just the final sorted array.
> 2. **Claiming best-case is $O(n)$:** Selection Sort has *no* early-termination condition. The best case is still $O(n^2)$. Writing $O(n)$ will fetch **zero marks** for the complexity sub-question.
> 3. **Confusing swaps with comparisons:** Examiners often award a separate 1–2 mark sub-part for "number of swaps." Stating $O(n^2)$ swaps (which is incorrect — it is $n-1$) will cost those marks.
> 4. **Stability confusion:** Writing "Selection Sort is stable" without justification is a guaranteed mark-losing statement. Always pair the claim with a counter-example.
> 5. **Forgetting the `n-1` pass justification:** If asked "why $n-1$ passes?", explicitly state: *after $n-1$ passes, the last remaining element is automatically the largest, so an $n$-th pass is redundant.*

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Selection Sort** repeatedly extracts the minimum from the unsorted region and places it at the front.
- **Time complexity:** $O(n^2)$ in **all three cases** (best, average, worst) — confirmed by the formula $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- **Space complexity:** $O(1)$ — fully in-place.
- **Number of comparisons:** Exactly $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — input-independent.
- **Number of swaps:** At most $n - 1$ — the algorithm's key advantage.
- **Stability:** **Not stable** in its standard form (long-distance swap disrupts equal-key order).
- **Adaptivity:** **Not adaptive** — runtime does not change based on input order.
- **Use it when:** Write operations are expensive, memory is limited, and the dataset is small.
- **Avoid it when:** Stability, best-case performance, or large datasets are priorities — use Merge Sort, Heap Sort, or Quick Sort instead.
- **Total passes:** Always $n - 1$; the $n$-th pass is implicit and unnecessary.
- **Exam mantra:** *"Many comparisons, few swaps — quadratic time, constant space."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insertion Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT522 - DATA STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insertion Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Insertion Sort** is a simple, comparison-based, **in-place**, and **stable** sorting algorithm that builds the final sorted array one element at a time. It iterates through an input array, consuming one input element per iteration, and grows a sorted output list. At each iteration, it removes one element from the unsorted portion (the input data) and inserts it into the correct position in the sorted portion of the array, shifting all larger elements one position to the right to make space.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEECT522 – Data Structures, Module 4: Sorting Techniques)**, Insertion Sort is classified as one of the **elementary comparison-based internal sorting algorithms**, taught alongside Bubble Sort and Selection Sort as a foundational technique that introduces the student to the concept of incremental sorted-list construction.

**Key Algorithmic Properties (KTU Terminology):**

- **In-place algorithm** — requires only $O(1)$ auxiliary memory (a constant amount of extra space beyond the input array).
- **Stable algorithm** — preserves the relative order of equal-keyed records. If $A[i] = A[j]$ and $i < j$, then after sorting, the elements will still satisfy the order $A[i]$ before $A[j]$.
- **Online algorithm** — can sort a list as it receives it, one element at a time.
- **Adaptive algorithm** — its time complexity improves when the input is already partially sorted.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Sorting Playing Cards in Your Hand**

Imagine you are playing a game of **Rummy** or **Bridge**, and you are dealt a hand of 13 cards. As the dealer hands you the cards one by one, what does a typical player instinctively do? You pick up each new card and **insert it into the correct position** among the cards you are already holding in your hand, pushing the other cards to the right to make room. By the time all 13 cards have been dealt, your hand is **already sorted in rank order** — without you ever having to take all the cards out, sort them externally, and place them back in.

This is *exactly* how **Insertion Sort** works:
- Your **hand of sorted cards** = the **left sub-array** $A[0 \ldots i-1]$ (already sorted).
- The **new card being picked up** = the **current element** $A[i]$ (the key being inserted).
- The **shuffling of cards to the right** = the **shifting** of elements greater than the key one position to the right.

## 1.3 High-Yield Quick Facts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worst-case Time Complexity:** $O(n^2)$
> **Best-case Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — occurs when the input is already sorted.
> **Average-case Time Complexity:** $O(n^2)$
> **Auxiliary Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ — true in-place sort.
> **Stable?** **Yes.**
> **Online?** **Yes** — can process streaming data.

These constants and metrics are the **most-frequently tested** in KTU Module 4 viva and written examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Walkthrough

The Insertion Sort algorithm operates by maintaining two logical regions within the input array $A[0 \ldots n-1]$:

1. **Sorted Region** — Initially just $A[0]$ (a single element is trivially sorted). Grows by one element per outer-loop iteration.
2. **Unsorted Region** — Initially $A[1 \ldots n-1]$. Shrinks by one element per outer-loop iteration.

The algorithm proceeds in the following structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Outer Loop Initialization:** Set $i = 1$ (since $A[0]$ is already a sorted sub-array of length 1).
- **Step 2 — Key Selection:** Pick the element at index $i$ and store it in a temporary variable called the **key** (often named `key` or `temp`). This element is the "card" that is about to be inserted.
- **Step 3 — Inner Loop (Shifting Phase):** Initialize $j = i - 1$. While $j \geq 0$ AND $A[j] > \text{key}$, perform $A[j+1] = A[j]$ (shift right) and decrement $j$.
- **Step 4 — Insertion Phase:** Once the inner loop terminates (either $j < 0$ or $A[j] \leq \text{key}$), place the key at $A[j+1]$.
- **Step 5 — Loop Increment:** Increment $i$ and repeat Steps 2–4 until $i$ reaches $n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** The inner loop guarantees that every element in the sorted region $A[0 \ldots i-1]$ is $\leq$ the key, ensuring the invariant "$A[0 \ldots i]$ is sorted" holds after every iteration. This is the **loop invariant** of Insertion Sort and is the proof technique expected in KTU 14-mark algorithm analysis questions.

## 2.2 Standard Pseudocode (KTU Board Style)

The following pseudocode follows the conventional textbook style adopted in KTU 2024 Scheme reference materials (Cormen-style CLRS pseudocode adapted for instructional clarity):

```
ALGORITHM: InsertionSort(A[0..n-1])
INPUT:    An array A of n comparable elements
OUTPUT:   Array A sorted in non-decreasing order

1.  FOR i ← 1 TO n - 1 DO
2.        key ← A[i]
3.        j   ← i - 1
4.        WHILE j ≥ 0 AND A[j] > key DO
5.              A[j + 1] ← A[j]      // shift right
6.              j ← j - 1
7.        END WHILE
8.        A[j + 1] ← key             // place key
9.  END FOR
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Line 5** performs the **shifting** — it does *not* perform a swap. This distinction is critical: Insertion Sort uses **assignment-based movement**, which is the reason it is generally faster in practice than Bubble Sort (which performs a full swap per inversion).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Worst Case** | **Average Case** | **Best Case** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time Complexity | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ | Best case = already sorted input |
| Number of Comparisons | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $\dfrac{n^2}{4}$ | $n - 1$ | Inner loop comparisons |
| Number of Shifts (Writes) | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $\dfrac{n^2}{4}$ | $0$ | Each shift is one assignment |
| Auxiliary Space | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | True in-place sort |
| Stability | **Stable** | **Stable** | **Stable** | Equal keys retain order |
| Online Property | Yes | Yes | Yes | Can sort streaming data |
| Adaptive Property | Yes | Yes | Yes | Faster on nearly-sorted input |

**Mathematical Form of the Time Complexity:**

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \left( \text{comparisons at iteration } i \right)$$

For **worst case** (reverse-sorted input, $i$ comparisons at iteration $i$):

$$T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)$$

For **best case** (already sorted input, only 1 comparison per iteration):

$$T_{\text{best}}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} 1 = n - 1 = O(n)$$

For **average case** (each element is equally likely to be inserted at any of the $i+1$ positions):

$$T_{\text{avg}}(n) \approx \frac{1}{4} n^2 = O(n^2)$$

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where Insertion Sort is used in industry:**
> - **Small array sorting (n < 50):** Modern hybrid algorithms like **Timsort** (Python, Java) and **Introsort** (C++) use Insertion Sort as the base case for small sub-arrays because of its low overhead and cache efficiency.
> - **Nearly-sorted data:** Databases and ETL pipelines use Insertion Sort variants for "almost sorted" datasets where it runs in near-linear time.
> - **Online systems:** When data arrives in a stream (e.g., log aggregators, real-time sensor data), Insertion Sort is the only elementary $O(1)$-space online sort.
> - **Embedded systems:** Limited memory environments benefit from its $O(1)$ auxiliary space.
> - **Stable sorting requirement:** When two records have equal keys but the original order matters (e.g., secondary sort by timestamp), Insertion Sort guarantees stability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example (Trace Through)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sort the following array using Insertion Sort and show the state of the array after every outer-loop iteration.**
>
> Input: $A = [\,5,\ 2,\ 4,\ 6,\ 1,\ 3\,]$, $n = 6$.

We will perform a **complete trace** by showing the array state, the current key, and the inner-loop activity for every iteration $i = 1$ through $i = 5$. This level of detail is exactly what a KTU 14-mark question demands.

### Iteration $i = 1$

- **Sorted region:** $A[0 \ldots 0] = [\,5\,]$
- **Unsorted region:** $A[1 \ldots 5] = [\,2,\ 4,\ 6,\ 1,\ 3\,]$
- **Key selected:** $\text{key} = A[1] = 2$
- **Inner loop:** $j = 0$, $A[0] = 5$, condition $A[0] > \text{key}$ $\Rightarrow$ $5 > 2$ is **TRUE**.
  - Shift: $A[1] = A[0] = 5$. Array becomes $[\,5,\ \mathbf{5},\ 4,\ 6,\ 1,\ 3\,]$.
  - Decrement: $j = -1$. Loop exits.
- **Insertion:** $A[j+1] = A[0] = \text{key} = 2$. Array becomes $[\,\mathbf{2},\ 5,\ 4,\ 6,\ 1,\ 3\,]$.
- **Comparisons this iteration:** 1. **Shifts:** 1.

**Array state after $i = 1$:** $[\,2,\ 5,\ 4,\ 6,\ 1,\ 3\,]$ — sorted prefix of length 2.

### Iteration $i = 2$

- **Sorted region:** $A[0 \ldots 1] = [\,2,\ 5\,]$
- **Key selected:** $\text{key} = A[2] = 4$
- **Inner loop:** $j = 1$, $A[1] = 5$, condition $5 > 4$ is **TRUE**.
  - Shift: $A[2] = A[1] = 5$. Array becomes $[\,2,\ 5,\ \mathbf{5},\ 6,\ 1,\ 3\,]$.
  - $j = 0$, $A[0] = 2$, condition $2 > 4$ is **FALSE**. Loop exits.
- **Insertion:** $A[j+1] = A[1] = 4$. Array becomes $[\,2,\ \mathbf{4},\ 5,\ 6,\ 1,\ 3\,]$.
- **Comparisons:** 2. **Shifts:** 1.

**Array state after $i = 2$:** $[\,2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6,\ 1,\ 3\,]$ — sorted prefix of length 3.

### Iteration $i = 3$

- **Key selected:** $\text{key} = A[3] = 6$
- **Inner loop:** $j = 2$, $A[2] = 5$, condition $5 > 6$ is **FALSE** on the very first check.
  - Loop exits immediately. **No shifts performed.**
- **Insertion:** $A[j+1] = A[3] = 6$. Array **unchanged**.
- **Comparisons:** 1. **Shifts:** 0.

**Array state after $i = 3$:** $[\,2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6,\ 1,\ 3\,]$ — sorted prefix of length 4.

### Iteration $i = 4$

- **Key selected:** $\text{key} = A[4] = 1$
- **Inner loop:** $j = 3$, $A[3] = 6 > 1$ $\Rightarrow$ shift: $A[4] = 6$. $j = 2$.
  - $A[2] = 5 > 1$ $\Rightarrow$ shift: $A[3] = 5$. $j = 1$.
  - $A[1] = 4 > 1$ $\Rightarrow$ shift: $A[2] = 4$. $j = 0$.
  - $A[0] = 2 > 1$ $\Rightarrow$ shift: $A[1] = 2$. $j = -1$. Loop exits.
- **Insertion:** $A[0] = 1$. Array becomes $[\,\mathbf{1},\ 2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6,\ 3\,]$.
- **Comparisons:** 4. **Shifts:** 4.

**Array state after $i = 4$:** $[\,1,\ 2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6,\ 3\,]$ — sorted prefix of length 5.

### Iteration $i = 5$

- **Key selected:** $\text{key} = A[5] = 3$
- **Inner loop:** $j = 4$, $A[4] = 6 > 3$ $\Rightarrow$ shift: $A[5] = 6$. $j = 3$.
  - $A[3] = 5 > 3$ $\Rightarrow$ shift: $A[4] = 5$. $j = 2$.
  - $A[2] = 4 > 3$ $\Rightarrow$ shift: $A[3] = 4$. $j = 1$.
  - $A[1] = 2 > 3$ is **FALSE**. Loop exits.
- **Insertion:** $A[2] = 3$. Array becomes $[\,1,\ 2,\ \mathbf{3},\ 4,\ 5,\ 6\,]$.
- **Comparisons:** 4. **Shifts:** 3.

**Final sorted array:** $[\,1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6\,]$ ✅

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Iteration-by-Iteration Summary

| **Iteration ($i$)** | **Key** | **Array Before** | **Array After** | **Comparisons** | **Shifts** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | $[5,2,4,6,1,3]$ | $[2,5,4,6,1,3]$ | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 4 | $[2,5,4,6,1,3]$ | $[2,4,5,6,1,3]$ | 2 | 1 |
| 3 | 6 | $[2,4,5,6,1,3]$ | $[2,4,5,6,1,3]$ | 1 | 0 |
| 4 | 1 | $[2,4,5,6,1,3]$ | $[1,2,4,5,6,3]$ | 4 | 4 |
| 5 | 3 | $[1,2,4,5,6,3]$ | $[1,2,3,4,5,6]$ | 4 | 3 |
| **Total** | — | — | — | **12** | **9** |

> [!TIP]
> **Verification of the formula:** Total comparisons = $12$. Plugging $n = 6$ into the worst-case comparison formula $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{6 \cdot 5}{2} = 15$. Our value $12 < 15$ because the input was **not the worst case** (worst case would be a reverse-sorted input). This reinforces the **adaptive** property of Insertion Sort.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Worst-Case Time Complexity

We want to prove that the worst-case running time of Insertion Sort is $\Theta(n^2)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant operation.** Each execution of the **inner `WHILE` loop** performs one comparison (`A[j] > key`) and one assignment (`A[j+1] ← A[j]`). The outer loop runs $n - 1$ times.

**Step 2 — Count inner-loop iterations in the worst case.** In the worst case (input sorted in **reverse order**), the key must travel all the way to the leftmost position, meaning the inner loop performs $i$ comparisons at iteration $i$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n - 1$.

**Step 3 — Sum the arithmetic series:**

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i = 1 + 2 + 3 + \ldots + (n-1)
$$

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \frac{(n-1) \cdot n}{2} = \frac{n^2 - n}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply asymptotic notation.** Since the highest-order term is $\frac{n^2}{2}$, and constant factors are dropped in Big-O notation:

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \frac{n^2 - n}{2} = O(n^2)
$$

**Step 5 — Lower bound justification.** To prove this is also $\Omega(n^2)$, observe that *any* comparison-based sort that examines every adjacent pair at least once requires at least quadratic time on reverse-sorted input. Insertion Sort touches every inversion exactly once via its shift operation, and a reverse-sorted array of size $n$ has $\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ inversions. Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \Theta(n^2)
$$

## 3.4 Loop Invariant Proof (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Loop Invariant of Insertion Sort:**
> *"At the start of each iteration $i$ of the outer `FOR` loop, the sub-array $A[0 \ldots i-1]$ consists of the elements originally in $A[0 \ldots i-1]$, but in sorted order."*

**Proof Structure (must include all three parts for full marks):**

- **Initialization (Base Case):** When $i = 1$, the sub-array $A[0 \ldots 0]$ contains only the single element $A[0]$. A single element is trivially sorted, so the invariant holds. ✓
- **Maintenance (Inductive Step):** Assume the invariant holds at the start of iteration $i$. The body of the loop selects `key = A[i]` and inserts it into its correct sorted position within $A[0 \ldots i-1]$, shifting larger elements one position right. After insertion, $A[0 \ldots i]$ contains the original $i + 1$ elements in sorted order. The invariant is preserved for the next iteration ($i + 1$). ✓
- **Termination:** The loop terminates when $i = n$. By the invariant, the sub-array $A[0 \ldots n-1]$ (the entire array) consists of the original $n$ elements in sorted order. This is exactly the post-condition we want. ✓

This three-part proof structure is a **direct application of mathematical induction** and is the canonical KTU 14-mark answer template for any sorting algorithm analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of Insertion Sort, mapping the data flow between the major operational units of the algorithm. This is the Mermaid-safe equivalent of a physical schematic for an algorithmic topic.

    innerLoop -- YES --> shiftOp["SHIFT A OF J PLUS 1 EQUALS A OF J DECREMENT J"]
    shiftOp --> innerLoop
    innerLoop -- NO --> insertOp["INSERT A OF J PLUS 1 EQUALS KEY"]
    insertOp --> incI["INCREMENT I EQUALS I PLUS 1"]
    incI --> outerLoop
    outerLoop -- NO --> outputBlock["OUTPUT SORTED ARRAY A"]
