# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, As per 2024 Scheme]`

**Define Insertion Sort. State any two of its salient properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — full credit requires all three sub-points):**

Insertion Sort is a comparison-based, in-place sorting algorithm that builds the final sorted array one element at a time by repeatedly picking the next unsorted element and inserting it into its correct position within the already-sorted left portion of the array.

**Two salient properties (1.5 marks each):**

- **Stability:** It is a stable sort — equal-keyed elements retain their original relative order. This is because the inner `WHILE` condition is strict ($A[j] > \text{key}$), so an element equal to the key is never shifted past it.
- **Adaptive nature with best case $O(n)$:** When the input is already sorted, the inner `WHILE` loop exits immediately on its first comparison at every iteration, leading to a total of $n - 1$ comparisons and $O(n)$ running time.

*(Other acceptable properties: in-place, online, simple to implement, low-overhead for small n.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**Differentiate between Insertion Sort and Selection Sort in terms of best-case time complexity and stability.**

**Model Answer (tabular format for 3 marks):**

| **Property** | **Insertion Sort** | **Selection Sort** |
|---|---|---|
| Best-case time complexity | $O(n)$ — adaptive | $O(n^2)$ — not adaptive |
| Stability | Stable | Unstable (in the standard formulation) |
| Mechanism | Inserts next element into sorted region | Selects minimum and places it at the next position |

**Examiner's note:** The **stability** distinction is the most-likely sub-part. Students must mention that Insertion Sort's strict inequality `A[j] > key` is what makes it stable, while Selection Sort's swap-based final placement of the minimum can jump over equal elements.

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Algorithm + Trace + Complexity

> **`[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern, 14-Mark Module 4 Question]`**
>
> **(a)** Write the algorithm for Insertion Sort to sort an array of $n$ elements in ascending order. Explain the algorithm with a suitable example by showing the contents of the array after each pass. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Analyze the time complexity of Insertion Sort in the best case, average case, and worst case. State its space complexity and explain why it is called a stable sorting algorithm. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 marks)

**Algorithm (3 marks — must be in clean pseudocode, NOT Python):**

```
ALGORITHM InsertionSort(A[0..n-1])
1.  FOR i ← 1 TO n-1 DO
2.        key ← A[i]
3.        j   ← i - 1
4.        WHILE j ≥ 0 AND A[j] > key DO
5.              A[j+1] ← A[j]
6.              j ← j - 1
7.        END WHILE
8.        A[j+1] ← key
9.  END FOR
10. RETURN A
```

**Trace Example (4 marks — full array after each outer-loop iteration):**

Take $A = [\,8,\ 3,\ 5,\ 1\,]$.

- After $i = 1$, key $= 3$, array $\to$ $[\,3,\ 8,\ 5,\ 1\,]$
- After $i = 2$, key $= 5$, array $\to$ $[\,3,\ 5,\ 8,\ 1\,]$
- After $i = 3$, key $= 1$, array $\to$ $[\,1,\ 3,\ 5,\ 8\,]$

---

### Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 marks)

**Best case $O(n)$ (2 marks):** When the input is already sorted, the inner `WHILE` loop's condition `A[j] > key` is **false** at the very first check for every iteration $i$. Thus, only $n - 1$ comparisons and no shifts occur, giving $T_{\text{best}}(n) = n - 1 = O(n)$.

**Average case $O(n^2)$ (2 marks):** On average, the key is equally likely to be inserted at any of the $i + 1$ positions in the sorted region of length $i$. This gives approximately $\frac{i}{2}$ comparisons per iteration, summing to $\frac{1}{4} n^2 = O(n^2)$.

**Worst case $O(n^2)$ (2 marks):** When the input is reverse-sorted, the key must travel to position $0$ at every iteration, requiring $i$ comparisons and $i$ shifts. Total:

$$
T_{\text{worst}}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)
$$

**Space complexity and stability (1 mark):** Space complexity is $O(1)$ — the algorithm uses only the variables `key` and `j` in addition to the input array. It is **stable** because the comparison `A[j] > key` uses strict greater-than; an element equal to `key` will **not** be shifted past it, preserving the original relative order of equal elements.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern, 14-Mark Module 4 Question]`**
>
> **(a)** Explain the Insertion Sort algorithm with a real-world analogy. Trace the algorithm on the array $A = [\,9,\ 5,\ 1,\ 4,\ 3\,]$ and show the array contents after every pass. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Prove the correctness of Insertion Sort using the loop-invariant technique. Also state and justify the time and space complexity. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part B(a) (7 marks)

**Real-world analogy (2 marks):** Sorting a hand of playing cards — as new cards are picked up, each is inserted into its correct position among the cards already sorted in the hand, with larger cards shifted to the right to make space.

**Trace (5 marks):**

For $A = [\,9,\ 5,\ 1,\ 4,\ 3\,]$:

- **Pass $i=1$:** key $= 5$. Compare with $9$ ($9>5$, shift). Insert $5$ at index 0. Array $\to$ $[\,5,\ 9,\ 1,\ 4,\ 3\,]$
- **Pass $i=2$:** key $= 1$. Compare with $9$ (shift), then $5$ (shift). Insert $1$ at index 0. Array $\to$ $[\,1,\ 5,\ 9,\ 4,\ 3\,]$
- **Pass $i=3$:** key $= 4$. Compare with $9$ (shift), then $5$ (shift). Insert $4$ at index 1. Array $\to$ $[\,1,\ 4,\ 5,\ 9,\ 3\,]$
- **Pass $i=4$:** key $= 3$. Compare with $9$ (shift), $5$ (shift), $4$ (shift), $1$ (no shift, $1 < 3$). Insert $3$ at index 1. Array $\to$ $[\,1,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 9\,]$

Final sorted array: $[\,1,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 9\,]$.

---

### Model Solution — Part B(b) (7 marks)

**Loop Invariant (1 mark):** *"At the start of each iteration $i$ of the outer `FOR` loop, the sub-array $A[0 \ldots i-1]$ contains the elements originally in those positions, arranged in sorted order."*

**Initialization (2 marks):** When $i = 1$, the sub-array $A[0 \ldots 0]$ contains a single element, which is trivially sorted. The invariant holds.

**Maintenance (2 marks):** Assume the invariant holds at the start of iteration $i$. The body of the loop inserts `key = A[i]` into the sorted sub-array $A[0 \ldots i-1]$ by shifting elements greater than `key` one position to the right. After insertion, $A[0 \ldots i]$ consists of the original $i+1$ elements in sorted order. The invariant is therefore preserved for the next iteration.

**Termination (1 mark):** The loop ends when $i = n$. By the invariant, $A[0 \ldots n-1]$ is the sorted version of the original array. Hence the algorithm is correct.

**Complexity statement (1 mark):** Worst/average $O(n^2)$, best $O(n)$, space $O(1)$. Justification: derived from the inner-loop count $\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ in the worst case, and $n-1$ in the best case.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most-commonly lose marks in Insertion Sort questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "swap" with "shift" (–1 to –2 marks):** Insertion Sort performs **single assignments** (`A[j+1] ← A[j]`), not full three-step swaps. Writing a swap-based inner loop (e.g., `temp = A[j]; A[j] = A[j+1]; A[j+1] = temp;`) loses credit. It also changes the algorithm's write-count from $O(n^2)$ assignments to $O(n^2)$ swaps, which is a different complexity.
> 2. **Off-by-one error in loop bounds (–1 mark):** Writing `FOR i ← 1 TO n` instead of `FOR i ← 1 TO n-1` causes an out-of-bounds access in some languages. Use the inclusive-exclusive convention `i = 1, 2, ..., n-1`.
> 3. **Forgetting the post-insert assignment `A[j+1] ← key` (–1 mark):** Without this final line, the key value is **lost** (overwritten by shifts), and the algorithm produces wrong output.
> 4. **Stating Insertion Sort is unstable (–1 mark):** This is a common misconception. It **is stable** due to the strict `>` comparison. The correct justification must be explicitly written.
> 5. **Quoting best-case as $O(n^2)$:** Marks are deducted for not recognizing the $O(n)$ best case, which is the *defining* feature of Insertion Sort relative to Bubble Sort and Selection Sort.
> 6. **Incomplete loop-invariant proof:** Listing only **Initialization** and **Maintenance** but forgetting **Termination** loses 1–2 marks. All three parts are mandatory for full credit on a 7-mark correctness sub-question.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Insertion Sort (Module 4)**
>
> - **Mechanism:** Build a sorted left sub-array one element at a time by inserting each new key into its correct position via rightward shifts of larger elements.
> - **Pseudocode core:** outer `FOR i = 1 to n-1`, inner `WHILE j ≥ 0 AND A[j] > key`, shift `A[j+1] ← A[j]`, finally `A[j+1] ← key`.
> - **Time complexity — Best:** $O(n)$ — already sorted input, single comparison per iteration.
> - **Time complexity — Average & Worst:** $O(n^2)$ — derived from $\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
> - **Space complexity:** $O(1)$ auxiliary — true **in-place** sort.
> - **Stability:** **Stable** — strict `>` in the inner loop preserves the original order of equal elements.
> - **Online property:** **Yes** — can sort a stream as data arrives.
> - **Adaptive property:** **Yes** — faster on nearly-sorted inputs.
> - **Number of inversions relation:** A reverse-sorted array has $\binom{n}{2}$ inversions; Insertion Sort's shift count equals the number of inversions.
> - **Comparison with siblings:** Faster than Bubble Sort in practice (no full swaps) and faster than Selection Sort on nearly-sorted data; equivalent $O(n^2)$ in the worst case.
> - **Industry use:** Base case of Timsort (Python), Introsort (C++), and Pattern-defeating Quicksort for sub-arrays of size $\leq$ 32 or 16.
> - **Common exam pitfalls:** Strict-greater-than for stability proof, off-by-one in loop bounds, missing `A[j+1] ← key` line, and incomplete three-part loop invariant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 | Quick Sort — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Sort** is a highly efficient, comparison-based, **divide-and-conquer** internal sorting algorithm. It operates by selecting a **pivot element** from the array and **partitioning** the remaining elements into two sub-arrays — those *less than* the pivot and those *greater than* the pivot. The pivot then resides in its final sorted position. The process is applied **recursively** to the sub-arrays until the base case of one or zero elements is reached.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEECT522)**, Quick Sort is classified under the *Divide and Conquer* paradigm in Sorting Techniques, alongside Merge Sort, and forms a high-weightage topic for Part B (14-mark) questions in End Semester Examinations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Classroom Line-Up" Analogy** — Imagine a chaotic line of students of varying heights. The teacher picks **one student (the pivot)** and asks everyone shorter to stand on the *left* and everyone taller to stand on the *right*. The chosen student is now exactly in the correct final position. The teacher then repeats the same process on the left group and the right group independently, until everyone is sorted.

This mirrors Quick Sort perfectly:
- **Pivot** → the reference student chosen at each step.
- **Partitioning** → splitting the crowd into "shorter" and "taller" groups.
- **Recursion** → each sub-group is sorted independently.

## 1.3 Why Quick Sort Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Quick Sort has a **better average-case cache behavior** than Merge Sort due to in-place partitioning.
> - It is the **default sorting algorithm** in many standard library implementations (e.g., C's `qsort`, Unix).
> - Mastering its **partition logic, pivot selection strategies, and recurrence relation** is essential for the 14-mark Part B questions.

---

## 1.4 Three Pillars of Quick Sort

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Pivot Selection** | Choosing the partitioning element (first / last / middle / median-of-three / random) |
| **Partition Routine** | Rearranging the array such that pivot ends in its final sorted index |
| **Recursive Calls** | Applying Quick Sort to the left and right sub-arrays independently |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4 | Quick Sort — Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Framework

Quick Sort, in its most general recursive form, follows three logical steps:

1. **Base Case Check** — If the sub-array has `0` or `1` elements, it is already sorted → return immediately.
2. **Partition Step** — Select a `pivot` element and rearrange the array such that:
   - All elements with value `< pivot` lie to the **left** of the pivot.
   - All elements with value `> pivot` lie to the **right** of the pivot.
   - The pivot is placed at its **final sorted position** `q`.
3. **Recursive Conquering** — Recursively call Quick Sort on:
   - The sub-array `A[low .. q-1]`
   - The sub-array `A[q+1 .. high]`

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike **Merge Sort**, Quick Sort performs all its heavy work in the *partition* phase, while the *combine* (merge) phase is trivial (nothing to do).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Aspect | Formula / Condition | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Recurrence (Best Case)** | $T(n) = 2 \cdot T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | Pivot always splits array in half |
| **Recurrence (Average Case)** | $T(n) = \frac{2}{n}\sum_{q=1}^{n} T(q-1) + \Theta(n)$ | Expected over all pivot positions |
| **Recurrence (Worst Case)** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + \Theta(n)$ | Already sorted / reverse-sorted input |
| **Best-Case Time** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Balanced partitions |
| **Average-Case Time** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Random pivot gives expected balance |
| **Worst-Case Time** | $\Theta(n^{2})$ | Highly unbalanced partitions |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(\log n)$ | Recursive call stack (in-place) |
| **In-Place?** | **Yes** | Auxiliary space is $O(\log n)$ for stack |
| **Stable?** | **No** | Equal elements may swap relative order |
| **Partition Index** | $q = \text{Partition}(A, low, high)$ | Returns final position of pivot |
| **Number of Comparisons (Avg)** | $\approx 1.39 \, n \log_{2} n$ | Empirically faster constant factor than Merge Sort |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Board Examiner Note:** When answering time-complexity questions, you must explicitly mention the **Master's Theorem application** (Cases 1 or 2 yield $n \log n$) and the **recursion-tree summation** for the worst case yielding $n^{2}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> Quick Sort is the sorting engine behind:
> - **Database indexing systems** (e.g., in-memory B-tree node construction).
> - **Operating system kernel** priority queue pre-sorting.
> - **Numerical computation libraries** (e.g., `numpy.argsort` uses introsort — Quick Sort + Heap Sort hybrid).
> - **Genomic sequence alignment** where in-place sorting is memory-critical.

The reason engineers prefer Quick Sort over Merge Sort in memory-bound environments is its **in-place nature**, which provides superior **cache locality** and minimal auxiliary memory consumption.

## 2.4 Pivot Selection Strategies (High-Yield for KTU)

1. **First Element as Pivot** — Simplest, but degenerates on sorted input.
2. **Last Element as Pivot** — Common in textbooks; vulnerable to worst case.
3. **Random Pivot** — Expected $O(n \log n)$ regardless of input distribution.
4. **Median-of-Three** — Picks median of first, middle, and last elements; robust against ordered inputs.
5. **True Median** — Theoretically optimal, but computing it is $O(n)$ per partition, often impractical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 4 | Quick Sort — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Lomuto Partition Scheme — Full Derivation

The **Lomuto Partition** is the most commonly tested partition scheme in KTU examinations. We will derive it step-by-step.

### Algorithm Statement

Given sub-array `A[low .. high]`, with pivot = `A[high]`, partition returns index `i` such that all `A[low .. i-1] \le pivot` and `A[i] = pivot` and `A[i+1 .. high] > pivot`.

### Step-by-Step Pseudocode Logic

```
PARTITION(A, low, high):
    pivot ← A[high]              // Step 1: Choose last element as pivot
    i ← low - 1                  // Step 2: Boundary of "less-than" region

    FOR j ← low TO high - 1 DO   // Step 3: Scan the array
        IF A[j] ≤ pivot THEN
            i ← i + 1            // Step 4: Extend the less-than region
            SWAP A[i] WITH A[j]  // Step 5: Bring A[j] into the region
        END IF
    END FOR

    SWAP A[i + 1] WITH A[high]   // Step 6: Place pivot in final position
    RETURN i + 1                 // Step 7: Return pivot's final index
END PARTITION
```

### Exhaustive Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trace Quick Sort on array $A = [10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5]$** using Lomuto partition, last-element pivot.

**Initial Call:** `QuickSort(A, 0, 5)`

#### Pass 1 — First Partition Call

- `low = 0`, `high = 5`
- `pivot = A[5] = 5`
- `i = -1`

Scanning loop:

| j | A[j] | A[j] ≤ 5? | i (after) | Array state |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 10 | No | -1 | [10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5] |
| 1 | 7  | No | -1 | [10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5] |
| 2 | 8  | No | -1 | [10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5] |
| 3 | 9  | No | -1 | [10, 7, 8, 9, 1, 5] |
| 4 | 1  | **Yes** | 0  | [1, 7, 8, 9, 10, 5] |

Final swap: `A[0+1] = A[1]` swapped with `A[5] = 5`

Resulting array: $[1, 5, 8, 9, 10, 7]$

Pivot `5` is now at index `1` (its final sorted position). Return `q = 1`.

**Recursive Call 1:** `QuickSort(A, 0, 0)` → Base case (single element), return.

**Recursive Call 2:** `QuickSort(A, 2, 5)`

#### Pass 2 — Partition on Sub-array `A[2..5] = [8, 9, 10, 7]`

- `low = 2`, `high = 5`
- `pivot = A[5] = 7`
- `i = 1`

Scanning loop (j runs from 2 to 4):

| j | A[j] | A[j] ≤ 7? | i (after) | Array state |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 8 | No | 1 | [1, 5, 8, 9, 10, 7] |
| 3 | 9 | No | 1 | [1, 5, 8, 9, 10, 7] |
| 4 | 10 | No | 1 | [1, 5, 8, 9, 10, 7] |

Final swap: `A[1+1] = A[2]` swapped with `A[5] = 7`

Resulting array: $[1, 5, 7, 9, 10, 8]$

Pivot `7` is now at index `2`. Return `q = 2`.

**Recursive Call 3:** `QuickSort(A, 2, 1)` → Invalid (low > high), return.
**Recursive Call 4:** `QuickSort(A, 3, 5)`

#### Pass 3 — Partition on Sub-array `A[3..5] = [9, 10, 8]`

- `low = 3`, `high = 5`
- `pivot = A[5] = 8`
- `i = 2`

Scanning loop (j runs from 3 to 4):

| j | A[j] | A[j] ≤ 8? | i (after) | Array state |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | 9  | No | 2 | [1, 5, 7, 9, 10, 8] |
| 4 | 10 | No | 2 | [1, 5, 7, 9, 10, 8] |

Final swap: `A[2+1] = A[3]` swapped with `A[5] = 8`

Resulting array: $[1, 5, 7, 8, 10, 9]$

Pivot `8` is now at index `3`. Return `q = 3`.

**Recursive Call 5:** `QuickSort(A, 3, 2)` → Invalid, return.
**Recursive Call 6:** `QuickSort(A, 4, 5)`

#### Pass 4 — Partition on Sub-array `A[4..5] = [10, 9]`

- `low = 4`, `high = 5`
- `pivot = A[5] = 9`
- `i = 3`

Scanning loop (j = 4):

| j | A[j] | A[j] ≤ 9? | i (after) | Array state |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 10 | No | 3 | [1, 5, 7, 8, 10, 9] |

Final swap: `A[3+1] = A[4]` swapped with `A[5] = 9`

Resulting array: $[1, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10]$

Pivot `9` is now at index `4`. Return `q = 4`.

**Recursive Call 7:** `QuickSort(A, 4, 3)` → Invalid, return.
**Recursive Call 8:** `QuickSort(A, 5, 5)` → Base case, return.

### Final Sorted Array

$$
A_{\text{sorted}} = [1, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10]
$$

---

## 3.2 Recurrence Relation Analysis — Step-by-Step Derivation

### Best Case (Balanced Partition)

When pivot splits array into two equal halves:

$$
T(n) = 2 \cdot T(n/2) + c \cdot n
$$

Applying the **Master's Theorem**, with $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = cn$:

$$
n^{\log_{b} a} = n^{\log_{2} 2} = n^{1} = n
$$

Since $f(n) = \Theta(n)$ matches $n^{\log_{b} a}$, this is **Case 2** of the Master's Theorem:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

### Worst Case (Highly Unbalanced Partition)

When pivot is always the smallest (or largest) element (e.g., sorted input with first-element pivot):

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + c \cdot n = T(n-1) + c \cdot n
$$

Expanding via recursion tree:

$$
T(n) = c \cdot n + c \cdot (n-1) + c \cdot (n-2) + \dots + c \cdot 1
$$

$$
T(n) = c \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} k = c \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^{2})
$$

### Average Case (Randomized Pivot)

Over all $n$ possible pivot positions $q$:

$$
T(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{q=1}^{n} \big[ T(q-1) + T(n-q) \big] + c \cdot n
$$

By symmetry, $\sum_{q=1}^{n} T(q-1) = \sum_{q=1}^{n} T(n-q)$, so:

$$
T(n) = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{q=0}^{n-1} T(q) + c \cdot n
$$

Solving this recurrence (via substitution $S(n) = T(n)/n$ or generating functions) yields:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Quick Sort | Merge Sort | Heap Sort |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Best Time** | $n \log n$ | $n \log n$ | $n \log n$ |
| **Average Time** | $n \log n$ | $n \log n$ | $n \log n$ |
| **Worst Time** | $n^{2}$ | $n \log n$ | $n \log n$ |
| **Space** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Stable** | No | Yes | No |
| **In-Place** | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Cache-Friendly** | **High** | Moderate | Low |
| **Practical Constant Factor** | **Lowest** | Moderate | Highest |

# Module 4 | Quick Sort — KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define Quick Sort. Mention any two advantages of Quick Sort over Merge Sort.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Sort** is a **divide-and-conquer** sorting algorithm that partitions an array around a chosen **pivot element** such that elements smaller than the pivot are placed to its left and elements larger are placed to its right, after which the two sub-arrays are sorted recursively.

**Two Advantages over Merge Sort:**

1. **In-Place Operation:** Quick Sort requires only $O(\log n)$ auxiliary stack space, whereas Merge Sort requires $O(n)$ auxiliary memory for the merge step.
2. **Superior Cache Locality:** Quick Sort exhibits better practical performance due to sequential memory access patterns, leading to fewer cache misses compared to Merge Sort's recursive copying.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is the role of a pivot in Quick Sort? What happens if the pivot is always the smallest element?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **pivot** is the reference element used to partition the array into two sub-arrays. It determines the final position of that element in the sorted array after partitioning.

**Consequence of always choosing the smallest pivot:**

- Each partition produces a **highly unbalanced split**: one sub-array of size $n-1$ and another of size $0$.
- The recurrence becomes $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + \Theta(n) = \Theta(n^{2})$.
- This is the **worst-case scenario**, occurring with sorted or reverse-sorted input when the first/last element is chosen as the pivot.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Quick Sort algorithm using the **Lomuto partition scheme** with a suitable pseudocode. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Sort the following array using Quick Sort and show all partition steps:

$$
A = [\, 33, 12, 45, 8, 27, 16, 9, 21 \,]
$$

Assume the **last element is chosen as the pivot**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Quick Sort Algorithm (Lomuto Partition Scheme):**

```
QUICKSORT(A, low, high):
    IF low < high THEN
        q ← PARTITION(A, low, high)
        QUICKSORT(A, low, q - 1)
        QUICKSORT(A, q + 1, high)
    END IF
END QUICKSORT
```

```
PARTITION(A, low, high):
    pivot ← A[high]
    i ← low - 1
    FOR j ← low TO high - 1 DO
        IF A[j] ≤ pivot THEN
            i ← i + 1
            SWAP A[i] WITH A[j]
        END IF
    END FOR
    SWAP A[i + 1] WITH A[high]
    RETURN i + 1
END PARTITION
```

**Explanation of Key Steps:**

1. The base case `low < high` prevents infinite recursion on empty/single-element sub-arrays.
2. `PARTITION` returns the final index `q` of the pivot.
3. The pivot is now in its **final sorted position** and is excluded from further recursive calls.
4. Recursive calls sort the two partitioned regions independently.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Initial Array:** $A = [33, 12, 45, 8, 27, 16, 9, 21]$, indices `0` to `7`.

**Pass 1:** `PARTITION(A, 0, 7)`, pivot = `A[7] = 21`, `i = -1`

| j | A[j] | A[j] ≤ 21? | i (after) | Array State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 33 | No | -1 | [33, 12, 45, 8, 27, 16, 9, 21] |
| 1 | 12 | **Yes** | 0 | [12, 33, 45, 8, 27, 16, 9, 21] |
| 2 | 45 | No | 0 | [12, 33, 45, 8, 27, 16, 9, 21] |
| 3 | 8  | **Yes** | 1 | [12, 8, 45, 33, 27, 16, 9, 21] |
| 4 | 27 | No | 1 | [12, 8, 45, 33, 27, 16, 9, 21] |
| 5 | 16 | **Yes** | 2 | [12, 8, 16, 33, 27, 45, 9, 21] |
| 6 | 9  | **Yes** | 3 | [12, 8, 16, 9, 27, 45, 33, 21] |

Final swap: `A[4] ↔ A[7]` → Array: $[12, 8, 16, 9, 21, 45, 33, 27]$

Pivot `21` lands at index `4`. Recursive calls: `QS(0, 3)` and `QS(5, 7)`.

**Pass 2:** `PARTITION(A, 0, 3)`, pivot = `A[3] = 9`, `i = -1`

| j | A[j] | A[j] ≤ 9? | i (after) | Array State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 12 | No | -1 | [12, 8, 16, 9, 21, 45, 33, 27] |
| 1 | 8  | **Yes** | 0 | [8, 12, 16, 9, 21, 45, 33, 27] |
| 2 | 16 | No | 0 | [8, 12, 16, 9, 21, 45, 33, 27] |

Final swap: `A[1] ↔ A[3]` → Array: $[8, 9, 16, 12, 21, 45, 33, 27]$

Pivot `9` at index `1`. Recursive calls: `QS(0, 0)` (base) and `QS(2, 3)`.

**Pass 3:** `PARTITION(A, 2, 3)`, pivot = `A[3] = 12`, `i = 1`

| j | A[j] | A[j] ≤ 12? | i (after) | Array State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 16 | No | 1 | [8, 9, 16, 12, 21, 45, 33, 27] |

Final swap: `A[2] ↔ A[3]` → Array: $[8, 9, 12, 16, 21, 45, 33, 27]$

Pivot `12` at index `2`. Recursive calls: `QS(2, 1)` (invalid) and `QS(3, 3)` (base).

**Pass 4:** `PARTITION(A, 5, 7)`, pivot = `A[7] = 27`, `i = 4`

| j | A[j] | A[j] ≤ 27? | i (after) | Array State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 45 | No | 4 | [8, 9, 12, 16, 21, 45, 33, 27] |
| 6 | 33 | No | 4 | [8, 9, 12, 16, 21, 45, 33, 27] |

Final swap: `A[5] ↔ A[7]` → Array: $[8, 9, 12, 16, 21, 27, 33, 45]$

Pivot `27` at index `5`. Recursive calls: `QS(5, 4)` (invalid) and `QS(6, 7)`.

**Pass 5:** `PARTITION(A, 6, 7)`, pivot = `A[7] = 45`, `i = 5`

| j | A[j] | A[j] ≤ 45? | i (after) | Array State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | 33 | **Yes** | 6 | [8, 9, 12, 16, 21, 27, 33, 45] |

Final swap: `A[7] ↔ A[7]` → Array: $[8, 9, 12, 16, 21, 27, 33, 45]$

Pivot `45` at index `7`. Both recursive calls are base cases.

### Final Sorted Array:

$$
A_{\text{sorted}} = [\, 8, \, 9, \, 12, \, 16, \, 21, \, 27, \, 33, \, 45 \,]
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **best-case** and **worst-case** time complexity of Quick Sort using recurrence relations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Hoare partition scheme** and compare it with the **Lomuto partition scheme** in terms of efficiency, number of swaps, and worst-case behavior. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Best Case Derivation:**

In the best case, the pivot is always the **median** of the sub-array, splitting it into two equal halves of size $n/2$ each.

$$
T(n) = 2 \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c \cdot n
$$

**Applying the Master's Theorem:**

Here $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = cn$.

$$
n^{\log_{b} a} = n^{\log_{2} 2} = n^{1} = n
$$

We compare $f(n) = cn$ with $n^{\log_{b} a} = n$. Since $f(n) = \Theta(n)$, this matches **Case 2** of the Master's Theorem.

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}
$$

**Worst Case Derivation:**

In the worst case, the pivot is always the **smallest** or **largest** element, producing a split of sizes $n-1$ and $0$.

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + c \cdot n = T(n-1) + c \cdot n
$$

**Solving by Recursion Tree Expansion:**

$$
T(n) = c \cdot n + c \cdot (n-1) + c \cdot (n-2) + \dots + c \cdot 1
$$

$$
T(n) = c \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} k = c \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Hoare Partition Scheme:**

The **Hoare partition** (proposed by C.A.R. Hoare in 1959) uses **two pointers** that move toward each other from opposite ends of the array. It performs **fewer swaps** on average than Lomuto.

```
HOARE_PARTITION(A, low, high):
    pivot ← A[low]                       // Step 1: First element as pivot
    i ← low - 1
    j ← high + 1

    REPEAT
        REPEAT
            j ← j - 1
        UNTIL A[j] ≤ pivot

        REPEAT
            i ← i + 1
        UNTIL A[i] ≥ pivot

        IF i < j THEN
            SWAP A[i] WITH A[j]
        END IF
    UNTIL i ≥ j

    RETURN j
END HOARE_PARTITION
```

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Parameter | Lomuto Partition | Hoare Partition |
|---|---|---|
| **Pivot Position** | Last element (or arbitrary) | First element (or arbitrary) |
| **Number of Swaps** | More (each smaller element triggers a swap) | **Fewer** (only on cross-over) |
| **Number of Comparisons** | Higher | **Lower** (~3x fewer) |
| **Returns Index** | Final position of pivot | **Partition point** (not necessarily pivot's final position) |
| **Worst Case** | $O(n^{2})$ with bad pivot | $O(n^{2})$ with bad pivot |
| **Efficiency on Sorted Data** | $O(n^{2})$ | $O(n^{2})$ |
| **Average Performance** | Good | **Better** (fewer swaps) |
| **Stability of Result** | Pivot at correct final index | Pivot may not be at final index |
| **Code Simplicity** | **Simpler** | More complex (two-pointer logic) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Hoare's scheme returns the **splitting point** (where partitions meet), not the pivot's final position. This means elements equal to the pivot can appear on **either side** of the returned index, requiring careful handling in recursion.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base case** `if(low < high)` — losing 1 mark on Part B.
> 2. **Confusing Lomuto and Hoare returns** — Hoare returns split point, not pivot index.
> 3. **Not showing all partition steps** in the trace — every swap and pointer update must be tabulated.
> 4. **Skipping the Master's Theorem justification** — simply writing $\Theta(n \log n)$ without showing the case analysis loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Omitting time complexity comparison** with Merge Sort and Heap Sort — at least one sentence comparing is expected.
> 6. **Failing to mention in-place nature** when asked about advantages — this is a frequently-tested KTU point.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Quick Sort:**

- ✅ Quick Sort is a **divide-and-conquer, in-place, comparison-based, unstable** sorting algorithm.
- ✅ **Average and Best Case:** $\Theta(n \log n)$ via Master's Theorem Case 2.
- ✅ **Worst Case:** $\Theta(n^{2})$ when pivot produces highly unbalanced partitions.
- ✅ **Space Complexity:** $O(\log n)$ due to recursive call stack.
- ✅ **Lomuto Partition:** simpler, pivot = last element, more swaps.
- ✅ **Hoare Partition:** two-pointer, fewer swaps, returns split point (not pivot index).
- ✅ **Pivot Selection Strategies:** first, last, middle, random, **median-of-three** (best practical).
- ✅ Quick Sort has the **lowest practical constant factor** among $O(n \log n)$ sorts.
- ✅ **Not stable** — equal elements may change relative order.
- ✅ **Recurrence (best):** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$.
- ✅ **Recurrence (worst):** $T(n) = T(n-1) + cn$.
- ✅ **Master's Theorem Case 2** is the standard KTU board answer for best/average cases.
- ✅ Quick Sort is **NOT** preferred when stability is required — use Merge Sort instead.
- ✅ **Randomized Quick Sort** has expected $O(n \log n)$ runtime regardless of input distribution.
- ✅ **Introsort** (used in production libraries) combines Quick Sort + Heap Sort to guarantee $O(n \log n)$ worst case.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Merge Sort and Heap Sort — Section 1

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Merge Sort — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Merge Sort** is a **divide-and-conquer** sorting algorithm that recursively divides an unsorted array of size **n** into two equal (or near-equal) halves, sorts each half independently using the same algorithm, and then **merges** the two sorted sub-arrays into a single, fully sorted array.

The algorithm strictly follows the **divide → conquer → combine** paradigm formalized by John von Neumann in 1945. It guarantees an asymptotic time complexity of **O(n log n)** in the **best, average, and worst cases**, making it one of the most reliable comparison-based sorting techniques used in production systems.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Tournament Bracket:**

Imagine **64 chess players** arriving at a national championship. Instead of trying to rank them all at once (which would be chaotic), the organizers:
1. **Divide** the 64 players into 32 random pairs.
2. **Conquer** each pair by playing a single match — the winner proceeds.
3. **Combine** the 32 winners into 16 pairs, repeat until 2 finalists remain, then play the final.

This is exactly what merge sort does with numbers — it doesn't compare every number with every other number (which would be O(n²)); it works on **logarithmic "rounds"** of pairwise merging, hence the **n log n** behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Merge Sort is a **stable** sorting algorithm — equal elements retain their original relative order. This property is critical in applications like sorting student records by name first, then by marks.

---

### 1.1.2 Heap Sort — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heap Sort** is a **comparison-based, in-place** sorting algorithm that leverages the **binary heap** data structure (specifically, a **max-heap** for ascending order). It first **builds a max-heap** from the unsorted input array, then repeatedly **extracts the maximum element**, swaps it to the end of the array, and **heapifies** the reduced heap — until the array is fully sorted.

Heap Sort was invented by **J. W. J. Williams** in 1964 and provides a guaranteed **O(n log n)** time bound with **O(1)** auxiliary space (in-place).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Corporate Hierarchy:**

Imagine a company where every manager is **strictly more senior** than both of their direct reports (a max-heap). The CEO is at the top.
- The CEO is the **maximum value** in the company.
- You "fire" the CEO, and the two immediate subordinates compete; the more senior one gets promoted to CEO.
- This promotion cascades downward — each new appointment triggers a re-shuffle ("heapify down").
- The fired CEOs form a perfectly sorted list in **descending order** at the back of the office.

This is Heap Sort — no extra office (no extra array) is needed; the heap structure lives **inside** the original array itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** Heap Sort is **not stable** — the swapping operations can disrupt the relative order of equal elements. It is, however, **in-place** with **O(1)** extra memory, which is its primary advantage over Merge Sort.

---

### 1.1.3 Comparative Snapshot

| Property | Merge Sort | Heap Sort |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategy** | Divide & Conquer (recursion) | Selection using Heap (in-place) |
| **Best Case** | **O(n log n)** | **O(n log n)** |
| **Average Case** | **O(n log n)** | **O(n log n)** |
| **Worst Case** | **O(n log n)** | **O(n log n)** |
| **Space Complexity** | **O(n)** auxiliary | **O(1)** in-place |
| **Stability** | **Stable** | **Not stable** |
| **Recursion** | Naturally recursive | Iterative (after build) |
| **Use Case** | Linked lists, external sort | Memory-constrained systems |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Merge Sort — Three-Phase Operational Analysis

### Phase 1: **DIVIDE** (Top-Down Recursion)

- Given an array `A[low...high]` of size **n = high - low + 1**.
- Compute the **midpoint**: `mid = ⌊(low + high) / 2⌋`.
- This partitions the array into two contiguous sub-arrays:
  - Left half: `A[low...mid]` of size `⌈n/2⌉`
  - Right half: `A[mid+1...high]` of size `⌊n/2⌋`
- The division continues recursively until each sub-array contains a **single element** (trivially sorted).

### Phase 2: **CONQUER** (Base Case)

- A sub-array of size **1** (or **0**) is by definition sorted.
- The recursion bottoms out at this base case: `T(1) = Θ(1)`.

### Phase 3: **COMBINE** (The Merge Procedure)

- Given two **already sorted** sub-arrays `L[0...n₁-1]` and `R[0...n₂-1]`, the merge procedure produces a single sorted output `A[low...high]`.
- **Algorithm:** Use three pointers `i`, `j`, `k`.
  - Compare `L[i]` and `R[j]`; copy the smaller into `A[k]`; advance the corresponding pointer.
  - When one sub-array is exhausted, copy the remainder of the other.
- Each element is compared and moved at most once → **merge cost = Θ(n)**.

### Why Merge Sort Achieves O(n log n) — The Recursion Tree Insight

At each **level ℓ** of the recursion tree (where ℓ ranges from `0` to `log₂ n`):
- The total number of sub-problems doubles → `2^ℓ` sub-problems.
- The size of each sub-problem halves → `n / 2^ℓ` elements.
- **Work per level** = `(number of sub-problems) × (cost per sub-problem)` = `2^ℓ × Θ(n / 2^ℓ)` = **`Θ(n)`**.
- There are exactly **`log₂ n + 1` levels**.

Total work = `(log₂ n + 1) × Θ(n)` = **`Θ(n log n)`**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** The recurrence `T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn` is solved via the **Master Theorem, Case 2** (a = 2, b = 2, f(n) = cn). Since `n^(log_b a) = n^1 = n` and `f(n) = Θ(n)`, the solution is `T(n) = Θ(n log n)`.

---

## 2.2 Heap Sort — Two-Phase Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Binary Heap Data Structure (Prerequisite)

A **binary heap** is a **complete binary tree** that satisfies the **heap property**:
- **Max-Heap:** Every parent node ≥ both its children → root is the global maximum.
- **Min-Heap:** Every parent node ≤ both its children → root is the global minimum.

**Array Representation** (1-indexed for cleaner math; 0-indexed in code):
- For node at index `i`:
  - **Parent:** `⌊i / 2⌋`
  - **Left Child:** `2i`
  - **Right Child:** `2i + 1`

For 0-indexed array:
- **Parent:** `⌊(i - 1) / 2⌋`
- **Left Child:** `2i + 1`
- **Right Child:** `2i + 2`

**Height of a heap of n nodes:** `h = ⌊log₂ n⌋`

### Phase 1: **BUILD-MAX-HEAP** (Bottom-Up Heapify)

- Call `MAX-HEAPIFY(A, i)` for `i` from `⌊n/2⌋` down to `1` (1-indexed) or `⌊(n-2)/2⌋` down to `0` (0-indexed).
- Leaves (indices `⌊n/2⌋+1` to `n`) are already valid 1-element heaps — so they are skipped.
- **Time complexity of BUILD-MAX-HEAP: Θ(n)** (not Θ(n log n) — a common student misconception!).

### Phase 2: **SORTING LOOP** (Repeated Extraction)

For `i` from `n` down to `2` (1-indexed):
1. **Swap** `A[1]` (the maximum) with `A[i]`.
2. **Reduce heap size** by 1 (logically exclude `A[i]` from the heap).
3. Call `MAX-HEAPIFY(A, 1)` to restore the max-heap property on the reduced heap.

**Cost per iteration:** O(log n) for the heapify → **Total: O(n log n)**.

### Why Build-Heap is O(n) and not O(n log n) — The Intuition

- Most nodes in a heap are near the **bottom** (leaves), where they require **0 or 1** heapify operations.
- Few nodes are near the **top** (root), where they require up to **log n** operations.
- The sum `Σ (nodes at level ℓ) × (work per node) = Σ (n / 2^ℓ) × ℓ` converges to **O(n)** when summed from ℓ = 0 to log n.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Trap:** Students often claim BUILD-MAX-HEAP is O(n log n) because each of the `n/2` calls to MAX-HEAPIFY "could" take O(log n). This is **incorrect** — most calls operate on subtrees of very small height, giving an amortized bound of **O(n)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Merge Sort Recurrence** | `T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn` | Divide: 2 halves; Combine: linear merge | `c` is a constant |
| **Merge Sort Time** | `Θ(n log₂ n)` | All three cases (best/avg/worst) | Asymptotic |
| **Merge Sort Space** | `Θ(n)` | Auxiliary array for merging | **Not in-place** |
| **Merge Cost per Level** | `Θ(n)` | 2^ℓ sub-problems × n/2^ℓ elements each | Constant per level |
| **Number of Levels** | `⌊log₂ n⌋ + 1` | Recursion tree depth | Integer |
| **Heap Height** | `h = ⌊log₂ n⌋` | For n-element heap | Integer |
| **Parent Index (1-idx)** | `⌊i / 2⌋` | Parent of node i | 1-indexed |
| **Left Child (1-idx)** | `2i` | Left child of node i | 1-indexed |
| **Right Child (1-idx)** | `2i + 1` | Right child of node i | 1-indexed |
| **Parent Index (0-idx)** | `⌊(i-1)/2⌋` | Parent of node i | 0-indexed |
| **Left Child (0-idx)** | `2i + 1` | Left child of node i | 0-indexed |
| **Right Child (0-idx)** | `2i + 2` | Right child of node i | 0-indexed |
| **MAX-HEAPIFY Cost** | `O(log n)` | Single restoration on subtree | Worst case |
| **BUILD-MAX-HEAP Cost** | `Θ(n)` | Bottom-up construction | **Tight bound** |
| **Heap Sort Time** | `Θ(n log n)` | Build O(n) + n × extract O(log n) | All cases |
| **Heap Sort Space** | `O(1)` | In-place (no extra array) | **Key advantage** |
| **Stability (Merge)** | **Stable** | Preserves equal-element order | Merge Sort |
| **Stability (Heap)** | **Unstable** | Swaps can reorder equals | Heap Sort |
| **Merge Comparisons (worst)** | `n - 1` | One fewer than elements | Maximum |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Merge Sort** is the algorithm of choice for **sorting linked lists** (no random access needed), **external sorting** of files larger than RAM, and is used internally by **Java's `Arrays.sort()`** for object arrays (Timsort — a merge sort variant).
- **Heap Sort** is used in **embedded systems** and **kernel-level memory management** where deterministic O(n log n) performance with zero extra memory is mandatory. Heaps also power **priority queues**, **Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm**, and **Huffman coding** for data compression.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Merge Sort Full Trace

**Input Array:** `A = [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]`
**Indices:** `low = 0`, `high = 6` → `n = 7`

### Step 3.1.1 — Top-Down Recursion Tree

| Level ℓ | Call | mid | Sub-arrays |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | `MS(A, 0, 6)` | `3` | `[38, 27, 43, 3]` ∣ `[9, 82, 10]` |
| **1** | `MS(A, 0, 3)` | `1` | `[38, 27]` ∣ `[43, 3]` |
| **1** | `MS(A, 4, 6)` | `5` | `[9, 82]` ∣ `[10]` |
| **2** | `MS(A, 0, 1)` | `0` | `[38]` ∣ `[27]` |
| **2** | `MS(A, 2, 3)` | `2` | `[43]` ∣ `[3]` |
| **2** | `MS(A, 4, 5)` | `4` | `[9]` ∣ `[82]` |
| **3** | Base case | — | All singletons |

### Step 3.1.2 — The Detailed Merge Trace

**Merge 1: Merge `[38]` and `[27]`**

$$L = [38], \quad R = [27]$$

- Compare `L[0] = 38` vs `R[0] = 27` → **27 is smaller** → write `27` to `A[0]`.
- `R` exhausted → copy remainder of `L`: write `38` to `A[1]`.
- **Result:** `A = [27, 38, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]`

**Merge 2: Merge `[43]` and `[3]`**

$$L = [43], \quad R = [3]$$

- Compare `43` vs `3` → **3 is smaller** → write `3` to `A[2]`.
- `R` exhausted → copy `43` to `A[3]`.
- **Result:** `A = [27, 38, 3, 43, 9, 82, 10]`

**Merge 3: Merge `[27, 38]` and `[3, 43]`**

$$L = [27, 38], \quad R = [3, 43]$$

- `i=0, j=0, k=0`: Compare `27` vs `3` → write `3` (k=2). `j=1`.
- `i=0, j=1, k=1`: Compare `27` vs `43` → write `27` (k=2). `i=1`.
- `i=1, j=1, k=2`: Compare `38` vs `43` → write `38` (k=3). `i=2`. L exhausted.
- Copy remainder of R: `43` → `A[3]`. k=4.
- **Result:** `A = [3, 27, 38, 43, 9, 82, 10]`

**Merge 4: Merge `[9]` and `[82]`** → `[9, 82]`. No change at A[4..5].

**Merge 5: Merge `[9, 82]` and `[10]`**

$$L = [9, 82], \quad R = [10]$$

- Compare `9` vs `10` → write `9` (k=4). `i=1`.
- Compare `82` vs `10` → write `10` (k=5). `j=1`. R exhausted.
- Copy remainder: `82` → `A[6]`. k=6.
- **Result:** `A = [3, 27, 38, 43, 9, 10, 82]`

**Merge 6 (Final): Merge `[3, 27, 38, 43]` and `[9, 10, 82]`**

$$L = [3, 27, 38, 43], \quad R = [9, 10, 82]$$

- `i=0, j=0, k=0`: `3` vs `9` → **3**. k=1, i=1.
- `i=1, j=0, k=1`: `27` vs `9` → **9**. k=2, j=1.
- `i=1, j=1, k=2`: `27` vs `10` → **10**. k=3, j=2.
- `i=1, j=2, k=3`: `27` vs `82` → **27**. k=4, i=2.
- `i=2, j=2, k=4`: `38` vs `82` → **38**. k=5, i=3.
- `i=3, j=2, k=5`: `43` vs `82` → **43**. k=6, i=4. L exhausted.
- Copy remainder of R: `82` → `A[6]`. k=7.
- **Final Result:** `A = [3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]` ✅

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Heap Sort Full Trace

**Input Array (0-indexed):** `A = [4, 10, 3, 5, 1]`
**n = 5**

### Step 3.2.1 — Build Max-Heap

We call `MAX-HEAPIFY` for `i` from `⌊(5-2)/2⌋ = 1` down to `0`.

**Call 1: `MAX-HEAPIFY(A, 1)`**
- Subtree rooted at index 1 (value 10). Children: index 3 (value 5), index 4 (value 1).
- Largest among `{10, 5, 1}` = 10 (already at root). No swap.
- **Heap:** `[4, 10, 3, 5, 1]`

**Call 2: `MAX-HEAPIFY(A, 0)`**
- Subtree rooted at index 0 (value 4). Children: index 1 (value 10), index 2 (value 3).
- Largest among `{4, 10, 3}` = 10 (at index 1). **Swap A[0] and A[1]**.
- Array: `[10, 4, 3, 5, 1]`
- Recurse on index 1 (now holds 4). Children: index 3 (value 5), index 4 (value 1).
- Largest among `{4, 5, 1}` = 5 (at index 3). **Swap A[1] and A[3]**.
- Array: `[10, 5, 3, 4, 1]`
- Recurse on index 3 (now holds 4). Index 3 has no children (children would be at 7, 8 — out of bounds).
- Stop.
- **Built Max-Heap:** `A = [10, 5, 3, 4, 1]`

### Step 3.2.2 — Sorting Loop (Extract Max Repeatedly)

**Iteration 1 (heap size 5):**
- Swap A[0] ↔ A[4]: Array becomes `[1, 5, 3, 4, 10]`. Heap now `[1, 5, 3, 4]`.
- `MAX-HEAPIFY(A, 0)` on `[1, 5, 3, 4]`: Largest of `{1, 5, 3}` = 5 (index 1). Swap A[0]↔A[1]: `[5, 1, 3, 4, 10]`. Recurse on index 1: Largest of `{1, 4}` (children at 3) = 4. Swap A[1]↔A[3]: `[5, 4, 3, 1, 10]`. No further children.
- **Array:** `[5, 4, 3, 1, 10]`

**Iteration 2 (heap size 4):**
- Swap A[0] ↔ A[3]: Array becomes `[1, 4, 3, 5, 10]`. Heap now `[1, 4, 3]`.
- `MAX-HEAPIFY(A, 0)` on `[1, 4, 3]`: Largest of `{1, 4, 3}` = 4 (index 1). Swap A[0]↔A[1]: `[4, 1, 3, 5, 10]`. Index 1 has no children. Stop.
- **Array:** `[4, 1, 3, 5, 10]`

**Iteration 3 (heap size 3):**
- Swap A[0] ↔ A[2]: Array becomes `[3, 1, 4, 5, 10]`. Heap now `[3, 1]`.
- `MAX-HEAPIFY(A, 0)` on `[3, 1]`: Already max-heap. Stop.
- **Array:** `[3, 1, 4, 5, 10]`

**Iteration 4 (heap size 2):**
- Swap A[0] ↔ A[1]: Array becomes `[1, 3, 4, 5, 10]`. Heap now `[1]`.
- Heap of size 1 — trivially sorted. No heapify needed.
- **Final Sorted Array:** `[1, 3, 4, 5, 10]` ✅

---

## 3.3 Recurrence Derivation — Merge Sort Complexity

Starting from the **Master Theorem, Case 2** application:

$$T(n) = 2T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + cn$$

**Level 0 (root):** Cost = `cn`

**Level 1:** Two sub-problems of size `n/2` each → Cost = `2 × c(n/2) = cn`

**Level 2:** Four sub-problems of size `n/4` each → Cost = `4 × c(n/4) = cn`

**General Level ℓ:** `2^ℓ` sub-problems of size `n/2^ℓ` each → Cost = `2^ℓ × c(n/2^ℓ) = cn`

**Number of levels:** Recursion stops when `n/2^ℓ = 1` → `ℓ = log₂ n`

**Total cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{\ell=0}^{\log_2 n} (\text{cost at level } \ell) \\
&= \sum_{\ell=0}^{\log_2 n} cn \\
&= cn \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) \\
&= \Theta(n \log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that **Merge Sort runs in Θ(n log n) time in all three cases** — there is no worst-case degradation.

---

## 3.4 Heap Sort Complexity Breakdown

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{heap-sort}}(n) &= T_{\text{build-heap}}(n) + n \times T_{\text{heapify}}(n) \\
&= \Theta(n) + n \times O(\log n) \\
&= O(n \log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The `n × O(log n)` term **dominates** the `Θ(n)` build phase, so the overall bound is **O(n log n)**. The build-heap term becomes negligible for large n.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Heap Sort Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT["Input Array A n elements"] --> BUILD["PHASE 1 BUILD MAX HEAP Bottom up heapify for i n/2 to 1"]
    BUILD --> HEAPOK["Valid Max Heap Root is maximum"]
    HEAPOK --> LOOPSTART{"i greater than 1"}
    LOOPSTART -->|YES| SWAP["SWAP A 1 with A i Place max at end"]
    SWAP --> SHRINK["REDUCE heap size to i 1"]
    SHRINK --> HEAPIFY["MAX HEAPIFY root 1 Restore heap property"]
    HEAPIFY --> LOOPSTART
    LOOPSTART -->|NO| SORTED["Sorted Array A ascending order"]
    SORTED --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT"]

    style INPUT fill:#4dabf7,color:#fff
    style BUILD fill:#845ef7,color:#fff
    style HEAPOK fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style SWAP fill:#ff922b,color:#fff
    style HEAPIFY fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style SORTED fill:#20c997,color:#fff
    style OUTPUT fill:#495057,color:#fff
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- The **blue block** receives unsorted input.
- The **purple block** is the O(n) build phase.
- The **green block** is the validation checkpoint (max-heap invariant holds).
- The **orange/red loop** runs `n - 1` times, each iteration doing a swap (orange) and a heapify (red).
- The **teal block** emits the final sorted output.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ORIGINAL["ORIGINAL ARRAY A"]

    end
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Visual Takeaway:** Merge Sort requires **two auxiliary arrays** (L and R) during each merge, totaling O(n) extra memory. This is why Heap Sort is preferred when memory is at a premium.
