# Digit Analysis — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual Definition
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> *Differentiate between the Digit Analysis method and the Modulo Division method of hash function construction. State one situation where Digit Analysis is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Digit Analysis | Modulo Division |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis of Computation** | Statistical frequency of digit positions across the key set | Arithmetic remainder $K \mod M$ |
| **Pre-analysis** | Required (Chi-Square test) | Not required |
| **File type** | Static files only | Both static and dynamic |
| **Distribution** | Excellent (data-tuned) | Good (depends on $M$) |
| **Computational cost** | High (one-time) | Very low (per operation) |

**Preferred situation:** When the file is **static** (e.g., a postal code lookup table) and the key set is known in advance, Digit Analysis yields near-optimal distribution with minimal collisions, justifying the upfront statistical cost.

---

### Question A2 — Formula Recall
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> *In the Digit Analysis method, how is the number of digits $m$ to be extracted determined when the hash table size is $M$? Justify with an example where $M = 1000$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The number of digits to extract is:

$$m = \lceil \log_{10} M \rceil$$

**Justification:** The extracted $m$-digit number must be able to address all $M$ slots, so its maximum value ($10^m - 1$) must be $\geq M - 1$.

**Example:** For $M = 1000$:

$$m = \lceil \log_{10} 1000 \rceil = \lceil 3 \rceil = 3$$

Three digits must be extracted because a 2-digit number can only address slots $0$–$99$, which is insufficient.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module 4 contains *one full 14-mark question* with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both choices must be answered at full depth. Each carries sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **Digit Analysis with Frequency Computation**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the Digit Analysis method of constructing a hash function. Discuss the role of the Chi-Square test in selecting digit positions.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Given the following 8 keys, construct a hash function using Digit Analysis with $M = 100$:
> $$S = \{ 481723, \, 591456, \, 672189, \, 783912, \, 894645, \, 905378, \, 216801, \, 327534 \}$$
> Show the frequency table, Chi-Square computation, position selection, and final hash table. Use **linear probing** for collision resolution (if any).

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Method Description (3 marks):**
Digit Analysis is a static hash function technique. Unlike Modulo Division, it does **not** use a fixed arithmetic formula. Instead:
1. The complete set of keys is collected.
2. The frequency of each digit at each position is recorded.
3. Digit positions with the most uniform (least biased) distribution are identified.
4. Those positions are extracted from each key to form a smaller number, which is reduced modulo $M$.

**Step 2 — Role of Chi-Square (2 marks):**
The Chi-Square statistic $\chi^2_i$ quantifies *deviation from uniform distribution* at position $i$:

$$\chi^2_i = \sum_{d=0}^{9} \frac{(f_{i,d} - \mu_i)^2}{\mu_i}$$

A *low* $\chi^2_i$ indicates the digit position behaves like a random uniform distribution, making it suitable for extraction.

**Step 3 — Constraints (2 marks):**
- File must be static.
- Bias-free positions must be carefully filtered (constant positions can artificially lower $\chi^2$ in small samples).
- $M$ is chosen as a prime number for better spread.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Frequency Table (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pos 5:} \quad & [0, 0, 1, 2, 1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1] \\
\text{Pos 4:} \quad & [0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1] \\
\text{Pos 3:} \quad & [0, 2, 0, 2, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 2] \\
\text{Pos 2:} \quad & [1, 0, 0, 2, 0, 1, 1, 2, 0, 1] \\
\text{Pos 1:} \quad & [1, 0, 1, 0, 0, 2, 0, 1, 2, 1] \\
\text{Pos 0:} \quad & [0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 0, 0, 0, 1, 2] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Chi-Square Computations (2 marks):** $\mu = 8/10 = 0.8$.

$$
\chi^2_5 = \tfrac{5.6}{0.8} = 7.0, \quad \chi^2_4 = \tfrac{4.8}{0.8} = 6.0, \quad \chi^2_3 = \tfrac{9.6}{0.8} = 12.0
$$
$$
\chi^2_2 = \tfrac{7.6}{0.8} = 9.5, \quad \chi^2_1 = \tfrac{7.6}{0.8} = 9.5, \quad \chi^2_0 = \tfrac{7.6}{0.8} = 9.5
$$

**Position Selection (1 mark):** Lowest are positions **4 (6.0)** and **5 (7.0)**. Extract $m = \lceil \log_{10} 100 \rceil = 2$ digits from positions 5 and 4.

**Hash Computation (2 marks):** $h(K) = (d_5 \cdot 10 + d_4) \mod 100$.

| Key | $d_5$ | $d_4$ | Extracted | $h(K)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 481723 | 4 | 8 | 48 | 48 |
| 591456 | 5 | 9 | 59 | 59 |
| 672189 | 6 | 7 | 67 | 67 |
| 783912 | 7 | 8 | 78 | 78 |
| 894645 | 8 | 9 | 89 | 89 |
| 905378 | 9 | 0 | 90 | 90 |
| 216801 | 2 | 1 | 21 | 21 |
| 327534 | 3 | 2 | 32 | 32 |

**No collisions** — hash table fills slots $\{21, 32, 48, 59, 67, 78, 89, 90\}$ directly.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **Comparison and Application**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Compare Digit Analysis, Mid-Square, and Folding methods of hashing. Use a comparison matrix covering: basis, complexity, static/dynamic suitability, collision resistance, and KTU examination weightage.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A university maintains a static file of **5000 employee IDs**, each exactly **8 digits long**. Design a Digit Analysis hash function for a table of size $M = 1009$ (prime). Specify:
> - The number of digits to extract.
> - The procedure for selecting which digit positions to extract.
> - The final hash function formula.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

| Criterion | Digit Analysis | Mid-Square | Folding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Statistical frequency | Square the key and take middle digits | Partition key into parts and sum |
| **Time Complexity (per op)** | $O(1)$ after pre-analysis | $O(1)$ | $O(m)$ where $m$ = number of parts |
| **Pre-analysis cost** | High | Low | Low |
| **Static files** | Excellent | Good | Good |
| **Dynamic files** | Poor (needs re-analysis) | Good | Good |
| **Collision resistance** | High (data-tuned) | Medium | Medium |
| **KTU 14-Mark likelihood** | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Distribution quality** | Excellent (with care) | Variable | Variable |

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Determine $m$ (1 mark):**

$$m = \lceil \log_{10} 1009 \rceil = \lceil 3.003 \rceil = 4 \text{ digits}$$

**Step 2 — Position Selection Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Collect all 5000 employee IDs.
2. For each of the 8 digit positions $i = 0, 1, \dots, 7$, build the frequency vector $F_i$.
3. Compute $\chi^2_i$ for each position using $\mu = 5000/10 = 500$.
4. Reject any position where one digit accounts for > 30% of occurrences (empirical bias threshold).
5. From the remaining positions, sort by $\chi^2_i$ ascending and select the **top 4**.

**Step 3 — Final Hash Function (3 marks):**
If the selected positions are $\{i_0, i_1, i_2, i_3\}$ (e.g., $\{2, 5, 1, 6\}$), then:

$$h(K) = \left( d_{i_0} \cdot 10^3 + d_{i_1} \cdot 10^2 + d_{i_2} \cdot 10 + d_{i_3} \right) \mod 1009$$

> [!NOTE]
> The modulo-1009 step guarantees the address fits in $[0, 1008]$, and primality of $M$ prevents pathological periodic collisions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state $M$ is prime** — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Selecting a constant digit position** without flagging it — Examiner deducts 1 mark (you have shown a "perfect" $\chi^2$ on a useless position).
> 3. **Skipping the Chi-Square calculation** and just listing frequencies — Examiner deducts up to 2 marks because the *analysis* is the core of Digit Analysis.
> 4. **Applying $\mod M$ incorrectly** (e.g., computing $M \mod K$ instead of $K \mod M$) — Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 5. **Failing to mention static-file assumption** — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Not using $\lceil \log_{10} M \rceil$ for $m$** and instead hardcoding a number — Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 7. **Writing $\chi^2$ formula with a vertical pipe `|` for "divides" inside a markdown table** — Table breaks; answer appears malformed; Examiner deducts 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Pin This in Your Memory**

- [x] **Digit Analysis is a STATIC method** — works only when the key set is known in advance.
- [x] The **core idea** is to *select statistically uniform digit positions*, not to apply a fixed arithmetic formula.
- [x] **Chi-Square test** $\chi^2_i = \sum (f_{i,d} - \mu)^2 / \mu$ is used to rank position quality — *lower is better*.
- [x] **Constant digit positions are deceptive traps** — they can have low $\chi^2$ in small samples but are useless for hashing.
- [x] The **number of digits to extract** is $m = \lceil \log_{10} M \rceil$.
- [x] The **final hash function** is always of the form $h(K) = \text{extracted}(K) \mod M$.
- [x] **$M$ should be prime** for minimum secondary clustering.
- [x] Digit Analysis produces **excellent distribution** but has **high one-time pre-analysis cost**.
- [x] **Do NOT use Digit Analysis for dynamic files** — re-analysis would be needed on every insertion.
- [x] **Compare and contrast** is a frequent 14-mark question — know the comparison matrix cold.
- [x] **Exam writing tip:** Always show frequency table → Chi-Square → ranking → extraction → final $h(K)$ — this 5-step template is what examiners look for.
- [x] **Markdown safety:** Use `\mid` or `\vert` in LaTeX, never bare `|`, when writing inside markdown tables.
